topics.title: Study of Internal Combustion Engine and Its Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study of Internal Combustion Engine and Its Components # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** > An **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is a prime mover that converts the chemical energy of a fuel into useful mechanical work (rotational energy) by **burning the fuel inside the working cylinder**, in contrast to an external combustion engine where combustion occurs outside the working chamber. In marine engineering practice, the IC engine is the **heart of the propulsion system**, powering almost every commercial vessel in the world fleet — from small fishing boats to massive container carriers and naval frigates. The four-stroke trunk-piston and two-stroke crosshead engines built by manufacturers like **MAN B&W, Wärtsilä, and Caterpillar-MAK** are the industry benchmarks. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Burst Balloon" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain English Analogy:** > Imagine a sealed metal syringe. You push the plunger in to compress air, then introduce a tiny spark. The air-fuel mixture **explodes** inside the syringe, violently pushing the plunger out. The energy of that push is captured as a turning force on a shaft. That is exactly how an IC engine works — the cylinder is the syringe, the piston is the plunger, the spark (or diesel injection) is the explosion, and the crankshaft captures the push. This single mental model explains the entire **four-stroke cycle** in one breath: - **Suck** (intake) → **Squeeze** (compression) → **Bang** (combustion/power) → **Blow** (exhaust) ## 1.3 Classification of IC Engines (Board-Exam Favourite) IC engines are classified along several orthogonal axes. Memorize these well — they appear almost every semester. | Classification Basis | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 | Type 4 | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Cycle** | Otto (SI) | Diesel (CI) | Dual | — | | **Strokes per cycle** | 2-Stroke | 4-Stroke | — | — | | **Fuel** | Petrol/Gasoline | Diesel | LPG/CNG | Bio-diesel | | **Ignition** | Spark Ignition (SI) | Compression Ignition (CI) | — | — | | **Cooling** | Air-Cooled | Water-Cooled | — | — | | **Speed** | Slow (60-150 rpm) | Medium (150-1000 rpm) | High (>1000 rpm) | — | | **Application (Marine)** | Main Propulsion | Auxiliary Generator | Emergency Gen-set | Deck Machinery | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marine Specific Note (PCNSL607 focus):** > - **Main propulsion**: Slow-speed 2-stroke crosshead diesel engines (e.g., MAN B&W 12K98ME-C). > - **Auxiliary power**: Medium/high-speed 4-stroke trunk piston engines (e.g., Wärtsilä Auxpac). > - **High-speed craft**: Gas turbines or 4-stroke high-speed diesels. ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following numerical values are considered **standard reference data** for board examinations: - **Air-Fuel ratio (Stoichiometric):** Petrol ≈ **14.7 : 1**, Diesel ≈ **14.5 : 1** - **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Petrol ≈ **44 MJ/kg**, Diesel ≈ **42.5 MJ/kg** - **Compression ratio:** SI engine: **8:1 to 12:1**, CI engine: **14:1 to 22:1** - **Ignition temperature:** Petrol ≈ **230 °C – 250 °C**, Diesel ≈ **210 °C – 230 °C** - **Firing order (typical 4-cyl):** 1-3-4-2 # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Strokes of a 4-Stroke Cycle A 4-stroke engine completes one working cycle in **two full revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft. Each stroke is 180°. ### Stroke 1 — Intake Stroke (0° to 180°) - The intake valve opens. - Piston moves from **Top Dead Center (TDC) → Bottom Dead Center (BDC)**. - A vacuum is created, drawing the fresh air-fuel mixture (SI) or pure air (CI) into the cylinder. - The crankshaft rotates through 180°. ### Stroke 2 — Compression Stroke (180° to 360°) - Both valves are closed. - Piston moves from **BDC → TDC**, compressing the charge. - In an SI engine: compression ratio is moderate (≈ 10:1); spark is delivered near TDC. - In a CI engine: air is compressed to a very high ratio (≈ 18:1), raising temperature above the auto-ignition point of diesel (~210 °C). - The crankshaft rotates through another 180°. ### Stroke 3 — Power/Expansion Stroke (360° to 540°) - Combustion occurs. The high-pressure gas forces the piston from **TDC → BDC**. - This is the **only** stroke that delivers useful work to the crankshaft. - Both valves remain closed (except in special scavenging designs). - The crankshaft rotates through 180°. ### Stroke 4 — Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°) - The exhaust valve opens. - Piston moves from **BDC → TDC**, expelling the burnt gases. - The cycle resets and the next intake stroke begins. > [!NOTE] > **Why are 4-stroke engines "self-balancing"?** > The four strokes are perfectly symmetric (2 up, 2 down), so primary inertial forces from reciprocating masses are intrinsically balanced — no external counterweights are required. This makes 4-stroke engines smoother and quieter. ## 2.2 The Two-Stroke Cycle (Marine Workhorse) A 2-stroke engine completes one power cycle in **one revolution (360°)** of the crankshaft. It does this by replacing the discrete intake and exhaust strokes with **scavenging** — the use of a separate blower to push fresh air into the cylinder while simultaneously displacing exhaust gases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marine Relevance:** > Almost all large ocean-going vessels use the **2-stroke, low-speed, direct-drive, crosshead, reversible marine diesel engine** for main propulsion. Examples include the **MAN B&W G-type** and **Wärtsilä-Sulzer RTA** series. These engines operate at 60–120 rpm and are coupled directly to the propeller (no gearbox). ### Scavenging Types (Board-Exam Favourite) 1. **Cross-Scavenging** — Fresh air enters from one side, exhaust leaves from the other (lowest efficiency). 2. **Loop Scavenging** — Air enters through ports near BDC, exhaust leaves through a valve in the head (uniflow loop). 3. **Uniflow Scavenging** — Air enters via side ports, exhaust leaves through poppet valves in the cylinder head (highest efficiency, used in modern marine diesels). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are exhaustively used in KTU board examinations for the Marine Engineering Lab. Memorize units carefully.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Indicated Power | $IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | kW | $p_{im}$ = indicated mean effective pressure (kPa), $L$ = stroke (m), $A$ = piston area (m²), $N$ = rpm, $n$ = no. of cylinders | | 2 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ | kW | $T$ = brake torque (N·m) | | 3 | Frictional Power | $FP = IP - BP$ | kW | Power lost to friction | | 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | fraction or % | — | | 5 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | fraction | $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel flow (kg/s), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg) | | 6 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | fraction | — | | 7 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot 3600}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better | | 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass of air}}{\text{Theoretical mass of air at NTP}}$ | % | — | | 9 | Air-Standard Otto Efficiency | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | fraction | $r$ = compression ratio, $\gamma$ = 1.4 for air | | 10 | Air-Standard Diesel Efficiency | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | fraction | $\rho$ = cut-off ratio | | 11 | Swept Volume | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$ | m³ | $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke | | 12 | Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{U}_p = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | Should be $< 10$ m/s for diesel | > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (no vertical pipes inside rows):** Absolute-value-style notation uses $\vert$ or $\mid$, never a raw $\vert$, to avoid breaking the table. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fuel Audit on a Ship**: The Engine-room logbook uses the **BSFC** formula to monitor fuel quality — a sudden rise in BSFC indicates fouled injectors, leaking valves, or poor combustion. - **Performance Curves**: Brake Power vs. RPM, BSFC vs. BP, and $\eta_{mech}$ vs. BP form the **propeller-law performance map** of a marine diesel. - **Heat Balance Sheet**: A typical marine diesel distributes heat as follows: **~40% to exhaust**, **~30% to coolant**, **~5% to radiation**, and only **~40% to useful work** (BSEC). Modern electronically controlled engines recover more from exhaust via waste-heat recovery systems (WHRS). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Air-Standard Otto Cycle Efficiency The Otto cycle is the theoretical air-standard cycle for **Spark Ignition (SI)** engines. It consists of: - Process 1→2: Isentropic compression - Process 2→3: Constant-volume heat addition - Process 3→4: Isentropic expansion - Process 4→1: Constant-volume heat rejection ### Step 1 — Define compression ratio The compression ratio is the geometric ratio of the maximum to minimum volume in the cylinder. $$r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$$ where: - $V_1$ = Volume at BDC (start of compression) = $V_c + V_s$ - $V_2$ = Volume at TDC (end of compression) = $V_c$ (clearance volume) ### Step 2 — Apply the isentropic relation for processes 1→2 and 3→4 For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) process of an ideal gas: $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$ This is the isentropic temperature relation: $TV^{\gamma - 1} = \text{constant}$. ### Step 3 — Express heat added and heat rejected At constant volume, $Q = m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T$. $$Q_{in} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$$ $$Q_{out} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)$$ ### Step 4 — Apply isentropic relations for 3→4 and 4→1 By the symmetry of the Otto cycle, applying the isentropic relation to process 3→4: $$\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$ Therefore: $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$ ### Step 5 — Form the ratio for thermal efficiency Thermal efficiency is the fraction of heat converted to work: $$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ ### Step 6 — Factor out the temperatures Using the relation $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$ and $T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$, factor both numerator and denominator: $$T_3 - T_2 = r^{\gamma - 1}(T_4 - T_1)$$ Therefore: $$\dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma - 1}(T_4 - T_1)} = \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$ ### Step 7 — Final Otto efficiency expression $$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$ **Physical insight:** Higher compression ratio → higher efficiency. This is why **CI engines (high r) are inherently more efficient** than SI engines (low r). ## 3.2 Derivation: Indicated Power from Mean Effective Pressure ### Step 1 — Define work done per cycle per cylinder Work done per cycle = Average pressure × Swept volume: $$W_{cycle} = p_{im} \cdot V_s = p_{im} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$$ where: - $p_{im}$ = Indicated mean effective pressure in **Pa** (kN/m²) - $D$ = Bore (m), $L$ = Stroke (m) ### Step 2 — Number of power strokes per second For a **4-stroke** engine, one power stroke occurs every **2 revolutions** per cylinder. So the number of power strokes per second per cylinder: $$N_{ps} = \dfrac{N}{2 \cdot 60}$$ For a **2-stroke** engine, one power stroke occurs every **1 revolution**: $$N_{ps} = \dfrac{N}{60}$$ ### Step 3 — Multiply and add the number of cylinders For $n$ cylinders, the total indicated power is: $$IP = W_{cycle} \cdot N_{ps} \cdot n = p_{im} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N}{k \cdot 60} \cdot n$$ where $k$ = number of revolutions per power stroke (1 for 2-stroke, 2 for 4-stroke). ### Step 4 — Final expression $$\boxed{IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{k \cdot 60}} \quad \text{(in kW when } p_{im} \text{ is in kPa)}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 style):** > A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder marine diesel engine has bore $D = 250$ mm, stroke $L = 300$ mm, and runs at $N = 400$ rpm. The indicated mean effective pressure is $p_{im} = 800$ kPa. Calculate the Indicated Power. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1: Convert all units to SI base.** - $D = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ - $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$ - $N = 400 \text{ rpm}$ - $p_{im} = 800 \text{ kPa} = 800 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} = 800 \text{ kN/m}^2$ - $n = 4$, $k = 2$ (4-stroke) **Step 2: Compute piston area.** $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.25)^2 = 0.049087 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3: Apply the IP formula.** $$IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{k \cdot 60}$$ $$IP = \dfrac{(800 \times 10^3) \cdot (0.30) \cdot (0.049087) \cdot (400) \cdot (4)}{2 \cdot 60}$$ **Step 4: Numerator calculation.** $$800 \times 10^3 \times 0.30 = 240{,}000$$ $$240{,}000 \times 0.049087 = 11{,}780.88$$ $$11{,}780.88 \times 400 = 4{,}712{,}352$$ $$4{,}712{,}352 \times 4 = 18{,}849{,}408$$ **Step 5: Denominator calculation.** $$2 \times 60 = 120$$ **Step 6: Final division.** $$IP = \dfrac{18{,}849{,}408}{120} = 157{,}078.4 \text{ W} \approx 157.08 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{IP \approx 157.1 \text{ kW}}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Components vs. Function vs. Failure Mode | Component | Primary Function | Material | Common Failure Mode (Lab Observation) | |---|---|---|---| | **Cylinder Block/Head** | Houses the combustion chamber; routes coolant | Cast iron / nodular cast iron | Cracking due to thermal fatigue, gasket blow-by | | **Piston** | Transmits combustion force to gudgeon pin | Al-Si alloy (SI) / cast iron (CI) | Scuffing, ring groove wear, crown cracking | | **Piston Rings (Compression + Oil)** | Seal combustion gases; control oil film | Alloy steel / cast iron | Breakage, sticking in grooves, blow-by | | **Connecting Rod** | Links piston to crankshaft | Forged steel / SG iron | Bearing failure, big-end bolt stretch | | **Crankshaft** | Converts reciprocating to rotary motion | Forged alloy steel | Fatigue cracking at fillets, journal wear | | **Camshaft** | Times valve operation | Hardened steel | Lobe wear, snapped drive gear | | **Valves (Intake/Exhaust)** | Permit charge entry & exhaust exit | Hardened steel (exh) / SS (int) | Burnt seats, stem guide wear | | **Valve Springs** | Close valves rapidly | Spring steel | Fatigue breakage, loss of tension | | **Timing Gear/Timing Chain** | Synchronizes camshaft with crankshaft | Hardened steel | Tooth wear, chain stretch | | **Fuel Injector (CI)** | Atomizes fuel at high pressure | Hardened alloy steel | Nozzle coking, spray hole erosion | | **Spark Plug (SI)** | Ignites the air-fuel mixture | Nickel/platinum alloys | Fouling, electrode erosion | | **Lubricating Oil Pump** | Circulates oil under pressure | Cast iron body | Pressure drop, gear wear | | **Cooling Water Pump** | Maintains jacket water circulation | Cast iron / bronze | Impeller erosion, seal leakage | | **Governor** | Maintains constant speed under load | Mechanical/hydraulic/electronic | Hunting, droop error, instability | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Marine Diesel Engine This Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions of major sub-systems** of a marine IC engine as they would be studied in a lab cut-away model or schematic. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["AIR INTAKE SYSTEM"] --> node2["FILTER AND SILENCER"] node2 --> node3["TURBOCHARGER COMPRESSOR"] node3 --> node4["AFTERCOOLER CHARGE COOLER"] node4 --> node5["INTAKE MANIFOLD"] node5 --> node6["INTAKE VALVE"] node6 --> node7["CYLINDER AND PISTON ASSEMBLY"] nodeH1["FUEL SUPPLY SYSTEM"] --> nodeH2["FUEL PUMP"] nodeH2 --> nodeH3["HIGH PRESSURE PIPES"] nodeH3 --> nodeH4["FUEL INJECTOR NOZZLE"] nodeH4 --> node7 node7 --> node8["EXHAUST VALVE"] node8 --> node9["EXHAUST MANIFOLD"] node9 --> node10["TURBOCHARGER TURBINE"] node10 --> node11["WASTE HEAT BOILER"] node11 --> node12["STACK SILENCER"] node7 --> node13["CRANKSHAFT AND MAIN BEARINGS"] node13 --> node14["OUTPUT FLYWHEEL AND SHAFT"] nodeL1["LUB OIL PUMP"] --> nodeL2["LUB OIL COOLER AND FILTER"] nodeL2 --> node7 nodeL2 --> node13 nodeC1["SEA WATER PUMP"] --> nodeC2["FRESH WATER COOLER"] nodeC2 --> node7 nodeG1["GOVERNOR ACTUATOR"] --> nodeH2 nodeG1 --> node13 nodeSC["STARTING AIR SYSTEM"] --> node7 classDef hot fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#330000 classDef cold fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000033 classDef mech fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#0d260d classDef ctrl fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#1a1300 class node7,node8,node9,node10,node11,node12 hot class node1,node2,node3,node4,node5,node6,nodeC1,nodeC2 cold class nodeH1,nodeH2,nodeH3,nodeH4,node13,node14,nodeL1,nodeL2 mech class nodeG1,nodeSC ctrl ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Four-Stroke Working Cycle — Valve Timing Diagram This diagram shows the state of intake and exhaust valves across the full 720° cycle of a 4-stroke engine. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Crank as CRANK ANGLE participant In as INTAKE VALVE participant Ex as EXHAUST VALVE participant Cy as CYLINDER ACTION Note over Crank,Cy: Full 4-Stroke Cycle = 720 degrees = 2 revolutions Crank->>In: 0 deg - Valve OPENS Crank->>Cy: 0 to 180 deg - Piston moves TDC to BDC Note over Cy: INTAKE STROKE - fresh charge drawn in Crank->>In: 180 deg - Valve CLOSES (slight lag in real engines) Crank->>Cy: 180 to 360 deg - Piston moves BDC to TDC Note over Cy: COMPRESSION STROKE - both valves closed Crank->>Cy: 360 deg - Combustion occurs near TDC Note over Cy: POWER STROKE - 360 to 540 deg - TDC to BDC Crank->>Ex: 480 deg approx - Exhaust valve OPENS (before BDC) Crank->>Cy: 540 to 720 deg - Piston moves BDC to TDC Note over Cy: EXHAUST STROKE - burnt gases expelled Crank->>Ex: 720 deg - Valve CLOSES Note over In,Ex: In real engines, valves open EARLY and close LATE
to account for valve lag and improve volumetric efficiency ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Conversion Path in an IC Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CHEMICAL ENERGY
in fuel"] --> B["HEAT ENERGY
from combustion"] B --> C["PRESSURE ENERGY
on piston crown"] C --> D["MECHANICAL WORK
on crankshaft"] D --> E["BRAKE POWER
at flywheel"] E --> F["USEFUL WORK
propeller or generator"] B -.->|loss| G["HEAT TO COOLANT
jacket water"] B -.->|loss| H["HEAT TO EXHAUST
flue gas"] D -.->|loss| I["FRICTIONAL POWER
bearings and pumps"] C -.->|loss| J["RADIATION AND CONVECTION"] classDef energy fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#99004d,color:#330019 classDef loss fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,color:#1a1a1a class A,B,C,D,E,F energy class G,H,I,J loss ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:** > - The **first flowchart** shows the sub-system architecture: Air + Fuel + Lub Oil + Coolant + Start Air all converge on the cylinder, where combustion converts chemical to mechanical energy, and exhaust flow recovers some energy via the turbocharger. > - The **sequence diagram** represents the valve timing: in real engines, valves do not open/close exactly at TDC/BDC — they have **lead** (early) and **lag** (late) timings measured in crank-angle degrees. > - The **third diagram** is the energy audit / Sankey-style flow showing that typically only **~40%** of fuel energy becomes useful work; the rest is lost to exhaust, coolant, and friction. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **"Differentiate clearly between a 2-stroke and a 4-stroke IC engine. State two marine applications of each."** **Model Answer (concise, KTU-key friendly):** | Feature | 2-Stroke Engine | 4-Stroke Engine | |---|---|---| | Power strokes per revolution | One (every 360°) | One (every 720°) | | Scavenging required | Yes (separate blower) | No (intake stroke pumps) | | Power-to-weight ratio | Higher (twice as many power strokes) | Lower | | Thermal efficiency | Slightly lower (scavenging losses) | Slightly higher | | Marine use – Main propulsion | Low-speed crosshead (e.g., 2-stroke MAN B&W 12K98ME-C) | Medium-speed trunk piston (e.g., 4-stroke Wärtsilä Auxpac) | | Marine use – Auxiliary | Less common for gen-sets | Widely used for shipboard diesel generators | | Governor complexity | Higher | Lower | > **Board Tip (1 mark):** Always draw a small neat table — evaluators love visual clarity. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **"With a neat sketch, explain the function of a piston ring in a marine diesel engine."** **Model Answer:** A piston ring is a split, spring-loaded circular ring fitted into grooves on the piston crown. Two primary functions: 1. **Sealing (Compression rings – usually 2 to 3):** Prevents high-pressure combustion gases from leaking past the piston into the crankcase. This maintains cylinder pressure and hence indicated mean effective pressure (imep). 2. **Oil Control (Oil scraper ring – usually 1 at the bottom):** Scrapes excess lubricating oil from the cylinder wall back into the sump, preventing oil consumption and contamination of combustion gases. > **Board Tip:** A quick sketch showing the piston with compression rings above and oil control ring below, plus arrows for gas leak and oil scrape, scores full marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU End Semester Exam, Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern as per 2024 Scheme]** ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 **(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat labelled sketch, describe the construction and working of a 4-stroke marine diesel engine. Explain the function of the turbocharger and intercooler in such an engine.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke marine diesel engine has a bore of 200 mm, stroke of 280 mm, and runs at 350 rpm. The indicated mean effective pressure is 750 kPa. The fuel consumption is 14 kg/hr, and the calorific value of diesel is 42,000 kJ/kg. Calculate (i) the Indicated Power, and (ii) the Indicated Thermal Efficiency.* --- **Model Solution to Question A (a):** **Construction (4 marks):** The 4-stroke marine diesel consists of: - **Cylinder block** with replaceable **liner** (wet-type, water-cooled). - **Cylinder head** containing inlet/exhaust valves, fuel injector, and indicator cock. - **Piston** (Al-Si alloy) with **compression rings** and **oil scraper ring**, connected via **gudgeon pin** to the connecting rod. - **Connecting rod** with big-end split, bolted to the **crankshaft journal**. - **Camshaft** driven by **timing gears** from the crankshaft, actuating valves via push-rods, rockers, and tappets. - **Lubricating oil pump**, **sea water pump**, **fresh water cooler**, and **fuel injection system**. **Working (3 marks):** The 4-stroke cycle — **Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust** — completes in 720° of crank rotation, with one power stroke every 2 revolutions. Combustion is by **compression ignition** — air is compressed to ~30 bar, raising temperature above ~600 °C, after which diesel is injected and auto-ignites. The expanding gases drive the piston down, and the exhaust valve opens near BDC to expel gases. **Turbocharger & Intercooler (within working explanation):** - **Turbocharger:** A turbine driven by exhaust gas energy drives a compressor that pressurizes the intake air (boost pressure typically 1.5 – 2.5 bar). This packs more air mass per cycle, increasing indicated power and thermal efficiency. - **Intercooler / Aftercooler:** Compressed air from the turbo is hot; passing it through a heat exchanger cooled by seawater (or charge-air water cooling) reduces its temperature, increasing its density further. --- **Model Solution to Question A (b):** **Given:** - $n = 4$ cylinders, $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $L = 280 \text{ mm} = 0.28 \text{ m}$ - $N = 350 \text{ rpm}$, $p_{im} = 750 \text{ kPa} = 750 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$ - $\dot{m}_f = 14 \text{ kg/hr} = 14/3600 \text{ kg/s} = 0.003889 \text{ kg/s}$ - $CV = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} = 42 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$ - $k = 2$ (4-stroke) **Part (i) — Indicated Power:** **Step 1:** Piston area. $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.031416 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Apply IP formula. $$IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{k \cdot 60}$$ **Step 3:** Numerator evaluation. $$p_{im} \cdot L = 750 \times 10^3 \times 0.28 = 210{,}000$$ $$210{,}000 \times 0.031416 = 6{,}597.34$$ $$6{,}597.34 \times 350 = 2{,}309{,}069$$ $$2{,}309{,}069 \times 4 = 9{,}236{,}276$$ **Step 4:** Denominator. $$k \cdot 60 = 2 \times 60 = 120$$ **Step 5:** Final IP. $$IP = \dfrac{9{,}236{,}276}{120} = 76{,}968.97 \text{ W} \approx 76.97 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{IP \approx 77.0 \text{ kW}}$$ **Part (ii) — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:** **Step 1:** Heat supplied per second. $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 0.003889 \times 42 \times 10^6 = 163{,}333 \text{ J/s} = 163.33 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2:** Indicated power in kW (from part i). $$IP = 76.97 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3:** Apply efficiency formula. $$\eta_{it} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{76.97}{163.33} = 0.4713$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{it} \approx 47.13\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit trap** — When $p_{im}$ is given in kPa, the resulting IP is in kW (NOT W). Be careful — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the kilo-prefix. > 2. **Stroke-factor trap** — Forgetting to divide by 2 in 4-stroke (or using it wrongly for 2-stroke) is the most common mistake. Write "k = 2 for 4-stroke" explicitly. > 3. **Fuel flow conversion** — $14 \text{ kg/hr}$ must be converted to $\text{kg/s}$ (divide by 3600), not 60. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 **(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare Otto and Diesel cycles. Derive the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle. Why are CI engines inherently more efficient than SI engines?* **(b) [7 Marks]** *List the major components of a 4-stroke marine diesel engine and state the function of each. Why is the cylinder head made of cast iron and the piston of aluminium alloy?* --- **Model Solution to Question B (a):** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Aspect | Otto Cycle (SI) | Diesel Cycle (CI) | |---|---|---| | Fuel | Petrol / gasoline | Diesel | | Compression ratio | 8 to 12 | 14 to 22 | | Ignition | Spark plug | Compression (auto) | | Heat addition | Constant volume | Constant pressure | | Heat rejection | Constant volume | Constant volume | | Air-standard efficiency | $1 - 1/r^{\gamma - 1}$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | | Marine use | Small boats, generator sets | All main propulsion engines | **Derivation of Otto efficiency (3 marks) — see Section 3.1 above for full derivation.** **Why CI engines are more efficient (1 mark):** A higher compression ratio is possible in CI engines because the fuel is not introduced during compression (only air is compressed), so knock/auto-ignition during compression is not a problem. Since Otto efficiency depends on $r^{\gamma - 1}$, doubling $r$ from 10 to 20 raises efficiency substantially. --- **Model Solution to Question B (b):** **Components and Functions (5 marks):** 1. **Cylinder Block & Liner** — Houses piston; liner is the renewable working surface. 2. **Cylinder Head** — Closes the cylinder; contains valves, injector, indicator cock. 3. **Piston with Rings** — Receives gas pressure; transmits force to the rod; seals the chamber. 4. **Connecting Rod** — Links piston to crankshaft; converts reciprocating to rotary motion. 5. **Crankshaft** — Output shaft; carries the flywheel; drives the camshaft via timing gear. 6. **Camshaft & Drive** — Operates inlet and exhaust valves at correct timing. 7. **Valve Gear (Push Rod, Rocker, Tappet)** — Transmits cam lobe lift to the valve. 8. **Fuel Injector & HP Pump** — Meter and atomize fuel into the cylinder at high pressure. 9. **Lub Oil Pump & Coolant Pump** — Circulate lubricants and coolant to all moving parts. 10. **Governor** — Maintains engine speed within ±2% of nominal under load changes. **Material Selection (2 marks):** - **Cylinder Head — Cast Iron:** Must withstand high combustion pressures (60–100 bar), high temperatures (~600 °C), and cyclic thermal stresses. Cast iron has excellent compressive strength, vibration damping, and high-temperature fatigue resistance. - **Piston — Aluminium-Silicon Alloy:** Aluminium has high thermal conductivity (dissipates heat quickly), low density (reduces reciprocating mass and inertial loads), and good machinability. Silicon content improves hardness and reduces thermal expansion. The lower mass also reduces bearing loads and improves engine response. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Avoid one-word functions.** Writing "valves open and close" loses 1 mark — write "intake valve admits fresh charge during intake stroke; exhaust valve expels burnt gases during exhaust stroke." > 2. **Material reasoning must include thermal/strength property.** Stating "aluminium is light" alone is not enough — add *why low mass matters* (reduced reciprocating inertia, lower bearing loads). > 3. **Don't confuse CI piston material** — in large low-speed 2-stroke marine engines, the piston is often **cast iron** with internal oil cooling, not aluminium. Mention this if the question is specifically about large main engines. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Before the Exam — Memorize These Bullet Points:** ### ✅ Core Concepts - IC engine = **internal** combustion of fuel; **prime mover** for marine propulsion. - **4 strokes** in 720°: Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust. - **2 strokes** in 360°: Power + Scavenging combined. - **SI vs CI**: Spark plug vs auto-ignition by compression; **CI has higher compression ratio** (14-22) → higher efficiency. - **2-stroke** has **twice** the power strokes per revolution of a 4-stroke of the same displacement. ### ✅ Key Formulas (must memorize with units) - $IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{k \cdot 60}$ (kW when $p_{im}$ in kPa) - $BP = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ - $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ - $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ - $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot 3600}{BP}$ kg/kWh ### ✅ Components to Know Cold - **Cylinder head, block, liner** — cast iron (high T & P strength). - **Piston** — Al-Si alloy in small/medium engines, cast iron in large 2-stroke. - **Rings** — compression (top) and oil scraper (bottom). - **Valves** — inlet is larger; exhaust uses heat-resistant alloy. - **Camshaft** — geared to crankshaft at 1:2 ratio (in 4-stroke). - **Governor** — Woodward mechanical or electronic for speed regulation. - **Turbocharger** — recovers exhaust energy to boost intake pressure. ### ✅ Marine-Specific Memory Hooks - **Main propulsion** → low-speed **2-stroke crosshead** (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä). - **Auxiliary gen-set** → medium/high-speed **4-stroke trunk piston** (Caterpillar, Wärtsilä). - **Emergency gen-set** → small 4-stroke diesel, auto-start, blackout recovery. - **Slow speed = heavy but efficient**; **High speed = light but maintenance-heavy**. ### ✅ Common Numerical Traps to Avoid 1. **4-stroke vs 2-stroke** in the IP formula — use the correct $k$. 2. **Unit conversion** — mm → m, kg/hr → kg/s, kPa → Pa, kW → W (or keep in kW with $p_{im}$ in kPa). 3. **CV in kJ/kg vs MJ/kg** — divide by 1000 if needed. 4. **Compression ratio in efficiency formula** — use the **absolute** $r$ (e.g., 18), not the **percentage**.

topics.title: Study of Boilers and Preparation for Firing Up of Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Boilers and Preparation for Firing Up of Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Marine Boiler** is a closed, pressure-rated vessel used on board ships to generate steam by transferring heat from the combustion of fuel (HFO, diesel, or LNG) to feed water. The generated steam drives the main propulsion turbine (where fitted), auxiliary turbines, oil-fired boilers' burners, fuel oil heaters, and various process services. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCNSL607)**, study of boilers encompasses recognition of boiler mountings, accessories, safety devices, water-tube/fire-tube construction differences, and the standardized **preparation for firing up** sequence mandated by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, ABS, DNV) and the **SOLAS Convention**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCNSL607 - Module 2):**
> The laboratory record requires students to (a) identify the type and main components of a marine boiler, (b) explain the purpose of every mounting and safety valve, and (c) demonstrate the **pre-firing checks** as per the ship's Standing Orders. Marks are typically awarded for sequence-correct procedures, not merely listing steps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker That Powers a Ship"

Imagine a domestic **pressure cooker** on a gas stove. Water goes in, the lid is sealed, heat is applied, and steam builds up inside. On a ship, this exact principle is scaled up a thousand-fold:

| Domestic Analogy | Marine Boiler Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pressure cooker body | Boiler shell / steam drum |
| Cooking gas burner | Oil-fired burner (FO/LDO) |
| Pressure regulator weight | Safety valve spring setting |
| Steam whistle | Whistle / main steam line |
| Cooker gasket | Manhole / handhole gaskets |
| Water level indicator | Gauge glass & water level column |

The key difference is that a marine boiler is a **precision-engineered, code-stamped pressure vessel** operating typically at **7 bar to 60 bar** (modern high-pressure water-tube units on LNG carriers reach **up to 100 bar**) and superheated temperatures between **250 °C and 540 °C**. A single uncontrolled release of energy could rupture the vessel, hence the elaborate pre-firing ritual.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for KTU Exams:**
> - Marine boiler working pressure range: **7–60 bar** (auxiliary); **up to 100 bar** (main high-pressure, modern)
> - Steam temperature range: **Saturated (179 °C @ 10 bar) to Superheated (510–540 °C)**
> - Boiler efficiency (modern composite design): **≥ 88%**
> - Typical steam output: **25,000 – 100,000 kg/h** for a single auxiliary unit

### 1.3 Classification of Marine Boilers (KTU Must-Know)

Marine boilers are broadly classified as:

1. **Fire-tube boilers** (Scotch, Cochran, Locomotive) — used as **auxiliary / donkey boilers** for low-pressure services.
2. **Water-tube boilers** (D-type, A-type, Babcock & Wilcox, Foster Wheeler, LaMont) — used as **main and high-pressure auxiliary boilers**.
3. **Waste Heat Boilers (WHB)** — recover exhaust gas heat from main engines.
4. **Composite / Dual-pressure boilers** — modern combined designs.
5. **Exhaust Gas/Economizer/Superheater integrated units** — found on modern merchant vessels and LNG carriers (ME-GI/ME-GIE plants).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU viva, always remember: **"Fire-tube = simple, low pressure, auxiliary. Water-tube = complex, high pressure, main."** Examiners reward students who link pressure-rating with type.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Marine Auxiliary Oil-Fired Boiler

The boiler functions as a **closed thermodynamic loop**, executing the **Rankine cycle** in miniature. The pre-firing sequence ensures every link in this loop is verified before ignition.

**Step-by-step operational flow (with 'Why' justification):**

- **Step 1: Water Level Verification** — *Why:* A low water level exposes furnace tubes, leading to **tube burnout** within seconds. The crown of the furnace can reach 1500 °C+, far above steel's critical temperature.
- **Step 2: Ventilation (Pre-purge)** — *Why:* Any residual oil mist or unburnt fuel in the furnace will detonate on ignition (causing a **furnace explosion / backfire**). 4–6 air changes at 0.5–1 m/s purge velocity clears combustible residues. **SOLAS mandates minimum 2 purges with flame failure detection.**
- **Step 3: Low Fire Ignition Trial** — *Why:* A pilot flame first establishes a stable ignition source before main oil admission. This prevents oil accumulation.
- **Step 4: Flame Monitoring** — *Why:* **Photoelectric flame scanners** (UV or IR type) verify continuous combustion. A flame failure triggers a **3-second safety trip** (FO valve closes, FD/ID fans coast down).
- **Step 5: Pressure Build-up to Working Pressure** — *Why:* Steam must reach rated pressure to ensure downstream consumers (FO heaters, turbines, laundry, galley) function correctly.
- **Step 6: Slow Draft Transition & Light-up of Additional Burners** — *Why:* Thermal shock to refractory and tubing must be avoided. A ramp of 1 °C/min is standard.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-locked** — they appear repeatedly in KTU Part B questions and are expected to be memorized verbatim.

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot L_v$ | Heat absorbed to vaporize feed water (latent load) | kJ/h |
| 2 | $m_s = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{(h_g - h_f) + c_p \cdot \Delta T_{SH}}$ | Steam generation rate from enthalpy balance | kg/s |
| 3 | $V_{purge} = N \cdot V_{furnace}$ | Purge volume = number of air changes × furnace volume | m³ |
| 4 | $t_{purge} = \dfrac{V_{purge}}{Q_{air}}$ | Purge time = purge volume / airflow rate | seconds |
| 5 | $P_{sv} = 1.03 \times P_{design}$ | Safety valve set pressure (per IBR / Class rules) | bar |
| 6 | $A_{sv} = \dfrac{m_{steam}}{K \cdot P_{sv}}$ | Required safety valve discharge area | mm² |
| 7 | $W_{water} = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic test water requirement (where $\rho$ = density, $A$ = cross-section, $h$ = fill height) | kg |
| 8 | $V_{steam} = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{P \cdot M}$ | Steam specific volume (ideal gas approximation for low P) | m³/kg |
| 9 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (h_{out} - h_{in})}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV_{fuel}}$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 10 | $D_{NaOH} = \dfrac{V_{water} \times (P - P_{target})}{10^4}$ | Phosphate dosing (where P = current, P_target = required alkalinity) | ml or L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU answer scripts, when writing equations, **never** use the `|` character inside tables for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead — this preserves markdown rendering on the online evaluation portal.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pre-firing sequence studied in this experiment is not academic — it is the **exact protocol** the Second Engineer follows every morning at 0400 hours in a ship's Engine Room. The same logic underpins:

- **Power plant boilers** (NTPC, Tata Power stations) — 660 MW supercritical units use analogous light-up procedures.
- **Process industry heaters** (refineries, petrochemicals) where incorrect pre-firing has caused catastrophic furnace explosions like the 2005 Texas City refinery incident.
- **Nuclear auxiliary boilers** where the consequences of incorrect light-up are compounded.

Understanding this experiment makes the student eligible for **Marine Engineer's Watchkeeping Certificate** issuance by the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS), Government of India**, which is the actual industry deliverable of PCNSL607.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Purge Time for a Marine Oil-Fired Boiler

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B style):**
A marine auxiliary boiler has a furnace volume of **18 m³**. The forced-draft fan delivers **3.6 m³/s** of air. SOLAS requires a minimum of **6 air changes** before ignition. Calculate the minimum purge time.

**Given Data (extracted from question):**
- Furnace volume: $V_{furnace} = 18\ m^3$
- Fan airflow: $Q_{air} = 3.6\ m^3/s$
- Required air changes: $N = 6$ (SOLAS minimum)

**Step 1 — Establish the purge volume equation**

The total volume of air that must pass through the furnace is the furnace volume multiplied by the number of complete air changes required. This is because each air change replaces 100% of the air inside the furnace with fresh air, ensuring all residual unburnt fuel vapours are displaced.

$$V_{purge} = N \cdot V_{furnace}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

We substitute the given values into the equation. Note that the unit of $V_{purge}$ will be m³ since both $N$ (dimensionless) and $V_{furnace}$ (m³) yield m³.

$$V_{purge} = 6 \times 18\ m^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the purge volume**

Multiplying 6 by 18 gives 108. The unit m³ carries forward.

$$V_{purge} = 108\ m^3$$

**Step 4 — Establish the purge time equation**

The purge time is the total purge volume divided by the volumetric airflow rate of the forced-draft fan. This makes intuitive sense — a fan delivering more air per second will clear the furnace faster.

$$t_{purge} = \dfrac{V_{purge}}{Q_{air}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values into the time equation**

We substitute the calculated purge volume and the given fan flow rate.

$$t_{purge} = \dfrac{108\ m^3}{3.6\ m^3/s}$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation**

Performing the division: 108 ÷ 3.6 = 30. The units m³/m³/s simplify to seconds.

$$t_{purge} = 30\ s$$

**Result:** The minimum purge time is **30 seconds** before pilot ignition. The engineer should round up to **45–60 seconds** in practice to account for instrumentation lag and duct dead volumes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Safety Valve Sizing for a Marine Boiler

**Problem Statement:**
A marine boiler generates **12,000 kg/h** of saturated steam at a design pressure of **10 bar**. The safety valve is set at **1.03 × design pressure**. The discharge coefficient $K$ = 0.5 (for saturated steam). Calculate the required minimum discharge area of the safety valve in mm².

**Given:**
- Steam output: $m = 12{,}000\ kg/h = 3.33\ kg/s$
- Design pressure: $P_d = 10\ bar$
- Set pressure: $P_{sv} = 1.03 \times 10 = 10.3\ bar = 1.03\ \times 10^6\ Pa$
- Discharge coefficient: $K = 0.5$

**Step 1 — Set pressure calculation**

The safety valve opening pressure is taken as 3% above the design (working) pressure, as mandated by IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations) and classification societies. This buffer prevents the valve from lifting during normal pressure fluctuations.

$$P_{sv} = 1.03 \times P_d$$

Substituting the design pressure value:

$$P_{sv} = 1.03 \times 10\ bar$$

Computing the multiplication yields the set pressure:

$$P_{sv} = 10.3\ bar$$

**Step 2 — Convert to SI units (Pa)**

Since SI units are required for the discharge area formula, we convert bar to Pascal. Recall that 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa.

$$P_{sv} = 10.3 \times 10^5\ Pa$$

$$P_{sv} = 1.03 \times 10^6\ Pa$$

**Step 3 — Apply the safety valve discharge area formula**

The required area depends on the steam mass flow rate that the valve must safely vent. The empirical discharge equation uses the set pressure and a coefficient $K$ that accounts for valve geometry and steam quality.

$$A_{sv} = \dfrac{m}{K \cdot P_{sv}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values**

Inserting $m = 3.33$ kg/s, $K = 0.5$, and $P_{sv} = 1.03 \times 10^6$ Pa:

$$A_{sv} = \dfrac{3.33}{0.5 \times 1.03 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator**

First, calculate the product of $K$ and $P_{sv}$:

$$0.5 \times 1.03 \times 10^6 = 5.15 \times 10^5$$

**Step 6 — Final division**

Now divide the mass flow rate by the denominator:

$$A_{sv} = \dfrac{3.33}{5.15 \times 10^5}$$

$$A_{sv} = 6.466 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2$$

**Step 7 — Convert m² to mm²**

Since 1 m² = 10⁶ mm², multiply by 10⁶:

$$A_{sv} = 6.466 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6\ mm^2$$

$$A_{sv} = 6.466\ mm^2$$

**Result:** Minimum required discharge area ≈ **6.5 mm²**. In practice, a safety valve of at least **20–25 mm²** nominal area is fitted with a 10% margin.

> [!NOTE]
> The simplified formula above uses the mass flux form. For exam purposes, the **formulas in the table are sufficient** — examiners don't expect ASME-derived orifice calculations in Part B.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pre-firing Steps in Fire-tube vs Water-tube Boilers

| Pre-firing Check | Fire-tube Boiler | Water-tube Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| Water level check | Single gauge glass on shell | Multiple gauge glasses on steam drum + downcomers |
| Pre-purge duration | 2–3 air changes | 4–6 air changes (mandatory) |
| Refractory dry-out | Not required | Mandatory slow dry-out for new boilers (8–12 hours) |
| Hydrostatic test | Annually | Annually + drum inspection |
| Safety valves | 1 spring-loaded | 2 spring-loaded (one for testing) |
| Low water cut-out | Float-type | Probe-type + triple redundancy |
| Light-up time | 30–45 min | 1.5–2 hours (thermal stress management) |
| Class surveyor present | Yes (every 4 years) | Yes (annual internal inspection) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Pre-firing Sequence of a Marine Oil-fired Boiler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Watchkeeper Reports for Duty]) --> stepB[Read Standing Orders and Boiler Log]
    stepB --> stepC{External Visual Inspection}
    stepC --> stepD[Check Manhole and Handhole Gaskets]
    stepD --> stepE[Verify Fuel Oil Supply and Temperature]
    stepE --> stepF[Check Water Level in Gauge Glass]
    stepF --> stepG{Is Water Level Normal?}
    stepG -- No --> stopA([STOP - Feed Water / Investigate Cause])
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH[Test Low Water Level Cut-out]
    stepH --> stepI[Test Flame Failure Trip]
    stepI --> stepJ[Test Safety Valve Lifting Lever]
    stepJ --> stepK[Start Forced Draft Fan]
    stepK --> stepL[Pre-purge 6 Air Changes]
    stepL --> stepM[Ignite Pilot Burner]
    stepM --> stepN{Is Pilot Flame Established?}
    stepN -- No --> tripA([Flame Failure Trip - Repeat Purge])
    stepN -- Yes --> stepO[Admit Main Oil - Low Fire]
    stepO --> stepP[Monitor Steam Pressure Rise]
    stepP --> stepQ{Pressure Reaches Working Setpoint?}
    stepQ -- No --> stepR[Continue Ramp-up]
    stepQ -- Yes --> stepS[Transfer to Automatic Combustion Control]
    stepS --> finishA([Boiler Online - Log Entry])
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Major Components of a Marine Water-tube Boiler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedWater[Feed Water System]
        FW[Feed Water Tank] --> FP[Feed Pump]
    end
    subgraph Boiler[Boiler Pressure Parts]
        ECON[Economizer] --> DRUM[Steam and Water Drum]
        DRUM --> DS[Downcomers]
        DS --> HDR[Header and Risers]
        HDR --> DRUM
        DRUM --> SH[Superheater]
    end
    subgraph Combustion[Combustion System]
        BURN[Oil Burner] --> FURN[Furnace]
        FURN --> GAS[Hot Gas Path]
        GAS --> ECON
    end
    subgraph Steam[Steam Output]
        SH --> MAIN[Main Steam Line]
    end
    FP --> ECON
    BURN --> FURN
```

### 4.3 Diagram — Functional Safety Interlocks (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Flame Scanner UV] --> L1{Flame Detected}
    S2[Water Level Probe] --> L2{Level Normal}
    S3[Steam Pressure Transmitter] --> L3{Pressure within Range}
    S4[FD Fan Running Contact] --> L4{Fan Running}
    L1 --> AND1{AND Gate}
    L2 --> AND1
    L3 --> AND1
    L4 --> AND1
    AND1 -- All True --> OK[Combustion Permitted]
    AND1 -- Any False --> TRIP[Master Trip - 3 sec FO Valve Closure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for students:** The AND-gate logic means **all four interlocks must be satisfied** before oil admission. The 3-second flame failure detection is a **SOLAS Chapter II-2 / IGF code requirement** — examiners love testing this number.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any six safety mountings fitted on a marine boiler and state the function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Safety Valve** — relieves overpressure by lifting at set pressure.
2. **Water Gauge Glass** — indicates water level inside the boiler.
3. **Pressure Gauge** — shows working pressure in bar.
4. **Low Water Level Cut-out** — trips burner on low water to prevent burnout.
5. **High Steam Pressure Trip** — shuts off oil on excess pressure.
6. **Flame Failure Device** — closes FO valve on loss of flame within 3 sec.
7. *(Optional)* **Manhole/Handhole** — provides access for internal inspection.
8. *(Optional)* **Blow-down Valve** — removes sludge and scales.

> [!TIP]
> KTU award: 0.5 mark per correct point, 0 mark for incorrect — so quantity + accuracy both matter.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by 'pre-purge' of a marine boiler? Why is it carried out?*

**Model Answer:**

**Pre-purge** is the process of passing fresh air through the boiler furnace and gas passes for a defined period (typically 4–6 air changes) **before** the ignition of fuel. It is carried out to:
- Remove any residual unburnt fuel oil vapour from a previous firing cycle.
- Eliminate pockets of combustible gas mixture that could cause a **furnace explosion / backfire** during ignition.
- Satisfy the **SOLAS** and classification society requirement for safe boiler light-up.
- Confirm the integrity of the **flame failure detection system** by establishing a no-flame baseline.

A typical pre-purge duration for a 60 t/h boiler is **45–60 seconds** at 0.5 m/s air velocity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either Option A OR Option B**. Both options are designed to test different cognitive levels (Apply / Analyze).

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Babcock & Wilcox water-tube marine boiler**. Compare it with a Cochran fire-tube boiler on at least 4 points.

**(b) [7 marks]** A marine auxiliary boiler has the following specifications:
- Working pressure: **8 bar**
- Steam output: **8,000 kg/h**
- Furnace volume: **12 m³**
- Forced-draft fan: **2.5 m³/s**
- SOLAS minimum air changes: **5**

Calculate:
1. The minimum pre-purge time required.
2. The set pressure of the safety valve.
3. The minimum safety valve discharge area (assume $K = 0.5$).

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **preparation for firing up of a marine boiler** as per the ship's standing orders. Cover a minimum of 12 sequential checks.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **functions of the following boiler mountings and accessories**, with a labelled sketch of each:
1. Safety valve (Spring-loaded type)
2. Water level column with gauge glass
3. Pressure gauge with siphon
4. Feed check valve
5. Blow-down valve

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Part B — Question A

#### Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Babcock & Wilcox Boiler — Construction (3 marks):**

The B&W boiler is a **horizontal, straight-tube, three-pass water-tube boiler** with the following key components:
- **Steam and water drum** — upper, contains steam space + water space.
- **Mud drum** — lower, collects sediment and sludge.
- **Bank of tubes** — inclined or straight, connecting upper and lower drums.
- **Headers** — distribution chambers for water entry and steam exit.
- **Furnace** — large refractory-lined chamber where combustion occurs.
- **Superheater** — fitted in the second pass.
- **Mountings** — safety valves (2 nos), gauge glass, pressure gauge, manhole, blow-down valve.

**Working (2 marks):** Feed water enters the drum, descends through downcomers, rises through the heated tubes, gets converted to steam, returns to the steam space, and exits through the superheater to the main steam line.

**Comparison with Cochran Fire-tube Boiler (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Aspect | B&W (Water-tube) | Cochran (Fire-tube) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure rating | Up to 60 bar | Up to 10 bar |
| Steam output | High (up to 100 t/h) | Low (max 5 t/h) |
| Response to load | Quick | Slow (heavy shell mass) |
| Risk of explosion | Low (small water content) | High (large water volume) |

---

#### Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Pre-purge volume** (1.5 marks)

$$V_{purge} = N \cdot V_{furnace} = 5 \times 12 = 60\ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Pre-purge time** (1.5 marks)

$$t_{purge} = \dfrac{V_{purge}}{Q_{air}} = \dfrac{60}{2.5} = 24\ s$$

Engineer should add 50% safety margin → **~36 seconds minimum**.

**Step 3 — Safety valve set pressure** (1.5 marks)

$$P_{sv} = 1.03 \times P_d = 1.03 \times 8 = 8.24\ bar = 8.24 \times 10^5\ Pa$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow conversion** (1 mark)

$$m = \dfrac{8{,}000}{3{,}600} = 2.22\ kg/s$$

**Step 5 — Safety valve area** (1.5 marks)

$$A_{sv} = \dfrac{2.22}{0.5 \times 8.24 \times 10^5} = 5.39 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2 = 5.39\ mm^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert **kg/h → kg/s**. Marks lost: 1 out of 7. Another common error is omitting the **safety margin** on purge time — examiners deduct 0.5 mark for unrealistic exact numbers. Always write "≈ 36 s with safety margin" instead of the bare 24 s.

---

### 5.4 Model Solutions for Part B — Question B

#### Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks (12 Sequential Checks, 0.5 mark each + 1 mark for sequence)

| # | Check | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read standing orders, boiler log, last entry | Identify defects carried forward |
| 2 | External inspection of boiler casing, refractory, ducting | Detect cracks, hot spots |
| 3 | Verify fuel oil supply, temperature (90–110 °C for HFO) | Atomisation quality |
| 4 | Check lube oil for FD fan and feed pump bearings | Prevents seizure |
| 5 | Verify feed water tank level and quality | TDS < 3500 ppm ideally |
| 6 | Check gauge glass — water level between ½ and ¾ | Prevents tube burnout |
| 7 | Test low water level cut-out (drain gauge glass) | Confirms trip function |
| 8 | Test flame failure device (close FO valve, check trip in 3 s) | Confirms safety interlock |
| 9 | Test safety valve lifting lever manually | Ensures valve is free |
| 10 | Start FD fan, run pre-purge for 4–6 air changes | Removes unburnt fuel |
| 11 | Ignite pilot burner, verify flame scanner signal | Establishes ignition source |
| 12 | Admit main oil on low fire, gradually ramp up | Avoid thermal shock |
| 13 | Bring pressure to working setpoint, transfer to auto | Completes light-up |

---

#### Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks (Functions, 1 mark each + 2 marks for sketches)

1. **Safety Valve (Spring-loaded):** Lifts automatically at set pressure (1.03 × design) to vent excess steam. The spring tension is adjusted by the compression screw. A lifting lever is provided for manual testing.

2. **Water Level Column with Gauge Glass:** Provides direct visual indication of water level inside the drum. Has a top and bottom isolating valve and a drain valve for blow-down testing.

3. **Pressure Gauge with Siphon:** The siphon (water trap) isolates the Bourdon tube from hot steam. The gauge reads pressure in bar; red mark indicates the design/maximum permissible.

4. **Feed Check Valve:** A non-return valve fitted on the feed line. It allows feed water to enter the boiler but prevents backflow of water/steam when feed pump stops.

5. **Blow-down Valve:** Located at the lowest point of the boiler, used to remove **sludge, scale, and high-TDS water** to maintain water quality. Operated daily as per water treatment schedule.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in B(b):** Students often write "pressure gauge measures pressure" without mentioning the **siphon** function. The siphon is a **must-mention** because it protects the Bourdon tube from direct steam heat — losing 1 mark here is routine.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Boiler pressure range:** 7 bar (auxiliary fire-tube) → 60 bar (main water-tube) → 100 bar (modern LNG carrier high-pressure).
- ✅ **Pre-purge:** 4–6 air changes, ~0.5 m/s air velocity, duration 30–60 s for typical auxiliary.
- ✅ **Flame failure trip time:** **3 seconds** (SOLAS / IGF Code requirement) — this number is a KTU favourite.
- ✅ **Safety valve set pressure:** **1.03 × design pressure** (IBR rule).
- ✅ **Two safety valves** are mandatory on water-tube boilers (one in service, one isolated for testing).
- ✅ **Low water cut-out** is the **most critical** safety device — water-tube boilers have **triple redundancy** (probe + float + gauge glass watch).
- ✅ **Gauge glass** water level should be maintained at **½ to ¾** of the visible range.
- ✅ **Feed water TDS** target: **< 3500 ppm**; **pH** target: **10.5–11.5**; alkalinity (P-alkalinity): **100–150 ppm**.
- ✅ **HFO pre-heat temperature** for atomisation: **90–110 °C** (viscosity < 15 cSt).
- ✅ **Boiler thermal efficiency** (modern composite): **≥ 88%**; older units: **80–85%**.
- ✅ **Babcock & Wilcox** = water-tube, high pressure, high capacity. **Cochran** = fire-tube, low pressure, low capacity, vertical.
- ✅ **Sequence of light-up:** Visual inspection → Water level → Test trips → FD fan → Pre-purge → Pilot → Main oil → Pressure ramp → Auto mode.
- ✅ **Tube burnout** occurs in **seconds** if exposed due to low water — this is the most catastrophic boiler failure mode.
- ✅ **Furnace explosion** risk is highest during **light-up and shutdown** — pre-purge and post-purge both mandatory.
- ✅ **Classification society inspection:** Annual internal + 4-yearly hydraulic test for pressure parts.

> **End of PCNSL607 — Module Experiment 2 Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Timing Diagram of Four Stroke Vertical Diesel Engine and Post Timing Diagram of Two Stroke Petrol Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Valve Timing Diagram of Four Stroke Vertical Diesel Engine and Post Timing Diagram of Two Stroke Petrol Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Valve Timing Diagram** is a graphical representation (typically plotted on a degree-of-crank-angle basis) that depicts the exact instants at which the intake and exhaust valves of an **Internal Combustion (IC) engine** open and close, relative to the position of the piston at Top Dead Centre (TDC) and Bottom Dead Centre (BDC). For a **four-stroke vertical diesel engine**, the diagram is plotted on the **x-axis as crank angle (0° to 720°)** and the **y-axis as piston displacement / valve lift / port opening status**.

A **Port Timing Diagram** is the equivalent representation for a **two-stroke petrol engine**, where, instead of poppet valves operated by camshafts, **uncovered ports** (intake, transfer, and exhaust) in the cylinder liner control the gas exchange process. The piston acts as the sliding valve that opens and closes these ports as it reciprocates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment is purely graphical and observational. Students are expected to draw the diagrams to scale on graph paper using standard crank angle conventions and label every event with its standard abbreviation (IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC for the 4-stroke; IPO, TPO, EPO, EPC, TPC for the 2-stroke).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **valve timing diagram as a "choreography sheet" for an engine's breath cycle** — just as a symphony conductor tells the violins and the trumpets exactly when to come in, the timing diagram tells the **intake valve** (the "inhale" singer) and the **exhaust valve** (the "exhale" singer) precisely when to begin and stop playing during the four-stroke ballet.

For the 4-stroke engine, **mechanical valves** are the singers. For the 2-stroke petrol engine, imagine the **piston itself as a sliding curtain** that covers and uncovers **windows (ports)** in the cylinder wall — as the curtain moves up and down, fresh charge rushes in and burnt gases escape. The "timing" is therefore the precise height (i.e., crank angle) at which the curtain exposes each window.

### 1.3 Key Conventions and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Crank Angle Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **0°** is taken as the **Inner Dead Centre (IDC)** of the piston during the **power stroke** (TDC of compression/expansion).
> - **180°** is the **Outer Dead Centre (ODC)** of the power stroke (BDC).
> - **360°** is the next TDC (start of exhaust stroke).
> - **540°** is the next BDC.
> - **720°** completes the full 4-stroke cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Marine 4-Stroke Diesel Timing (Slow-Speed):**
> - **IVO** (Intake Valve Opens) = 10° to 20° **before TDC**
> - **IVC** (Intake Valve Closes) = 20° to 35° **after BDC**
> - **EVO** (Exhaust Valve Opens) = 40° to 60° **before BDC**
> - **EVC** (Exhaust Valve Closes) = 10° to 15° **after TDC**

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical 2-Stroke Petrol Port Timing (SI Engine):**
> - **EPO** (Exhaust Port Opens) = 65° to 70° **before BDC**
> - **TPO** (Transfer Port Opens) = 50° to 55° **before BDC**
> - **TPC** (Transfer Port Closes) = 50° to 55° **after BDC**
> - **EPC** (Exhaust Port Closes) = 50° to 55° **after BDC**
> - The **intake port (to crankcase)** is opened earlier by the descending piston in the crankcase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Four-Stroke Diesel Engine

The four-stroke diesel cycle is completed over **two full revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft. The four strokes are:

1. **Suction (Intake) Stroke** — Piston moves from TDC to BDC; the **intake valve is open**, drawing fresh air into the cylinder. The exhaust valve remains closed.
2. **Compression Stroke** — Piston moves from BDC to TDC; both valves are closed; air is compressed to a high pressure (typically **30 to 55 bar** in marine slow-speed diesels) and a high temperature (above the auto-ignition temperature of **~300°C**).
3. **Power (Expansion) Stroke** — Fuel is injected near TDC; combustion occurs at approximately **constant pressure**; the high-pressure gases push the piston down to BDC, delivering the work stroke.
4. **Exhaust Stroke** — The piston moves from BDC to TDC; the exhaust valve is open, pushing out the burnt gases.

**Why valve events are advanced/retarded (not at exact TDC/BDC):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Valves do NOT open/close instantaneously.** The cam profile takes finite time to lift the valve, and gas flow has inertia. Events are therefore timed to coincide with **maximum volumetric efficiency** and **minimum pumping loss**.
> - **IVO before TDC:** Ensures the valve is **fully open** by the time the piston starts moving down, so flow area is maximum when the suction vacuum is greatest. Also helps in **blow-down** of residual gases.
> - **IVC after BDC:** Uses the **ram effect** of the incoming air column to pack more charge into the cylinder — a phenomenon called **"valve lag"** or **"inertia charging."**
> - **EVO before BDC:** Allows **blow-down** of high-pressure exhaust gases while pressure differential is maximum, reducing work required by the piston to push exhaust out.
> - **EVC after TDC:** Ensures complete scavenging of residual burnt gases using the inertia of the exhaust gas column.

### 2.2 Operational Concept — Two-Stroke Petrol Engine (Port Timing)

The two-stroke engine completes its working cycle in **one revolution (360°)**. The crankcase is used as a scavenging pump. There are three ports in the cylinder liner:

- **Intake Port (I)** — Connects the carburettor to the crankcase. Opened/closed by the piston skirt.
- **Transfer Port (T)** — Transfers the fresh charge from the crankcase to the cylinder above the piston.
- **Exhaust Port (E)** — Allows burnt gases to escape to the exhaust manifold.

**Sequence of Events:**

| Crank Angle Range | Piston Motion | Port Status | Process |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0° → 50° before BDC | Descending | E opens first, T opens later | **Blow-down + Scavenging** |
| 50° before BDC → BDC | Descending | All ports open | **Scavenging (loop or cross flow)** |
| BDC → 50° after BDC | Ascending | E closes first (T stays open) | **Delivery of charge** |
| 50° after BDC → TDC | Ascending | All ports closed | **Compression** |
| TDC (ignition) | Near TDC | All closed | **Combustion + Power** |
| TDC → next BDC | Descending | All closed initially | **Expansion (power stroke)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the exhaust port opens first:** The exhaust gases are at high pressure (high back-pressure) at the end of the power stroke. Opening the exhaust port first allows the gases to **blow down** naturally (Blow-down period), and the resulting low pressure in the cylinder then allows the transfer port (opening shortly after) to push fresh charge in effectively (**scavenging period**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Relation | Typical Value (Marine) |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Crank angle for 4-stroke cycle | $\theta_{cycle}$ | $2 \times 360° = 720°$ | 720° |
| Crank angle for 2-stroke cycle | $\theta_{cycle}$ | $1 \times 360° = 360°$ | 360° |
| Piston velocity | $v_p$ | $v_p = r\omega\left[\sin\theta + \frac{r}{L}\sin 2\theta\right]$ | varies |
| Mean piston speed | $\bar{v_p}$ | $\bar{v_p} = 2LN$ (m/s) | 6–9 m/s |
| Volumetric efficiency (4-stroke) | $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$ | 0.80–0.92 |
| Scavenging efficiency (2-stroke) | $\eta_{sc}$ | $\eta_{sc} = \frac{m_{air,retained}}{m_{air,supplied}}$ | 0.65–0.85 |
| Valve overlap angle | $\theta_{overlap}$ | $\theta_{overlap} = \text{EVC} + \text{IVO} - 360°$ (in 4-stroke) | 20°–60° |
| Port overlap angle (2-stroke) | $\theta_{overlap}$ | $\theta_{overlap} = \text{EPO opening} - \text{EPO closing (mod 360°)}$ | 100°–120° |
| Theoretical scavenging time | $t_{sc}$ | $t_{sc} = \frac{\theta_{sc}}{6N}$ (s) | 0.005–0.01 s |
| IMEP (Indicated Mean Effective Pressure) | $P_{im}$ | $P_{im} = \frac{W_{i}}{V_{s}}$ | 8–15 bar |
| Specific fuel consumption | $sfc$ | $sfc = \frac{m_{f}}{W_{i}}$ | 160–220 g/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Sign Convention:**
> - All "**before TDC/BDC**" events are marked as **negative degrees** or as a **lead** angle.
> - All "**after TDC/BDC**" events are marked as **positive degrees** or as a **lag** angle.
> - The **valve overlap** in a 4-stroke engine is the angular interval during which both intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously — this occurs around **TDC of the exhaust-intake crossover** (i.e., at the end of exhaust stroke / start of intake stroke, at **360°** in the 0°–720° diagram).

### 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

- **Engine Tuning & Diagnostics:** A deviation of the actual valve timing from the manufacturer's specification (read off the flywheel timing marks) indicates **worn camshaft lobes, stretched timing chains/gears, or incorrect tappet clearance**.
- **Performance Optimisation:** In slow-speed marine 2-stroke diesels, advancing/retarding port timing alters the **scavenging efficiency** and **specific fuel consumption** (sfc), directly impacting bunker fuel cost (a major operating expense for a vessel).
- **Emission Control:** Modern marine engines use **variable valve timing (VVT)** and **exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)** to meet **IMO Tier II / Tier III** emission regulations; the port/valve timing diagram is the foundation for these advanced strategies.
- **Troubleshooting:** Black smoke, misfiring, poor starting, and low power can all be traced back to incorrect valve/port timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Valve Overlap Angle (4-Stroke)

The **valve overlap** is the period during which both the intake and exhaust valves are open at the same time, occurring around the **TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke** (i.e., the TDC at **360°** in the standard 0°–720° plot).

Let:
- $EVC$ = Exhaust Valve Closes angle (in degrees from TDC at 0°, measured in the +ve direction, i.e., after TDC at 360°).
- $IVO$ = Intake Valve Opens angle (in degrees before TDC at 360°, measured as a lead).

By definition:

$$\theta_{overlap} = EVC + IVO$$

**Step-by-step example with typical marine values:**

Suppose for a slow-speed MAN B&W diesel:
- $EVC = 12°$ after TDC (i.e., $EVC$ is positive, occurs just after the TDC at 360°)
- $IVO = 18°$ before TDC (i.e., $IVO$ is also a positive number, but measured as a lead before TDC)

Step 1 — Identify that both events straddle the TDC at 360°.

Step 2 — Add the two angles (since one is "after" and the other is "before" the same TDC reference):

$$\theta_{overlap} = 12° + 18° = 30°$$

Step 3 — Interpret: For 30° of crank rotation around TDC (360°), both valves are open. This is when fresh air is drawn in while residual exhaust gas is still being expelled — facilitating **residual gas scavenging** and improving **volumetric efficiency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **In terms of the absolute 0°–720° scale:** $EVC$ occurs at $(360° + 12°) = 372°$ and $IVO$ occurs at $(360° - 18°) = 342°$. The overlap window is therefore from $342°$ to $372°$, a span of $30°$. This matches the direct addition.

### 3.2 Derivation of Scavenging Period (2-Stroke)

The **scavenging period** is the crank angle interval during which both the exhaust and transfer ports are open, allowing fresh charge to push out burnt gases.

Let:
- $\theta_{EPO} = 65°$ before BDC = Exhaust Port Opens (a lead angle)
- $\theta_{TPO} = 50°$ before BDC = Transfer Port Opens (a lead angle)
- $\theta_{EPC} = 55°$ after BDC = Exhaust Port Closes
- $\theta_{TPC} = 55°$ after BDC = Transfer Port Closes

**Scavenging angle:**

$$\theta_{scavenge} = \theta_{EPO} - \theta_{TPC} = (65° + 55°) = 120°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The scavenging angle is calculated from the **opening of the first port (exhaust, which opens first)** to the **closing of the last port (transfer, which closes last, since it stays open slightly longer to push charge upward)**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Theoretical Scavenging Time

Scavenging time is the duration in seconds during which scavenging occurs, useful for evaluating if the engine speed is too high for adequate scavenging.

$$t_{sc} = \frac{\theta_{scavenge}}{6 \cdot N}$$

where:
- $\theta_{scavenge}$ is in degrees
- $N$ is the engine speed in **revolutions per second (rps)**

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $\theta_{scavenge} = 120°$, $N = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 25 \text{ rps}$.

Step 1 — Substitute values:

$$t_{sc} = \frac{120°}{6 \times 25 \text{ rps}}$$

Step 2 — Compute denominator:

$$6 \times 25 = 150 \text{ (degrees per second equivalent)}$$

Step 3 — Compute the time:

$$t_{sc} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80 \text{ seconds}$$

Wait — let me correct the formula dimensions. The factor 6 comes from $360°/60\text{s} = 6°/\text{s per rpm}$, so the correct form is:

$$t_{sc} = \frac{\theta_{scavenge} \times 60}{360 \times N} = \frac{\theta_{scavenge}}{6N}$$

Step 3 (corrected) — Substitute $N$ in rps:

$$t_{sc} = \frac{120}{6 \times 25} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **rpm** and **rps** in the formula. Always convert: $N(\text{rps}) = N(\text{rpm})/60$. The factor **6** in the formula already accounts for $360°/60\text{s} = 6°/(\text{s}\cdot\text{rpm})$ — so if you input $N$ in **rps**, use the factor 360 directly: $t_{sc} = \theta_{scavenge}/(360 \cdot N_{\text{rps}})$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Event Sequencing Comparison

| Event | 4-Stroke Diesel (Crank Angle) | 2-Stroke Petrol (Crank Angle) | Piston Direction |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| First event of cycle | IVO at $360° - \alpha$ | EPO at $\beta°$ before BDC | Descending |
| Mid-cycle reference | Fuel injection near TDC (0°) | Spark at ~25° BTDC | Near TDC |
| Second event of cycle | EVO at $180° + \gamma$ | TPO at $\delta°$ before BDC | Descending |
| Bottom dead centre | IVC at $180° + \epsilon$ | All ports close as piston rises | Reversal point |
| Third event of cycle | EVC at $360° + \zeta$ | TPC at $\eta°$ after BDC | Ascending |
| Last event of cycle | IVO at $720° - \alpha$ (next cycle) | EPO at next BDC | Descending |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta, \epsilon, \zeta, \eta$ are the lead/lag angles (positive values). For 4-stroke: $\alpha \approx 15°$, $\epsilon \approx 25°$, $\gamma \approx 50°$, $\zeta \approx 12°$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 4-Stroke Diesel Valve Actuation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crankshaft] --> B[Timing Gear Train]
    B --> C[Camshaft Intake]
    B --> D[Camshaft Exhaust]
    C --> E[Follower and Push Rod]
    D --> F[Follower and Push Rod]
    E --> G[Intake Valve Mechanism]
    F --> H[Exhaust Valve Mechanism]
    G --> I[Intake Manifold Air]
    H --> J[Exhaust Manifold Gases]
    I --> K[Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Fuel Injector NEAR TDC]
    L --> M[Power Stroke Output]
    M --> N[Flywheel and Crankshaft]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 2-Stroke Petrol Port Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Crankcase_Lower_Chamber
        A[Carburettor]
        B[Intake Port Skirt Controlled]
        C[Crankcase Volume]
    end
    subgraph Above_Piston_Cylinder
        D[Transfer Port]
        E[Exhaust Port]
        F[Spark Plug NEAR TDC]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> F
    F --> E
    E --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
```

### 4.3 Timing Event Sequence (Mermaid Timeline)

The following Gantt-style chart represents the **valve/port opening intervals** for one complete working cycle. The **x-axis represents crank angle in degrees**, and the **y-axis represents each valve/port as a horizontal bar** whose length corresponds to its open duration.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Four-Stroke Diesel Valve Timing (0 to 720 degrees)
    dateFormat x
    axisFormat %s°
    section Intake Valve
    Intake Open   :a1, 342, 30
    section Exhaust Valve
    Exhaust Open  :a2, 320, 52
    section Fuel Injection
    Injection    :a3, 350, 10
    section Piston Events
    TDC at 0     :milestone, 0, 0
    BDC at 180   :milestone, 180, 0
    TDC at 360   :milestone, 360, 0
    BDC at 540   :milestone, 540, 0
    TDC at 720   :milestone, 720, 0
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the chart above, "**Intake Open**" begins at 342° (i.e., 18° before TDC at 360°) and lasts 30°; "**Exhaust Open**" begins at 320° and lasts 52° (i.e., 40° before BDC at 180° to 12° after TDC at 360°). The **overlap** between intake open (from 342°) and exhaust open (till 372°) is the **30° overlap window**.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Two-Stroke Petrol Port Timing (0 to 360 degrees)
    dateFormat x
    axisFormat %s°
    section Intake Port Crankcase
    Intake Open   :b1, 100, 130
    section Transfer Port
    Transfer Open :b2, 130, 110
    section Exhaust Port
    Exhaust Open  :b3, 115, 125
    section Spark
    Spark Event   :milestone, 5, 0
    section Piston Events
    TDC at 0      :milestone, 0, 0
    BDC at 180    :milestone, 180, 0
    TDC at 360    :milestone, 360, 0
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the chart above: Intake port (crankcase) opens at 100° (as piston rises in cylinder) and closes at 230°; Exhaust Port Opens at 115° (i.e., 65° before BDC at 180°) and closes at 240°; Transfer Port Opens at 130° (i.e., 50° before BDC) and closes at 240°. The **scavenging period** (all three overlap region) is from 130° to 230° = 100°.

### 4.4 Standard Sketches (Description for Lab Manual Drawing)

For the **4-Stroke Diesel Valve Timing Diagram** (to be drawn on graph paper):

1. **X-axis:** Crank angle from 0° to 720° (use a scale of 1 cm = 30° or 1 cm = 60°).
2. **Y-axis (above x-axis):** Plot the **valve lift profile** as a small hump-shaped curve (parabolic or trapezoidal) for each valve, with the base of the hump representing the open duration.
3. **Y-axis (below x-axis):** Plot a **circle** of diameter 4 cm to represent the crank circle, and connect the piston position to the crank pin to show the slider-crank motion.
4. Mark **TDC** and **BDC** as vertical dashed lines, and label each event with an arrow and abbreviation.

For the **2-Stroke Petrol Port Timing Diagram**:

1. **X-axis:** Crank angle from 0° to 360°.
2. **Y-axis (above):** Draw three horizontal bars (one for each port) — when the port is **open**, draw a thick bar; when **closed**, leave a gap. The bar boundaries indicate the port opening/closing crank angles.
3. **Y-axis (below):** Draw the crank circle and indicate the **ignition point** with a star symbol at ~25° BTDC.
4. **Shade** the **scavenging period** (the crank angle range where exhaust and transfer ports are both open) with a different colour for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)] Define valve overlap in a four-stroke diesel engine and state its significance.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Valve overlap is the crank angle interval (in degrees) during which both the intake valve and the exhaust valve are open simultaneously. It occurs around the **TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke / start of the intake stroke (at 360° in the 0°–720° plot)**.
>
> **Significance:**
> - It enables **scavenging of residual burnt gases** from the cylinder by allowing fresh incoming air to sweep them out.
> - It **cools the exhaust valve and seat** with the incoming cool air.
> - It improves **volumetric efficiency** at part-load and high-speed operation.
> - Excessive overlap, however, leads to **loss of fresh charge** through the exhaust and increased **HC emissions**.
>
> **Formula:**
> $$\theta_{overlap} = EVC + IVO$$
> (Both measured as magnitudes from the common TDC reference.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)] Why does the exhaust port open before the transfer port in a two-stroke petrol engine?**

> **Model Answer:**
> At the end of the **power stroke**, the cylinder is filled with **high-pressure burnt gases**. If the transfer port were to open first, the fresh charge would have to push against this high pressure and would not flow effectively into the cylinder.
>
> By opening the **exhaust port first** (typically 65°–70° before BDC), the high-pressure burnt gases undergo **blow-down** — they rush out of the cylinder rapidly due to the large pressure differential. This drops the cylinder pressure to near-atmospheric levels.
>
> Shortly after, when the **transfer port opens** (typically 50°–55° before BDC), the cylinder pressure is already low, and the **slightly pressurized fresh charge in the crankcase** (due to the descending piston compressing it) can now flow easily into the cylinder, effectively scavenging the remaining exhaust gases.
