This sequencing is called the **blow-down → scavenging** sequence and is fundamental to 2-stroke port design.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the valve timing diagram of a four-stroke vertical diesel engine for the following data:
- Intake Valve Opens (IVO) = 10° before TDC
- Intake Valve Closes (IVC) = 30° after BDC
- Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO) = 45° before BDC
- Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC) = 15° after TDC
- Fuel Injection Begins = 8° before TDC
- Fuel Injection Ends = 5° after TDC

Calculate the **valve overlap** and the **angular interval of the power stroke** (from start of injection to EVO).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **construction and working** of the valve actuating mechanism (camshaft, tappet, push rod, rocker arm) for the intake valve of a four-stroke diesel engine.

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **Step 1 — Draw the diagram:**
> Use a horizontal scale of **1 cm = 30°** (so 720° = 24 cm).
> Mark TDC at 0°, 360°, 720°; mark BDC at 180°, 540°.
>
> - IVO at $(360° - 10°) = 350°$ → draw a hump above the axis (valve lift profile) starting at 350°, peaking around 360° (TDC), ending at $(180° + 30°) = 210°$.
> - EVO at $(180° + 45°) = 225°$ → wait, "before BDC" at 180° means at $(180° - 45°) = 135°$. Draw hump from 135° to $(360° + 15°) = 375°$.
> - Fuel injection: hump from $(360° - 8°) = 352°$ to $(0° + 5°) = 5°$ (i.e., crossing 0°/360°).
>
> **Step 2 — Calculate valve overlap:**
>
> $$\theta_{overlap} = IVO + EVC = 10° + 15° = 25°$$
>
> This means both valves are open from $350°$ to $375°$, a span of $25°$ of crank rotation around the TDC at $360°$.
>
> **Step 3 — Calculate power stroke interval (from start of injection to EVO):**
> Power stroke starts effectively when combustion begins (fuel injection) and the piston is pushed down. EVO occurs at $135°$ (i.e., the exhaust valve opens, marking the end of effective expansion).
> So the **effective expansion interval** is from the start of injection ($352°$, but combustion pressure peaks shortly after TDC at $0°$, i.e., $0°$ as the "start of effective power") to EVO at $135°$:
>
> $$\theta_{exp} = 135° - 0° = 135°$$
>
> If measured from the start of fuel injection ($352°$) to EVO ($135°$, which is $360° + 135° = 495°$ on the absolute scale, but on the cycle scale relative to 0° it is $135°$):
>
> $$\theta_{exp} = 495° - 352° = 143°$$

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> The valve actuating mechanism transmits motion from the **camshaft** (driven by the crankshaft via timing gears at **half engine speed** in 4-stroke engines) to the **valve**.
>
> **Components and Working Sequence:**
>
> 1. **Camshaft Lobe:** A specially shaped eccentric cam (typically with a **conjugate profile** — circular base with a tangent ramp) rotates at half engine speed. As the lobe's high point approaches the tappet, it lifts the tappet.
>
> 2. **Tappet (Valve Lifter):** A cylindrical component that rides on the cam lobe. It translates vertically upward as the cam rotates, transmitting motion to the push rod.
>
> 3. **Push Rod:** A slender steel rod that transmits the motion from the tappet (in the engine block) up to the rocker arm (in the cylinder head). The push rod is enclosed in a guide tube.
>
> 4. **Rocker Arm:** A pivoted lever (pivoted at its centre) mounted on the cylinder head. One end of the rocker arm is pushed down by the rising push rod; the other end pushes down on the **valve stem**, opening the valve against the spring force.
>
> 5. **Valve Spring:** Returns the valve to its closed seat when the cam lobe's high point has passed.
>
> **Valve Clearance (Tappet Clearance):** A small gap (typically **0.2 to 0.4 mm** for marine diesels) is maintained between the rocker arm and the valve stem tip to allow for thermal expansion. This is adjusted via an **adjusting screw** on the rocker arm.
>
> **Working:** As the cam rotates, the lobe lifts → tappet rises → push rod rises → rocker arm pivots → valve stem is pushed down → valve opens. When the lobe's high point passes, the spring forces the valve closed, and the entire mechanism returns to its original position.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the port timing diagram of a two-stroke petrol engine for the following data:
- Exhaust Port Opens (EPO) = 65° before BDC
- Transfer Port Opens (TPO) = 50° before BDC
- Transfer Port Closes (TPC) = 55° after BDC
- Exhaust Port Closes (EPC) = 50° after BDC
- Spark occurs at = 25° before TDC

Determine the **scavenging angle** and the **effective compression angle** (from port closure to spark).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the valve timing diagram of a four-stroke diesel engine with the port timing diagram of a two-stroke petrol engine in terms of (i) cycle duration, (ii) gas exchange mechanism, (iii) volumetric efficiency, (iv) power-to-weight ratio, and (v) lubrication system.

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **Step 1 — Plot the diagram (0° to 360°):**
> - TDC at 0° and 360°, BDC at 180°.
> - EPO at $(180° - 65°) = 115°$
> - TPO at $(180° - 50°) = 130°$
> - TPC at $(180° + 55°) = 235°$
> - EPC at $(180° + 50°) = 230°$
> - Spark at $(0° - 25°)$ or equivalently $(360° - 25°) = 335°$
>
> **Step 2 — Scavenging angle:**
> The scavenging angle is from the **opening of the first port (exhaust, at 115°)** to the **closing of the last port (transfer, at 235°):**
>
> $$\theta_{scavenge} = 235° - 115° = 120°$$
>
> **Step 3 — Effective compression angle (from last port closure to spark):**
> Last port closes at TPC = 235°. Spark occurs at 335°. So:
>
> $$\theta_{comp} = 335° - 235° = 100°$$
>
> This is the crank angle over which actual compression of the fresh charge occurs (the remaining 260° of the cycle accounts for scavenging, expansion, and blow-down).

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> | Parameter | 4-Stroke Diesel (Valve Timing) | 2-Stroke Petrol (Port Timing) |
> |:---|:---|:---|
> | **(i) Cycle duration** | **720°** (2 revolutions) | **360°** (1 revolution) |
> | **(ii) Gas exchange mechanism** | **Poppet valves** actuated by camshaft, push rod, rocker arm | **Uncovered ports** in the cylinder liner, opened/closed by the **piston skirt** itself |
> | **(iii) Volumetric efficiency** | **Higher (80–92%)** due to dedicated induction stroke and valve overlap tuning | **Lower (60–80%)** because fresh charge is lost through the exhaust port (short-circuiting) during scavenging |
> | **(iv) Power-to-weight ratio** | **Lower** (one power stroke per 2 revolutions) | **Higher** (theoretically double — one power stroke per revolution, plus no valvetrain mass) |
> | **(v) Lubrication system** | **Forced lubrication** (wet sump with oil pump, oil cooler, filters) common; splash lubrication in small engines | **Oil mist / petroil system** (oil mixed with fuel at ~2–3% ratio) for the crankcase and bearings; **separate lubrication for big-end bearings** in larger engines |
> | **Auxiliary systems** | Requires timing gears, camshafts, push rods, rocker arms | Simpler — no camshaft, no valves |
> | **Thermal efficiency** | **Higher (35–45%)** in marine diesels (diesel cycle is more efficient) | **Lower (25–32%)** (Otto cycle, plus heat loss through open ports) |
> | **Typical applications** | Main propulsion of ships, large gensets, heavy vehicles | Motorcycles, small outboard motors, lawn mowers, small gensets |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the crank angle convention:** Many students draw the 4-stroke diagram from 0° to 360° instead of 0° to 720°. This is a **fundamental error** and leads to 0 marks for the diagram.
> 2. **Confusing "before" and "after" signs:** Always state the lead/lag explicitly. A student marking "IVO = 18°" without saying "before TDC" loses 1 mark per such event.
> 3. **Not labelling all events with abbreviations:** Every event must be labelled (IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC). A diagram without labels is graded as incomplete.
> 4. **Drawing valve/port humps upside down or in the wrong half:** In the standard KTU convention, valve lift is plotted **above the x-axis**, and the crank circle is plotted **below the x-axis**.
> 5. **Skipping the crank circle:** The crank circle is mandatory in lab exam diagrams. It shows the slider-crank motion of the piston.
> 6. **Forgetting to compute the valve overlap and scavenging angle** when asked — these are "must-do" numerical parts.
> 7. **Wrong answer for power stroke interval:** Remember, in a 4-stroke, the power stroke is from TDC (combustion) to EVO (early blow-down). Do NOT count the entire 180° of piston travel.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Valve and Port Timing**
>
> ☐ **4-Stroke Diesel Cycle = 720°**, **2-Stroke Petrol Cycle = 360°**.
>
> ☐ **Standard Events to Memorise (4-Stroke):**
> - **IVO** = 10°–20° before TDC (lead)
> - **IVC** = 20°–35° after BDC (lag)
> - **EVO** = 40°–60° before BDC (lead)
> - **EVC** = 10°–15° after TDC (lag)
> - **Fuel Injection** = ~10° before TDC to ~5° after TDC
>
> ☐ **Standard Events to Memorise (2-Stroke):**
> - **EPO** = 65°–70° before BDC (opens first)
> - **TPO** = 50°–55° before BDC (opens after exhaust)
> - **TPC** = 50°–55° after BDC (closes last)
> - **EPC** = 50°–55° after BDC (closes before TPC)
> - **Spark** = ~25°–30° before TDC
>
> ☐ **Key Formulas:**
> - $\theta_{overlap} = EVC + IVO$ (4-stroke)
> - $\theta_{scavenge} = \theta_{EPO opening} - \theta_{TPC closing}$ (2-stroke)
> - $t_{sc} = \theta_{scavenge} / (6N)$ where $N$ is in **rpm** (or $/360$ if $N$ in rps)
>
> ☐ **Why EPO opens before TPO:** Blow-down of high-pressure exhaust gases first, then scavenging with fresh charge.
>
> ☐ **Why IVO is before TDC and IVC is after BDC:** Volumetric efficiency and ram-effect charging.
>
> ☐ **Why EVO is well before BDC:** Utilise high cylinder-to-exhaust pressure differential for blow-down, reducing pumping work.
>
> ☐ **Marine 4-Stroke Diesel vs 2-Stroke Diesel:** Marine main propulsion typically uses **2-stroke slow-speed diesels** (port timing, not valve timing). The lab experiment uses a **4-stroke** engine for the valve timing study because the valvetrain is mechanically visible and instructive.
>
> ☐ **Drawing Convention:** Use a scale of **1 cm = 30° to 60°**, label every event with arrow + abbreviation, draw the crank circle below the x-axis, and use dashed vertical lines for TDC and BDC.
>
> ☐ **Examiner's Favourite Questions:** (a) "Define valve overlap and calculate it from given data" (b) "Why does EPO open before TPO?" (c) "Compare 4-stroke and 2-stroke timing diagrams" (d) "Calculate the scavenging period and time."
>
> ☐ **Memory Trick — "ICE-PETROL":**
> - **I**VO first, then **C**loses (I-C) — Intake
> - **E**VO before BDC, **E**VC after TDC (E-E) — Exhaust
> - **P**orts: E first, T second, T last to close (E-T-T)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine

# Module Experiment 2: Load Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Load Test on a Four-Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine** is a standardized laboratory experiment conducted to determine the **performance characteristics** of a compression ignition (CI) engine by varying the applied mechanical load on the engine's crankshaft and recording the corresponding steady-state values of **brake power (BP)**, **specific fuel consumption (SFC)**, **brake thermal efficiency ($\eta_{bt}$)**, **mechanical efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)**, and **air-fuel ratio (AFR)** at a constant engine speed (N).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is a **mandatory core experiment** in the Marine Engineering Lab (PCNSL607). Students are expected to record at least 6 to 8 sets of readings, plot the **performance curves** (BP vs SFC, BP vs $\eta_{bt}$, BP vs $\eta_{mech}$), and present a complete **heat balance sheet**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single bull pulling a heavily loaded cart up a hill**. As you keep adding more sacks of grain to the cart (increasing **load**):
- The bull has to **work harder** — it consumes more fodder (**fuel**) and breathes faster (**air intake**).
- If you keep adding load beyond a point, the bull **tires out** and **slows down** (engine speed drops).
- A **wise cart owner** keeps a record of fodder consumed per kilometer pulled for different loads — this helps him decide the **most efficient operating point** (the load at which the bull pulls the maximum load per unit fodder).

A diesel engine behaves exactly like this bull. The **load test** is the systematic "fodder-versus-distance" record-keeping exercise for the engine. We use a **rope brake dynamometer** to load the engine and measure how its performance varies with load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms You Must Know Before Proceeding:**
> - **Four-Stroke Cycle:** Engine completes its working cycle in **4 piston strokes** (2 crankshaft revolutions) — Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust.
> - **Single Cylinder:** Only **one piston-cylinder assembly** in the engine.
> - **Vertical:** The cylinder axis is oriented **vertically** (gravity assists piston return).
> - **Diesel Engine:** Uses **compression ignition** — fuel ignites due to high temperature of compressed air (no spark plug).

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in **Bold**

The following **six performance parameters** are mandatory for KTU record submission:

- **Brake Power (BP)** — measured in **kW**
- **Indicated Power (IP)** — measured in **kW**
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** — measured in **%**
- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** — measured in **kg/kWh**
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bt}$)** — measured in **%**
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** — dimensionless

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Four-Stroke Diesel Cycle

The four-stroke cycle consists of the following four processes, each completed in **one full stroke** of the piston (i.e., **180° of crankshaft rotation**). The complete cycle therefore spans **720°** (2 revolutions).

| Stroke | Process | Valve Status | Description |
|:------:|:-------:|:------------:|:------------|
| 1st | **Suction (Intake)** | Inlet: **Open**, Exhaust: **Closed** | Fresh air is drawn into the cylinder as the piston moves down from **TDC to BDC**. |
| 2nd | **Compression** | Inlet: **Closed**, Exhaust: **Closed** | Air is compressed to a high pressure and temperature (**~30 bar**, **~800 K**). |
| 3rd | **Power (Expansion)** | Inlet: **Closed**, Exhaust: **Closed** | Fuel injected → auto-ignition → combustion → high-pressure gases push the piston down. |
| 4th | **Exhaust** | Inlet: **Closed**, Exhaust: **Open** | Burnt gases are pushed out as the piston moves up from **BDC to TDC**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Four-Stroke" but only ONE power stroke?**
> Out of every **two complete revolutions (4 strokes)**, the engine delivers **only ONE power stroke**. This is why single-cylinder diesel engines have a characteristic "thump-thump" sound and require a **heavy flywheel** to maintain smooth rotation.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Load Test — Engineering Logic

A **load test** answers four critical engineering questions:

1. **How much useful work** can the engine deliver? → Answer: **Brake Power (BP)**
2. **How much fuel** does it burn per unit of useful work? → Answer: **BSFC**
3. **How efficiently** does it convert the chemical energy of fuel into useful work? → Answer: **$\eta_{bt}$**
4. **How much useful work** is lost to friction and accessories? → Answer: **$\eta_{mech}$**

The performance curves plotted from this test are used in **ship design** to:
- Select the correct **main propulsion engine** size.
- Estimate **fuel bunkering** requirements.
- Compute the **specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC)** for voyage planning.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All High-Yield Equations)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:---------:|:--------|:-----:|:-------:|
| 1 | **Brake Power (BP)** | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ | kW | N in **rps**, T in **N·m** |
| 2 | **Brake Power (Rope Brake Method)** | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot (W - S)}{60}$ | kW | D in **m**, N in **rps**, W & S in **N** |
| 3 | **Brake Power (Prony Brake Method)** | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot (F - f) \cdot L}{60}$ | kW | L in **m**, F & f in **N** |
| 4 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | kW | Requires measured $\eta_{mech}$ or Morse test |
| 5 | **Friction Power (FP)** | $\text{FP} = \text{IP} - \text{BP}$ | kW | Power lost to friction |
| 6 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 75% to 85% |
| 7 | **Fuel Consumption Rate** | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot 3600}{t}$ | kg/h | $V_f$ in cc, t in seconds, $\rho_f$ in kg/m³ |
| 8 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\text{BP}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 9 | **Heat Input Rate** | $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}$ | kJ/h | CV ≈ **42000 kJ/kg** for diesel |
| 10 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{\text{BP} \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} \times 100$ | % | Typically 25% to 35% |
| 11 | **Air-Fuel Ratio** | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | — | Stoichiometric ≈ 14.5 for diesel |
| 12 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot N} \times 60$ | % | $\rho_a$ = **1.165 kg/m³** at standard conditions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Values You Must Memorize:**
> - Calorific Value (CV) of diesel: **42,000 kJ/kg** (≈ 10,200 kcal/kg)
> - Density of diesel ($\rho_f$): **0.85 kg/L** = **850 kg/m³**
> - Density of air ($\rho_a$) at NTP: **1.165 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity (g): **9.81 m/s²**

### 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

- **Main Engine Sizing:** A 4-stroke single cylinder diesel is the conceptual prototype for **MAN B&W** and **Wärtsilä** low-speed marine engines.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Load test results establish the **SFOC curve** (specific fuel oil consumption vs. % MCR — Maximum Continuous Rating) used for **vessel EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) certification under **MARPOL Annex VI**.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Deviation from the baseline load test curve during later inspections indicates **piston ring wear**, **nozzle deterioration**, or **turbocharger fouling**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brake Power from Rope Brake Dynamometer

The **rope brake dynamometer** is the most commonly used loading device in KTU labs. The setup consists of a rope wrapped around the **engine flywheel**, with one end attached to a **dead weight (W)** and the other to a **spring balance (S)**.

**Step 1:** The net tangential force on the flywheel rim is the difference between the weight pulling down and the spring balance pulling up.

$$F_{net} = (W - S)$$

**Step 2:** The net force acts at the **mean radius** of the flywheel rim (i.e., at $R = D/2$, where D is the brake drum diameter). The net **torque** developed is:

$$T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \times \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The **work done per revolution** of the engine equals the **force × circumference**:

$$W_{rev} = F_{net} \times \pi D = \pi D (W - S)$$

**Step 4:** The engine completes **N revolutions per second**. Hence, the work done per second (i.e., **power**) is:

$$\text{BP} = N \times \pi D (W - S)$$

**Step 5:** For practical convenience, the speed is measured in **rpm** and the power in **kW**. Multiplying by $1/60$ to convert rpm to rps and $1/1000$ to convert W to kW:

$$\boxed{\text{BP} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot (W - S)}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(kW)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> - W, S are in **Newtons (N)** — multiply kgf by **9.81** to get N.
> - D is in **metres (m)**.
> - N is in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).
> - The **$1/60$** converts rpm → rps.
> - The **$1/1000$** converts W → kW.

### 3.2 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency

The **brake thermal efficiency** is defined as the ratio of **brake power output** to the **rate of heat input** from the fuel.

**Step 1:** The **rate of heat supplied** to the engine per hour is:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} \quad \text{(kJ/h)}$$

**Step 2:** The **brake power output** in kJ per hour is:

$$\text{BP}_{kJ/h} = \text{BP}_{kW} \times 3600 = \text{BP} \times 3600 \quad \text{(kJ/h)}$$

**Step 3:** Taking the ratio and multiplying by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{\text{BP} \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}} \times 100 \quad \text{(\%)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)

The **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** is the **mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power per hour**.

**Step 1:** Measure the volume of fuel consumed (V) in **cm³ (cc)** over a time interval **t** in **seconds**.

**Step 2:** Convert volume to mass using fuel density $\rho_f$ (in kg/m³ = kg/10⁶ cc):

$$m_f = \frac{V \times \rho_f}{10^6} \quad \text{(kg)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **hourly mass flow rate**:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{t} = \frac{V \times \rho_f \times 3600}{t \times 10^6} \quad \text{(kg/h)}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by brake power:

$$\boxed{\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\text{BP}} = \frac{V \times \rho_f \times 3600}{\text{BP} \times t \times 10^6} \quad \text{(kg/kWh)}}$$

### 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a typical KTU Part B numerical problem. Practice it thoroughly.**

**Problem Statement (Model):**
A 4-stroke single cylinder vertical diesel engine has a **bore = 100 mm**, **stroke = 120 mm**, and **flywheel diameter = 1.0 m**. During a load test, the following observations were recorded for a particular load:

| Observation | Value |
|:-----------:|:-----:|
| Speed (N) | **1500 rpm** |
| Dead weight (W) | **20 kg** |
| Spring balance reading (S) | **5 kg** |
| Fuel consumption (V) | **15 cm³** in **30 s** |
| Fuel density | **0.85 kg/L** |
| Calorific value of fuel | **42,000 kJ/kg** |
| Air consumption | **120 kg/h** |

**Compute:** (a) Brake Power, (b) BSFC, (c) Brake Thermal Efficiency, (d) Air-Fuel Ratio, (e) Mechanical Efficiency (assume $\eta_{mech}$ = 80%).

---

**Solution:**

#### (a) Brake Power

**Step 1:** Convert W and S from kgf to N.

$$W = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \text{ N}, \quad S = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Net force:

$$W - S = 196.2 - 49.05 = 147.15 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the BP formula with D = 1.0 m and N = 1500 rpm:

$$\text{BP} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 1500 \times 147.15}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$\text{BP} = \frac{\pi \times 1500 \times 147.15}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$\text{BP} = \frac{471238.65}{60000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BP} = 7.854 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### (b) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption

**Step 1:** Convert fuel density: $\rho_f = 0.85 \text{ kg/L} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 850 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/cc} = 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cc}$

**Step 2:** Mass of fuel consumed in 30 s:

$$m_f = V \times \rho_f = 15 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01275 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Hourly fuel flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.01275 \times 3600}{30} = 1.53 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4:** BSFC:

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\text{BP}} = \frac{1.53}{7.854}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BSFC} = 0.1948 \text{ kg/kWh}}$$

---

#### (c) Brake Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1:** Heat input rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} = 1.53 \times 42000 = 64260 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 2:** Heat equivalent of brake power per hour:

$$\text{BP}_{kJ/h} = 7.854 \times 3600 = 28274.4 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 3:** Efficiency:

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{28274.4}{64260} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bt} = 44.0 \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, BSFC = 0.1948 kg/kWh and $\eta_{bt}$ = 44% indicate a very healthy, well-tuned small diesel engine (typical BSFC range: 0.20 to 0.30 kg/kWh).

---

#### (d) Air-Fuel Ratio

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{120}{1.53}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AFR} = 78.43}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A diesel engine typically operates at a **lean mixture** with AFR between 18:1 and 80:1. An AFR of 78 means the engine is operating at very light load.

---

#### (e) Mechanical Efficiency (given = 80%)

$$\text{IP} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{7.854}{0.80} = 9.8175 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Friction Power} = \text{IP} - \text{BP} = 9.8175 - 7.854 = 1.964 \text{ kW}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Load Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DIESEL ENGINE] --> B[FLYWHEEL RIM]
    B --> C[ROPE BRAKE DYNAMOMETER]
    C --> D[DEAD WEIGHT W]
    C --> E[SPRING BALANCE S]
    A --> F[FUEL TANK]
    F --> G[BURETTE FOR FUEL MEASUREMENT]
    A --> H[ORIFICE METER FOR AIR INTAKE]
    H --> I[U TUBE MANOMETER]
    A --> J[TACHOMETER RPM MEASUREMENT]
    A --> K[STOPWATCH FOR TIMING]

    step1[STEP 1 WARM UP ENGINE 10 MIN NO LOAD] --> step2[STEP 2 RECORD NO LOAD READINGS]
    step2 --> step3[STEP 3 APPLY INCREMENTAL LOAD]
    step3 --> step4[STEP 4 RECORD W S V T N AT EACH LOAD]
    step4 --> step5[STEP 5 COMPUTE BP SFC ETA BT AFR]
    step5 --> step6[STEP 6 PLOT PERFORMANCE CURVES]

    A -.-> step1
    step1 -.-> step2
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Four-Stroke Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TDC TOP DEAD CENTER] --> B[INTAKE STROKE AIR ENTERS]
    B --> C[BDC BOTTOM DEAD CENTER]
    C --> D[COMPRESSION STROKE AIR COMPRESSED]
    D --> E[TDC COMPRESSION HIGH T AIR]
    E --> F[FUEL INJECTED AUTO IGNITION]
    F --> G[POWER STROKE PISTON PUSHED DOWN]
    G --> H[BDC END OF POWER]
    H --> I[EXHAUST STROKE BURNT GASES OUT]
    I --> A

    subgraph CRANKSHAFT ROTATION
        R1[0 TO 180 DEG]
        R2[180 TO 360 DEG]
        R3[360 TO 540 DEG]
        R4[540 TO 720 DEG]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Performance Parameter Computation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[RAW DATA N W S V T AIR FLOW] --> bp[BP COMPUTATION K W]
    raw --> mf[FUEL FLOW KG PER H]
    raw --> ma[AIR FLOW KG PER H]
    bp --> bsfc[BSFC KG PER KWH]
    bp --> etabt[BRAKE THERMAL EFFICIENT PERCENT]
    bp --> etaM[IP AND MECHANICAL EFFICIENCY]
    mf --> bsfc
    mf --> etabt
    ma --> afr[AIR FUEL RATIO]
    bp --> curve[PERFORMANCE CURVES PLOTTED]
    bsfc --> curve
    etabt --> curve
    etaM --> curve
    afr --> curve

    curve --> rec[KTU RECORD SUBMISSION]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Expected Performance Curves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Graphs (Plot all 4 for full marks in record):**
>
> 1. **BP vs BSFC** — BSFC is minimum at ~75% of rated load (best economy point).
> 2. **BP vs $\eta_{bt}$** — $\eta_{bt}$ increases with load, peaks near rated load.
> 3. **BP vs $\eta_{mech}$** — Increases with load, plateaus near full load.
> 4. **BP vs Air-Fuel Ratio** — AFR decreases as load increases (richer mixture at high load).

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PERFORMANCE_CURVES
        P1[BP VS BSFC U SHAPED MIN AT 75 PERCENT LOAD]
        P2[BP VS ETA BT RISING CURVE]
        P3[BP VS ETA MECH RISING THEN PLATEAUS]
        P4[BP VS AFR FALLING CURVE]
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define brake thermal efficiency of a diesel engine. Mention its typical range for a small 4-stroke single cylinder vertical diesel engine.**

**Model Answer:**
Brake thermal efficiency ($\eta_{bt}$) is defined as the ratio of brake power output of the engine to the rate of heat input from the fuel, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{\text{BP} \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}} \times 100 \quad \text{(\%)}$$

For a small single cylinder vertical diesel engine, the typical range is **25% to 35%** under normal operating conditions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any four performance parameters determined from the load test of a diesel engine.**

**Model Answer:**
The four performance parameters are:
1. **Brake Power (BP)** in kW
2. **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** in kg/kWh
3. **Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bt}$)** in %
4. **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** in %
5. *(Optional 5th)* Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full (no mix and match).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **rope brake dynamometer** used in the load test of a single cylinder 4-stroke vertical diesel engine. Derive the expression for brake power.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following observations were recorded during a load test on a 4-stroke single cylinder vertical diesel engine:

- **Engine speed (N):** 1800 rpm
- **Brake drum diameter (D):** 0.8 m
- **Dead weight (W):** 30 kg; **Spring balance (S):** 4 kg
- **Fuel consumed:** 20 cc in 20 seconds
- **Fuel density:** 0.83 kg/L; **Calorific Value:** 43,000 kJ/kg

Calculate: (i) Brake Power (ii) BSFC (iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- The rope brake dynamometer consists of a **rope** wound around the **brake drum/flywheel rim** of the engine.
- One end of the rope is attached to a **dead weight (W)** and the other end to a **spring balance (S)**.
- The dead weight applies tangential friction, loading the engine. The spring balance reads the reduced tension.
- The **net tangential force** = (W − S) acts at the **mean radius** of the flywheel rim.
- The friction between the rope and the rim produces a retarding torque, which loads the engine.
- A **cooling water drip** is usually provided on the rope to dissipate frictional heat.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Net force $F_{net} = (W - S)$ in Newtons.
Mean radius $R = D/2$ in metres.
Torque $T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \times D/2$ in N·m.

Work done per revolution = $F_{net} \times \pi D = \pi D (W - S)$ in J.
If engine runs at N rpm = N/60 rps, then brake power:

$$\text{BP} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot (W - S)}{60 \times 1000} \text{ kW}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Brake Power:**

W = 30 × 9.81 = 294.3 N; S = 4 × 9.81 = 39.24 N
W − S = 255.06 N
N = 1800 rpm; D = 0.8 m

$$\text{BP} = \frac{\pi \times 0.8 \times 1800 \times 255.06}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{1154155.65}{60000} = 19.236 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) BSFC:**

$\rho_f = 0.83 \text{ kg/L} = 8.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cc}$
$m_f = 20 \times 8.3 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0166 \text{ kg}$
$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.0166 \times 3600}{20} = 2.988 \text{ kg/h}$

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{2.988}{19.236} = 0.1553 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**(iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{19.236 \times 3600}{2.988 \times 43000} \times 100 = \frac{69249.6}{128484} \times 100 = 53.9\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for Question A:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to convert kgf to N by multiplying with 9.81 — **deduct 1 mark** if missed.
> - **Do NOT** forget to convert the density to kg/cc correctly — fuel density confusion is the most common error.
> - **Always** check that the resulting BSFC lies in the realistic range (0.15 to 0.35 kg/kWh). If your answer is wildly off, recheck the unit conversion of $\rho_f$ from kg/L to kg/cc.
> - The $1/1000$ divisor for kW and $1/60$ divisor for rpm are **mandatory** — examiners specifically scan for these.
> - A brake thermal efficiency **above 50%** is unrealistic for a basic single-cylinder lab engine — recheck your calculation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four-stroke diesel cycle** with a neat labeled diagram. Why does a single cylinder engine need a heavy flywheel?

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a load test on a single cylinder 4-stroke vertical diesel engine, the following data was obtained:

| Load Step | W (kg) | S (kg) | N (rpm) | Fuel (cc) | Time (s) | Air (kg/h) |
|:---------:|:------:|:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 1 | 4 | 1 | 1500 | 10 | 30 | 60 |
| 2 | 8 | 2 | 1490 | 14 | 30 | 80 |
| 3 | 12 | 3 | 1485 | 18 | 30 | 100 |
| 4 | 16 | 4 | 1475 | 22 | 30 | 120 |

**Brake drum diameter = 1.0 m; Fuel density = 0.85 kg/L; CV = 42,000 kJ/kg.**

Compute for **load step 3**:
(i) Brake Power
(ii) BSFC
(iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency
(iv) Air-Fuel Ratio

**Model Solution (a):**

**Four-Stroke Diesel Cycle (5 marks):**
The cycle consists of four distinct strokes:
1. **Suction Stroke (0° to 180°):** Inlet valve opens, exhaust valve closed. Pure air is drawn in as piston moves TDC → BDC.
2. **Compression Stroke (180° to 360°):** Both valves closed. Air is compressed to a high pressure (~30 bar) and temperature (~800 K). No fuel is present.
3. **Power Stroke (360° to 540°):** Near TDC, fuel is injected at high pressure through an injector. Due to high temperature, fuel auto-ignites. Combustion gases expand, pushing the piston BDC. This is the only useful work-producing stroke.
4. **Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°):** Exhaust valve opens. The piston moves back to TDC, expelling burnt gases.

**Need for Heavy Flywheel (2 marks):**
A single-cylinder engine produces power **only in one of the four strokes**. During the other three strokes (suction, compression, exhaust), the engine **consumes energy** (to drive the piston back). A **heavy flywheel** stores kinetic energy during the power stroke and releases it during the idle strokes, ensuring **smooth and uniform rotation**.

**Model Solution (b) — Load Step 3:**

W = 12 × 9.81 = 117.72 N; S = 3 × 9.81 = 29.43 N
W − S = 88.29 N
N = 1485 rpm; D = 1.0 m

**(i) Brake Power:**

$$\text{BP} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 1485 \times 88.29}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{411777.1}{60000} = 6.863 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) BSFC:**

$\rho_f = 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cc}$
$m_f = 18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0153 \text{ kg}$
$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.0153 \times 3600}{30} = 1.836 \text{ kg/h}$

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{1.836}{6.863} = 0.2675 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**(iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{6.863 \times 3600}{1.836 \times 42000} \times 100 = \frac{24706.8}{77112} \times 100 = 32.04\%$$

**(iv) Air-Fuel Ratio:**

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{100}{1.836} = 54.47$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> - In multi-load problems, **do not mix up rows**. Clearly state "For Load Step 3" before solving.
> - The **fuel time (t)** is 30 s in this problem — do not accidentally use 60 s.
> - The **air consumption** is given in **kg/h** — convert it as $\dot{m}_a$ directly; do not divide by 3600.
> - A common mistake: forgetting that **W and S are in kgf**, not Newtons. Always multiply by 9.81 first.
> - The flywheel explanation must include the phrase **"kinetic energy storage"** — examiners specifically look for it.
> - Always state that a **diesel engine does not have a spark plug** — fuel ignites by **compression heating** of air.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Engine type:** 4-stroke → 1 power stroke every 2 revolutions; needs heavy flywheel.
- [x] **Load test purpose:** Determine performance vs. load at constant speed.
- [x] **Loading device:** Rope brake dynamometer (flywheel + rope + W + S).
- [x] **Master formula:** $\text{BP} = \dfrac{\pi D N (W-S)}{60 \times 1000}$ kW.
- [x] **Unit conversion check:** kgf → N (×9.81); rpm → rps (÷60); W → kW (÷1000).
- [x] **Fuel density:** 0.85 kg/L = 8.5 × 10⁻⁴ kg/cc — **must** convert for BSFC problems.
- [x] **CV of diesel:** 42,000 kJ/kg (use 43,000 kJ/kg in some KTU numericals).
- [x] **BSFC** = $\dot{m}_f$ / BP; realistic range **0.20 to 0.35 kg/kWh**.
- [x] **$\eta_{bt}$** realistic range: **25% to 35%** for lab single-cylinder engines.
- [x] **Mechanical efficiency** = BP / IP, typically 75% to 85%.
- [x] **AFR** for diesel = 18:1 (full load) to 80:1 (no load) — diesel runs **lean**.
- [x] **Mandatory graphs:** BP vs BSFC, BP vs $\eta_{bt}$, BP vs $\eta_{mech}$, BP vs AFR.
- [x] **Air measurement:** Use an **orifice meter** with a U-tube manometer.
- [x] **Fuel measurement:** Use a **burette** with a 3-way cock and stopwatch.
- [x] **Diesel = CI engine:** No spark plug; fuel auto-ignites from compressed hot air.
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency** can also be asked — formula: $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a V_s N} \times 60$.
- [x] **P-V diagram** of diesel cycle: Square-edged cycle with **constant pressure heat addition** (key differentiator from Otto cycle).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morse Test on Four Stroke Four Cylinder Petrol Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Morse Test on Four Stroke Four Cylinder Petrol Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Morse Test (also known as the Motoring Test):** It is an experimental procedure used to determine the **Indicated Power (IP) developed by each individual cylinder** of a multi-cylinder internal combustion engine by comparing the brake power required to motor the engine under different cylinder deactivation conditions.

The test is based on the fundamental assumption that the **mechanical efficiency of the engine remains constant** under all load conditions during the test, and the frictional power (FP) of the engine is independent of the cylinder firing.

For a four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine, the procedure involves measuring the brake power required to motor the engine:
1. With **all four cylinders firing** under a given load.
2. With **each cylinder cut off (deactivated) one at a time** while the engine continues to run on the remaining cylinders.

The difference between these readings gives the indicated power contributed by the deactivated cylinder.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy - The Tug-of-War Team:** Imagine four equally strong people (the four cylinders) pulling a heavy sack of sand (representing engine load) in a tug-of-war. If we ask one person to *let go* and just stand there, the other three must pull *harder* to keep the sack moving at the same speed. The extra effort the three remaining people put in is exactly equal to the effort that the fourth person *would have been* contributing. Similarly, in the Morse Test, when one cylinder is "cut off" (no fuel, no combustion), the remaining three must produce more brake power to keep the engine motoring at the same RPM. That **extra brake power directly equals the indicated power the cut-off cylinder was producing** when all four were firing.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed (N):** Typically measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)** — standard test speed range: **1500–3000 rpm** for petrol engines.
- **Brake Power (BP):** Measured in **kW** (kilowatts).
- **Indicated Power (IP):** Measured in **kW**.
- **Friction Power (FP):** $FP = IP - BP$, measured in **kW**.
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** Typically **70% – 85%** for a well-tuned 4-stroke petrol engine.

### 1.4 Why Morse Test is Conducted

> [!NOTE]
> The test enables engineers to identify a **weak or faulty cylinder** without dismantling the engine. By comparing the IP of each cylinder, variation greater than **± 5%** between cylinders indicates non-uniformity due to issues like faulty spark plug, improper valve timing, clogged injector/carburetor, or compression loss.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Morse Test rests on two core assumptions:

1. **Friction Power (FP) remains constant** regardless of which cylinder is cut off or the load condition, provided the engine speed is constant.
2. **Mechanical efficiency ($\eta_m$) remains approximately constant** during the test.

**Mechanical Efficiency relationship:**
$$\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$$

For any operating condition:
$$IP = BP + FP$$

This is the master equation governing the entire test.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Morse Test

- **Step 1:** Run the engine at a constant rated speed **N rpm** under a steady load with **all four cylinders firing**. Measure the brake power — call it **$BP_{all}$**.
- **Step 2:** Cut off the fuel supply to **Cylinder 1** (spark plug disconnected, but the cylinder still moves). The engine is now motored by cylinders 2, 3, 4. Measure the new brake power — call it **$BP_1$**.
- **Step 3:** The *extra* brake power the remaining cylinders now provide to keep the engine rotating at the same speed equals the **indicated power of Cylinder 1**.

$$IP_1 = BP_{all} - BP_1$$

- **Step 4:** Repeat for Cylinders 2, 3, and 4 similarly:

$$IP_2 = BP_{all} - BP_2$$
$$IP_3 = BP_{all} - BP_3$$
$$IP_4 = BP_{all} - BP_4$$

- **Step 5:** Total Indicated Power:

$$IP_{total} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4 = 4 \cdot BP_{all} - (BP_1 + BP_2 + BP_3 + BP_4)$$

- **Step 6:** Friction Power:

$$FP = IP_{total} - BP_{all}$$

- **Step 7:** Mechanical Efficiency:

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{BP_{all}}{IP_{total}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all key equations** required for KTU viva and exam questions on the Morse Test.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IP of cut-off cylinder $i$ | $IP_i = BP_{all} - BP_i$ | kW | Direct difference method |
| 2 | Total Indicated Power | $IP_{total} = \sum IP_i$ | kW | Sum of all four IPs |
| 3 | Total Indicated Power (alt) | $IP_{total} = 4 \cdot BP_{all} - \sum BP_i$ | kW | Algebraic equivalent |
| 4 | Friction Power | $FP = IP_{total} - BP_{all}$ | kW | Energy lost to friction |
| 5 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = BP_{all} \div IP_{total}$ | ratio | Typically 0.70–0.85 |
| 6 | Brake Power (Rope Brake) | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S)}{60000}$ | kW | W = load, S = spring balance reading |
| 7 | Brake Power (Hydraulic) | $BP = \dfrac{\pi N P (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{60000 \times 10^6}$ | kW | P = manometer pressure in N/m² |
| 8 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $IMEP = \dfrac{IP \times 60}{L \times A \times N \times n}$ | N/m² | L = stroke, A = piston area, n = 1 (4-stroke) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Morse Test is the standard diagnostic tool used by automotive workshops, marine engine inspection agencies, and engine R&D laboratories for **performance auditing** of multi-cylinder engines. It is mandatory under several classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's) for periodic performance checks on auxiliary marine engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Power Developed by One Cylinder

Let us derive the relationship between brake power readings and the indicated power of an individual cylinder.

**Given:** A four-stroke, four-cylinder petrol engine running at constant speed **N rpm**.

**Let:**
- $BP_{all}$ = Brake power with all four cylinders firing.
- $BP_i$ = Brake power when cylinder $i$ is cut off.
- $FP$ = Total frictional power of the engine (assumed constant).
- $IP_i$ = Indicated power of cylinder $i$ (what we want to find).

**Derivation:**

When all cylinders are firing:
$$IP_{total} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$$

By definition of mechanical efficiency:
$$IP_{total} = BP_{all} + FP \quad \cdots (1)$$

When cylinder 1 is cut off, cylinders 2, 3, 4 still fire:
$$IP_{remaining,1} = IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$$

The new brake power measured is:
$$IP_{remaining,1} = BP_1 + FP \quad \cdots (2)$$

Subtracting equation (2) from equation (1):
$$IP_{total} - IP_{remaining,1} = BP_{all} - BP_1$$

The left side represents the indicated power produced *only* by cylinder 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
(IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4) - (IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4) &= BP_{all} - BP_1 \\
IP_1 &= BP_{all} - BP_1
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **master Morse Test equation**:
$$\boxed{IP_i = BP_{all} - BP_i}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine running at **2500 rpm** was subjected to a Morse Test. The brake power readings obtained were:
> - All four cylinders firing: **$BP_{all}$ = 18.50 kW**
> - Cylinder 1 cut off: $BP_1$ = 13.40 kW
> - Cylinder 2 cut off: $BP_2$ = 13.80 kW
> - Cylinder 3 cut off: $BP_3$ = 13.20 kW
> - Cylinder 4 cut off: $BP_4$ = 13.60 kW

**Required:** (a) Indicated Power of each cylinder, (b) Total IP, (c) Friction Power, (d) Mechanical Efficiency.

---

**Step 1: IP of each cylinder**

Using $IP_i = BP_{all} - BP_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_1 &= 18.50 - 13.40 = 5.10 \text{ kW} \\
IP_2 &= 18.50 - 13.80 = 4.70 \text{ kW} \\
IP_3 &= 18.50 - 13.20 = 5.30 \text{ kW} \\
IP_4 &= 18.50 - 13.60 = 4.90 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Total Indicated Power**

$$IP_{total} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$$

$$IP_{total} = 5.10 + 4.70 + 5.30 + 4.90 = 20.00 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification (alternate formula):**
$$IP_{total} = 4 \cdot BP_{all} - (BP_1 + BP_2 + BP_3 + BP_4)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_{total} &= 4 \times 18.50 - (13.40 + 13.80 + 13.20 + 13.60) \\
&= 74.00 - 54.00 = 20.00 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Friction Power**

$$FP = IP_{total} - BP_{all}$$

$$FP = 20.00 - 18.50 = 1.50 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Mechanical Efficiency**

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{BP_{all}}{IP_{total}}$$

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{18.50}{20.00} = 0.925 = 92.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The IPs of the four cylinders vary slightly ($4.70$ to $5.30$ kW). The percentage deviation of the weakest cylinder (2) from the average ($IP_{avg} = 5.00$ kW) is only $\frac{\mid 5.00 - 4.70 \mid}{5.00} \times 100 = 6\%$, which is on the borderline. This would warrant a closer inspection of Cylinder 2 in a real KTU lab report.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Cylinder | $BP_i$ (kW) | $IP_i$ (kW) | % Deviation from Mean |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 13.40 | 5.10 | +2.00% |
| 2 | 13.80 | 4.70 | −6.00% |
| 3 | 13.20 | 5.30 | +6.00% |
| 4 | 13.60 | 4.90 | −2.00% |
| **Total/Mean** | **54.00** | **20.00 (mean = 5.00)** | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Petrol Engine 4-Stroke 4-Cylinder] --> B[Coupling Flange]
    B --> C[Dynamometer]
    C --> D[Load Application System]
    C --> E[Tachometer]
    A --> F[Fuel Tank with Measuring Unit]
    A --> G[Cylinder Cut-off Switch per Cylinder]
    H[Manometer] --> C
    I[Cooling Water Supply] --> A
    J[Exhaust Manifold] --> K[Silencer]
    A --> J
```

### 4.2 Procedural Flowchart of the Morse Test

```mermaid
graph TD
    StepA[Start: Warm up engine to rated temperature] --> StepB[Run engine at constant N rpm with all 4 cylinders firing]
    StepB --> StepC[Record BPall using dynamometer]
    StepC --> StepD[Cut off fuel/spark of Cylinder 1]
    StepD --> StepE[Adjust load to maintain same N rpm and motor engine]
    StepE --> StepF[Record BP1]
    StepF --> StepG[Restore Cylinder 1, repeat for Cylinder 2]
    StepG --> StepH[Record BP2]
    StepH --> StepI[Restore Cylinder 2, repeat for Cylinder 3]
    StepI --> StepJ[Record BP3]
    StepJ --> StepK[Restore Cylinder 3, repeat for Cylinder 4]
    StepK --> StepL[Record BP4]
    StepL --> StepM[Compute IPi = BPall minus BPi for each cylinder]
    StepM --> StepN[Compute IPtotal sum of IPi]
    StepN --> StepO[Compute FP = IPtotal minus BPall]
    StepO --> StepP[Compute Mechanical Efficiency eta m]
    StepP --> StepQ[Tabulate and report results]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Energy Flow During Test

```mermaid
graph LR
    ChemE[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> IPtotal[Total Indicated Power IPtotal]
    IPtotal --> BPall[Brake Power BPall Output Shaft]
    IPtotal --> FP[Friction Power FP Loss]
    FP --> F1[Piston Ring Friction]
    FP --> F2[Bearing Friction]
    FP --> F3[Windage and Pumping Loss]
    FP --> F4[Accessory Drive Loss]
    IPtotal --> IP1[Cylinder 1 IP]
    IPtotal --> IP2[Cylinder 2 IP]
    IPtotal --> IP3[Cylinder 3 IP]
    IPtotal --> IP4[Cylinder 4 IP]
```

### 4.4 Apparatus Required (KTU Viva List)

> [!NOTE]
> - Four-stroke, four-cylinder petrol engine test rig (typically **4.4 kW – 7.5 kW** rated)
> - **Hydraulic dynamometer** (preferred) or rope brake drum
> - **Tachometer** (digital, non-contact type)
> - **U-tube manometer** (for hydraulic dynamometer)
> - **Spring balance** and dead-weight set (for rope brake)
> - **Fuel measuring burette** with stopwatch
> - Cylinder cut-off switches (one per cylinder) mounted on rig
> - Thermocouples or thermometers for cooling water inlet/outlet and exhaust
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Q1.** State the fundamental assumption on which the Morse Test is based. *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Morse Test is based on the assumption that the **friction power (FP) of the engine remains constant** at a given speed, regardless of which cylinder is cut off or the load condition. Consequently, the **mechanical efficiency** is also assumed constant during the test. This allows the difference in brake power readings to be directly attributed to the indicated power of the deactivated cylinder.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Q2.** Why is the Morse Test not directly applicable to a two-cylinder engine? *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
In a two-cylinder engine, when one cylinder is cut off, the entire load must be borne by only one cylinder. This causes a **drastic change in operating conditions**, including altered combustion timing, increased thermal stress, and changed mechanical efficiency. The fundamental assumption of **constant frictional power** breaks down. Additionally, the engine may fail to run smoothly or even stall, making the readings unreliable. Hence the Morse Test is practically recommended only for **engines with four or more cylinders** (typically four, six, or eight).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`** **Question A.** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **experimental procedure to conduct the Morse Test** on a four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine. List the apparatus required. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Apparatus:** Four-stroke petrol engine test rig, hydraulic dynamometer (or rope brake), digital tachometer, manometer, spring balance, fuel measuring unit, cylinder cut-off switch panel, exhaust gas analyzer (optional), thermometer.
2. **Procedure:**
   - Step 1: Start the engine and allow it to warm up to its rated operating temperature (coolant at ~80 °C, lubricating oil at ~75 °C).
   - Step 2: Adjust the throttle to maintain a **constant rated speed** (e.g., 2500 rpm). Apply a known load through the dynamometer.
   - Step 3: Note the speed **N**, the dynamometer load **W** (or manometer reading), and the spring balance reading **S**. Compute $BP_{all} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S)}{60 \times 1000}$ kW.
   - Step 4: Cut off the fuel/spark of **Cylinder 1** using the cut-off switch (ensuring it does not produce power but still rotates).
   - Step 5: Adjust the load to maintain the **same speed N**. Record the new brake power $BP_1$.
   - Step 6: Restore Cylinder 1. Repeat Steps 4–5 for **Cylinders 2, 3, and 4** sequentially.
   - Step 7: Compute $IP_i = BP_{all} - BP_i$ for each cylinder.
   - Step 8: Tabulate results and calculate $\eta_m = BP_{all} / IP_{total}$.

**(b)** During a Morse Test on a four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine running at **2800 rpm**, the following readings were recorded:
- All cylinders firing: $BP_{all}$ = **22.0 kW**
- No. 1 cut off: 16.8 kW; No. 2 cut off: 17.4 kW; No. 3 cut off: 17.0 kW; No. 4 cut off: 16.4 kW

Calculate: **(i)** Indicated Power of each cylinder, **(ii)** Total IP, **(iii)** Friction Power, **(iv)** Mechanical Efficiency. Identify any weak cylinder. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) IP of each cylinder** using $IP_i = BP_{all} - BP_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
IP_1 &= 22.0 - 16.8 = 5.2 \text{ kW} \\
IP_2 &= 22.0 - 17.4 = 4.6 \text{ kW} \\
IP_3 &= 22.0 - 17.0 = 5.0 \text{ kW} \\
IP_4 &= 22.0 - 16.4 = 5.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Total Indicated Power:**

$$IP_{total} = 5.2 + 4.6 + 5.0 + 5.6 = 20.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $IP_{total} = 4 \times 22.0 - (16.8 + 17.4 + 17.0 + 16.4) = 88.0 - 67.6 = 20.4 \text{ kW} \checkmark$

**(iii) Friction Power:**

$$FP = IP_{total} - BP_{all} = 20.4 - 22.0 = -1.6 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative Friction Power** is mathematically inadmissible and indicates an **experimental error** — typically the dynamometer was not loaded enough during the "all cylinders firing" run, or the speed was not held strictly constant. In a real exam, you must state this anomaly clearly.

**(iv) Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{22.0}{20.4} \times 100 = 107.8\%$$

This confirms an **experimental error** — efficiency cannot exceed 100%.

**Weak Cylinder Identification:** Mean IP = $20.4 / 4 = 5.10$ kW.
Cylinder 2 has IP = 4.6 kW, deviation = $\frac{\mid 5.10 - 4.60 \mid}{5.10} \times 100 = 9.8\%$.
Since this **exceeds ±5%**, **Cylinder 2 is the weakest**.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Question B (Alternative)** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Define **Mechanical Efficiency** and **Friction Power**. Derive the expression for the indicated power of a cylinder using the Morse Test principle. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** It is the ratio of brake power delivered at the output shaft to the indicated power developed inside the cylinders.

$$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

**Friction Power (FP):** It is the power lost in overcoming internal mechanical resistances such as piston ring friction, bearing friction, windage, and pumping losses.

**Derivation:** *(Same as Section 3.1 of these notes)* — provided in full.

**(b)** Discuss the **sources of error** and **precautions** to be observed while conducting the Morse Test. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of Error:**
1. **Speed Variation:** Even minor RPM changes alter frictional power, violating the core assumption.
2. **Incomplete Cut-off:** If the cylinder is not fully deactivated (e.g., residual combustion due to hot deposits), its IP is not zero.
3. **Dynamometer Calibration Drift:** Inaccurate load readings.
4. **Variation in Mechanical Efficiency:** Assumed constant but actually changes slightly with load and cylinder combination.
5. **Thermal Effects:** Cooling water temperature fluctuation alters friction.
6. **Manometer Reading Error** (for hydraulic dynamometer).

**Precautions:**
1. Ensure the engine has reached **steady-state thermal conditions** before recording.
2. Maintain **speed within ±10 rpm** of the rated value using a precise digital tachometer.
3. Cut off the cylinder **fully** — disconnect both spark and fuel supply.
4. Use a **calibrated dynamometer** and verify zero-load friction reading.
5. Allow a **stabilization period** (at least 30 seconds) after each cut-off before recording.
6. Conduct the test **quickly** to minimize drift in atmospheric conditions.
7. Repeat each reading **at least twice** and take the average.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** by forgetting to convert time units (e.g., writing $\pi N (W-S)/60$ without converting N from rpm to rps).
> - Students often **report negative friction power without comment** — this is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in KTU papers. Always analyze and explain.
> - Forgetting to mention that the Morse Test is **inapplicable to 1- or 2-cylinder engines** costs 1 mark.
> - Not identifying the **weakest cylinder with percentage deviation calculation** loses 1 mark.
> - Writing the formula $BP = \pi N (W - S) / 60$ but using **wrong sign convention** (forgetting that spring balance reading S must be subtracted when using rope brake with pulley arrangement) is a classic KTU pitfall.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Morse Test**

- **Invented by:** Morse and Harnish (hence the name).
- **Core Assumption:** Friction Power (FP) and mechanical efficiency remain **constant** at a given speed.
- **Master Equation:** $IP_i = BP_{all} - BP_i$ for each cylinder.
- **Total IP:** Either sum of individual IPs OR $4 \cdot BP_{all} - \sum BP_i$.
- **Friction Power:** $FP = IP_{total} - BP_{all}$ (should always be positive).
- **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_m = BP_{all} / IP_{total} \times 100$ (always between 70–85%).
- **Acceptable Cylinder Uniformity:** All cylinder IPs should be within **±5%** of the mean.
- **Not Suitable for:** 1- or 2-cylinder engines (assumption fails).
- **Typical Procedure:** Warm-up → record $BP_{all}$ → sequentially cut off each cylinder → record $BP_i$ → compute.
- **Unit Watch:** BP and IP always in **kW**; speed in **rpm**; torque in **N·m**.
- **Real Engine BP Formula:** $BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000}$ kW where $T = (W - S) \times R$ for rope brake.
- **Diagnostic Use:** Identifies weak cylinders, faulty spark plugs, carburetor maldistribution, valve timing errors.
- **Precautions:** Constant speed, steady thermal state, complete cut-off, calibrated dynamometer.
- **Negative FP or $\eta_m > 100\%$** always indicates an experimental error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Horizontal Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Load Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Horizontal Diesel Engine # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Load Test (Experimental Definition):** > A **load test** on an internal combustion engine is a controlled experimental procedure in which the engine is run at a **constant rated speed** while the **applied load** on the brake dynamometer is increased in discrete steps from **no-load (0%)** to **full-load (100%+)**. The corresponding readings of **brake load, fuel consumption, air consumption, cooling water flow, exhaust gas temperature, and time** are recorded at each load step to determine the **performance characteristics** of the engine. For a **four-stroke, single-cylinder, horizontal diesel engine**, the load test is performed on a **rope brake dynamometer** (or hydraulic dynamometer) coupled to the engine's shaft. The horizontal configuration is widely used in **small marine auxiliary engines**, **genset prime movers**, and **fishing vessel propulsion units** because it offers: - Lower center of gravity - Ease of maintenance access - Compact installation footprint in ship engine rooms The test yields critical **performance curves**: 1. Brake Power vs. Load 2. Specific Fuel Consumption vs. Brake Power 3. Brake Thermal Efficiency vs. Load 4. Mechanical Efficiency vs. Load 5. Heat Balance Sheet (cumulative energy audit) ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Mariner's Treadmill:** > Imagine a sailor pulling a thick rope over a wooden pulley attached to a heavy iron block — this is exactly how a **rope brake dynamometer** works. As the engine's crankshaft rotates, a rope wrapped around the **brake drum** presses against the drum due to a calibrated weight. The friction between rope and drum "loads" the engine — just like adding more iron blocks makes the sailor pull harder. Key parallels: - **Engine crankshaft rotation** → Rope sliding over rotating drum - **Applied dead weights** → Added load / resistance - **Fuel burnt harder** → Sailor pulls harder to keep pace - **Speed drop** → Sailor tires (RPM falls slightly as load rises) - **Governor action** → Maintains nearly constant RPM by increasing fuel So when a student records readings and plots graphs, they are essentially mapping **how hard the engine is working** versus **how much useful work (power) and fuel (energy) it delivers** — analogous to plotting a sailor's energy expenditure against the cargo he moves. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Constants (must memorize):** > - **Calorific Value of Diesel** = **42,000 kJ/kg** (≈ 10,200 kcal/kg, standard KTU reference value) > - **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat** = 1 kcal = **4.186 kJ** > - **Indicated Mean Effective Pressure** is typically measured separately (Morse test or direct indicator) — not computed in a basic load test. > - **Atmospheric Pressure** at KTU labs ≈ **1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa** > - **Standard Governor Setting** for test: **1500 RPM** (typical 4-stroke single cylinder horizontal diesel rated speed in lab) ## 1.4 Aim of the Experiment (KTU Standard Wording) > [!NOTE] > **Aim:** To conduct a load test on a four-stroke single cylinder horizontal diesel engine, determine its **brake power (BP), indicated power (IP), frictional power (FP), mechanical efficiency (η_mech), specific fuel consumption (SFC), brake thermal efficiency (η_bth), indicated thermal efficiency (η_ith), volumetric efficiency (η_vol)**, and draw the complete **heat balance sheet** at full load. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Load Test is Performed The load test is the **single most important performance evaluation** of any prime mover because it quantifies four engineering quantities simultaneously: | # | Quantity | Why It Matters | |---|----------|----------------| | 1 | **Brake Power (BP)** | Actual usable shaft power delivered to the load — paid for by the customer | | 2 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | Total power developed inside the cylinder — the "true" engine output | | 3 | **Frictional Power (FP)** | Power lost to friction, pumping, accessories — the "tax" of being an engine | | 4 | **Fuel Consumption (m_f)** | The energy input — what the operator pays for every hour | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Energy Equation (Universal ICE Energy Balance):** > $$\text{Heat Supplied by Fuel} \; (Q_{in}) = \text{Brake Power Output} + \text{Heat Lost (Cooling + Exhaust + Unaccounted)}$$ ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic The load test follows a strict **experimental protocol** that mirrors actual shipboard sea-trial procedures used by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, BV, DNV): **Step 1 — Warm-up Run (No-Load, 10–15 minutes)** - Engine is started and run at rated speed with **zero load** until cooling water outlet temperature stabilizes at manufacturer's value (typically **60–70 °C**). - Reason: Lubricating oil must reach operating viscosity (~ SAE 30 at 80 °C) to avoid friction measurement error. **Step 2 — Application of Graduated Loads** - Load is increased in steps of **20%** of rated load (0%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%, 110% overload). - At each step, the engine is allowed to run for **3–5 minutes** to stabilize. - Governor restores speed to **rated RPM ± 2%** by admitting more fuel. **Step 3 — Recording of Observations** - Spring balance readings (S₁, S₂) on rope brake - Time for fixed volume of fuel (t seconds for V ml) - Cooling water flow rate (m_w) - Cooling water inlet & outlet temperatures (T₁, T₂) - Air consumption via orifice meter - Exhaust gas temperature (T_exh) - Ambient temperature, pressure, humidity **Step 4 — Calculation of Performance Parameters** (using formulas in §2.3) **Step 5 — Plotting of Performance Curves** - BP vs. Load - SFC vs. BP - η_bth vs. BP - η_mech vs. BP - Heat balance bar chart at full load **Step 6 — Heat Balance Sheet Preparation** (cumulative over 1 hour at full load) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas use **SI units** unless otherwise stated. Memorize this entire table — it appears in **every** KTU university exam question on this topic. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes | |---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------| | 1 | Net brake load (W) | $W = (S_1 - S_2) + W_{drum}$ where W_drum = effective rope + drum weight | kgf or N | Convert kgf → N by × 9.81 | | 2 | Brake Power (BP) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N W R}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | N in RPM, W in N, R in metres | | 3 | Brake Power (alt) | $BP = \dfrac{W \cdot N}{9560}$ for W in kgf | kW | Quick-formula version | | 4 | Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP} \times 3600$ | kg/kWh | m_f in kg/s | | 5 | Fuel rate in kg/hr | $m_f = \dfrac{V \times \rho_f}{t} \times 3600$ | kg/hr | V in ml, t in sec, ρ in kg/L | | 6 | Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ | % | CV = 42000 kJ/kg | | 7 | Indicated Power (IP) from Morse Test | $IP = \dfrac{BP \times p_{m,\,running}}{p_{m,\,motor}}$ | kW | Only when Morse test done | | 8 | Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech) | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | % | Typical range 75–88% | | 9 | Frictional Power (FP) | $FP = IP - BP$ | kW | Always positive | | 10 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_ith) | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ | % | Always > η_bth | | 11 | Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol) | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual air mass}}{\rho_{air} \times V_{disp} \times \frac{N}{2} \times 60}$ | % | For 4-stroke: N/2 cycles/min | | 12 | Heat lost to cooling water | $Q_w = m_w c_{pw} (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/hr | c_pw = 4.186 kJ/kg·K | | 13 | Heat lost to exhaust gas | $Q_{exh} = m_{exh} c_{pg} (T_{exh} - T_{amb})$ | kJ/hr | c_pg ≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K (approx as air) | | 14 | Unaccounted heat | $Q_{unacc} = Q_{in} - (BP + Q_w + Q_{exh})$ | kJ/hr | Should be 5–15% of Q_in | ## 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The exact procedure used in this KTU lab experiment mirrors the **ISO 3046-1 standard** and the **ISO 8178** emission test cycle used by marine classification societies during ship sea trials. The performance curves plotted during this experiment are submitted to **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)** or **DNV** for **Type Approval Certification** of marine diesel engines. Specific real-world applications: - **Marine Generator Set Performance Verification** — Auxiliary engines on ships - **Fishing Vessel Main Engine** — Small horizontal engines power trawlers in Kerala's coastal fleet - **Inland Waterways Vessels** — Specification under Inland Vessels Act - **BSFC-based fuel budgeting** — Operators compute vessel bunker consumption using SFC - **Heat Balance for Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** — Marine engine exhaust boilers use exhaust heat data # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Rope Brake Dynamometer ### Physical Setup A rope is wrapped around the brake drum of radius **R (m)** attached to the engine crankshaft. One end of the rope carries a **dead weight W**, the other end is connected to a **spring balance** reading **S**. When the drum rotates, friction tension difference is **(W − S)**. ### Step 1: Net Effective Load The actual load causing friction torque on the drum: $$F_{net} = (W - S) + W_{rope}$$ where $W_{rope}$ is half the weight of the effective rope length (for a single rope configuration, the rope's own weight acts vertically on one side; KTU lab standard: **rope weight contribution is added directly to W** if explicitly mentioned in observation table). ### Step 2: Frictional Torque Torque resisting drum rotation equals force × drum radius: $$T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \times R \quad \text{[N·m]}$$ ### Step 3: Work Done per Revolution Each revolution overcomes friction over a tangential distance equal to the drum circumference $2 \pi R$: $$W_{rev} = F_{net} \times 2\pi R = 2\pi (W - S) R$$ ### Step 4: Power Definition Power = Work per unit time. With **N revolutions per minute**, work done per minute: $$W_{per\,min} = 2\pi (W - S) R \times N$$ ### Step 5: Convert to kW (SI) $$BP = \dfrac{2\pi (W - S) R N}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$ $$BP = \dfrac{2\pi (W - S) R N}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion shortcut KTU examiners expect:** > If **W is in kgf** and **R in metres**, the standard exam formula is: > $$\boxed{\,BP = \dfrac{(W - S) \times R \times N}{9560}\,} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ This 9560 constant comes from $\frac{60 \times 1000}{2\pi \times 9.81}$. ## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) ### Step 1: Volume of Fuel Consumed A calibrated burette supplies **V ml** of fuel in **t seconds**. ### Step 2: Mass Flow Rate $$\rho_{diesel} = 0.85 \text{ kg/L} = 0.00085 \text{ kg/ml}$$ $$m_f = \dfrac{V \times 0.85}{t} \quad \text{[kg/sec]}$$ ### Step 3: Convert to Mass per Hour $$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V \times 0.85 \times 3600}{t \times 1000} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$ or equivalently: $$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{3.06 \times V}{t} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$ ### Step 4: SFC Definition SFC = fuel consumed per unit brake power per hour: $$\boxed{\,SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \times 1000\,} \quad \text{[g/kWh]}$$ ### Step 5: Alternative Bracketed Form (used by KTU examiners) $$SFC = \dfrac{3600 \times m_f \text{(kg/s)}}{BP \text{(kW)}} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Brake Thermal Efficiency ### Step 1: Energy Input per Hour $$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = \dot{m}_f \times 42000 \quad \text{[kJ/hr]}$$ ### Step 2: Energy Output per Hour (Brake Power) $$Q_{out} = BP \times 3600 \quad \text{[kJ/hr]} \quad \text{(since 1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 3600 kJ/hr)}$$ ### Step 3: Thermal Efficiency Definition $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times 42000}$$ ### Step 4: Substituting m_f expression $$\boxed{\,\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600 \times t}{V \times 0.85 \times 42000} \times 100\,} \quad \text{[\%]}$$ > [!NOTE] > For diesel engines, **η_bth ranges 28–35%** at full load (unboosted). Turbocharged marine diesels reach **38–42%**. ## 3.4 Derivation of Heat Balance Sheet The heat balance sheet is an **energy audit** showing where 100% of fuel energy goes. It must always sum to **100% ± 1%** (rounding). ### Energy Inflows (always 100%) $$Q_{in,\,total} = \dot{m}_f \times CV \times 1 \text{ hour} = 100\%$$ ### Energy Outflows (must sum to 100%) | # | Component | Formula | Typical % (Diesel) | |---|-----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Brake Power (Useful) | $BP \times 3600 / Q_{in}$ | **30%** | | 2 | Heat to Cooling Water | $Q_w = m_w c_{pw}(T_2 - T_1)$ | **25–30%** | | 3 | Heat to Exhaust Gas | $Q_{exh} = (m_{air} + m_f) c_p (T_{exh} - T_{amb})$ | **30–35%** | | 4 | Unaccounted (radiation + lube oil + blowby) | Balance | **5–15%** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification Check:** > $$\sum Q_{out} = Q_{in}$$ > If unaccounted > 20%, check instrument calibration (typical KTU pitfall — students forget to convert mass flows). ## 3.5 Complete Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Exam Pattern) ### Given Data (4-stroke, single cylinder, horizontal diesel engine) | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Brake drum diameter | D = 60 cm = 0.60 m → R = 0.30 m | | Rated speed | N = 1500 RPM | | Dead weight | W = 30 kg | | Spring balance reading | S = 5 kg | | Fuel for 20 ml | t = 15 sec | | Fuel density | ρ = 0.85 kg/L | | Cooling water flow | m_w = 300 kg/hr | | Cooling water T₁ (inlet) | 25 °C | | Cooling water T₂ (outlet) | 55 °C | | Exhaust temperature | T_exh = 380 °C | | Ambient temperature | T_amb = 30 °C | | Air consumption | m_air = 6 kg/hr | | Calorific value | CV = 42000 kJ/kg | | Indicated power (assumed from separate Morse test) | IP = 5.0 kW | ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Net Load** $$W_{net} = W - S = 30 - 5 = 25 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 2: Brake Power** $$BP = \dfrac{W_{net} \times R \times N}{9560} = \dfrac{25 \times 0.30 \times 1500}{9560}$$ $$BP = \dfrac{11250}{9560} = 1.177 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Fuel Mass Flow Rate** $$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V \times \rho \times 3600}{t \times 1000} = \dfrac{20 \times 0.85 \times 3600}{15 \times 1000}$$ $$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{61200}{15000} = 4.08 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Step 4: Specific Fuel Consumption** $$SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 1000}{BP} = \dfrac{4.08 \times 1000}{1.177}$$ $$SFC = 3466 \text{ g/kWh} = 3.466 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write SFC in g/kWh but forget to multiply by 1000 when m_f is in kg/hr. The conversion: kg/hr → g/hr is × 1000. Then ÷ BP (in kW) gives g/kWh directly. **Step 5: Brake Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.177 \times 3600}{4.08 \times 42000} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{4237.2}{171360} \times 100 = 2.47\%$$ > [!NOTE] > This very low value is because this is a **small lab test engine** (≈1.2 kW). Real diesel engines deliver 28–35% η_bth. KTU exam engines typically give 5–12% because of small bore & high heat loss. **Step 6: Mechanical Efficiency (using given IP)** $$\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.177}{5.0} \times 100 = 23.54\%$$ **Step 7: Frictional Power** $$FP = IP - BP = 5.0 - 1.177 = 3.823 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8: Heat Balance Sheet (per hour)** **Energy Input:** $$Q_{in} = 4.08 \times 42000 = 171360 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ **Brake Power Heat Equivalent:** $$Q_{BP} = BP \times 3600 = 1.177 \times 3600 = 4237.2 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ $$\%\text{ BP} = \dfrac{4237.2}{171360} \times 100 = 2.47\%$$ **Heat Lost to Cooling Water:** $$Q_w = m_w c_{pw}(T_2 - T_1) = 300 \times 4.186 \times (55 - 25)$$ $$Q_w = 300 \times 4.186 \times 30 = 37674 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ $$\%Q_w = \dfrac{37674}{171360} \times 100 = 21.99\%$$ **Heat Lost to Exhaust Gas (approximating exhaust mass = air + fuel):** $$m_{exh} = m_{air} + m_f = 6 + 4.08 = 10.08 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$Q_{exh} = m_{exh} \times c_{pg} \times (T_{exh} - T_{amb}) = 10.08 \times 1.005 \times (380 - 30)$$ $$Q_{exh} = 10.08 \times 1.005 \times 350 = 3546.6 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ $$\%Q_{exh} = \dfrac{3546.6}{171360} \times 100 = 2.07\%$$ **Unaccounted Heat:** $$Q_{unacc} = 100 - (2.47 + 21.99 + 2.07) = 73.47\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Unaccounted heat > 50% is unusual. This signals either **instrument error**, **incomplete combustion** (engine running very rich), or **missing measurements** (e.g., blowby not measured). Always comment on the unaccounted heat magnitude in the viva — examiners award full marks only if you **interpret** the heat balance, not just compute it. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Schematic of the Experimental Test Bed (Mermaid Block Diagram) The complete lab setup is shown below as a **functional block-level architecture** showing signal, mechanical, and fluid-flow pathways between subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Diesel Engine
4 Stroke Single Cylinder Horizontal] nodeB[Engine Crankshaft] nodeC[Brake Drum
Diameter 60 cm] nodeD[Rope Brake Assembly] nodeE[Dead Weight W
Calibrated] nodeF[Spring Balance S] nodeG[Governor System
Mechanical Centrifugal] nodeH[Fuel Tank] nodeI[Burette 100 ml] nodeJ[Stop Watch] nodeK[Cooling Water Inlet T1] nodeL[Cooling Water Outlet T2] nodeM[Rotameter Flow Meter] nodeN[Orifice Plate Air Box] nodeO[U Tube Manometer] nodeP[Exhaust Gas Pipe] nodeQ[Thermocouple T Exh] nodeR[Tachometer RPM Sensor] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeG --> nodeA nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeA nodeJ --> nodeI nodeA --> nodeK nodeA --> nodeL nodeM --> nodeK nodeN --> nodeA nodeO --> nodeN nodeA --> nodeP nodeP --> nodeQ nodeR --> nodeB ``` ## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flow Diagram (Sequential Workflow) ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Start Engine and Warm Up 10 to 15 min at No Load] stepB[Set Rated Speed via Governor 1500 RPM] stepC[Apply 0 percent Load and Record Observations] stepD[Increase Load in 20 percent Steps] stepE[Wait 3 min for Stabilization at Each Load] stepF[Record Spring Balance S Time for Fuel Exhaust Temp etc] stepG[Increment Load Up to 110 percent Overload] stepH[Reduce Load Stepwise to Zero] stepI[Shut Down Engine after Cooling] stepJ[Tabulate All Observations in Standard Format] stepK[Compute BP SFC Efficiencies Heat Balance] stepL[Plot Performance Curves using Graph Paper] stepM[Compare with Rated Manufacturer Values and Submit Report] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepI stepI --> stepJ stepJ --> stepK stepK --> stepL stepL --> stepM ``` ## 4.3 Energy Flow Diagram for Heat Balance (Sankey-style Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR fuelIn[Fuel Energy Input Q in 100 percent] bpOut[Brake Power Useful 30 percent] coolOut[Heat to Cooling Water 28 percent] exhOut[Heat in Exhaust Gas 33 percent] unaccOut[Unaccounted Heat 9 percent] fuelIn --> bpOut fuelIn --> coolOut fuelIn --> exhOut fuelIn --> unaccOut ``` ## 4.4 Performance Curves — Typical Plot Shape Description | Curve | X-axis | Y-axis | Shape | Engineering Insight | |-------|--------|--------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Load (%) | BP (kW) | Linear rising | BP rises linearly with load | | 2 | BP (kW) | SFC (g/kWh) | U-shaped (minimum near 80% load) | Engine most fuel-efficient near full load | | 3 | BP (kW) | η_bth (%) | Rising, peaks near 90% load | Best thermal efficiency before friction rise | | 4 | BP (kW) | η_mech (%) | Slightly rising (75 → 88%) | Friction grows slower than BP | | 5 | BP (kW) | Exhaust Temp (°C) | Linear rising | Combustion gas temperature scales with load | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When asked "why does SFC have a minimum?", answer: *At light loads, friction losses dominate and fuel is burnt inefficiently (high SFC). At full load, thermal efficiency is highest (low SFC). Beyond 110% overload, combustion becomes incomplete and SFC rises again.* ## 4.5 Sectional View of a 4-Stroke Horizontal Diesel Engine (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB cyl[Cylinder Head with Injector] comb[Combustion Chamber] piston[Piston with Rings] conrod[Connecting Rod] crank[Crankshaft Horizontal] flywheel[Flywheel] inletvalve[Inlet Valve Air Only] exhaustvalve[Exhaust Valve] gov[Governor Linkage] injpump[Injection Pump] cyl --> comb comb --> piston piston --> conrod conrod --> crank crank --> flywheel cyl --> inletvalve cyl --> exhaustvalve gov --> injpump injpump --> cyl ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Expectation:** When asked to "sketch the engine", students must show **horizontal layout** with crankshaft, cylinder, flywheel, governor, and fuel injection pump clearly labelled.
