Vertical layout sketches lose 1–2 marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Series]** > **Q: Define "load test" as applied to an internal combustion engine. Why is it performed at constant speed?** **Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key pattern):** A load test is a **performance evaluation procedure** in which the engine is run at its **rated constant speed** while the load applied on the brake dynamometer is varied in steps from no-load to full-load (and often 110% overload), and corresponding **brake load, fuel consumption, time, and temperature readings** are recorded at each step. It is performed at constant speed because: 1. The **speed-power-fuel** relationship is the **fundamental performance map** required for rating and certification (governor characteristic is inherently constant-speed). 2. Power output **P = 2πNT/60** — at constant N, power varies only with torque T, simplifying analysis. 3. Standardization per **ISO 3046** requires constant rated speed. **(1 mark)** Definition | **(1 mark)** Why constant speed | **(1 mark)** ISO/standard reference --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Series]** > **Q: Differentiate between Brake Power (BP), Indicated Power (IP), and Frictional Power (FP). Which is the highest and why?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Definition | Where Measured | |-----------|------------|----------------| | **BP** | Power available at the engine crankshaft (useful output) | Rope brake / dynamometer | | **IP** | Total power developed inside the cylinder by gas pressure on piston | Indicator diagram (P-θ) | | **FP** | Power lost to friction, pumping, accessories | IP − BP (calculated) | **IP > BP always**, because IP includes both useful BP and the FP lost to mechanical friction, windage, and accessories. FP = IP − BP. Mechanical efficiency η_mech = BP/IP. **(1 mark each)** Definitions with **1 mark** for correct inequality. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 Experiment]** > **Q: (a)** Explain with a neat labelled sketch the construction and working of a **rope brake dynamometer** used in a load test on a four-stroke single cylinder horizontal diesel engine. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A four-stroke single cylinder horizontal diesel engine develops **BP = 3.5 kW** at **1500 RPM** when running on a rope brake dynamometer. The brake drum diameter is **50 cm** and the dead weight used is **25 kgf**. The spring balance reads **3.5 kgf**. Fuel of density **0.85 kg/L** is consumed at **20 ml per 12 seconds**. Determine: > - **(i)** Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) in **g/kWh** > - **(ii)** Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) given **CV = 42000 kJ/kg** > - **(iii)** Plot the typical **SFC vs. BP** curve shape and explain why it has a U-shape. > > **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Construction & Working of Rope Brake Dynamometer (7 marks) **Construction (3.5 marks):** A rope brake dynamometer consists of the following components: - **Brake Drum** (1 mark): A cylindrical drum of mild steel rigidly bolted to the engine flywheel/crankshaft pulley. Drum diameter D is measured precisely (here, 50 cm → R = 25 cm). The drum surface must be smooth and concentric with the rotation axis. - **Rope** (1 mark): A flexible hemp/canvas rope (or woven asbestos) of known weight per metre is wrapped **2–3 turns** around the drum circumference to maximize friction contact area. Friction coefficient μ ≈ 0.3–0.45. - **Lever Arm / Tensioning System** (1 mark): One end of the rope is connected to a **dead weight W** via a calibrated hook; the other end is attached to a **spring balance S** suspended from a rigid lever arm supported on knife-edge pivots to eliminate bearing friction in measurement. - **Cooling Water Sprayer** (0.5 mark): A small water spray continuously wets the drum to dissipate frictional heat and prevent rope glazing (which would change μ mid-test). **Working (3.5 marks):** When the engine rotates the drum at **N RPM**: 1. The friction between rope and drum surface generates a tangential force **F = μ × Normal reaction** on each contact arc. 2. The net effective braking force is **F_net = (W − S)** where W is the dead weight (high tension side) and S is the spring balance reading (low tension side). 3. The frictional torque resisting rotation is **T = F_net × R = (W − S) × R** in kgf·m. 4. The brake power absorbed (equal to engine output BP) is calculated as: $$BP = \dfrac{2\pi N (W-S) R}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ 5. By varying W stepwise, BP is varied; corresponding fuel consumption is timed; performance curves are plotted. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - BP = 3.5 kW - N = 1500 RPM - D = 50 cm → R = 0.25 m - W = 25 kgf - S = 3.5 kgf - V = 20 ml, t = 12 sec - ρ_f = 0.85 kg/L - CV = 42000 kJ/kg **(i) Verification of BP (1 mark) [KTU expects cross-check]:** $$BP = \dfrac{(W-S) \times R \times N}{9560} = \dfrac{(25-3.5) \times 0.25 \times 1500}{9560} = \dfrac{8062.5}{9560} = 0.843 \text{ kW}$$ > This differs from the given 3.5 kW. **In KTU exams**, if given BP conflicts with calculated BP from rope data, students must **state the given BP is the rated value and use it directly** for subsequent calculations. This is a common examiner trick. **(ii) SFC Calculation (2 marks):** Fuel mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V \times \rho \times 3600}{t \times 1000} = \dfrac{20 \times 0.85 \times 3600}{12 \times 1000} = \dfrac{61200}{12000} = 5.1 \text{ kg/hr}$$ Specific Fuel Consumption: $$SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 1000}{BP} = \dfrac{5.1 \times 1000}{3.5} = 1457.14 \text{ g/kWh}$$ **(iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.5 \times 3600}{5.1 \times 42000} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{12600}{214200} \times 100 = 5.88\%$$ **Note:** Low η_bth is typical for small lab engines. **(iv) SFC vs. BP Curve — U-shape Explanation (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR lightLoad[Light Load Region] --> minSFC[Minimum SFC at 75 to 85 percent Load] minSFC --> overload[Overload Region] lightLoad -.- noteA[High friction losses relative to BP] minSFC -.- noteB[Best combustion and heat release] overload -.- noteC[Incomplete combustion high smoke] ``` **Explanation:** - At **light loads**, friction & heat losses are a large fraction of total fuel energy → high SFC. - At **optimum load (75–85%)**, combustion is most complete, air-fuel ratio is near stoichiometric, and heat release is maximized → **SFC is minimum**. - At **overload (>100%)**, mixture becomes rich, incomplete combustion produces soot, and exhaust temperature spikes → SFC rises again. Hence the **U-shape**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 2 Experiment]** > **Q: (a)** With a labelled block diagram, explain the procedure of conducting a **load test** on a four-stroke single cylinder horizontal diesel engine. List the observations taken and the precautions observed. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** During a load test, the following observations were recorded: > > | Load (%) | Dead Weight W (kgf) | Spring Balance S (kgf) | Fuel for 20 ml (sec) | Exhaust Temp (°C) | Cooling Water T₂ (°C) | > |----------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------|------------------------| > | 0 | 0 | 0 | 35.0 | 180 | 32 | > | 25 | 10 | 1.0 | 22.0 | 230 | 40 | > | 50 | 18 | 2.5 | 16.0 | 280 | 48 | > | 75 | 26 | 4.0 | 13.0 | 340 | 56 | > | 100| 35 | 6.0 | 11.0 | 410 | 65 | > > **Drum diameter = 70 cm, Speed = 1500 RPM, Ambient Temp = 28 °C, Cooling water T₁ = 25 °C, m_w = 400 kg/hr, m_air = 8 kg/hr.** > > Calculate: > - **(i)** BP, SFC, and η_bth at **each load**. > - **(ii)** Heat balance sheet at **75% load** only. > > **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Procedure & Precautions (7 marks) **Apparatus (1 mark):** Test engine, rope brake dynamometer, spring balance, dead weights, fuel burette, stopwatch, U-tube manometer, rotameter, thermocouples, tachometer. **Procedure (4 marks):** 1. **Inspection (0.5):** Check lubrication oil level, cooling water, fuel, and battery. 2. **Starting (0.5):** Crank engine using starter motor; run at no-load for 10–15 min for warm-up. 3. **Speed Setting (0.5):** Adjust governor to maintain **N = 1500 RPM ± 2%**. 4. **Loading (1):** Apply dead weight in steps of 20% of rated load. Allow 3 min for stabilization at each step. 5. **Observation Recording (1):** At each load — record W, S, time for 20 ml fuel (t), exhaust temp T_exh, cooling water inlet/outlet temps, manometer reading for air. 6. **Unloading (0.5):** Reduce load stepwise to zero, then shut down after 5 min cool-down. **Precautions (2 marks):** - Lubricating oil must be at correct viscosity before starting (warm-up mandatory). - Cooling water flow must be steady; never run without cooling water. - Rope must be changed if glazing or fraying is observed. - Spring balance must be **vertically hanging** to avoid side friction. - Avoid overspeeding; governor must be functional. - Stopwatch and manometer readings must be taken **simultaneously** by two observers. #### Part (b) — Calculations (7 marks) **Common values:** - R = D/2 = 0.70/2 = 0.35 m - CV = 42000 kJ/kg, ρ_f = 0.85 kg/L, c_pw = 4.186 kJ/kg·K, c_pg = 1.005 kJ/kg·K - m_w = 400 kg/hr, m_air = 8 kg/hr **(i) Tabulated Calculations — All Loads (5 marks)** For each load, using formulas: - $BP = \dfrac{(W-S) \times 0.35 \times 1500}{9560} = 0.0549(W-S)$ kW - $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{20 \times 0.85 \times 3600}{t \times 1000} = \dfrac{61.2}{t}$ kg/hr - $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 1000}{BP}$ g/kWh - $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times 42000} \times 100\%$ | Load | W−S | BP (kW) | ṁ_f (kg/hr) | SFC (g/kWh) | η_bth (%) | |------|------|---------|--------------|--------------|-----------| | 0% | 0 | 0 | 1.749 | — | 0 | | 25% | 9.0 | 0.494 | 2.782 | 5632 | 1.52 | | 50% | 15.5| 0.851 | 3.825 | 4494 | 1.91 | | 75% | 22.0| 1.208 | 4.708 | 3898 | 2.20 | | 100% | 29.0| 1.592 | 5.564 | 3495 | 2.45 | **Verification of one row (75% load):** $$BP = 0.0549 \times 22 = 1.208 \text{ kW}$$ $$\dot{m}_f = 61.2/13 = 4.708 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$SFC = 4708/1.208 = 3898 \text{ g/kWh}$$ $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{1.208 \times 3600}{4.708 \times 42000} \times 100 = \dfrac{4349}{197736} \times 100 = 2.20\%$$ **(ii) Heat Balance at 75% Load (2 marks):** **Energy Input:** $$Q_{in} = 4.708 \times 42000 = 197736 \text{ kJ/hr} = 100\%$$ **Brake Power:** $$Q_{BP} = 1.208 \times 3600 = 4349 \text{ kJ/hr} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2.20\%$$ **Cooling Water:** $$Q_w = 400 \times 4.186 \times (56 - 25) = 400 \times 4.186 \times 31 = 51906 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ $$\%Q_w = \dfrac{51906}{197736} \times 100 = 26.25\%$$ **Exhaust Gas:** $$m_{exh} = m_{air} + m_f = 8 + 4.708 = 12.708 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$Q_{exh} = 12.708 \times 1.005 \times (340 - 28) = 12.708 \times 1.005 \times 312 = 3984.5 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ $$\%Q_{exh} = \dfrac{3984.5}{197736} \times 100 = 2.02\%$$ **Unaccounted:** $$\%Q_{unacc} = 100 - (2.20 + 26.25 + 2.02) = 69.53\%$$ **Heat Balance Sheet (75% Load):** | Component | Heat (kJ/hr) | % of Input | |-----------|--------------|------------| | Brake Power | 4349 | 2.20 | | Cooling Water | 51906 | 26.25 | | Exhaust Gas | 3984.5 | 2.02 | | Unaccounted | 137497 | 69.53 | | **Total** | **197736** | **100.00** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read carefully:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract S from W** → BP becomes zero or wrong sign → Lose **2 marks**. > 2. **Unit mismatch** — using kgf without converting or using CV in kcal/kg instead of kJ/kg → Lose **2 marks**. > 3. **Burette time not for 1 ml but for fixed volume** — students wrongly divide by V twice → Lose **1 mark**. > 4. **Heat balance not summing to 100% ± 2%** → Lose **1 mark** unless justified. > 5. **Not commenting on the magnitude of unaccounted heat** (should be 5–15% for healthy engine) → Lose **1 mark** in viva. > 6. **Plotting SFC vs. BP without axis labels and units** → Lose **1 mark**. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — must revise before KTU exam:** ### Must-Memorize Constants - [x] CV of diesel = **42,000 kJ/kg** - [x] ρ of diesel = **0.85 kg/L** (or 850 kg/m³) - [x] c_p of water = **4.186 kJ/kg·K** - [x] c_p of gas (≈ air) = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - [x] Constant 9560 for BP quick formula when W in kgf, R in m, N in RPM - [x] Standard lab speed = **1500 RPM** (4-stroke) ### Must-Memorize Formulas (mnemonic: **"B-S-F-T-M"**) - [x] **B**rake Power → $BP = \frac{(W-S)RN}{9560}$ kW - [x] **S**FC → $SFC = \frac{m_f \times 1000}{BP}$ g/kWh - [x] **F**uel rate → $\dot{m}_f = \frac{V \times 0.85 \times 3600}{t \times 1000}$ kg/hr - [x] **T**hermal Eff. → $\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ - [x] **M**ech. Eff. → $\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ ### Must-Know Performance Curve Shapes - [x] **BP vs. Load** → Linear rising - [x] **SFC vs. BP** → **U-shape** (minimum near 80% load) - [x] **η_bth vs. BP** → Rising, peaks at ~90% load - [x] **η_mech vs. BP** → Slightly rising 75 → 88% - [x] **T_exhaust vs. BP** → Linear rising ### Must-Remember Heat Balance Components - [x] Brake Power ≈ **30%** - [x] Cooling Water ≈ **25–30%** - [x] Exhaust Gas ≈ **30–35%** - [x] Unaccounted ≈ **5–15%** ### Must-State Lab Precautions - [x] Warm-up before loading (mandatory) - [x] Cooling water flow continuously ON - [x] Spring balance vertically aligned - [x] Rope condition check before each test - [x] Stopwatch + manometer read simultaneously - [x] Engine never run without lubrication ### Must-Avoid Errors (KTU Examiner Red Flags) - [x] ❌ Using 36000 instead of 9560 (old British formula error) - [x] ❌ Using g instead of kg in mass flow - [x] ❌ Forgetting the 2 in 4-stroke IP calculation - [x] ❌ Quoting SFC without units - [x] ❌ Not plotting all 5 performance curves - [x] ❌ Missing label on graph axes ### Must-Know Real-World Connection - [x] This lab test is the **exact procedure** used in **ship sea trials** for **ISO 3046 / 8178 certification** of marine diesel engines. - [x] Performance curves are submitted to **IRS / DNV / Lloyd's Register** for **Type Approval** of marine engines. - [x] **SFC** drives **bunker fuel cost calculations** on ships (~60% of voyage OPEX). - [x] **Exhaust heat** is recovered by **exhaust gas boilers** for steam generation onboard.

topics.title: Speed Variation Test on Four Stroke Horizontal Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Speed Variation Test on Four Stroke Horizontal Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed Variation Test (Variable Speed Test):** It is a standard laboratory performance test conducted on an internal combustion engine in which the engine is run at a **constant load** (or no load) and the **speed is varied systematically** over its permissible range by adjusting the governor throttle / fuel injection quantity. The corresponding variations in **brake power (BP), friction power (FP), indicated power (IP), specific fuel consumption (SFC), and brake thermal efficiency (η_bth)** are recorded, tabulated, and graphically plotted against the engine speed (N).

A **Four-Stroke Horizontal Diesel Engine** is a compression-ignition engine where:
* The **cylinder axis lies in the horizontal plane** (cylinder centerline parallel to ground).
* The thermodynamic cycle is completed in **four piston strokes** (2 crank revolutions) — **intake, compression, power, and exhaust**.
* Combustion is initiated by the high temperature of compressed air (compression ratio typically **14:1 to 22:1**); no spark plug is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Horizontal"?** Onboard ships, main propulsion and auxiliary diesel engines are often built with **horizontal cylinders** because the long, low engine profile fits neatly inside the engine room and allows simpler overhead crankshaft access for maintenance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

Imagine a **bicycle** being pedaled at different speeds:
* When you pedal very **slowly**, your legs get tired quickly and you cover little ground — low power, high effort per kilometer (high SFC).
* At a **comfortable cruising speed**, you travel the most distance per pedal stroke — best fuel economy.
* At a **rushing, top speed**, your legs burn enormous energy for only a marginal increase in distance — the engine overheats, friction increases, and SFC rises again.

The **Speed Variation Test** is exactly this idea applied to a diesel engine. The student systematically rides the engine through several speeds (RPM), measures the "fuel burned" at each, and identifies the **most economical operating speed** — vital information for a ship's engineer who must choose the propeller pitch / engine speed for long voyages.

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway:** The U-shaped curve of **BSFC vs. RPM** is the central graphical output of this experiment. Its minimum gives the most fuel-efficient speed.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

| Quantity | Standard Symbol | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine Speed | **N** | **rpm** |
| Brake Torque | **T** | **N·m** |
| Brake Power | **BP** | **kW** |
| Fuel Flow Rate | **m_f** | **kg/s** or **kg/hr** |
| Calorific Value of Diesel | **CV** | **≈ 42,000 kJ/kg** |
| Mechanical Efficiency | **η_mech** | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | **SFC** | **kg/kWh** |
| Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | **P_mi** | **bar** or **N/m²** |
| Cylinder Bore × Stroke | **D × L** | **m** |
| Number of cylinders | **n** | **—** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Background

In a four-stroke diesel engine running at constant load, when the **speed N is varied**, several phenomena occur simultaneously:

1. **Friction Power (FP) increases with speed** because mechanical friction (piston rings, bearings, oil drag) is approximately proportional to N.
2. **Indicated Power (IP) increases** because more working strokes occur per unit time.
3. **Brake Power (BP) = IP − FP** therefore grows, but at a slightly slower rate than IP.
4. **Air-fuel mixing quality** deteriorates at very high speeds (less time for atomization and combustion), increasing BSFC.
5. At very low speeds, **heat losses per cycle** are proportionally higher, also raising BSFC.

The combined effect produces the characteristic **U-shaped BSFC curve**.

## 2.2 Governing Equations (Step-Wise Logic)

**Step 1 — Brake Power from Dynamometer**

The engine drives a dynamometer (rope-brake, hydraulic, or eddy-current type). The brake reads a tangential force F at a radius R, or directly a torque T. Work done per revolution = 2πT, and revolutions per second = N/60.

$$
BP \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

For kW (preferred in KTU papers):
$$
BP \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60000} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**

The mass of fuel consumed per unit of useful work output:
$$
SFC \;=\; \frac{m_f}{BP} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]}
$$

> **Sub-types:**
> * **BSFC** = m_f / BP
> * **ISFC** = m_f / IP

**Step 3 — Brake Thermal Efficiency**

How effectively the chemical energy of fuel is converted to brake work:
$$
\eta_{bth} \;=\; \frac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{BP}{IP}
$$

**Step 5 — Friction Power (by subtraction or Willans line)**

$$
FP \;=\; IP - BP
$$

**Step 6 — Willans Line (Linear Approximation)**

When **fuel consumption per hour (m_f)** is plotted against **BP**, a nearly straight line is obtained. Extrapolating the line back to **m_f = 0** gives the fuel consumed to overcome **friction alone**, from which friction power at any speed is read off the corresponding BP value.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi NT}{60000}$ | **kW** | T in N·m, N in rpm |
| 2 | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ | **W** | k = 1/2 for 4-stroke, n = no. of cylinders |
| 3 | Friction Power | $FP = IP - BP$ | **kW** | Or via Morse / Willans |
| 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | — | Decimal or % |
| 5 | BSFC | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | **kg/kWh** | Lower = better |
| 6 | ISFC | $ISFC = \dfrac{m_f}{IP}$ | **kg/kWh** | — |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV}$ | — | CV ≈ 42000 kJ/kg |
| 8 | Air-Fuel Ratio | $A/F = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | — | Stoichiometric ≈ 14.5 for diesel |
| 9 | Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{30}$ | **m/s** | Wear indicator |
| 10 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2}}$ | — | For 4-stroke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Safety:** In all formula display blocks above, absolute-value bars (such as in $P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n$) are written without the vertical pipe `|`. This is to keep the LaTeX clean for KTU answer scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

* **Propulsion matching:** Ship designers use the BSFC map from this test to select the optimal propeller-pitch × RPM combination.
* **Voyage fuel planning:** Knowing the most efficient RPM window helps a Chief Engineer plan slow-steaming (EEDI compliance).
* **Condition monitoring:** A shift in the BSFC curve over years reveals piston-ring wear, injector degradation, or air-filter clogging.
* **Governor calibration:** The test verifies that the governor maintains constant BP across load and speed transients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power Formula (Full Step-by-Step)

Let the engine rotate at **N revolutions per minute**.

**Line 1 — Time for one revolution**
$$
t_{1rev} \;=\; \frac{60}{N} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Line 2 — Work done per revolution (force × distance at radius R)**
The brake applies tangential force F at radius R, so torque T = F × R. Work per revolution = Force × circumference:
$$
W_{1rev} \;=\; F \times 2\pi R \;=\; T \times 2\pi \;\; \text{[Joules]}
$$

**Line 3 — Revolutions per second**
$$
n_{rev/s} \;=\; \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Line 4 — Total work per second (which is power)**
$$
P \;=\; W_{1rev} \times n_{rev/s} \;=\; 2\pi T \times \frac{N}{60}
$$
$$
P \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \;\; \text{[W]}
$$

**Line 5 — Convert to kilowatts (divide by 1000)**
$$
\boxed{BP \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60000} \;\; \text{[kW]}}
$$

This is the universally accepted form used in KTU answer scripts.

## 3.2 Derivation of Indicated Power for a 4-Stroke Engine

**Line 1 — Work per power stroke per cylinder**
$$
W_{stroke} \;=\; P_{mi} \times L \times A \;\; \text{[Joules]}
$$
where $P_{mi}$ is indicated mean effective pressure, L is stroke length, A is cross-sectional area of piston = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$.

**Line 2 — Number of power strokes per second**
In a 4-stroke engine, **one power stroke occurs every 2 revolutions** per cylinder. So for **n** cylinders:
$$
\text{Power strokes/sec} \;=\; \frac{N}{60} \times \frac{n}{2}
$$

**Line 3 — Total Indicated Power**
$$
IP \;=\; W_{stroke} \times \text{power strokes/sec} \;=\; \frac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot N}{60}
$$
$$
\boxed{IP \;=\; \frac{P_{mi} \, L \, A \, n \, N}{120} \;\; \text{[W]}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Specimen KTU Style)

**Given data for a single-cylinder, 4-stroke horizontal diesel test rig:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Bore, D | **0.10 m** |
| Stroke, L | **0.12 m** |
| Net brake load, W | **120 N** (spring balance reading) |
| Spring balance constant, K | **1.2** (drum effective radius factor) |
| Rope drum diameter, d | **0.40 m** |
| Engine speed, N | **1500 rpm** |
| Fuel consumed in 5 min | **60 mL** (ρ_fuel = 0.85 kg/L) |
| CV of fuel | **42000 kJ/kg** |
| Indicated mean effective pressure, P_mi | **7.0 bar = 7 × 10⁵ N/m²** |

**Solution Steps:**

**Step A — Torque on brake drum**
$$
T \;=\; (W - S) \times K \times R
$$
where S = spring-balance slack (taken as 0 here) and R = d/2 = 0.20 m
$$
T \;=\; 120 \times 1.2 \times 0.20 \;=\; 28.8 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step B — Brake Power**
$$
BP \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60000} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 28.8}{60000}
$$
$$
BP \;=\; \frac{271433.6}{60000} \;=\; 4.524 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step C — Fuel consumption rate**
$$
m_f \;=\; \frac{60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.85 \times 3600}{5 \times 60} \;=\; 6.12 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg/hr}
$$
(Equivalently: 0.0612 kg/hr)

**Step D — BSFC**
$$
BSFC \;=\; \frac{m_f}{BP} \;=\; \frac{0.0612}{4.524} \;=\; 0.01353 \;\; \text{kg/kWh} \;=\; 0.292 \;\; \text{kg/kW-hr (typical 0.28-0.40)}
$$

**Step E — Indicated Power**
Piston area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
$$
IP \;=\; \frac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot N}{120} \;=\; \frac{(7\times10^5)(0.12)(7.854\times10^{-3})(1)(1500)}{120}
$$
$$
IP \;=\; \frac{989\,040}{120} \;=\; 8242 \;\; \text{W} \;=\; 8.242 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step F — Mechanical Efficiency**
$$
\eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{BP}{IP} \;=\; \frac{4.524}{8.242} \;=\; 0.549 \;=\; 54.9\%
$$

**Step G — Brake Thermal Efficiency**
$$
\eta_{bth} \;=\; \frac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV} \;=\; \frac{4.524 \times 3600}{0.0612 \times 42000}
$$
$$
\eta_{bth} \;=\; \frac{16286.4}{2570.4} \;=\; 0.6335 \;=\; 6.34\% \quad \text{(low because BP is small — typical of small test rigs)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student should tabulate this calculation across **5–6 different speeds** (e.g., 1000, 1250, 1500, 1750, 2000, 2250 rpm) by repeating Steps A through G at each speed.

## 3.4 Typical Observation Table Template

| Sl. No. | Speed N (rpm) | Spring balance reading W (N) | Torque T (N·m) | BP (kW) | Fuel m_f (kg/hr) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | η_bth (%) | η_mech (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 80 | 19.2 | 2.01 | 0.052 | 0.389 | 5.0 | 50.2 |
| 2 | 1250 | 100 | 24.0 | 3.14 | 0.057 | 0.273 | 5.8 | 52.7 |
| 3 | 1500 | 120 | 28.8 | 4.52 | 0.061 | 0.203 | 6.3 | 54.9 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

## 3.5 Curves to Be Plotted

| Curve | X-axis | Y-axis | Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N (rpm) | BP, IP, FP (kW) | All rising lines (FP more linear) |
| 2 | N (rpm) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | **U-curve** (key result) |
| 3 | N (rpm) | η_bth, η_mech (%) | Inverted-U / rising |
| 4 | BP (kW) | m_f (kg/hr) | **Willans line** (near-straight) |
| 5 | N (rpm) | m_f (kg/hr) | Slightly curved, rising |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test Rig

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the **functional architecture** of the speed-variation test rig (horizontal 4-stroke diesel coupled to a rope-brake / hydraulic dynamometer). Mermaid is used here as a fallback in lieu of a CAD physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[FUEL MEASURING BURETTE]
    B --> C[ENGINE INLET MANIFOLD]
    D[AIR INTAKE WITH AIR BOX] --> C
    C --> E[HORIZONTAL 4 STROKE DIESEL ENGINE]
    E --> F[FLYWHEEL AND SHAFT COUPLING]
    F --> G[DYNAMOMETER HYDRAULIC OR ROPE BRAKE]
    G --> H[LOADING VALVE OR ROPE TENSION]
    I[GOVERNOR THROTTLE LINKAGE] --> E
    J[TACHOMETER ON SHAFT] --> F
    K[STOPWATCH FOR FUEL FLOW] --> B
    L[INDICATOR FOR Pmi] --> E
    M[DATA LOGGING TABLE] --> N[CALCULATION OF BP IP FP BSFC]
    E --> M
    G --> M
    B --> M
    J --> M
    L --> M
    N --> O[PERFORMANCE GRAPHS BP VS N AND BSFC VS N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each block is a discrete functional module. The arrows show the **flow of energy, fuel, air, or measured data** between modules.

## 4.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START] --> A2[CHECK LUB OIL AND COOLING WATER LEVELS]
    A2 --> A3[PRIME FUEL LINE AND BLEED AIR]
    A3 --> A4[START ENGINE USING CRANK OR STARTER MOTOR]
    A4 --> A5[WARM UP FOR 5 TO 10 MINUTES AT NO LOAD]
    A5 --> A6[SET DESIRED LOAD ON DYNAMOMETER]
    A6 --> A7[READ TACHOMETER AND RECORD N IN RPM]
    A7 --> A8[NOTE SPRING BALANCE READINGS F1 F2 IN N]
    A8 --> A9[START STOPWATCH AND COLLECT FUEL IN BURETTE]
    A9 --> A10[STOP AT FIXED VOLUME 10 OR 20 ML]
    A10 --> A11[RECORD TIME IN SECONDS]
    A11 --> A12[RECORD INDICATOR Pmi IF AVAILABLE]
    A12 --> A13[CHANGE SPEED BY ADJUSTING GOVERNOR]
    A13 --> A14[REPEAT STEPS A6 TO A12 FOR 5 TO 6 SPEEDS]
    A14 --> A15[SHUT DOWN ENGINE CLEAN RIG]
    A15 --> A16[TABULATE AND CALCULATE BP IP FP BSFC]
    A16 --> A17[PLOT GRAPHS BP VS N AND BSFC VS N]
    A17 --> A18[INTERPRET RESULTS AND END]
```

## 4.3 Instrumentation Summary Table

| Instrument | Function | Mounting Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Tachometer** (digital/optical) | Measures N in rpm | Crankshaft end |
| **Rope-brake dynamometer** | Measures T | Engine flywheel rim |
| **Spring balance (×2)** | Measures rope tension F₁, F₂ | Both ends of rope |
| **Fuel measuring burette** | Volumetric fuel consumption | Fuel supply line |
| **Stopwatch** | Time for fixed volume | Manual |
| **Bourdon tube pressure gauge** | Indicates brake force if hydraulic dynamometer used | Loading line |
| **Engine indicator (optional)** | Records P-mi diagram | Indicator cock on cylinder head |
| **Thermocouples / thermometers** | Measure cooling water, exhaust temp | Respective circuits |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]**
**Define Speed Variation Test. What is its purpose in a marine engine lab?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
* The Speed Variation Test is a performance test in which an IC engine is run at a fixed load and its **speed is varied systematically** over the permissible range (e.g., 1000 to 2500 rpm in steps of 250 rpm). At each speed, the brake torque, fuel consumption, and (optionally) indicated mean effective pressure are recorded.
* **Purpose (3 sub-points for full marks):**
  1. To determine the **most economical operating speed** (RPM at minimum BSFC) of the engine.
  2. To study the variation of **BP, IP, FP, and η_mech with speed**.
  3. To plot the **Willans line** and estimate the friction power at any operating point — vital for marine propulsion matching.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model]**
**Why does the BSFC vs RPM curve show a U-shape? Explain in three points.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
1. **At low RPM:** Longer time per cycle causes **proportionally higher heat loss** to cylinder walls, and the air-fuel mixing time exceeds the optimum, leading to incomplete combustion → BSFC high.
2. **At optimum RPM:** Heat loss is balanced, combustion is complete, and friction is moderate → BSFC reaches its **minimum**.
3. **At high RPM:** Friction power rises sharply (∝ N), and time for combustion becomes insufficient for complete mixing → BSFC rises again, completing the **U-shape**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **experimental setup** used to conduct the Speed Variation Test on a **single-cylinder, four-stroke horizontal diesel engine** coupled to a rope-brake dynamometer. List all instruments and their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During the test, the following observations were recorded for the horizontal diesel engine (bore 100 mm, stroke 120 mm, single cylinder, rope-drum effective diameter 400 mm, spring balance constant 1.2, fuel density 0.85 kg/L, CV = 42000 kJ/kg):

| Speed N (rpm) | Spring balance W (N) | Fuel in 5 min (mL) |
|---|---|---|
| 1000 | 80 | 50 |
| 1500 | 120 | 60 |
| 2000 | 150 | 75 |

Calculate **(i) BP, (ii) m_f (kg/hr), (iii) BSFC, (iv) η_bth, (v) η_mech** for each speed, given that the indicated mean effective pressure P_mi is constant at **7 bar** in all cases. Tabulate the results and state at which speed the engine is most economical. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:**
D = 0.10 m, L = 0.12 m, P_mi = 7 × 10⁵ N/m², CV = 42000 kJ/kg, ρ = 0.85 kg/L, d = 0.40 m, K = 1.2, n = 1 (single cylinder, 4-stroke ⇒ k = 1/2).

**Piston area:**
$$
A \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Indicated Power (same at all speeds — but IP is proportional to N, so we recalculate per row):**
$$
IP_{N} \;=\; \frac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N}{120} \;\; \text{[W]}
$$

**Torque and BP (same procedure as Section 3.3):**
$$
T \;=\; W \times K \times \frac{d}{2} \;=\; W \times 1.2 \times 0.20 \;=\; 0.24 \, W
$$
$$
BP \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60000} \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Calculated Table:**

| N (rpm) | T (N·m) | BP (kW) | m_f (kg/hr) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | IP (kW) | η_mech (%) | η_bth (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1000 | 19.20 | 2.011 | 0.510 | 0.254 | 5.495 | 36.6 | 3.39 |
| 1500 | 28.80 | 4.524 | 0.612 | 0.135 | 8.242 | 54.9 | 6.34 |
| 2000 | 36.00 | 7.540 | 0.765 | 0.101 | 10.990 | 68.6 | 8.46 |

**Sample step for N = 1500 rpm, W = 120 N:**
* T = 0.24 × 120 = 28.8 N·m
* BP = (2π × 1500 × 28.8)/60000 = **4.524 kW**
* Volume = 60 mL ⇒ mass = 60 × 0.85/1000 = 0.051 kg in 5 min ⇒ m_f = 0.051 × 12 = 0.612 kg/hr
* BSFC = 0.612/4.524 = **0.135 kg/kWh**
* IP = (7 × 10⁵ × 0.12 × 7.854 × 10⁻³ × 1500)/120 = 8242 W = 8.242 kW
* η_mech = 4.524/8.242 = **54.9 %**
* η_bth = (4.524 × 3600)/(0.612 × 42000) = **6.34 %**

**Most Economical Speed:** From the BSFC column, the **minimum BSFC = 0.101 kg/kWh at 2000 rpm**. Hence, the engine is most fuel-efficient at **2000 rpm** under the given load condition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):**
> * Forgetting the **1/2 factor** for 4-stroke in IP formula → lose 1 mark.
> * Using **mL directly as kg** without multiplying by density (0.85 kg/L) → lose 1 mark.
> * Mixing up **BSFC with η_bth** (they both use m_f, but BSFC uses BP and η_bth uses CV) → lose 2 marks.
> * Not stating the unit of m_f conversion correctly (kg/hr, not kg/s) → lose 0.5 mark.
> * Omitting the **most-economical speed conclusion** → lose 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternate]**

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expressions for **(i) Brake Power, (ii) Indicated Power of a four-stroke IC engine, and (iii) Mechanical Efficiency**. State the units clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **Willans Line** method for determining the friction power of a diesel engine. How is it constructed? Why is it nearly straight? What is its limitation? Sketch a typical Willans line diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B:

**(a) Derivations** — refer to Sections 3.1 and 3.2 of this document for the full step-by-step derivations. The final boxed formulas are:
$$
BP \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60000} \;\; \text{kW}
$$
$$
IP \;=\; \frac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot N}{120} \;\; \text{W}
$$
$$
\eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{BP}{IP} \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T / 60000}{P_{mi} L A n N / 120}
$$

**Simplification of η_mech:**
$$
\eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{2\pi T \times 120}{60000 \times P_{mi} L A n} \;=\; \frac{\pi T}{250 \, P_{mi} \, L \, A \, n}
$$

**(b) Willans Line Method:**

**Concept:** Plot the **fuel consumption per hour (m_f in kg/hr)** on the Y-axis and the **brake power (BP in kW)** on the X-axis for several loads at the **same engine speed**. The points fall approximately on a **straight line**.

**Construction (4 points for full marks):**
1. Run the engine at a **fixed speed** (e.g., 1500 rpm).
2. Vary the load in 5–6 steps from no-load to full-load.
3. Record BP and m_f at each load.
4. Plot m_f (Y) vs BP (X) — the line obtained is the **Willans line** for that speed.

**Interpretation:**
* The **slope** = (m_f₂ − m_f₁)/(BP₂ − BP₁) = additional fuel per additional kW of BP.
* The **Y-intercept** (where BP = 0, i.e., line extrapolated to vertical axis) = fuel required to overcome **friction alone** at that speed.
* Convert this intercept fuel rate to friction power using η_bth or directly from the Willans line as the BP-equivalent friction work.

**Why nearly straight?** Because the **indicated thermal efficiency** is approximately constant over a wide range of loads (diesel engines have a flatter η_ith curve than petrol engines), so fuel rate is almost linear with indicated work, and friction (a small constant-ish term) causes only a small non-linearity.

**Limitations:**
* Ignition delay, mixing time, and combustion efficiency **change with load**, especially at very low or very high loads, causing deviation from linearity.
* The method gives a single value of FP at the test speed; to find FP at another speed, a separate Willans line must be drawn.

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
   m_f (kg/hr)
       |
   3.5 |- - - - - *  
       |         /  
   3.0 |- - *   / 
       |   / \ /  
   2.5 |  /   *  ← Willans line (straight)
       | /   /
   2.0 |/   /
       |   /
   1.5 *  /  ← y-intercept = friction-fuel rate
       | /
       |/_______________ BP (kW)
            1   2   3   4
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):**
> * Skipping the **1/2 factor** in the IP derivation for 4-stroke → lose 1 mark.
> * Not converting **Watts → kilowatts** correctly in the final answer → lose 0.5 mark.
> * For Willans line, forgetting to mention that the **Y-intercept corresponds to friction-fuel consumption** → lose 2 marks.
> * Missing the **limitation of Willans line** → lose 1 mark.
> * Not drawing a sketch (even rough) of the Willans line → lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU 2024 Test Points — Pin This in Your Notebook:**

* **Speed Variation Test** = constant load, variable speed (N varied, BP/FP/BSFC observed).
* **Horizontal engine** = cylinder axis parallel to ground; common in marine auxiliaries.
* **Four-stroke** = 1 power stroke per **2 revolutions** ⇒ factor **1/2** in IP formula.
* **Brake Power** = $\dfrac{2\pi N T}{60000}$ **kW** (T in N·m, N in rpm).
* **Indicated Power** = $\dfrac{P_{mi} \, L \, A \, n \, N}{120}$ **W** (4-stroke).
* **Friction Power** = IP − BP; alternatively from **Willans line** (intercept method) or **Morse test** (for multi-cylinder).
* **Mechanical Efficiency** = BP / IP, typically **0.70–0.90** for a healthy diesel.
* **BSFC** = m_f / BP, units **kg/kWh** (diesel typical 0.25–0.40).
* **Brake Thermal Efficiency** = (BP × 3600) / (m_f × CV), typical 25–40 % at full load; small test rigs may show much lower values.
* **BSFC vs RPM curve is U-shaped** — minimum gives the most economical speed.
* **Willans line** = m_f (Y) vs BP (X), nearly straight ⇒ Y-intercept = fuel needed to overcome friction.
* **Rope-brake dynamometer** uses two spring balances (F₁, F₂); net force = F₁ − F₂ (if both pull) or just W if one is fixed; **torque T = W × K × R** where K is spring balance constant and R is effective drum radius.
* **Fuel measurement** = burette method: record time for fixed volume (e.g., 10 or 20 mL), convert to kg/hr using fuel density.
* **Cooling water, lube oil pressure, and exhaust temperature** should be monitored throughout the test for safe operation.
* **Graphs to plot (in order of importance for KTU):**
  1. **BP, IP, FP vs N** (rising curves).
  2. **BSFC vs N** (U-shaped — central result).
  3. **η_bth, η_mech vs N** (rising / inverted-U).
  4. **m_f vs BP** (Willans line).

> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always conclude your KTU answer with a clear statement of the **most-economical speed** and the **trend of mechanical efficiency with speed**. This is where most students lose easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retardation Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retardation Test on Four Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Retardation Test** (also known as the **Running-Down Test** or **Motoring Test**) is an indirect experimental method used to determine the **Frictional Power (F_p)** of an internal combustion engine without dismantling it. For a **Four-Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine**, the test is conducted by motoring the engine to a stable speed using an external electric motor, cutting off both fuel injection and the motor supply simultaneously, and recording the time taken by the rotating assembly (flywheel, crankshaft, piston, connecting rod) to coast down between two pre-determined speeds.

The energy dissipated during this natural deceleration equals the work done against the engine's internal friction, allowing the frictional power to be back-calculated from the **Moment of Inertia (I)** of the rotating parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is classified under **Module Experiment 2** of the Marine Engineering Lab and is mandatory for the **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)**. The examiner expects you to record **observations in tabular form**, **plot two graphs** (N vs t and N² vs t), and present the **sample calculation** in the standard KTU record format.

> [!NOTE]
> **Test Synonyms You Must Know for Board Exams:**
> - Retardation Test
> - Running-Down Test
> - Motoring Test
> - Deceleration Test
> - Frictional Power Test by Coasting

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a spinning **ceiling fan** that has been switched off. The blades keep rotating for some time before stopping. The longer they spin, the lower the friction in the bearings. Similarly, when the diesel engine's fuel and the electric motor's power are cut off:

- The huge **flywheel** (acting as the "kinetic energy reservoir") keeps rotating.
- The **friction** in piston rings, bearings, valves, and pump assemblies slowly drains this kinetic energy.
- The engine **"retards"** (slows down) gradually.
- The **slower** this retardation, the **lower** the frictional power.

If you know the flywheel's rotational inertia and measure the time to slow from a high speed to a lower speed, you can precisely compute the friction losses — analogous to measuring how long it takes a heavy flywheel on a bicycle to stop after you stop pedalling.

### 1.3 Apparatus and Setup Keywords

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| 4-Stroke Single Cylinder Vertical Diesel Engine | Test specimen (e.g., Kirloskar TV1 type) |
| Electric Motor (DC Shunt / 3-Phase Induction) | Acts as starter and motoring device |
| Flywheel | Stores rotational kinetic energy (large **I**) |
| Rope-Brake / Hydraulic Dynamometer | Applies load to measure brake power |
| Tachometer (Digital) | Reads instantaneous engine speed N (rpm) |
| Stop Watch (Least Count 0.1 s) | Records retardation time |
| Fuel Tank & Injection Pump | Supplies diesel (cut off during test) |
| Cooling Water Circulator | Maintains jacket temperature |

> [!TIP]
> **Why an Electric Motor is Co-Axially Coupled:** In a marine lab setup, the electric motor performs **three roles** — (i) **cranking** the engine for starting, (ii) **motoring** the engine at the test speed with fuel cut off, and (iii) **absorbing load** when acting in generator mode during loaded trials.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Energy Storage in Rotating Mass:**
The flywheel and rotating parts store kinetic energy given by the standard rigid-body rotation equation.

**Step 2 — Cut-Off Event:**
At speed **N₁ (rpm)**, both fuel injection and the electric motor's electrical supply are simultaneously disconnected. From this instant, **no external torque** acts on the shaft (friction is the only resisting torque).

**Step 3 — Coasting Phase:**
The shaft decelerates due to frictional torque **T_f (N·m)** acting at angular velocity **ω (rad/s)**. The instantaneous frictional power equals **T_f × ω**.

**Step 4 — Energy Equivalence:**
The loss in rotational kinetic energy between two speeds N₁ and N₂ equals the work done against friction over the retardation time **t (s)**.

**Step 5 — Average Frictional Power:**
Dividing the energy lost by the coasting time yields the **average** frictional power over the speed interval [N₂, N₁].

### 2.2 Assumptions Made in the Retardation Test

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Most students lose marks for not stating these assumptions explicitly. Always list them.

1. Friction torque is assumed **uniform** (or linear) over the small speed range considered.
2. The moment of inertia **I** of the rotating assembly is **constant** and known accurately.
3. **Windage losses** (air drag on flywheel) are neglected or lumped into friction.
4. No external load acts on the engine during coasting (dynamometer fully released).
5. The flywheel is the dominant inertia; contribution of crank, connecting rod, piston is negligible (or lumped).
6. Fuel pump remains mechanically driven, so its drive friction is included in **F_p**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in mathematical expressions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Mean angular velocity | $\omega_{avg} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \dfrac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Angular retardation (avg) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60}\cdot\dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{t}$ | rad/s² |
| 4 | Rotational kinetic energy stored | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2}$ | J (N·m) |
| 5 | Energy lost during retardation | $\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2}\,I\left(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2}\right)$ | J |
| 6 | **Frictional Power (Energy Method)** | $\mathbf{F_p = \dfrac{I\left(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2}\right)}{2\,t}}$ | **W** |
| 7 | Frictional Power (Power Form) | $F_p = T_f \cdot \omega_{avg} = I \cdot \omega_{avg} \cdot \alpha$ | W |
| 8 | Frictional Torque | $T_f = \dfrac{F_p}{\omega_{avg}} = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t\,\omega_{avg}}$ | N·m |
| 9 | Friction Mean Effective Pressure | $F_{mep} = \dfrac{60\,F_p \cdot n}{L\,A\,N_k}$ | bar or N/m² |
| 10 | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{p_{me}\,L\,A\,N}{2 \cdot 60}$ | W |
| 11 | Brake Power (Rope Brake) | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N(W-S)}{60 \cdot 1000}$ | kW |
| 12 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{BP + F_p} = \dfrac{IP - F_p}{IP} \times 100$ | % |
| 13 | Moment of Inertia (solid disc flywheel) | $I = \dfrac{M\,R^{2}}{2}$ | kg·m² |
| 14 | Moment of Inertia (calibration test) | $I = \dfrac{T_{known}\,t}{\omega_{avg}}$ | kg·m² |

Where the symbols denote:
- **I** = moment of inertia of rotating parts (kg·m²)
- **N₁, N₂** = initial and final speeds during retardation (rpm)
- **t** = time taken to coast from N₁ to N₂ (s)
- **T_f** = mean frictional torque (N·m)
- **L** = stroke length (m), **A** = cross-sectional area of piston (m²)
- **W, S** = dead weight and spring balance readings (N)
- **n** = number of cylinders (= 1 here)
- **k** = 1 for 4-stroke, 2 for 2-stroke

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The retardation test is **indispensable** in **marine engineering** because:

1. **Onboard Diagnosis:** Marine main engines (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) are tested at the dockyard before commissioning using coast-down tests to benchmark baseline friction.
2. **Engine Health Monitoring:** An **increase in frictional power** during service indicates worn piston rings, bearing degradation, or lube-oil contamination — early warning before catastrophic failure.
3. **Comparison with Willan's Line Method:** Retardation test is the most direct method for finding F_p at **no-load**. Willan's line extrapolation is used at part loads.
4. **Validation of Morse Test:** For multi-cylinder engines, the Morse test gives relative friction; retardation test gives absolute values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Significance:** Modern marine diesel engines use the retardation principle indirectly through **"drag-test"** protocols where the turbocharger is used to load the engine during coast-down, giving highly accurate friction maps.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Frictional Power Formula

**Given:**
- Rotating assembly has moment of inertia **I** (kg·m²).
- Engine is coasting (no fuel, no motor torque).
- Initial angular velocity: **ω₁** at time **t = 0**.
- Final angular velocity: **ω₂** at time **t = t**.

**Step 1 — Write Newton's rotational equation:**

The only resisting torque is friction **T_f**, opposing motion. By Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_f$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration (ω decreases with t).

**Step 2 — Integrate the equation over the retardation interval:**

$$I \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \omega \, d\omega = -T_f \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

Applying limits:

$$I \left[\frac{\omega^{2}}{2}\right]_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} = -T_f \cdot t$$

$$\frac{I}{2}\left(\omega_2^{2} - \omega_1^{2}\right) = -T_f \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a positive energy-loss expression:**

Multiplying both sides by **−1**:

$$\frac{I}{2}\left(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2}\right) = T_f \cdot t$$

The left-hand side represents the **kinetic energy lost by the flywheel** during retardation. The right-hand side is the **work done against friction**.

**Step 4 — Express frictional power:**

Frictional power is work done per unit time:

$$F_p = \frac{T_f \cdot t}{t} = \frac{I\left(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2}\right)}{2\,t} \quad \text{(in Watts)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to rpm form (KTU exam-friendly):**

Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60}, \qquad \omega_2 = \frac{2\pi N_2}{60}$$

Squaring and subtracting:

$$\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2} = \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^{2}\left(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2}\right) = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{3600}\left(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2}\right)$$

Substituting back:

$$F_p = \frac{I}{2\,t} \cdot \frac{4\pi^{2}}{3600}\left(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{F_p = \frac{\pi^{2}\,I\left(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2}\right)}{1800\,t} \quad \text{Watts}}$$

**Step 6 — Express in kW (convenient for KTU numericals):**

$$\boxed{F_p = \frac{\pi^{2}\,I\left(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2}\right)}{1.8 \times 10^{6}\,t} \quad \text{kW}}$$

### 3.2 Equivalent Derivation via Power Method

**Starting Point:**
Instantaneous frictional power = friction torque × angular velocity.

$$F_p = T_f \cdot \omega$$

If friction torque is constant over the interval, then **F_p varies with ω** because ω is changing. Taking the **average** over the retardation period:

$$F_{p,avg} = T_f \cdot \omega_{avg} = T_f \cdot \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$$

From Newton's law integrated form: $T_f = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t\,\omega_{avg}}$

Substituting:

$$F_{p,avg} = \frac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t\,\omega_{avg}} \cdot \omega_{avg} = \frac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t}$$

**Both derivations are identical** — confirming the formula is robust.

### 3.3 Detailed Sample Calculation (Standard KTU Record Format)

**Given Observation Table (Recorded during experiment):**

| Speed Range (rpm) | Mean Speed N̄ (rpm) | Retardation Time t (s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1500 → 1400 | 1450 | 30 |
| 1400 → 1300 | 1350 | 34 |
| 1300 → 1200 | 1250 | 39 |
| 1200 → 1100 | 1150 | 45 |

**Engine Data:**
- Moment of inertia of flywheel + rotating parts: **I = 12.6 kg·m²**
- Stroke length L = 0.110 m
- Bore D = 0.0875 m
- Number of cylinders n = 1
- Brake power at test speed: BP = 4.2 kW (from rope brake)

**Sample Calculation for Speed Range 1500 → 1400 rpm:**

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to angular velocities:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1400}{60} = 146.61 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the frictional power formula:**

$$F_p = \frac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t} = \frac{12.6 \times (157.08^{2} - 146.61^{2})}{2 \times 30}$$

Computing the squares:

$$157.08^{2} = 24{,}674.13$$

$$146.61^{2} = 21{,}494.61$$

Subtracting:

$$24{,}674.13 - 21{,}494.61 = 3179.52$$

Substituting:

$$F_p = \frac{12.6 \times 3179.52}{60} = \frac{40{,}061.95}{60} = 667.70 \;\text{W} \approx 0.668\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate frictional torque:**

$$T_f = \frac{F_p}{\omega_{avg}} = \frac{667.70}{\frac{157.08 + 146.61}{2}} = \frac{667.70}{151.845} = 4.397\;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate piston area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0875)^{2} = 6.013 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Friction Mean Effective Pressure (F_mep):**

$$F_{mep} = \frac{60\,F_p \cdot n}{L\,A\,N_k} = \frac{60 \times 667.70 \times 1}{0.110 \times 6.013 \times 10^{-3} \times 1450}$$

Computing denominator:

$$0.110 \times 6.013 \times 10^{-3} \times 1450 = 0.9591$$

$$F_{mep} = \frac{40{,}062}{0.9591} = 41{,}774\;\text{N/m}^{2} \approx 0.418\;\text{bar}$$

**Step 6 — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{BP + F_p} \times 100 = \frac{4.2}{4.2 + 0.668} \times 100 = 86.28\%$$

**Step 7 — Indicated Power:**

$$IP = BP + F_p = 4.2 + 0.668 = 4.868\;\text{kW}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Speed Interval | Mean N̄ (rpm) | t (s) | F_p (W) | T_f (N·m) | F_mep (bar) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1500 → 1400 | 1450 | 30 | 667.70 | 4.397 | 0.418 |
| 1400 → 1300 | 1350 | 34 | 593.40 | 4.198 | 0.399 |
| 1300 → 1200 | 1250 | 39 | 519.30 | 3.965 | 0.378 |
| 1200 → 1100 | 1150 | 45 | 444.80 | 3.692 | 0.352 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trend to Comment in the Record:** As engine speed reduces, both **F_p** and **T_f** decrease approximately linearly, confirming friction torque is mildly speed-dependent. This validates the assumption of nearly uniform friction over small speed intervals.

### 3.5 Derivation of the Two Graph Equations

**Graph 1 — N vs t (Coasting Curve):**
The general equation of motion during uniform retardation is:

$$N = N_1 - \left(\frac{N_1 - N_2}{t}\right) \cdot \tau$$

where $\tau$ is instantaneous time. The slope $\dfrac{-dN}{dt}$ gives **retardation in rpm/s**.

**Graph 2 — N² vs t (Energy Linearization):**
Starting from $F_p = \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,I\,(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2})}{1800\,t}$, if F_p is assumed constant:

$$N_1^{2} - N^{2} = \left(\frac{1800\,F_p}{\pi^{2}\,I}\right)\tau$$

A plot of **N² vs t** is a **straight line** with negative slope $\dfrac{1800\,F_p}{\pi^{2}\,I}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why N² vs t is preferred for KTU Lab Records:** The linear fit allows direct determination of slope using least-squares, giving a more accurate average F_p than the point-wise calculation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experimental Test Bench

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the major sub-systems of the retardation-test test bed. It avoids drawing the physical engine itself and instead shows the **signal flow** of mechanical and electrical power.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supplyMains[AC Mains Supply] --> motorController[Motor Starter and Controller]
    motorController --> electricMotor[3 Phase Induction Motor 5 HP]
    electricMotor --> couplingBlock[Flexible Coupling Flange Type]
    couplingBlock --> flywheelNode[Flywheel and Rotating Assembly I]
    flywheelNode --> crankshaftNode[Crankshaft and Piston Unit]
    crankshaftNode --> injectionPump[Diesel Injection Pump]
    injectionPump --> fuelTank[Diesel Tank]
    flywheelNode --> dynamoNode[Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    dynamoNode --> loadScale[Dead Weight W and Spring Balance S]
    flywheelNode --> tachSensor[Tachometer Pickup]
    tachSensor --> digitalDisplay[Digital RPM Display]
    flywheelNode --> stopwatchLink[Stop Watch for t]
    electricMotor --> mainsSwitch[Mains Switch Cut Off Point]
```

### 4.2 Procedural Flowchart of the Retardation Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Start Experiment] --> warmup[Run engine at no load for 15 minutes to reach steady state]
    warmup --> recordN1[Set engine speed to N1 slightly above rated speed]
    recordN1 --> cutFuel[Cut off fuel injection completely]
    cutFuel --> motorMode[Switch motor to motoring mode and stabilize at N1]
    motorMode --> cutMotor[Cut off motor supply simultaneously with stopwatch start]
    cutMotor --> watchDrop[Observe tachometer until speed drops to N2]
    watchDrop --> stopTimer[Stop stopwatch and record time t]
    stopTimer --> repeatRange[Repeat for next lower range N2 to N3]
    repeatRange --> calcFp[Calculate Fp using formula]
    calcFp --> plotGraphs[Plot N vs t and N squared vs t]
    plotGraphs --> endNode[Report Fp, Tf, eta mech and Fmep]
```

### 4.3 Data Reduction and Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obsInput[Observed Data N1, N2, t, BP] --> omegaConv[Convert N to omega rad per s]
    omegaConv --> keDiff[Compute 0.5 into I into omega1 squared minus omega2 squared]
    keDiff --> fpCalc[Compute Fp equals KE difference divided by 2t]
    fpCalc --> tfCalc[Compute Tf equals Fp divided by omega avg]
    tfCalc --> fmepCalc[Compute Fmep using L A N k]
    fmepCalc --> etaCalc[Compute eta mech equals BP by BP plus Fp]
    etaCalc --> tabulate[Tabulate all results for each speed range]
    tabulate --> finalReport[Final Lab Record Submission]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Energy-Flow During Retardation

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the **conceptual energy transformation chain** from the moment fuel and motor are cut off until the flywheel comes to rest.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    keState1[KE at N1 equals 0.5 into I into omega1 squared] --> coastingEvent[Coasting Event t seconds]
    coastingEvent --> keState2[KE at N2 equals 0.5 into I into omega2 squared]
    coastingEvent --> frictionSink[Energy Dissipated as Friction]
    keState1 --> frictionSink
    keState2 --> frictionSink
    frictionSink --> thermalLoss[Converted to Heat in bearings, rings, valves]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question A1]** [3 Marks]
> **Q:** Define the retardation test. Why is it called an indirect method for determining frictional power?

**Model Answer:**
The retardation test is an experimental method for determining the **frictional power** of an engine by measuring the rate of decay of rotational kinetic energy of the flywheel during a free coasting run with fuel and external power cut off.
It is called an **indirect method** because the friction is not measured directly; instead, the **kinetic energy lost** by the rotating mass (which equals the work done against friction) is measured and back-calculated using the known moment of inertia. Friction is inferred from a derived quantity rather than sensed directly via a friction dynamometer.

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question A2]** [3 Marks]
> **Q:** Why is an electric motor coupled to the engine during the retardation test? List three reasons.

**Model Answer:**
The co-axially coupled electric motor serves the following three critical functions:
1. **Cranking the engine** during initial starting when fuel is first injected.
2. **Motoring the engine** at the desired test speed with fuel supply cut off, achieving a steady no-load motoring condition.
3. **Sustaining the test speed** momentarily after fuel cut-off, so that the operator can release the motor switch and the flywheel starts coasting from a precise, known speed.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question A, Part (a)]** [7 Marks]
**Q:** Describe with a neat block diagram the **experimental procedure** to determine the frictional power of a 4-stroke single cylinder vertical diesel engine using the retardation test. Mention the role of the flywheel moment of inertia.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Apparatus Required:**
4-stroke single cylinder vertical diesel engine (test specimen), co-axial 3-phase electric motor with starter, flywheel of known moment of inertia I, rope-brake dynamometer, digital tachometer, stop-watch (LC = 0.1 s), fuel tank and cooling water system.

**(ii) Procedure:**
1. Start the electric motor and run the engine at **no load** for 10–15 minutes to attain thermal steady state.
2. Note the rated speed and set the engine to a speed **slightly above** the rated speed using the motor as a driver.
3. With the engine now being motored, **cut off the fuel injection** completely.
4. Wait until the engine speed stabilizes at speed **N₁** (typically 50 rpm above rated speed).
5. At a precisely observed instant, **simultaneously cut off the motor supply** and start the stop-watch.
6. Watch the tachometer and note the time taken for speed to drop from **N₁ to N₂** (e.g., 1500 rpm to 1400 rpm).
7. Repeat steps 4–6 for subsequent ranges (1400 → 1300, 1300 → 1200, etc.) to obtain multiple data points.
8. Record the corresponding brake power BP at each load point if a dynamometer is engaged.

**(iii) Role of Flywheel Moment of Inertia:**
The flywheel provides a **large and accurately known moment of inertia I**, which dominates the kinetic energy reservoir of the rotating assembly. The energy stored is $\frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega^{2}$. Without a high I, the retardation time would be too short to measure accurately. The flywheel's known I is the **calibrating constant** that converts measured deceleration time to frictional power.

**(iv) Calculations:**
Apply $F_p = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t}$ for each speed range, then compute $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{BP+F_p} \times 100$.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question A, Part (b)]** [7 Marks]
**Q:** In a retardation test on a 4-stroke single cylinder vertical diesel engine, the flywheel moment of inertia is **I = 15 kg·m²**. The engine retards from **1500 rpm to 1400 rpm in 22 seconds**. If the brake power measured just before coasting was **5 kW**, calculate:
(i) Frictional power F_p in kW
(ii) Frictional torque T_f in N·m
(iii) Mechanical efficiency η_mech

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to angular velocities:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1400}{60} = 146.61 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate frictional power:**

$$F_p = \frac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t} = \frac{15 \times (157.08^{2} - 146.61^{2})}{2 \times 22}$$

Computing the squares:

$$157.08^{2} = 24{,}674.13, \qquad 146.61^{2} = 21{,}494.61$$

Difference = 3179.52

$$F_p = \frac{15 \times 3179.52}{44} = \frac{47{,}692.8}{44} = 1083.93 \;\text{W} \approx \mathbf{1.084\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mean angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{157.08 + 146.61}{2} = 151.845 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate frictional torque:**

$$T_f = \frac{F_p}{\omega_{avg}} = \frac{1083.93}{151.845} = \mathbf{7.139\;\text{N·m}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate mechanical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{BP + F_p} \times 100 = \frac{5.0}{5.0 + 1.084} \times 100 = \mathbf{82.18\%}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Frictional Power F_p | 1.084 kW |
| Frictional Torque T_f | 7.139 N·m |
| Mechanical Efficiency η_mech | 82.18% |

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question B, Part (a)]** [7 Marks]
**Q:** Derive the expression for **frictional power** in terms of the moment of inertia of the flywheel and the retardation time. Clearly state the assumptions used in deriving this formula.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider the rotating system (flywheel + crank + piston) with moment of inertia I, rotating initially at ω₁ and coasting down to ω₂ in time t. With no fuel and no external torque, only friction torque T_f acts.

**Newton's second law for rotation:**

$$I\,\frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_f$$

Multiplying both sides by ω dt:

$$I\,\omega\,d\omega = -T_f\,\omega\,dt$$

But $T_f\,\omega = F_p$ (instantaneous frictional power). Integrating:

$$I\int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \omega\,d\omega = -\int_0^t F_p\,dt$$

If F_p is assumed constant over the interval:

$$\frac{I}{2}\left(\omega_2^{2} - \omega_1^{2}\right) = -F_p\,t$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{F_p = \frac{I\left(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2}\right)}{2\,t} \;\text{Watts}}$$

In rpm form:

$$F_p = \frac{\pi^{2}\,I\left(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2}\right)}{1800\,t}\;\text{Watts}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The moment of inertia I of the rotating assembly is constant and accurately known.
2. The frictional torque T_f is approximately constant (or varies linearly) over the small speed interval [N₂, N₁].
3. No external load is applied during coasting; dynamometer is fully released.
4. Windage and air-drag losses on the flywheel are negligible or included in F_p.
5. The engine is in thermal steady state so that frictional losses are stable.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question B, Part (b)]** [7 Marks]
**Q:** In a retardation test, the moment of inertia of the rotating parts is **8 kg·m²**. The engine coasts from **1480 rpm to 1380 rpm in 18 seconds**. Calculate:
(i) Frictional power in kW
(ii) Frictional torque in N·m
(iii) Indicated Power if BP = 3.2 kW
(iv) Friction mean effective pressure if bore = 80 mm, stroke = 110 mm

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Angular velocities:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1480}{60} = 154.985 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1380}{60} = 144.513 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Frictional Power:**

$$F_p = \frac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t} = \frac{8 \times (154.985^{2} - 144.513^{2})}{2 \times 18}$$

Computing squares:

$$154.985^{2} = 24{,}020.36, \qquad 144.513^{2} = 20{,}884.02$$

Difference = 3136.34

$$F_p = \frac{8 \times 3136.34}{36} = \frac{25{,}090.7}{36} = 696.96 \;\text{W} \approx \mathbf{0.697\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Frictional Torque:**

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{154.985 + 144.513}{2} = 149.749 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$T_f = \frac{F_p}{\omega_{avg}} = \frac{696.96}{149.749} = \mathbf{4.654\;\text{N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Indicated Power:**

$$IP = BP + F_p = 3.2 + 0.697 = \mathbf{3.897\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 5 — Piston area and stroke:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^{2} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2}$$

For 4-stroke engine, k = 1, n = 1 cylinder.

**Step 6 — Friction Mean Effective Pressure:**

$$F_{mep} = \frac{60 \times F_p \times n}{L \times A \times N_k} = \frac{60 \times 696.96 \times 1}{0.110 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 1430}$$

Denominator:

$$0.110 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 1430 = 0.7908$$

$$F_{mep} = \frac{41{,}817.6}{0.7908} = 52{,}879\;\text{N/m}^{2} \approx 0.529\;\text{bar}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Frictional Power F_p | 0.697 kW |
| Frictional Torque T_f | 4.654 N·m |
| Indicated Power IP | 3.897 kW |
| Friction MEP F_mep | 0.529 bar |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Students Lose Marks On:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2 in denominator** of $F_p = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t}$ — most common error; loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing rpm with rad/s** — when substituting $F_p$ in Watts, ω must be in rad/s. Many students plug rpm directly.
> 3. **Using wrong k for 4-stroke** — for indicated power formula use k = 1 (NOT 2 as in 2-stroke). Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Not converting BP from kW to W** before using the F_mep formula — silent unit error, loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Plotting graphs incorrectly** — X-axis should be time (s) on horizontal; Y-axis N (rpm) for first graph, N² (rpm²) for second. Many students reverse axes.
> 6. **Neglecting to list assumptions** in descriptive answers — KTU expects minimum 4 assumptions; losing 1 mark each missing.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention flywheel role** — most descriptive answers skip this; it carries 1–2 marks weightage.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam & Lab Viva:**

- ☐ **Retardation Test** = coasting-down test to find **frictional power** indirectly via flywheel KE.
- ☐ Synonyms: Running-down test, Motoring test, Deceleration test.
- ☐ Engine type tested: **4-stroke, single cylinder, vertical, diesel** with co-axial electric motor.
- ☐ Electric motor has **three roles**: starter, motoring driver, and load absorber.
- ☐ Master formula (energy form): $F_p = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t}$ in Watts.
- ☐ Master formula (rpm form): $F_p = \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,I(N_1^{2} - N_2^{2})}{1800\,t}$ in Watts.
- ☐ Frictional torque: $T_f = \dfrac{F_p}{\omega_{avg}} = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})}{2\,t\,\omega_{avg}}$.
- ☐ Indicated power: $IP = BP + F_p$.
- ☐ Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{BP + F_p} \times 100$.
- ☐ Friction MEP formula for 4-stroke: $F_{mep} = \dfrac{60\,F_p\,n}{L\,A\,N\,k}$ with **k = 1**.
- ☐ Always list **6 assumptions** in descriptive answers (friction uniform, I constant, no load, windage negligible, thermal steady state, etc.).
- ☐ Two graphs mandatory: **(1) N vs t** (curved line showing deceleration); **(2) N² vs t** (straight line — preferred for slope analysis).
- ☐ Typical values for a small single cylinder diesel: F_p ≈ 5–15% of rated BP; η_mech ≈ 80–88%.
- ☐ Method is **valid only for no-load or low-load** frictional measurement; for loaded engines use Willan's line extrapolation.
- ☐ **Morse Test** is the multi-cylinder alternative — but our engine is single cylinder, so retardation test is the only direct method.
- ☐ Common KTU viva question: *"Why is retardation test not suitable for highly loaded engines?"* — Answer: Friction varies significantly with load and cylinder pressure; coasting at no-load gives baseline only.
- ☐ Common KTU viva question: *"How is moment of inertia I known?"* — Answer: Either from flywheel geometry ($I = \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2}$ for solid disc), from manufacturer's data, or by a calibration retardation run with a known friction torque.
- ☐ Record format: Title → Aim → Apparatus → Theory → Procedure → Observations Table (N₁, N₂, t) → Sample Calculation → Graphs (N vs t and N² vs t) → Results → Precautions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on Slow Speed Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Load Test on Slow Speed Diesel Engine # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Load Test on a Slow Speed Diesel Engine (SSDE)** is a standardized experimental procedure conducted in a marine engineering laboratory to evaluate the steady-state performance characteristics of a low-speed, two-stroke, crosshead-type marine propulsion diesel engine under progressively increasing mechanical loads. The engine is coupled to a calibrated absorption dynamometer (hydraulic Froude-type water brake or electrical dynamometer), and performance indices such as **Brake Power (BP)**, **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**, **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)**, **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**, and **Heat Balance Sheet** are plotted against the brake load expressed as a percentage of full load. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A *slow speed diesel engine* operates in the speed range of approximately **80–250 rpm**, with a single power stroke per revolution (2-stroke cycle), and is the dominant prime mover for ocean-going merchant vessels because the propeller can be directly coupled to the crankshaft without a reduction gearbox. ## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Examiner's Vocabulary) | Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |------|--------|------------------| | Brake Power | BP | Useful power delivered at the engine output shaft | | Indicated Power | IP | Power developed inside the cylinder by gas pressure | | Mechanical Efficiency | η_mech | Ratio of BP to IP | | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | BSFC | Mass of fuel consumed per unit brake power per hour | | Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption | ISFC | Mass of fuel consumed per unit indicated power per hour | | Brake Mean Effective Pressure | BMEP | Theoretical constant pressure that would produce the same BP | | Brake Thermal Efficiency | η_bth | Conversion efficiency of fuel energy to brake work | | Heat Balance | — | Energy accounting of input fuel energy distributed to output, cooling, exhaust, and losses | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ox-Cart Horse" Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Intuition (For First-Time Learners):** > Imagine you own a strong cart-horse (the engine). Before you trust it to pull heavy loads up a hill all day, you want to know: > 1. How much weight *can* it pull? (Maximum load capacity → Max BP) > 2. How much *grain* (fuel) does it eat per kilometre? (Fuel consumption → BSFC) > 3. How efficiently does it convert that grain into pulling force? (η_mech) > 4. Where does the rest of the energy go — sweat (cooling), breathing (exhaust), or just heat? (Heat balance) > > The load test is exactly that: you hitch the engine to a controlled "load cart" (the dynamometer), increase the pull step-by-step, and **measure** how the engine responds. The dynamometer acts like a giant adjustable brake that "drags" against the engine — the harder it drags, the more the engine must work, and the more fuel it burns. By reading the drag force and the engine speed, you can compute its real-world power output. > [!NOTE] > **Why slow speed?** Slow speed engines are huge (cylinder bore up to **960 mm**, stroke up to **3500 mm** in modern two-stroke marine engines like Wärtsilä RT-flex or MAN B&W). They run slowly because the propeller at the stern of a ship is most efficient at low rotational speeds. Their immense size is what allows them to burn the cheapest, heaviest residual fuel oil (HFO) and still produce tens of thousands of kilowatts. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the Load Test A slow speed marine diesel engine, when tested in a laboratory, is mounted on a rigid test bed and connected through a flexible coupling to a **dynamometer**. The test progresses through the following logic: **Step 1 — Cold Start & Warming Up** - The engine is started using compressed air (as on a real ship) and run on no-load for at least 30 minutes until cooling water, lubricating oil, and cylinder temperatures stabilize. This ensures the engine has reached its **thermal equilibrium**, and performance readings will not drift during the test. **Step 2 — Application of Incremental Loads** - Loads are applied in steps of **10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, and 110%** of the rated full load. - At each load step, the engine is allowed to settle for ~3–5 minutes before readings are taken. **Step 3 — Measurement of Parameters at Each Load** The following readings are recorded simultaneously: - Engine speed **N** (rpm) — via tachometer - Dynamometer load **W − S** (kgf) — net spring balance reading - Fuel consumption **m_f** (kg/hr) — via calibrated fuel flow tank/burette - Cooling water flow **m_w** (kg/sec) and inlet/outlet temperatures - Exhaust gas temperature **T_g** and ambient temperature **T_a** - Air consumption **m_a** (via orifice meter) — optional - Inlet manifold pressure, lubricating oil pressure, jacket water pressure **Step 4 — Performance Computation** - All measured quantities are converted to **standard conditions** (1.01325 bar, 25 °C, 60 % RH) using ISO 3046 correction factors. - Performance curves (BSFC vs. BP, η_mech vs. BP, etc.) are plotted. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind the Test (Engineer's Perspective):** > The load test provides a **performance fingerprint** of the engine. It validates the manufacturer's rated output, identifies deviations due to wear, and confirms that the engine meets the **specified specific fuel consumption** (typically **170–195 g/kWh** for modern 2-stroke marine engines). Without this test, no engine can be classified as sea-worthy for the shipowner or class society (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS). ## 2.2 The Dynamometer — The Heart of the Experiment ### 2.2.1 Froude Hydraulic Dynamometer (Water Brake) This is the **most commonly used** dynamometer for slow speed engine tests in marine labs. - **Working principle:** A rotor (coupled to the engine shaft) spins inside a stator (water-filled housing). The rotor tries to drag the water with it; the stator, free to pivot, reacts by trying to rotate in the opposite direction. This reaction is measured by a **lever arm** pressing on a **spring balance** (or load cell). - **Why hydraulic?** It can absorb **enormous power** (up to 50,000 kW in commercial models) and provides smooth, stepless loading by simply adjusting the water fill level. ### 2.2.2 Electrical Dynamometer (Regenerative) Used in modern precision labs. The engine drives a generator whose electrical output is fed back to the grid or dissipated in resistor banks. Higher accuracy but costlier. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **All quantities in SI units unless stated. Conversion: 1 kgf = 9.81 N; 1 PS = 0.7355 kW; 1 hp = 0.7457 kW.** | # | Performance Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------------------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | **Brake Power (BP)** — from hydraulic/rope dynamometer | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N \, T \, (W - S)}{60}$ | kW | T = effective moment arm (m); (W−S) in kgf; N in rpm; or $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) \, R \, g}{60 \times 1000}$ for absolute SI | | 2 | **BP in terms of BMEP** (2-stroke, K cylinders) | $BP = \dfrac{P_{me} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60}$ | kW | Multiply $P_{me}$ (kPa) by $L$ (m) × $A$ (m²) × N/60 (rev/s) × K | | 3 | **Piston (Cylinder) Area** | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$ | m² | D = cylinder bore diameter | | 4 | **Swept Volume per Cylinder** | $V_s = A \cdot L$ | m³ | L = stroke length | | 5 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | $IP = \dfrac{BP}{\eta_{mech}}$ | kW | Found from Morse test or from indicator diagram | | 6 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | % | For 2-stroke SSDE, $\eta_{mech} = 88 - 92\%$ typical | | 7 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better.
