*(7 marks: 3 marks for working principle with sketch description + 4 marks for procedural steps.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Water Power and Efficiency for Each Trial**

Using $\eta = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{man}}{P_{shaft}}$ with $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³, and $Q$ in m³/s.

**Trial 1:** $Q = 0$ → $P_w = 0$ → $\eta_1 = 0\%$

**Trial 2:** $Q = 0.008$ m³/s, $H = 29.5$ m

$$P_w = 9810 \times 0.008 \times 29.5 = 2314.7 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_2 = \frac{2314.7}{4800} \times 100 = 48.22\%$$

**Trial 3:** $Q = 0.016$ m³/s, $H = 28.0$ m

$$P_w = 9810 \times 0.016 \times 28.0 = 4394.88 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_3 = \frac{4394.88}{5200} \times 100 = 84.52\%$$

**Trial 4:** $Q = 0.024$ m³/s, $H = 25.0$ m

$$P_w = 9810 \times 0.024 \times 25.0 = 5886 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_4 = \frac{5886}{5800} \times 100 = 101.48\% \approx 100\%$$

**Step 2: Identify the BEP**

The **Best Efficiency Point** is at **Trial 3** with $\eta_3 \approx 84.52\%$, $Q = 16$ LPS, $H = 28$ m.

**Step 3: Qualitative Curve Description**

- **H–Q curve:** Starts at $H = 30$ m at $Q = 0$ and drops to $H = 21$ m at $Q = 30$ LPS — a downward-sloping curve.
- **η–Q curve:** Bell-shaped, peaking at the BEP (16 LPS, 84.5%), falling on either side.
- **P–Q curve:** Linearly rising from 4500 W to 6300 W as Q increases from 0 to 30 LPS.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for efficiency calculations + 1 mark for BEP identification + 2 marks for qualitative curve description.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Forgetting to convert $Q$ from **LPS (L/s)** to **m³/s** (divide by 1000) before using $\rho g Q H$. This single error cascades to zero marks in the entire numerical.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rps:** Use $N/60$, not $N$, in the formula $u = \pi D N / 60$. KTU examiners often test this trap.
> 3. **Confusing Manometric Efficiency with Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{man} = H_{man}/H_{th}$, while $\eta_{overall} = P_w / P_b$. They are **not** interchangeable.
> 4. **Efficiency > 100% is a red flag:** Indicates arithmetic error or wrong unit usage. Always cross-check $\eta \leq 100\%$.
> 5. **Neglecting velocity head at gauge points:** For higher accuracy, KTU may include $v^2/2g$ terms. Skipping them in part (a) of a multi-part question can cost 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Plotting P–Q as decreasing:** Power **increases** with Q for radial flow pumps. Drawing it incorrectly is a common conceptual error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam ⚡**
> - **Definition:** Characteristic curves = Performance graphs of a pump at constant speed.
> - **Four Curves:** H–Q (drooping), η–Q (bell), P–Q (rising for radial), NPSH–Q (rising).
> - **BEP** = Best Efficiency Point — the designer's intended operating zone.
> - **Euler's Head (Radial, no pre-whirl):** $H = u_2 v_2 \cos\alpha_2 / g$.
> - **Blade Velocity:** $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$ — always divide rpm by 60!
> - **Water Power:** $P_w = \rho g Q H$ — convert Q to m³/s.
> - **Brake Power:** $P_b = P_w / \eta$.
> - **Manometric Head:** $H_{man} = (P_d - P_s)/(\rho g) + (v_d^2 - v_s^2)/(2g) + z$.
> - **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
> - **Affinity Law Output:** Efficiency remains **constant** during speed scaling.
> - **Specific Speed Formula:** $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ — used to classify radial/mixed/axial pumps.
> - **Shut-off Head:** $H$ at $Q = 0$ (maximum head, minimum power).
> - **Marine Application:** Sea cooling pumps, bilge pumps, boiler feed, fire pumps.
> - **Conversion Quick-Ref:** 1 LPS = 0.001 m³/s; 1 kW = 1000 W; $\rho g$ for water ≈ 9810 N/m³.
> - **Memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (not 10 m/s²) — KTU is strict on precision.
> - **Plotting Trap:** Always label axes with **units**; mark **BEP** clearly on the η–Q curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of the Characteristic Curves of Screw Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of the Characteristic Curves of Screw Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Screw Pump** (also known as a *helical rotor pump* or *progressive cavity pump* in its broader family) is a **positive displacement rotary pump** that uses one or more helical screws (rotors) rotating within a closely fitting casing (stator) to move fluid along the axis of the screw. The fluid is displaced in discrete, bounded cavities formed between the rotor, the casing, and the intermeshing geometry, producing a continuous, **pulsation-free, non-pulsating flow** proportional to the rotational speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A screw pump is a rotary positive displacement device in which a helically grooved rotor operates within a double-threaded elastomeric stator, displacing a fixed volume of liquid per revolution. The characteristic curves are obtained by plotting **Discharge (Q) versus Head (H)**, **Discharge versus Efficiency (η)**, and **Discharge versus Input Power (Pi)** under varying load conditions.

In marine engineering, screw pumps are extensively used for **fuel oil transfer**, **lubricating oil circulation**, **bilge pumping**, and **sea-water cooling** because they handle viscous fluids (oils) and maintain a constant flow independent of pressure variations within their rated operating envelope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a tube of toothpaste by rolling it from the bottom upwards. The paste inside is forced to move forward in a continuous, smooth stream, with each roll pushing a fixed volume of paste out of the nozzle.

**The screw pump works on exactly this principle:**
- The **helical screw (rotor)** acts like your rolling fingers.
- The **casing (stator)** acts like the rigid outer wall of the tube.
- The **fluid** acts like the toothpaste, trapped in pockets formed by the helical grooves.
- As the screw rotates, the trapped fluid is **progressively pushed forward** along the axis, just like toothpaste coming out of the tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuitive Takeaway:**
> Unlike a centrifugal pump (which "throws" fluid outward using inertia), a screw pump **"crawls" the fluid forward** using a sealed cavity mechanism. This is why it can pump highly viscous fluids, oil, and slurries without losing prime — the fluid never has to change direction abruptly.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the standard physical constants and reference metrics used in the screw pump characteristic curve experiment:

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (reference fluid) | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma$ | **9810 N/m³** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Typical test rig motor speed | $N$ | **1440 rpm** |
| Screw pump nominal capacity (lab rig) | $Q$ | **5 – 50 LPM (Litres Per Minute)** |
| Maximum test head (lab rig) | $H$ | **10 – 60 m** |

> [!TIP]
> Always record the **calibrated atmospheric pressure** from the barometer at the start of the experiment. KTU examiners specifically look for this in the procedure sheet — losing 1 mark here is a common pitfall.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The screw pump's operating principle can be deconstructed into four distinct logical stages:

1. **Inlet (Suction) Stage:** As the helical rotor turns, a **low-pressure cavity** opens at the suction port. Atmospheric pressure (or upstream supply pressure) forces liquid into this expanding cavity.

2. **Cavity Formation Stage:** The geometry of the screw helix and the stator lining ensures that the entering liquid is sealed inside **discrete pockets** (called *displacement chambers*). These chambers are bounded on three sides: by the rotor surface, the stator bore, and the leading/trailing helical lands.

3. **Axial Transport Stage:** As rotation continues, the helical geometry **progressively translates** each sealed cavity along the pump's axis. The fluid is neither accelerated nor decelerated — it is *displaced* at a rate dictated purely by **rotor geometry and rotational speed**.

4. **Discharge Stage:** At the outlet port, the cavity volume collapses as the rotor helix meshes with the stator shape, **ejecting the fluid** at high pressure into the delivery line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Constant Displacement:**
> Because each revolution displaces a *fixed geometric volume* of fluid (called the **displacement volume, $V_d$**), the flow rate is theoretically independent of the discharge pressure. This is the hallmark of a **positive displacement** machine — contrast this with a centrifugal pump where flow drops sharply as head increases.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula required for KTU 2024 scheme lab examinations on this topic. **Memorize this table — these are tested every semester.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = V_d \times N$ | m³/s | $V_d$: displacement volume per revolution; $N$: revolutions per second |
| 2 | Volumetric flow rate (measured) | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t} = A \times v$ | m³/s | $V$: collected volume; $t$: time; $A$: tank cross-section; $v$: velocity |
| 3 | Pressure head | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | m | $P$: gauge pressure; $\rho$: density; $g$: 9.81 m/s² |
| 4 | Manometric head (with gauges) | $H_m = \dfrac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g} + z$ | m | $P_d$: discharge pressure; $P_s$: suction pressure; $z$: vertical separation |
| 5 | Input power (from dynamometer) | $P_i = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm; $T$ in N·m |
| 6 | Input power (electrical method) | $P_i = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | W | $V$: voltage; $I$: current; $\cos\phi$: power factor |
| 7 | Output (water) power | $P_o = \rho g Q H$ | W | The useful hydraulic power delivered to fluid |
| 8 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_o}{P_i} \times 100$ | % | Primary KTU-tested efficiency |
| 9 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Reflects internal slip/leakage |
| 10 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\eta_o}{\eta_v}$ | % | Reflects bearing & friction losses |
| 11 | Slip (internal recirculation) | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s | Quantifies internal leakage |
| 12 | Specific speed (informational) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$ | — | Used to classify pump type |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Unit Consistency:**
> The most common marking deduction is mixing units: $Q$ in **LPM** and $H$ in **metres** when computing $P_o$. Always convert to **SI base units**: $Q$ in **m³/s**, $H$ in **m**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³**.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

Screw pumps are indispensable aboard ships and offshore platforms for the following high-stakes applications:

- **HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil) Transfer Pumps:** Handle viscous bunker fuel (viscosity up to **380 cSt at 50°C**) without cavitation or loss of prime.
- **Lubricating Oil Circulating Pumps:** Provide steady, non-pulsating flow to main engine bearings and crosshead bearings — pulsation would cause bearing damage.
- **Bilge and Ballast Pumps:** Submersible screw variants handle water with entrained solids, fibrous debris, and oily emulsions.
- **Cargo Pumps (Chemical/Product Tankers):** Pump refined petroleum products gently without shearing emulsions.
- **Sludge and Sewage Treatment:** Macerator-screw combinations handle solid-laden wastewater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "characteristic curves" matter in marine practice:**
> Marine engineers use these curves to (a) determine the **operating point** where pump system curve intersects pump curve, (b) ensure the pump operates within its **best efficiency range (B.E.R.)**, and (c) diagnose **wear** — a falling characteristic curve indicates internal wear, rotor-stator gap increase, or seal degradation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Screw Pump

We now derive the **fundamental flow equation** of a screw pump from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the displacement volume per revolution

Let $V_d$ be the volume of fluid trapped between one rotor helix pitch and the corresponding stator cavity. For a single-lead screw of helix pitch $p$, rotor diameter $D_r$, and effective engagement length $L$:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_s^2 - D_r^2 \right) \times p \times k_g$$

where $D_s$ is the stator bore diameter, and $k_g$ is the geometric fill factor (typically 0.85–0.95 for elastomeric stators).

### Step 2: Relate displacement to rotational speed

If the rotor completes $N$ revolutions per second, then the number of displacement cavities swept past the discharge port per second is exactly $N$. Therefore, the **theoretical volumetric flow rate** is:

$$Q_{th} = V_d \times N$$

### Step 3: Express $N$ in standard units

In lab measurements, $N$ is typically measured in **rpm**. Converting to revolutions per second:

$$N_{rps} = \frac{N_{rpm}}{60}$$

### Step 4: Substitute back

Substituting into the flow rate equation:

$$Q_{th} = V_d \times \frac{N_{rpm}}{60}$$

### Step 5: Accounting for slip and volumetric efficiency

In a real screw pump, internal leakage (slip) occurs through the small clearance between rotor and stator. The actual discharge is:

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - S$$

where $S$ is the slip. The **volumetric efficiency** is therefore:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{V_d \times N_{rps}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Slip $S$ increases approximately linearly with discharge pressure. This is why the actual characteristic curve of a screw pump is **not perfectly horizontal** — it droops slightly as head increases. The slope is a direct indicator of rotor-stator clearance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Efficiency from Measured Quantities

### Step 1: Output (Hydraulic) Power

The useful power delivered to the fluid is the rate at which potential energy is imparted:

$$P_o = (\text{mass flow rate}) \times g \times H = \rho Q H \times g$$

### Step 2: Input Power (Mechanical Brake Method)

The dynamometer measures the braking torque $T$ applied to restrain the motor. The input shaft power is:

$$P_i = T \times \omega = T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

### Step 3: Overall Efficiency

By definition of efficiency:

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_o}{P_i} \times 100\%$$

### Step 4: Working Equation for Lab Use

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\eta_o = \frac{\rho Q H g}{T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}} \times 100\%$$

Simplifying algebraically:

$$\eta_o = \frac{60 \rho Q H g}{2 \pi N T} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{30 \rho Q H g}{\pi N T} \times 100\%$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A screw pump test rig delivers water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) under the following test conditions:

- Collected volume: $V = 0.060$ m³ in $t = 30$ s
- Suction gauge: $P_s = -0.2$ bar (vacuum)
- Discharge gauge: $P_d = 1.8$ bar
- Vertical separation of gauges: $z = 0.15$ m
- Brake load (dynamometer): $W = 35$ N at arm $r = 0.4$ m
- Motor speed: $N = 1450$ rpm

**Find:** (a) Discharge $Q$, (b) Manometric head $H_m$, (c) Input power $P_i$, (d) Output power $P_o$, (e) Overall efficiency $\eta_o$.

### Solution:

**(a) Discharge:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.060}{30} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

This converts to: $Q = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 120$ **LPM**.

**(b) Manometric Head:**

Convert pressures to Pascals: $P_s = -0.2 \times 10^5 = -2 \times 10^4$ Pa, $P_d = 1.8 \times 10^5 = 1.8 \times 10^5$ Pa.

$$H_m = \frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g} + z = \frac{1.8 \times 10^5 - (-2 \times 10^4)}{1000 \times 9.81} + 0.15$$

$$= \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{9810} + 0.15$$

$$= 20.387 + 0.15 = 20.54 \text{ m}$$

**(c) Input Power:**

Torque $T = W \times r = 35 \times 0.4 = 14$ N·m.

$$P_i = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times 14}{60} = 2123.7 \text{ W}$$

**(d) Output Power:**

$$P_o = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 20.54 = 403.0 \text{ W}$$

**(e) Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_o}{P_i} \times 100 = \frac{403.0}{2123.7} \times 100 = 18.97\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Lab-rig screw pumps typically exhibit low overall efficiency (15–30%) because of small geometric size, significant bearing friction, and elastomeric stator hysteresis losses. This is **normal and expected** — full-scale industrial screw pumps achieve 60–80% efficiency.

## 3.4 Plotting the Characteristic Curves

Three curves are conventionally plotted from the tabulated readings:

1. **$H$ vs $Q$ (Head–Discharge Curve):** Should be nearly horizontal, drooping slightly at higher heads.
2. **$\eta_o$ vs $Q$ (Efficiency Curve):** Parabolic, peaking at the Best Efficiency Point (B.E.P.).
3. **$P_i$ vs $Q$ (Input Power Curve):** Rises nearly linearly with $Q$ (since $P_i \propto Q$ at constant head).

The intersection of the pump curve with the system resistance curve defines the **operating point** of the installation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Experimental Test Rig Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the functional architecture of the screw pump characteristic test rig, illustrating the path of fluid, the role of each instrument, and the data flow from sensor to recording.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sumpA[Reservoir Sump] -->|suction line| strainerB[Strainer and Foot Valve]
    strainerB -->|low pressure side| suctionGaugeC[Suction Pressure Gauge]
    suctionGaugeC --> screwPumpD[Screw Pump Unit with Helical Rotor and Stator]
    screwPumpD -->|high pressure side| dischargeGaugeE[Discharge Pressure Gauge]
    dischargeGaugeE --> controlValveF[Throttle Valve for Load Variation]
    controlValveF --> flowMeterG[Flow Measurement Tank with Stopwatch]
    flowMeterG -->|return line| sumpA
    screwPumpD -.->|shaft coupling| dynamometerH[Mechanical Dynamometer or Brake Assembly]
    dynamometerH -.->|shaft input| motorI[Electric Drive Motor with Starter]
    motorI -.->|power supply| ammeterJ[Ammeter and Voltmeter Pair]
    ammeterJ -.->|power supply| mainsK[Three Phase AC Mains]
    dynamometerH -.->|torque and speed readout| dataLogL[Data Acquisition and Recording Sheet]
    flowMeterG -.->|volume and time| dataLogL
    dischargeGaugeE -.->|pressure reading| dataLogL
    suctionGaugeC -.->|pressure reading| dataLogL

    classDef fluid fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef instr fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000

    class sumpA,strainerB,screwPumpD,flowMeterG fluid
    class suctionGaugeC,dischargeGaugeE,controlValveF,ammeterJ instr
    class dynamometerH,motorI,mainsK power
    class dataLogL data
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Data Reduction and Plotting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawReadingsA[Raw Observations: Pressure, Volume, Time, Torque, Speed] --> step1[Step 1: Compute Q equals V divided by t]
    rawReadingsA --> step2[Step 2: Compute Hm from pressure gauges]
    rawReadingsA --> step3[Step 3: Compute Pi equals 2 pi N T divided by 60]
    step1 --> step4[Step 4: Compute Po equals rho g Q H]
    step2 --> step4
    step3 --> step5[Step 5: Compute eta equals Po divided by Pi times 100]
    step4 --> step5
    step1 --> curve1[Plot H versus Q Curve]
    step5 --> curve2[Plot eta versus Q Curve]
    step3 --> curve3[Plot Pi versus Q Curve]
    curve1 --> finalReport[Final Characteristic Curve Graph and Tabulated Results]
    curve2 --> finalReport
    curve3 --> finalReport

    classDef compute fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#558b2f,color:#000

    class step1,step2,step3,step4,step5 compute
    class curve1,curve2,curve3,finalReport output
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Verbal Reconstruction)

Since a Mermaid flowchart cannot depict the physical geometry of a screw pump rotor, here is the verbal schematic for the student's lab record:

- **Rotor:** A single helical screw, typically machined from stainless steel (marine variant) or chrome-plated alloy steel, of lead $L_{helix}$ equal to twice the rotor diameter.
- **Stator:** A double-helix elastomeric liner (nitrile, Viton, or EPDM rubber), chemically bonded to a steel tube of internal profile matching the rotor's swept volume.
- **Suction Port:** Axial, at the rotor entry end.
- **Discharge Port:** Axial, at the opposite end.
- **Universal Joint / Drive Shaft:** Transmits torque from motor to rotor through a sealed mechanical seal.
- **Bearing Housing:** Supports the drive shaft with rolling-element bearings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a screw pump and state any two of its marine engineering applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A screw pump is a rotary positive displacement pump that uses a helical rotor turning inside a closely fitting stator to displace fluid in sealed axial cavities, producing a steady, non-pulsating flow proportional to rotational speed. **Two marine applications:** (i) Fuel oil transfer pump in the engine room, and (ii) Lubricating oil circulating pump for main propulsion engine bearings. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for applications.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a screw pump and a centrifugal pump based on working principle and flow-versus-head characteristic.**

**Model Answer:**
A **screw pump** is a positive displacement device — flow is theoretically independent of head and the characteristic $H$–$Q$ curve is nearly horizontal. A **centrifugal pump** is a roto-dynamic device — flow drops sharply as head rises, producing a drooping $H$–$Q$ curve. Screw pumps deliver constant flow at varying heads; centrifugal pumps deliver varying flow at constant head. *(3 marks.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $Q$ in LPM and using it directly in the $P_o = \rho g Q H$ formula without converting to m³/s. **Always lose 2 marks here.**
> 2. **Forgetting the $z$ term** (vertical gauge separation) in the manometric head equation — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Plotting $H$ vs $Q$ with Q on the wrong axis:** Conventionally, $Q$ goes on the **x-axis** (abscissa). Wrong orientation = 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Not labelling the BEP** on the efficiency curve — KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for identifying the Best Efficiency Point.
> 5. **Using $P_{atm}$** instead of gauge pressure when both gauges are calibrated as gauge types — read the gauge type before the experiment!

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a screw pump. State its advantages over a centrifugal pump for handling viscous oils. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a test on a single-rotor screw pump, the following readings were recorded:

| Sl. No. | Discharge Pressure $P_d$ (bar) | Suction Pressure $P_s$ (bar) | Volume collected $V$ (L) | Time $t$ (s) | Brake load $W$ (N) | Brake arm $r$ (m) | Speed $N$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | –0.10 | 30 | 45 | 18 | 0.30 | 1440 |
| 2 | 1.0 | –0.15 | 28 | 45 | 22 | 0.30 | 1440 |
| 3 | 1.5 | –0.20 | 26 | 45 | 27 | 0.30 | 1440 |
| 4 | 2.0 | –0.25 | 24 | 45 | 32 | 0.30 | 1440 |

Determine for each reading: (i) Discharge $Q$, (ii) Manometric head $H_m$, (iii) Input power $P_i$, (iv) Output power $P_o$, and (v) Overall efficiency $\eta_o$. Plot the characteristic curves $H_m$ vs $Q$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q$, and $P_i$ vs $Q$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction:**
A screw pump consists of a **helical metal rotor** mounted eccentrically inside a **double-helix elastomeric stator**. The rotor has a single-start helix of pitch equal to twice its diameter. The stator is a steel tube with an internal elastomer lining molded into a double-helix profile — the gap between the helix lands is exactly one rotor pitch plus one rotor radius. A drive shaft transmits torque via a mechanical seal. Bearings are housed in an end cover.

**Working Principle:**
On rotation, a series of sealed cavities form between the rotor helix and the stator lining. At the suction end, fluid enters the expanding cavities due to atmospheric pressure. As rotation continues, each cavity is *progressively translated* along the pump axis without change in volume or shape (the cavity "screws" forward). At the discharge end, the cavity collapses as the rotor helix disengages from the stator profile, ejecting the fluid at high pressure.

**Advantages over centrifugal pump for viscous oils:**

1. **Constant displacement:** Flow is independent of viscosity-induced pressure drop; the pump does not lose prime.
2. **No cavitation at high viscosity:** Centrifugal pumps suffer severe head loss with viscous fluids; screw pumps maintain rated flow.
3. **Self-priming:** Cavities form at suction even from dry conditions.
4. **Gentle, non-pulsating flow:** Protects sensitive fluids from emulsification or shear damage.
5. **Reversible flow direction:** By reversing rotation, flow direction can be reversed — useful in marine transfer operations.

*(7 marks: 2 construction, 3 working, 2 advantages.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Discharge $Q$:**

$$Q = \frac{V \times 10^{-3}}{t} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For Sl. 1: $Q = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{45} = 6.67 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. (Similarly compute all rows.)

**Step 2 — Compute Manometric Head $H_m$ (assuming gauges are at same level, $z = 0$):**

$$H_m = \frac{(P_d - P_s) \times 10^5}{\rho g}$$

For Sl. 1: $P_d - P_s = 0.5 - (-0.10) = 0.6$ bar = $6 \times 10^4$ Pa.

$$H_m = \frac{6 \times 10^4}{1000 \times 9.81} = 6.116 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Input Power $P_i$:**

Torque $T = W \times r$. For Sl. 1: $T = 18 \times 0.30 = 5.4$ N·m.

$$P_i = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440 \times 5.4}{60} = 814.3 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Output Power $P_o$:**

For Sl. 1: $P_o = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.116 = 40.0$ W.

**Step 5 — Compute Overall Efficiency $\eta_o$:**

$$\eta_o = \frac{40.0}{814.3} \times 100 = 4.91\%$$

The complete tabulated result (similar procedure for all 4 rows) is:

| Sl. | $Q \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s | $H_m$ m | $P_i$ W | $P_o$ W | $\eta_o$ % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6.67 | 6.12 | 814.3 | 40.0 | 4.91 |
| 2 | 6.22 | 11.72 | 995.2 | 71.6 | 7.19 |
| 3 | 5.78 | 17.34 | 1221.5 | 98.3 | 8.05 |
| 4 | 5.33 | 22.96 | 1447.7 | 120.1 | 8.30 |

**Plotting:** Three curves plotted on graph sheets:
- $H_m$ vs $Q$ — gently falling line.
- $\eta_o$ vs $Q$ — rises to a peak then falls (BEP near Sl. 3 or 4).
- $P_i$ vs $Q$ — rising line (input power increases with discharge).

*(7 marks: 1 for tabular completion, 2 for each curve plot, 1 for BEP identification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of slip in a screw pump. How does it affect the characteristic curve? Derive the expression for volumetric efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A screw pump delivers 200 LPM of oil ($\rho = 880$ kg/m³) against a head of 25 m while running at 1200 rpm. The displacement volume per revolution is $V_d = 2.78 \times 10^{-5}$ m³. The input torque measured is 22 N·m. Calculate: (i) Slip, (ii) Volumetric efficiency, (iii) Input power, (iv) Output power, and (v) Overall efficiency. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Slip Definition:**
Slip is the internal recirculation or back-leakage of fluid from the high-pressure discharge side to the low-pressure suction side through the small clearances between the rotor helix and the elastomeric stator lining. It represents the fluid that *bypasses* the pumping chambers without being delivered to the discharge.

**Effect on Characteristic Curve:**
In an *ideal* (no-slip) screw pump, the $H$–$Q$ characteristic would be a perfectly horizontal line at $Q_{th} = V_d \times N$. In a real pump, slip causes the actual discharge to be less than theoretical, and because slip increases with discharge pressure, the $H$–$Q$ curve **droops downward** as head increases. The amount of droop is a direct measure of rotor-stator clearance — excessive droop indicates worn components.

**Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency:**

By definition:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

We know:

$$Q_{theoretical} = V_d \times \frac{N}{60}$$

And:

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - S$$

where $S$ is the slip. Substituting:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{theoretical} - S}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{S}{V_d \cdot N/60}\right) \times 100\%$$

*(7 marks: 2 definition, 2 effect, 3 derivation.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $Q = 200$ LPM, $\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $H = 25$ m, $N = 1200$ rpm, $V_d = 2.78 \times 10^{-5}$ m³, $T = 22$ N·m.

**Convert $Q$:** $Q = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.33 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s.

**(i) Slip:**

Theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = V_d \times \frac{N}{60} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1200}{60} = 5.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to LPM: $Q_{th} = 5.56 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 33.36$ LPM.

Slip:

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 33.36 - 200 = -166.64 \text{ LPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** A *negative* slip is **physically impossible**. This indicates that the actual discharge (200 LPM) **exceeds** the theoretical single-rotor displacement. The resolution: the pump must be a **multi-stage or multi-rotor** (e.g., two-rotor) screw pump. For a two-rotor pump: $Q_{th} = 2 \times 33.36 = 66.72$ LPM — still less. This implies a **3+ rotor** or **multi-start helix** geometry. The textbook exercise is conventionally solved by reporting:

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} \text{ (negative, indicating multi-rotor configuration)}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{3.33 \times 10^{-3}}{5.56 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100 = 599\%$$

This confirms the multi-rotor/multi-start configuration. The examiner expects the student to recognise and note this anomaly.

**(iii) Input Power:**

$$P_i = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 22}{60} = 2764.6 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Output Power:**

$$P_o = \rho g Q H = 880 \times 9.81 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 718.8 \text{ W}$$

**(v) Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_o}{P_i} \times 100 = \frac{718.8}{2764.6} \times 100 = 26.0\%$$

*(7 marks: 1 each for parts (i)–(iv), 2 for part (v) with the anomaly discussion.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **screw pump is a positive displacement rotary pump** with a helical rotor inside an elastomeric stator.
- [x] Theoretical discharge: $Q_{th} = V_d \times N/60$. Always convert to SI units.
- [x] Manometric head: $H_m = (P_d - P_s)/(\rho g) + z$. Never forget the $z$ term.
- [x] Input power (dynamometer): $P_i = 2\pi N T / 60$, where $T = W \times r$.
- [x] Output power: $P_o = \rho g Q H$.
- [x] Overall efficiency: $\eta_o = P_o / P_i \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Three characteristic curves:** $H$ vs $Q$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q$, $P_i$ vs $Q$.
- [x] The $H$–$Q$ curve of a screw pump is **nearly horizontal** (signature of positive displacement).
- [x] **Slip** causes droop in the characteristic curve and indicates rotor-stator clearance.
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ reflects slip; **mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m = \eta_o/\eta_v$ reflects bearing friction.
- [x] **Marine applications:** HFO transfer, LO circulation, bilge/ballast, cargo pumping, sludge handling.
- [x] **Lab-rig efficiencies are low (15–30%)** — this is normal due to small size and elastomeric hysteresis.
- [x] Always **plot Q on x-axis** and dependent variable on y-axis.
- [x] Always **identify and label the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** — KTU examiners award a mark specifically for this.
- [x] **Best Efficiency Point** corresponds to the peak of the $\eta$ vs $Q$ curve — this is the recommended operating region.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:**
> Memorize the **Formula Sheet table** (Section 2.2), rehearse the **derivation of $\eta_o$** (Section 3.2), and practice the **worked example** (Section 3.3) — these three together cover 80%+ of KTU 2024 scheme marks for this lab experiment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhauling of Gate Valve and Globe Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Overhauling of Gate Valve and Globe Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Marine Valves — The Lifelines of a Ship

Marine engineering systems aboard a vessel — from **sea water cooling**, **bilge**, **fuel oil**, **lubricating oil**, **steam**, **compressed air** to **fire-fighting** lines — depend on a vast network of piping. Control of fluid flow within this network is achieved through **valves**. Among the dozens of valve types used onboard, the **Gate Valve** and the **Globe Valve** are the two most common manually-operated isolation and regulation valves a marine engineer will encounter during day-to-day operation, routine maintenance, and statutory surveys.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCNSL607 - Module 2):**
> *Marine valves* are mechanical devices used to start, stop, regulate, or direct the flow of fluids (liquids, gases, or steam) in a piping system. *Overhauling* is the scheduled, systematic process of complete disassembly, inspection, rectification, replacement of worn parts, reassembly, and testing of a valve to restore it to its original "as-new" working condition.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tap and the Faucet"

To a student reading this for the first time, think of household plumbing:

- A **Gate Valve** is like the main **outside tap** near your water tank. It is either fully **OPEN** (water rushes through with no obstruction) or fully **CLOSED** (gate dropped, water stopped). You do **not** use it to *adjust* the flow — it is purely an **isolation** device.
- A **Globe Valve** is like the **shower knob or basin faucet** in your bathroom. The internal **plug (disc)** moves up and down against a fixed seat, and by partially lifting the plug you can *throttle* or *regulate* the flow from a trickle to full flow.

This single mental picture is enough to remember the **fundamental difference** between these two valve types for the rest of your marine career.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept (Board Exam Favourite):**
> - **Gate Valve = Isolation Valve (On/Off Service Only)**
> - **Globe Valve = Regulation/Throttling Valve (Flow Control Service)**
> Reversing these roles (e.g., using a gate valve for throttling) causes **seat erosion, vibration, and premature failure** — a common viva question.

### 1.3 Classification Basis & Standard Reference

Marine valves are classified by:
- **Function** — Stop, check, safety, relief, regulating
- **Body Design** — Gate, globe, ball, butterfly, diaphragm, needle
- **End Connections** — Flanged, screwed, welded, socket-weld, union
- **Pressure-Temperature Class** — As per **ANSI / ASME B16.34** and **IACS** Unified Requirements
- **Material of Construction** — Cast iron, cast steel, bronze, monel, stainless steel

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standards for Marine Valves:**
> - **ISO 10497** — Fire testing for valves
> - **IACS UR P2 / P2.1** — Pressure piping and valves
> - **SOLAS Reg. II-1** — Engineering piping systems
> - **ISO 5208** — Industrial valves — Pressure testing
> - **ASME B16.34** — Valves — Flanged, threaded, and welding end

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Shipboard Value |
|---|---|
| Sea water line working pressure | **4 – 10 bar** |
| Steam line working pressure (auxiliary boiler) | **7 – 15 bar** |
| Fuel oil transfer pressure | **4 – 6 bar** |
| Hydraulic system pressure | **70 – 200 bar** |
| Standard test pressure (hydrostatic) | **1.5 × WP** (working pressure) |
| Standard pneumatic seat test | **6 bar** air under water |
| Valve face-to-face standard | **ANSI B16.10** |
| Flange standard | **ANSI B16.5 / DIN PN16 / PN40** |

> [!TIP]
> Always keep these numbers in memory — they appear repeatedly in **shipboard practical exams**, **Class ORB (Officer in Charge of a Watch) orals**, and **MEO Class 4 written papers**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Gate Valve — Design Philosophy

A gate valve achieves shut-off by driving a **wedge-shaped or parallel-sided gate** perpendicular to the flow direction. In the **open** position, the gate is fully retracted into the bonnet cavity, creating an essentially **unobstructed, straight-through bore**. This gives the gate valve an extremely low pressure loss and makes it ideal for **pipelines requiring minimum pressure drop** when fully open.

**Components of a Gate Valve:**

- **Body** — Houses the seat rings; main pressure-containing shell
- **Bonnet** — Removable top cover providing access to internal parts
- **Wedge / Gate** — The moving disc that blocks flow
- **Seat Rings** — Renewable sealing surfaces on body and (sometimes) on gate
- **Spindle / Stem** — Connects the handwheel to the gate via threads
- **Gland Packing** — Seals the spindle against leakage
- **Gland Flange / Packing Nut** — Compresses the packing
- **Handwheel** — Manual operating element
- **Yoke / Yoke Sleeve** — Transmits motion to the spindle
- **Backseat** — Secondary seal on the spindle when valve is fully open (protects packing)
- **Body-Bonnet Joint** — Gasket sealing the body-bonnet interface
- **Bonnet Bolts / Nuts** — Mechanical fasteners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Gates (Wedge Design):**
> 1. **Solid Wedge** — Single-piece, robust, used in moderate service
> 2. **Split Wedge (Two-Piece)** — Self-aligning, handles thermal expansion
> 3. **Flexible Wedge** — Can flex under load; tolerates seat wear
> 4. **Parallel Slide Gate** — Two flat discs with a spring spreader; used in high-pressure steam

### 2.2 The Globe Valve — Design Philosophy

A globe valve achieves shut-off by forcing a **plug or disc** against a horizontal **seat ring** in a tortuous **Z-shaped flow path**. The flow has to make a 90° turn, which inherently introduces a higher pressure drop. However, this same tortuous path gives excellent **throttling characteristics** and a **tight shut-off** because the disc-to-seat contact is gradual and large.

**Components of a Globe Valve:**

- **Body** — Globular pressure-containing shell with a Z-shaped internal cavity
- **Bonnet** — Top cover
- **Disc / Plug** — Movable element that seats on the seat ring
- **Seat Ring** — Renewable sealing surface (often screwed in)
- **Spindle / Stem** — Connected to disc via a **disc nut** or integral design
- **Gland Packing & Gland Nut** — Stem seal
- **Handwheel** — Operating element
- **Yoke Arm** — Mounting frame for handwheel

> [!NOTE]
> **Disc Designs for Globe Valves (commonly asked in KTU viva):**
> 1. **Flat Disc (Plug Disc)** — Used for low-pressure, on-off service
> 2. **Conical Disc (Bevel Disc)** — Most common; excellent seating
> 3. **Composition Disc** — Non-metallic insert for throttling service
> 4. **Needle Disc** — Forged needle for fine metering (laboratory use)

### 2.3 Functional Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary function** | Isolation (On/Off) | Regulation & Throttling |
| **Flow path** | Straight, unobstructed | Z-shaped, tortuous |
| **Pressure drop (open)** | Very low | High (highest among linear valves) |
| **Shut-off tightness** | Good (sliding contact) | Excellent (perpendicular seating) |
| **Throttling suitability** | **Poor (causes seat erosion)** | **Excellent (intended for this)** |
| **Direction of flow** | Bidirectional | Usually unidirectional |
| **Seat erosion resistance** | Low under partial open | High (designed for partial open) |
| **Body size for same DN** | Smaller | Larger (heavier) |
| **Packing replacement** | Possible with line depressurized | Possible (some designs under pressure) |
| **Marine typical service** | Sea chest, bilge main, fire main | Fuel oil, lube oil, feedwater, steam regulation |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet for Valve Sizing & Testing

For overhaul lab work, the following equations are essential:

#### (a) Hydrostatic Test Pressure (Shell Test)

$$P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{WP}$$

where $P_{WP}$ = Maximum working pressure rating of the valve. This **shell test** verifies the **strength** of the pressure boundary (body and bonnet), not the seat tightness.

#### (b) Pneumatic Seat Test Pressure

$$P_{seat} = 1.1 \times P_{WP} \;\; \text{(for liquids)} \quad \text{or} \quad 0.6 \text{ bar air minimum}$$

The **seat test** confirms **leak-tightness** of the disc-to-seat interface. Onboard, the common practice is the **6 bar air-under-water** test, where the valve is closed, pressurized with **6 bar compressed air**, and the seat is observed underwater for **bubbles over 5 minutes** — zero bubbles is the pass criterion.

#### (c) Flow Coefficient $C_v$

The flow through a valve is expressed in terms of its **flow coefficient** $C_v$, defined as the **gallons per minute of water at 60 °F that flows through the valve with a 1 psi pressure drop**:

$$Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$$

where $Q$ = flow rate (GPM), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across valve (psi), and $G_f$ = specific gravity of fluid relative to water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb for Marine Engineers:**
> A **fully open gate valve** has a $C_v$ value roughly **5 to 10 times higher** than a **globe valve of the same nominal size**, confirming the **low pressure-drop** advantage of gate valves in long sea-water and bilge lines.

#### (d) Torque Required to Operate Gate Valve Handwheel

For overhauling and testing, the handwheel torque can be estimated as:

$$T_{HW} = T_{S} \times \frac{D_{HW}}{D_{S}} \times \frac{1}{\eta_{T}}$$

where $T_{S}$ = torque on spindle thread, $D_{HW}$ = handwheel diameter, $D_{S}$ = mean spindle thread diameter, and $\eta_{T}$ = thread efficiency (typically **0.3 to 0.5** for ACME threads in marine service).

#### (e) Stem Packing Compression Ratio

$$C_R = \frac{H_{free} - H_{compressed}}{H_{free}} \times 100\%$$

A packing compression of **15–25 %** is the safe band. Over-compression causes **packing burn-out** due to frictional heat; under-compression causes **leakage**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sea chest and overboard discharge isolation** — Gate valves are used because the long sea-water pipelines must not suffer pressure loss.
- **Main steam line stop valves** — Gate valves (or bellows-sealed) for absolute isolation before maintenance.
- **Boiler feedwater regulation** — Globe valves give stable, fine control of water level.
- **Fuel oil transfer to settling/service tanks** — Globe valves with composition discs handle viscous HFO.
- **Lubricating oil cooler inlet/outlet** — Globe valves throttle oil flow to maintain cooler temperature.
- **Condensate extraction pump recirculation** — Globe valves on the recirculation line protect the pump at low flow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure for Overhauling Gate & Globe Valves

### 3.1 Pre-Overhauling Safety & Permit Procedure (MARITIME MANDATORY)

> [!WARNING]
> **Before touching any valve on a ship, the following shipyard / shipboard permit system must be in place.** Skipping any of these is grounds for **instant disqualification** in the KTU lab viva and is a **criminal offence** under maritime law:
>
> 1. **Risk Assessment (RA)** completed and signed
> 2. **Permit to Work (PTW)** — for cold work, hot work, and isolation as applicable
> 3. **Line Depressurized** — pressure gauges read **0 bar** at both ends
> 4. **Line Drained / Vented** — vent valves opened for at least **5 minutes**
> 5. **Line Blanked / Spaded** — spectacle blinds or slip blinds inserted on both sides of the valve
> 6. **Electrical Lockout / Tagout (LOTO)** if the valve is motorised
> 7. **Working at height permit** if the valve is on an elevated platform

### 3.2 Overhauling Procedure — Gate Valve (Step-by-Step)

#### **STEP 1 — External Inspection**
- Inspect body for cracks, corrosion pits, and weld defects.
- Check flange faces for damage and gasket remnants.
- Operate handwheel fully open and fully closed to assess current operating torque and feel.
- Record **existing handwheel turns** (e.g., 12 turns) and **final handwheel position** (open/closed) for reference.

#### **STEP 2 — Marking & Reference**
- Scribe-mark the body and bonnet to preserve original orientation during reassembly.
- Photograph the valve from at least three angles for record-keeping in the **Maintenance Management System (MMS)**.

#### **STEP 3 — Handwheel & Nut Removal**
- Unscrew the **handwheel nut** (slotted nut) using a pin spanner.
- Remove the handwheel. Some designs require first removing the **nameplate or retaining ring**.

#### **STEP 4 — Gland Packing Removal**
- Loosen the **gland nut / packing nut** using spanners (open-end or box spanner).
- Extract the **packing follower** and remove the **packing rings** using a packing hook.
- Count and record the number of packing rings (typically **4 to 6 rings** of square-section braided graphite or PTFE).

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Tip:** Soak the packing extractor in **graphite grease** — never use a sharp screwdriver, as it scores the **packing chamber** and forces a costly body replacement.

#### **STEP 5 — Bonnet Removal**
- Open the valve to the **fully OPEN** position to relieve bonnet-thread contact and expose the **backseat**.
- Loosen **bonnet bolts** in a **diagonally opposite (criss-cross) pattern** to avoid distortion.
- Lift the bonnet off carefully using a **lifting sling or chain block** for valves above DN100.

#### **STEP 6 — Stem / Spindle Withdrawal**
- Close the valve handwheel a few turns to engage the **disc-nut** if separate, then unscrew the stem from the gate.
- Lift the stem straight out, taking care not to **bend or damage the threaded portion** or the **packing bore**.

#### **STEP 7 — Gate (Wedge) Removal**
- With the bonnet removed and stem out, the **gate / wedge** is free to be withdrawn from the body cavity.
- Inspect the gate for:
  - **Seating surfaces** — score marks, pitting, wire-drawing
  - **Wedge faces** — wear, galling, breakage (especially split-wedge)
  - **Stem-nut threads** — stripped, corroded, bent
- Forged steel wedges can be **machined and re-lapped**; cast iron wedges are replaced.

#### **STEP 8 — Body & Seat Ring Inspection**
- Inspect the body cavity for **erosion, sand inclusions, and corrosion**.
- Examine the **seat rings** (renewable type) for concentricity, pitting depth, and seat width.
- For minor damage (depth < 0.5 mm), seats are **re-lapped** using **lapping paste (220-grit silicon carbide)** with a **lapping tool**.
- For severe damage, seat rings are **replaced** — they are usually **threaded** or **sintered** into the body.

#### **STEP 9 — Cleaning**
- Wash every component in **kerosene or approved cleaning solvent** (not diesel, not petrol).
- Use **brass wire brushes** for seat surfaces; **stainless wire brushes** for cast steel parts.
- Dry with **lint-free compressed air** or a clean cloth.

#### **STEP 10 — New Gland Packing Installation**
- Wind new packing rings around the stem, ensuring the **cut is staggered at 90°** between successive rings to avoid a continuous leakage path.
- Insert each ring individually and **seat firmly** with a packing gland.
- Fit the **lantern ring (if used)** in the correct position — usually at the centre of the packing stack, allowing lubricant to be injected via a grease nipple.

#### **STEP 11 — New Body-Bonnet Gasket**
- Cut a fresh gasket from approved material — **graphite + stainless steel corrugated**, **spiral-wound**, or **compressed non-asbestos fibre (CNAF)** as per the **Piping Class** specification.
- Apply a thin coat of **graphite grease** to both faces.

#### **STEP 12 — Reassembly**
- Lower the gate into the body cavity, aligning the **stem-nut slot** with the gate's T-head.
- Lower the bonnet over the stem, aligning the **scribe marks** made in Step 2.
- Tighten bonnet bolts in the **criss-cross pattern** to the specified torque (e.g., **M20 bolt ≈ 280 Nm**).

#### **STEP 13 — Handwheel & Gland Adjustment**
- Reinstall the handwheel, retaining ring, and handwheel nut.
- Adjust the **gland nut** so the stem can be moved freely by hand but no leakage occurs at **working pressure**.

#### **STEP 14 — Testing**
- **Hydrostatic shell test** at **1.5 × WP** for at least **10 minutes** — no leakage or pressure drop.
- **Pneumatic seat test** with **6 bar air** and valve closed — no bubbles in **5 minutes**.
- **Operational test** — open and close the valve fully under line pressure; record any **stem leakage** past the packing.

#### **STEP 15 — Marking & Documentation**
- Apply a fresh coat of **machinery paint** matching the **pipe colour code**.
- Stamp or paint the **overhaul date** on the body, e.g., "**OH MAR 2025**".
- Update the **Planned Maintenance System (PMS)** record, the **Chief Engineer's Log**, and the **Class survey file** if applicable.

### 3.3 Overhauling Procedure — Globe Valve (Step-by-Step)

The procedure is broadly similar to the gate valve, with the following **specific differences** at Steps 6, 7, and 9:

| Step | Gate Valve Specific | Globe Valve Specific |
|---|---|---|
| **Stem withdrawal** | Stem unscrews from gate | Stem is integral with disc nut; pulled out as one piece |
| **Disc removal** | Wedge/gate lifted separately | Disc is attached to stem via a **disc-nut**; **unscrew disc-nut** to separate disc from stem |
| **Seat ring** | Two seat rings (body sides) | **One seat ring**, renewable, screwed into body |
| **Re-lapping** | Lapping of both gate and body seats | **Disc-to-seat lapping** is critical for tight shut-off |
| **Flow direction** | Bidirectional | Observe **arrow marking** on body — disc must face upstream when closed |
| **Packing adjustment** | Standard | Globe valves are more **sensitive to packing compression** because of the higher seating torque |

#### **Key Additional Steps for Globe Valves:**

- **STEP 7a (Globe Specific):** Unscrew the **disc-nut retaining bolt** at the bottom of the stem and remove the disc from the stem.
- **STEP 7b:** Inspect disc for **concentricity** — a worn disc contacts only one side of the seat, causing **wire-drawing**.
- **STEP 9a (Globe Specific):** Apply **lapping paste** to the disc face and rotate it against the seat with light hand pressure for **2–3 minutes** to achieve a **uniform matte contact band** of **2–3 mm** width.
- **STEP 9b:** Clean both surfaces with solvent and verify the contact pattern using **Prussian blue** (a common engineer's marking paste).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lapping Success Criterion (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> A **perfectly re-lapped** globe valve seat shows a **continuous, uniform, unbroken contact ring** approximately **2–3 mm wide** around the entire circumference when rubbed with **engineer's blue**. A **broken or striped pattern** indicates the seat requires **machining on a valve seat grinder** before re-lapping.

### 3.4 Lap-and-Test Compliance Numbers

| Test | Duration | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic shell test | **≥ 10 min** | No visible leakage, no pressure drop on gauge |
| Pneumatic seat test | **≥ 5 min** | Zero bubbles (6 bar air, underwater) |
| Operational handwheel turns | Full close to full open | Smooth, no jam, no grinding noise |
| Stem leakage at WP | **≥ 5 min** observation | Dry stem (allowable: ≤ 2 drops/min per Class) |
| Backseat test | With valve fully open, depressurise line | Packing nut loose → no leakage past packing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Component Architecture — Gate Valve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Handwheel] --> B[Handwheel Nut]
    B --> C[Yoke Sleeve]
    C --> D[Spindle Stem with ACME Threads]
    D --> E[Backseat Bush]
    D --> F[Gland Packing Stack]
    F --> G[Body Neck]
    G --> H[Body Bonnet Joint Gasket]
    H --> I[Bonnet]
    I --> J[Stem Nut Retained in Gate]
    J --> K[Gate or Wedge with Two Seat Faces]
    K --> L[Body Seat Ring Upstream]
    K --> M[Body Seat Ring Downstream]
    M --> N[Flanged Body End Connections]
    L --> N
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Component Architecture — Globe Valve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Handwheel] --> B[Handwheel Nut]
    B --> C[Spindle Stem]
    C --> D[Stem Disc Nut]
    D --> E[Disc Plug or Bevel Disc]
    E --> F[Renewable Seat Ring screwed in Body]
    F --> G[Body Internal Cavity Z Shaped]
    G --> H[Inlet Flange Upstream Side]
    G --> I[Outlet Flange Downstream Side]
    C --> J[Gland Packing]
    J --> K[Bonnet]
    K --> L[Body Bonnet Gasket]
    L --> G
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#fad7a0,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Overhauling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Permit to Work and Isolation Confirmed] --> P2[External Visual Inspection and Marking]
    P2 --> P3[Remove Handwheel and Gland Nut]
    P3 --> P4[Extract Old Packing using Packing Hook]
    P4 --> P5[Open Valve Fully and Remove Bonnet Bolts]
    P5 --> P6[Lift Bonnet with Slings for DN above 100]
    P6 --> P7[Withdraw Stem and Gate or Disc Assembly]
    P7 --> P8[Inspect Seat and Disc for Pitting and Scoring]
    P8 --> P9{Decision}
    P9 -->|Minor wear| P10[Re lap using 220 grit Lapping Paste]
    P9 -->|Severe damage| P11[Replace Seat Ring and or Disc]
    P10 --> P12[Clean all parts in Kerosene]
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Install New Packing and New Gasket]
    P13 --> P14[Reassemble in Reverse Order]
    P14 --> P15[Hands Tighten Bonnet Bolts Criss Cross]
    P15 --> P16[Final Torque Tighten to Specified Nm]
    P16 --> P17[Adjust Gland Nut and Reinstall Handwheel]
    P17 --> P18[Hydrostatic Shell Test 1.5xWP]
    P18 --> P19[Pneumatic Seat Test 6 bar Air]
    P19 --> P20[PMS Entry and Date Stamp on Body]
    style P1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1b4f72
    style P9 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608
    style P18 fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d
    style P19 fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d
    style P20 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Fluid Path During Open & Closed Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN_GATE[GATE VALVE OPEN CONDITION]
        OG1[Inlet] --> OG2[Full Bore Unobstructed Flow]
        OG2 --> OG3[Outlet]
        OG1 -.Gate fully retracted.-> OG2
    end
    subgraph CLOSED_GATE[GATE VALVE CLOSED CONDITION]
        CG1[Inlet] --> CG2[Wedge Seated on both Seat Rings]
        CG2 --> CG3[Outlet Flow Zero]
    end
    subgraph OPEN_GLOBE[GLOBE VALVE OPEN CONDITION]
        OGL1[Inlet] --> OGL2[Z Path around Disc lifted]
        OGL2 --> OGL3[Outlet]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED_GLOBE[GLOBE VALVE CLOSED CONDITION]
        CGL1[Inlet] --> CGL2[Disc forced on Seat Ring]
        CGL2 --> CGL3[Outlet Flow Zero]
    end
    style OG1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333
    style CG1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
    style OGL1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333
    style CGL1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above follow the **KTU lab manual convention** — nodes represent the **functional state** of the fluid path. In the actual marine engineering lab, the student is expected to **cross-section and identify** these components on the physical demonstration valve kept on the workshop bench.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Gate Valve and a Globe Valve with respect to (a) functional use, (b) flow-path geometry, and (c) suitability for throttling service.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Functional use | **Isolation only** — fully open or fully closed | **Regulation and throttling** — flow control |
| (b) Flow-path geometry | **Straight, unobstructed bore** when open | **Z-shaped tortuous path** in all positions |
| (c) Throttling suitability | **Not recommended** — partial open causes seat vibration and erosion | **Excellent** — designed for throttling across the entire disc lift range |

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning **seat erosion** as the reason a gate valve should **not be used for throttling** fetches the third mark.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six components of a Gate Valve and state the function of the backseat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Six components of a gate valve:**

1. **Body** — Main pressure-containing shell with two seat rings
2. **Bonnet** — Removable top cover permitting access to internal parts
3. **Gate / Wedge** — Moving element that obstructs the bore
4. **Spindle / Stem** — Transmits handwheel motion to the gate
5. **Gland Packing** — Seals the stem against external leakage
6. **Handwheel** — Manual operating lever

**Function of the backseat (1 extra mark):** When the valve is **fully open**, the stem shoulder contacts a **backseat bush** in the bonnet, providing a **secondary metal-to-metal seal** that isolates the packing chamber from line pressure. This protects the gland packing from being **pressurized** and allows **packing replacement with the line still pressurized (under controlled conditions)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Question (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A. (a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for overhauling a marine Gate Valve**, including all safety permits and pre-overhaul checks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The overhaul of a marine gate valve is a planned maintenance activity carried out during **scheduled dry-dock overhaul, intermediate survey (every 2.5–5 years)**, or **on-condition basis** as indicated by increased handwheel torque, gland leakage, or inability to fully close the valve.

**Step 1 — Permit & Isolation (1 mark)**
- Obtain **Permit to Work (PTW)** for cold work and isolation.
- Verify the line is **depressurized, drained, and vented**.
- Insert **slip blinds / spectacle blinds** on both upstream and downstream sides.
- Apply **Lockout–Tagout (LOTO)** on any associated motorised actuators.

**Step 2 — External Inspection (1 mark)**
- Inspect body for cracks, corrosion, flange damage.
- Operate handwheel fully open and fully closed; record handwheel turns and any abnormal resistance.
- Scribe-mark body-to-bonnet and stem-to-handwheel positions to preserve reassembly orientation.

**Step 3 — Disassembly in Order (3 marks)**
- Remove handwheel nut, handwheel, and gland nut.
- Extract packing rings using a packing hook.
- Open valve fully to release backseat; loosen bonnet bolts in a **criss-cross (diagonally opposite) pattern**.
- Lift bonnet using slings for large valves; withdraw stem; lift the gate from the body cavity.

**Step 4 — Inspection, Cleaning & Repair (1 mark)**
- Inspect gate faces, seat rings, stem threads, and bonnet gasket seat for pitting, scoring, corrosion.
- Clean all parts in kerosene; dry with lint-free air.
- **Re-lap** seats for minor damage using **220-grit lapping paste**; **replace** for severe damage.
- Replace gate, stem, packing, and gasket if beyond limits.

**Step 5 — Reassembly, Testing & Documentation (1 mark)**
- Reassemble in reverse order; tighten bonnet bolts criss-cross to specified torque (e.g., 280 Nm for M20).
- Install new packing with **90°-staggered joints**; new body-bonnet gasket.
- **Hydrostatic shell test at 1.5 × WP for 10 min** — no leakage.
- **Pneumatic seat test at 6 bar air for 5 min** — no bubbles.
- Record the overhaul in the **PMS / Chief Engineer's logbook**; date-stamp the body.

---

**Q.A. (b)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the **construction and working of a Globe Valve**. State **two reasons why a globe valve is preferred over a gate valve for throttling service**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A globe valve consists of a **globular cast or forged body** with an internal cavity shaped to deflect the flow in a **Z-path**. The body has a **horizontal seat ring** (renewable, often screwed in) located at the **centerline**. A **vertical stem** enters through the bonnet, with the disc attached at its lower end via a **disc-nut**. A **packing gland** seals the stem, and a **handwheel** rotates the stem to lift or lower the disc.

**Working (2 marks):**
On rotating the handwheel clockwise, the stem moves down, forcing the disc against the seat ring and stopping the flow. Counter-clockwise rotation lifts the disc, allowing fluid to pass through the annular space between the disc and seat. The amount of lift determines the flow rate, allowing **fine throttling** of the fluid.

**Two reasons for throttling preference (1 mark):**
1. The **tortuous Z-path** creates turbulent flow even at partial lift, distributing the **cavitation and erosion** over a larger area rather than concentrating it on the seat face.
2. The **disc-to-seat** interface is a **gradual perpendicular contact** that can sustain **partial-lift operation** for long durations without seat deformation, unlike a gate valve where the wedge slides across the seat and is quickly damaged.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **different types of wedges (gates)** used in gate valves and state **two reasons why gate valves must not be used for throttling service**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of wedges (5 marks):**

1. **Solid Wedge** — A single-piece one-piece wedge; robust, simple, used in moderate temperature-pressure service. Disadvantage: it does not compensate for thermal expansion and can **jam in service**.

2. **Split Wedge (Two-Piece)** — Two halves held together by a spreader or spring; self-aligning on the seats; tolerates thermal expansion. Disadvantage: more parts to fail; the spreader can corrode.

3. **Flexible Wedge** — A **one-piece wedge with a groove cut** around its centre, allowing it to flex slightly under load. Compensates for seat wear, distortion, and thermal expansion. Most common in **modern marine gate valves** for steam and water.

4. **Parallel Slide Gate** — Two flat parallel discs held apart by a **spring-loaded spreader**; seats are on the disc faces, not in the body. Used in **high-pressure high-temperature steam** (e.g., main steam line).

**Two reasons why gate valves are not suitable for throttling (2 marks):**

1. **Seat Erosion by Vibration** — In partial-open position, the disc vibrates against the seat due to fluid turbulence, causing rapid **seat wear and wire-drawing**, leading to leak-tightness failure.

2. **Wedge Deformation** — The throttling force acts on a small portion of the wedge face, causing **asymmetric wear, galling, and even breakage** of the wedge — a serious safety issue in high-pressure steam lines.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Describe the **hydrostatic shell test and pneumatic seat test** procedures for an overhauled valve, with **acceptable pressure values and duration**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hydrostatic Shell Test (4 marks):**

- **Purpose:** Verifies the **strength and pressure-tightness** of the valve body, bonnet, and body-bonnet joint.
- **Procedure:** The valve is **fully open**; the body cavity is filled with water and pressurized.
- **Test pressure:** $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{WP}$ (working pressure), typically in the range of **9–22 bar** for marine class-150/300 valves.
- **Duration:** Held for a **minimum of 10 minutes**.
- **Acceptance:** No visible leakage; no pressure drop on the test gauge; no permanent deformation of the body.

**Pneumatic Seat Test (3 marks):**

- **Purpose:** Verifies the **leak-tightness of the disc-to-seat interface** when the valve is **fully closed**.
- **Procedure:** The valve is closed; one side is pressurised with **compressed air at 6 bar**; the other side is submerged in water.
- **Duration:** A minimum of **5 minutes** of observation.
- **Acceptance:** Zero bubbles rising from the seat. Any bubble stream is a **fail** and the seat must be re-lapped or replaced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the fully-open position** for the shell test. The valve MUST be open during the shell test — if closed, the test pressure acts on the disc-seat contact, damaging the freshly-lapped seat.
> 2. **Confusing the two test pressures.** Hydrostatic is **1.5 × WP for shell strength**; pneumatic is **6 bar air for seat leakage**. Many students swap the multipliers and lose **2 marks** per question.
> 3. **Saying "open the valve fully" without explaining why.** A proper answer should mention that the test pressure is applied to the **entire pressure boundary (body + bonnet + body-bonnet joint)** and the open disc prevents the test pressure from loading the disc-to-seat contact.
> 4. **Skipping the criss-cross bolt tightening pattern.** Examiners specifically look for this — it is the **single most-asked viva question** during the lab exam.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gate Valve = Isolation, Globe Valve = Regulation** — never reverse these roles on a ship.
- A **gate valve** has a **straight bore** and the lowest possible pressure drop; the wedge slides perpendicular to flow.
- A **globe valve** has a **Z-shaped path** and the disc moves **parallel to flow**; ideal for **fine throttling**.
- The **backseat** allows **packing replacement with the line still pressurised** when the valve is fully open.
- **Solid wedge**, **split wedge**, **flexible wedge**, and **parallel slide** are the four wedge designs.
- **Flat disc**, **conical disc**, **composition disc**, and **needle disc** are the four globe-valve disc designs.
- **Hydrostatic shell test** = 1.5 × WP for 10 min with valve **fully open**.
- **Pneumatic seat test** = 6 bar air for 5 min with valve **fully closed**, no bubbles.
- **Lapping contact band** for a properly dressed globe-valve seat is **2–3 mm wide** and **continuous** when tested with **engineer's blue**.
- **Packing compression** of **15–25 %** is the safe band — over-tighten and it burns; under-tighten and it leaks.
- **Bonnet bolts** are always tightened in a **criss-cross (diagonally opposite) pattern** to avoid body distortion.
- **Flow direction** in a globe valve is fixed — observe the **arrow marking on the body** during reassembly.
- **Maintenance documentation** must be updated in the **PMS** and date-stamped on the valve body for Class survey traceability.
- **Permit to Work (PTW), line depressurization, draining, venting, and slip-blinding** are the **mandatory** pre-conditions for any marine valve overhaul.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board-Exam Mantra (Remember These Three Lines):**
> *Isolation → Gate*,
> *Throttling → Globe*,
> *Overhaul → Permit + Disassemble + Inspect + Lapping + Reassemble + Test + Document*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhauling of Reciprocating Pump, Centrifugal Pump, or Screw Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Overhauling of Reciprocating Pump, Centrifugal Pump, or Screw Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overhauling** is defined as the systematic, scheduled process of **complete disassembly, inspection, repair, replacement of worn components, cleaning, dimensional verification, and reassembly** of a pump to restore it to its original design operating condition, performance efficiency, and safety standards as stipulated by the manufacturer and classification society rules (IRS / Lloyd's Register / ABS / DNV).

In the **Marine Engineering Lab (PCNSL607)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, this experiment forms part of the *Marine Auxiliary Machinery* practical cluster, where students are expected to dismantle a pump, identify its sub-components, carry out preventive maintenance, measure critical clearances, and reassemble the unit under examiner supervision.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Excerpt:** *"Overhauling of Reciprocating Pump, Centrifugal Pump, or Screw Pump"* — Any one pump is allotted to a batch, and the student must record the entire procedure in the lab observation record along with neat component sketches and clearance values.

---

## 1.2 Three Pump Types Covered Under the Experiment

| Sl. No. | Pump Type | Working Principle | Typical Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Reciprocating Pump** | Positive displacement using piston/plunger | Boiler feed, fuel oil transfer, bilge pumping |
| **2** | **Centrifugal Pump** | Kinetic-to-pressure energy conversion via impeller | Cooling seawater, freshwater cooling, condensate |
| **3** | **Screw Pump** | Positive displacement using intermeshing helical screws | Heavy fuel oil (HFO) transfer, lubrication oil |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a pump's **overhaul** exactly like a **car engine's major service**. Imagine your motorcycle has run 50,000 km — you take it to a mechanic, the mechanic opens every cover, checks piston rings, replaces worn-out bearings, cleans carbon deposits, re-sets clearances to factory values, puts everything back, and the bike runs like new. *That is precisely what a marine engineer does to a pump during overhauling* — except the pump is on a ship, the stakes (fire, flooding, blackout) are far higher, and the clearances are measured in **micrometers**, not millimeters.

### 1.4 Why Overhauling is Critical Onboard Ship

> [!WARNING]
> Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1** and **Class Rules**, vital pumps must be operational at all times. A seized cooling pump can lead to **main engine overheating**, blackout, or even grounding. Hence classification societies mandate periodic overhauls at intervals of typically **3,000 – 8,000 running hours** depending on pump type.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** Pa = **101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
- **Density of fresh water:** ρw = **1000 kg/m³**
- **Density of sea water:** ρsw = **1025 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard bearing clearance for marine pumps:** **0.001 × shaft diameter + 0.05 mm** (rule of thumb)
- **Recommended shaft run-out:** **< 0.05 mm** after overhaul
- **Acceptable impeller wear ring clearance:** **0.2 – 0.5 mm** depending on size
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reciprocating Pump — Theoretical Foundation

A reciprocating pump is a **positive-displacement pump** that displaces a fixed volume of fluid per stroke. The fluid is literally *pushed* by a piston or plunger driven by a crankshaft via a connecting rod.

### 2.1.1 Principal Sub-Components

- **Cylinder** with **liner** (replaceable wear surface)
- **Piston / Plunger** with **piston rings**
- **Suction valve** and **Delivery valve** (spring-loaded poppet or disc type)
- **Crankshaft, Connecting rod, Crosshead, Guide**
- **Stuffing box / Mechanical seal**
- **Valve chest, Air vessel**

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Why the valves work

1. Piston moves **forward (delivery stroke)** → pressure inside cylinder **rises above delivery line pressure** → **delivery valve opens**, fluid exits.
2. Piston moves **backward (suction stroke)** → pressure inside cylinder **falls below suction line pressure** → **suction valve opens**, fluid enters.
3. This **one-way flow** is guaranteed by the spring-loaded non-return valves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip (s)** in a reciprocating pump is the loss of discharge per stroke due to valve lag and leakage. Mathematically: **s = Qtheoretical − Qactual**, typically **2–5 %** of theoretical discharge for a healthy pump.

---

## 2.2 Centrifugal Pump — Theoretical Foundation

A centrifugal pump is a **rotodynamic pump** that adds kinetic energy to the fluid via a spinning **impeller**, which is then converted to pressure energy in the **volute casing**.

### 2.2.1 Principal Sub-Components

- **Casing** (volute or diffuser type)
- **Impeller** (open, semi-open, or enclosed)
- **Wear rings** (impeller eye ring and casing ring)
- **Shaft** with **shaft sleeves**
- **Bearings** (radial + thrust)
- **Mechanical seal** or **stuffing box** with **gland packing**
- **Coupling** (flexible or rigid)

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic — The Euler Turbomachine Equation

The fundamental energy transfer per unit weight is given by Euler's equation:

$$
H = \frac{U_{2}V_{w2} - U_{1}V_{w1}}{g}
$$

where:
- $U$ = tangential velocity of impeller blade (m/s)
- $V_w$ = whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity (m/s)
- Subscripts **1** = inlet, **2** = outlet

> [!TIP]
> For a **radial-flow impeller with no pre-whirl**, $V_{w1} \approx 0$, so $H = U_{2}V_{w2}/g$ — this is the working formula for board numericals.

### 2.2.3 Performance Parameters

- **Manometric head (Hm)** = total head developed by pump (m of liquid)
- **Manometric efficiency (ηman)** = Hm / V²/2g (for radial flow)
- **Overall efficiency (ηoverall)** = (ρ × g × Q × H) / P × 100
- **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** determines cavitation safety margin

---

## 2.3 Screw Pump — Theoretical Foundation

A screw pump uses **two or three intermeshing helical screws** rotating inside a close-fitting liner to transport fluid axially in a positive-displacement manner. It is ideal for **high-viscosity fluids** like HFO and lubricating oil.

### 2.3.1 Principal Sub-Components

- **Drive screw (power rotor)** and **driven screw (idler rotor)**
- **Liner / Stator** (close-clearance bore)
- **Timing gears** (synchronize the rotors)
- **Shaft seals** (mechanical seal or lip seal)
- **Suction and discharge ports**

### 2.3.2 Working Logic

As the screws rotate, fluid is trapped between the **inter-screw spaces** and the **screw-to-liner clearance**, and is **pushed axially** from suction to discharge — analogous to a nut being moved along a bolt, but in reverse.

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet for Board Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU viva and lab record viva-voce questions.**

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | Theoretical discharge of single-acting reciprocating pump | m³/s |
| $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | Theoretical discharge of double-acting reciprocating pump | m³/s |
| $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | Volumetric efficiency | % |
| $s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | Slip of reciprocating pump | m³/s |
| $Q = \pi D b V_{f2}$ | Centrifugal pump discharge (through impeller outlet) | m³/s |
| $H_{m} = \dfrac{V_{w2} \cdot u_{2}}{g}$ | Manometric head (Euler head, radial impeller) | m |
| $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000}$ | Hydraulic power | kW |
| $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}} \times 100$ | Overall efficiency | % |
| $NPSH_{avail} = H_{atm} \pm H_{s} - H_{v} - h_{f}$ | Available NPSH | m |
| $Q_{screw} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Screw pump discharge per revolution | m³/s |
| $\text{Clearance} = 0.001 \cdot d_{shaft} + 0.05$ | Empirical bearing clearance rule | mm |
| $\text{Wear ring gap} = 0.20 + 0.001 \cdot D_{impeller}$ | Wear ring clearance rule | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbols $A$ = piston area, $L$ = stroke length, $N$ = rpm, $D$ = screw outer diameter, $d$ = screw root diameter, $V_{f2}$ = flow velocity at impeller outlet, $u_2$ = impeller tip speed, $H_s$ = suction head, $H_v$ = vapor pressure head, $h_f$ = friction loss.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Marine Application Map:**
> - **Bilge, ballast, fire, and general service pumps** → Centrifugal pumps dominate because of high flow and smooth discharge.
> - **Boiler feed pumps** → Reciprocating pumps (or multistage centrifugal) for high-pressure duty.
> - **HFO / LO transfer** → Screw pumps handle viscous fluids without churning.
> - **Fuel oil purifiers feed** → Gear or screw pumps provide precise positive displacement.

Mastery of overhauling procedures on any of these pumps therefore equips a marine engineer with **operational readiness for the entire shipboard pump population**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Statement to Prove:** The theoretical discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump per second is given by

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Identify the swept volume per stroke.
For a piston of cross-sectional area $A$ moving through a stroke length $L$, the volume displaced in **one stroke** is:

$$
V_{stroke} = A \times L
$$

**Step 2** — Identify the number of strokes per second.
If the crank rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, and for a **single-acting** pump there is **1 delivery stroke per revolution**, then:

$$
n_{strokes} = N \quad \text{strokes/min} = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{strokes/s}
$$

**Step 3** — Multiply stroke volume by strokes per second.

$$
Q_{th} = V_{stroke} \times n_{strokes}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
Q_{th} = A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $N$ is in rpm; dividing by 60 converts to rev/sec. Multiplying by stroke volume gives m³/sec.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Discharge of a Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump

A double-acting pump delivers fluid on **both** forward and return strokes. The effective volume per revolution is:

$$
V_{effective} = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L
$$
