where $a$ is the **cross-sectional area of the piston rod** (since one side of the piston has the rod passing through, reducing the effective area on that side).

Expanding:

$$
V_{effective} = A \cdot L + A \cdot L - a \cdot L = (2A - a) \cdot L
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $a \ll A$ (typical thin piston rod), the formula is often approximated as $Q_{th} \approx 2 A L N / 60$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Euler's Equation for Centrifugal Pump Head

**Velocity Triangle Analysis at Impeller Outlet:**

At any point on the impeller, the **absolute velocity V₂** of the fluid is the **vector sum** of:
- Blade tangential velocity: $\vec{u_2}$ (in direction of rotation)
- Relative velocity: $\vec{V_{r2}}$ (along the blade)

Decomposing $V_2$ along the tangential direction gives the **whirl component $V_{w2}$**.

From the velocity triangle:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2
$$

The work done per unit weight of fluid (Euler head) is:

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}
$$

**Simplification for radial vane (β₂ = 90°):** Here $\cos\beta_2 = 0$, so:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2^{2}}{g} = \frac{(\pi D_2 N / 60)^{2}}{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{Euler} = \frac{\pi^{2} D_2^{2} N^{2}}{3600 \, g} \;\; \text{m of fluid}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$. Squaring and dividing by $g$ gives head in meters. This is the **maximum theoretical head** a centrifugal pump can develop — actual head is reduced by **manometric efficiency**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Screw Pump Theoretical Discharge

Consider **two intermeshing screws** — power rotor of outer diameter $D$ and idler rotor of root diameter $d$, with axial pitch (lead) $L$ equal to one screw revolution's axial advance.

Volume trapped per pitch (between screws and liner) is:

$$
V_{pitch} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^{2} - d^{2} \right) \cdot L
$$

If the screw rotates at $N$ rpm:

$$
\boxed{Q_{screw} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^{2} - d^{2} \right) \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Critical Clearances to be Recorded During Overhauling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table MUST be entered in the lab observation record and signed by the lab instructor.

| **Component Pair** | **Symbol** | **Typical Value (mm)** | **Measurement Tool** | **Acceptance Criterion** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crankshaft main bearing | $c_1$ | 0.05 – 0.15 | Internal micrometer / bore gauge | $\leq 0.001 \cdot d_{shaft} + 0.05$ |
| Crankpin / big-end bearing | $c_2$ | 0.04 – 0.12 | Plastigauge / feeler gauge | as per maker's manual |
| Crosshead pin | $c_3$ | 0.03 – 0.08 | Bore gauge | maker's spec |
| Piston ring gap (in cylinder) | $c_4$ | 0.5 – 1.0 | Feeler gauge | $0.005 \cdot D_{bore}$ |
| Piston ring side clearance | $c_5$ | 0.02 – 0.05 | Feeler gauge | maker's spec |
| Impeller wear ring (radial) | $c_6$ | 0.20 – 0.50 | Feeler gauge (push) | $0.20 + 0.001 \cdot D_{impeller}$ |
| Mechanical seal face wear | $c_7$ | $< 0.05$ flatness | Optical flat + dial gauge | no visible grooves |
| Shaft runout | $c_8$ | $< 0.05$ | Dial test indicator (DTI) | $\leq 0.05$ mm |
| Liner-to-screw clearance (screw pump) | $c_9$ | 0.10 – 0.30 | Feeler gauge | maker's spec |
| Timing gear backlash | $c_{10}$ | 0.10 – 0.30 | Dial gauge + feeler | maker's spec |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Reciprocating Pump Discharge

**Problem:** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm**, stroke of **300 mm**, and runs at **60 rpm**. Calculate theoretical discharge in L/s.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Piston area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^{2} = 0.01767 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Step 2 — Apply formula:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 60}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.01767 \times 0.300 = 5.301 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Step 3 — Convert to L/s:

$$
Q_{th} = 5.301 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 5.301 \; \text{L/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = 5.30 \; \text{L/s}}
$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Centrifugal Pump Head from Impeller Geometry

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump has impeller outer diameter **300 mm**, rotates at **1450 rpm**, with $\beta_2 = 90°$. Calculate the theoretical Euler head.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Tip speed:

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.300 \times 1450}{60} = 22.78 \; \text{m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Euler equation:

$$
H = \frac{u_2^{2}}{g} = \frac{(22.78)^{2}}{9.81} = \frac{518.93}{9.81}
$$

$$
\boxed{H \approx 52.90 \; \text{m of liquid}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Square the tip speed, divide by gravity, gives head in metres — units check: (m/s)² ÷ (m/s²) = m ✓.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Generic Pump Overhauling Procedure (All Three Pump Types)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Receive Pump for Overhauling] --> B1[Isolate Pump from Piping]
    B1 --> C1[Close Suction and Delivery Valves]
    C1 --> D1[Open Vent and Drain Cocks]
    D1 --> E1[Drain Pump Casing Completely]
    E1 --> F1[Decouple Motor from Pump]
    F1 --> G1[Remove Coupling Guard and Spacer]
    G1 --> H1[Mount Pump on Work Bench]
    H1 --> I1[Mark Casing Joint for Reassembly]
    I1 --> J1[Remove Casing Bolt in Cross Pattern]
    J1 --> K1[Lift Casing Cover Carefully]
    K1 --> L1[Remove Rotor Assembly]
    L1 --> M1[Identify and Catalogue Components]
    M1 --> N1[Clean All Parts with Kerosene]
    N1 --> O1[Measure All Critical Clearances]
    O1 --> P1{Parts Worn?}
    P1 -->|Yes| Q1[Mark and Replace Defective Parts]
    Q1 --> R1[Inspect Bearings and Seals]
    P1 -->|No| R1
    R1 --> S1[Inspect Valves Seats for Pitting]
    S1 --> T1[Lap Valves or Reface Seats]
    T1 --> U1[Replace Gaskets and O Rings]
    U1 --> V1[Reassemble in Reverse Order]
    V1 --> W1[Tighten Bolts to Specified Torque]
    W1 --> X1[Refit Coupling and Align]
    X1 --> Y1[Turn Rotor by Hand Check Free Rotation]
    Y1 --> Z1[Conduct Trial Run and Record Performance]
    Z1 --> AA1[Complete Overhaul Record Card]
    AA1 --> AB1[End]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Centrifugal Pump Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    INLET[Suction Inlet Flange] --> CASING[Pump Casing Volute]
    CASING --> EYE[Impeller Eye Wear Ring]
    EYE --> IMP[Impeller]
    IMP --> SHAFT[Pump Shaft]
    SHAFT --> SLEEVE[Shaft Sleeve]
    SHAFT --> SEAL[Mechanical Seal]
    SEAL --> GLAND[Stuffing Box Housing]
    GLAND --> BRG1[Rear Bearing Pedestal]
    BRG1 --> BRG2[Front Bearing Pedestal]
    BRG2 --> COUPL[Pump Coupling Half]
    COUPL --> OUTLET[Coupling to Motor]
    CASING --> OUTLET2[Delivery Outlet Flange]
    BRG1 --> LUB1[Lub Oil Reservoir]
    BRG2 --> LUB1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reciprocating Pump Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SU[Suction Inlet] --> VC[Valve Chest]
    VC --> SV[Suction Valve]
    SV --> CYL[Cylinder and Liner]
    CYL --> PIS[Piston with Rings]
    PIS --> ROD[Connecting Rod]
    ROD --> CRK[Crankshaft]
    CRK --> MAINCK[Main Bearing]
    MAINCK --> FLYW[Flywheel]
    CYL --> DV[Delivery Valve]
    DV --> VCO[Valve Chest Outlet]
    VCO --> DE[Delivery Outlet]
    PIS --> GLAND[Stuffing Box Packing]
    GLAND --> GLAND2[Gland Follower]
    ROD --> XH[Crosshead]
    XH --> XHSL[Crosshead Slide]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Screw Pump Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SU[Suction Port] --> LIN[Liner Bushing]
    LIN --> POW[Power Screw Rotor]
    LIN --> IDL[Idler Screw Rotor]
    POW --> GEAR1[Drive Timing Gear]
    IDL --> GEAR2[Idler Timing Gear]
    GEAR1 --> GEAR2
    GEAR1 --> SEAL1[Drive Shaft Mechanical Seal]
    IDL --> SEAL2[Idler Shaft Lip Seal]
    POW --> SHAFT1[Drive Shaft]
    SHAFT1 --> COUP[Drive Coupling]
    COUP --> MOT[Drive Motor]
    POW --> DE[Discharge Port]
    IDL --> DE
```

---

## 4.5 Component Location Reference Map (Text-Based Schematic)

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine the centrifugal pump from suction-end (left) to coupling-end (right):**
> `[Suction Flange] → [Casing with Volute] → [Impeller on Shaft] → [Mechanical Seal] → [Rear Bearing] → [Coupling Half] → [Drive End]`
>
> Direction of fluid flow inside the pump:
> `Suction → Eye of Impeller → Outward through Impeller Vanes → Volute Casing → Delivery Nozzle`
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define the term "overhauling" of a pump. List any four activities involved in pump overhauling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Overhauling is the scheduled process of complete disassembly, inspection, repair or replacement, cleaning, and reassembly of a pump to restore its original design performance and reliability.

**Four activities (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Dismantling the pump and removing the rotor assembly.
2. Cleaning all components with kerosene or approved solvent.
3. Measuring critical clearances (bearings, wear rings, piston rings) and comparing with maker's specifications.
4. Replacing worn bearings, seals, gaskets, and lapping valve seats before reassembly.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **What is meant by "slip" in a reciprocating pump? Why does slip occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Slip is the difference between the theoretical discharge calculated from the swept volume and the actual discharge delivered by the pump.

**Causes (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Valve lag** — finite time for suction and delivery valves to open and close causes leakage.
2. **Valve leakage** — worn valve seats or broken springs allow backflow.
3. **Fluid leakage past piston rings or packing** — worn sealing surfaces.

Slip **decreases** with increase in delivery pressure for a healthy pump.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the construction and working principle of a **centrifugal pump**. Identify all major components. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for overhauling a centrifugal pump**, listing the tools used, clearances to be measured, and acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working Principle (7 marks)

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A centrifugal pump consists of the following major components:

1. **Casing** — volute-shaped chamber that collects fluid from the impeller and converts velocity head to pressure head. May be single or double volute (for radial thrust balance on large pumps).
2. **Impeller** — rotating disc with curved vanes, of three types: **open, semi-open, enclosed** (closed). Marine centrifugal pumps typically use **enclosed (shrouded) impellers** for better efficiency.
3. **Shaft** — transmits torque from coupling to impeller; precision-ground, often made of stainless steel.
4. **Wear rings** — replaceable rings on casing and impeller that maintain a small clearance, minimizing back-leakage from delivery to suction.
5. **Bearings** — usually **rolling-element** (ball or roller) for radial support, with a separate thrust bearing for axial loads.
6. **Mechanical seal** — replaces stuffing box in modern pumps; consists of rotating face and stationary face of silicon carbide/tungsten carbide.
7. **Coupling** — flexible coupling connects pump to drive motor.

**Working Principle (3.5 marks):**
1. The motor drives the impeller via the coupling and shaft.
2. Fluid enters the **eye (suction inlet)** at the impeller centre, where a low-pressure region is created (vortex formation).
3. The impeller imparts **kinetic energy** to the fluid through its rotating vanes — fluid is flung radially outward by centrifugal force.
4. In the **volute casing**, this kinetic energy is **diffused (decelerated)** and converted to **pressure energy** (per Bernoulli's principle).
5. High-pressure fluid exits through the **delivery nozzle**.
6. The **suction-side low pressure** continuously draws more fluid into the eye, ensuring steady flow.

#### Part (b) — Overhauling Procedure (7 marks)

**Tools and Instruments Required (1 mark):**
- Spanner sets, torque wrench, bearing puller, dial test indicator (DTI), feeler gauge, bore gauge, internal micrometer, micrometer, Plastigauge, lifting tackle, kerosene tank, lint-free cloth.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Isolation:** Close suction and delivery valves; open vent and drain.
2. **Decoupling:** Remove coupling guard, mark coupling halves, decouple pump from motor.
3. **Lifting and Bench Mounting:** Lift pump to workbench using chain block.
4. **Marking:** Mark casing joint halves with file marks for reassembly alignment.
5. **Casing Removal:** Remove casing bolts in cross pattern; lift cover using lifting eye.
6. **Rotor Removal:** Withdraw rotor assembly carefully; rest on rubber-padded V-block.
7. **Cleaning:** Soak all parts in kerosene; scrub with non-metallic brush.
8. **Dismantling Bearings:** Use bearing puller — never hammer directly.
9. **Inspection:**
   - Impeller — check for erosion, vane damage, balance.
   - Shaft — measure runout with DTI on V-block (must be **< 0.05 mm**).
   - Wear rings — measure radial clearance with feeler gauge.
   - Bearings — rotate slowly; check for noise, roughness, pitting.
   - Mechanical seal faces — check under magnifying glass for grooves.
10. **Replacement:** Replace bearings, seals, gaskets, O-rings.
11. **Reassembly:** Reverse of dismantling; tighten bolts to **maker's torque** in **star pattern**.
12. **Alignment:** Re-align pump-motor coupling with dial gauge — **parallel offset < 0.05 mm**, **angular offset < 0.05 mm/100 mm**.
13. **Trial Run:** Run for **4 hours** under load, monitor temperature, vibration, leaks.

**Acceptance Criteria Table (1 mark):**

| Item | Criterion |
|---|---|
| Shaft runout | < 0.05 mm |
| Wear ring gap | per maker (typically 0.20 – 0.50 mm) |
| Bearing temperature | < 80 °C above ambient |
| Vibration | < 4.5 mm/s RMS (ISO 10816) |
| Trial run duration | ≥ 4 hours continuous |

---

> ### **Question B (Alternative — 14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a screw pump**. State two marine applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **complete overhauling procedure of a reciprocating pump**, listing parts inspected and clearances recorded. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Screw Pump (7 marks)

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A screw pump consists of:
1. **Two (or three) intermeshing helical rotors** — a **drive (power) screw** and **idler screw**, both rotating inside a close-fitting liner.
2. **Liner** — replaceable wear sleeve, often of rubber or bronze, providing the seal between screw and housing.
3. **Timing gears** — synchronise the rotation of power and idler rotors to prevent metal-to-metal contact.
4. **Bearings** — heavy-duty rolling element bearings supporting both shafts.
5. **Mechanical seal** on drive shaft.
6. **Suction and discharge ports** at opposite ends of the rotor housing.

**Working Principle (3.5 marks):**
1. Both rotors rotate in **opposite directions**, their helical flights intermeshing and forming sealed cavities.
2. As the screws rotate, fluid is **trapped between successive flights of the screws and the surrounding liner**.
3. The trapped fluid is **progressed axially** from the suction end to the discharge end — analogous to the action of nuts on a threaded bolt.
4. Because the cavities are continuous and sealed, the pump provides **smooth, pulse-free positive displacement** even for highly viscous fluids.

**Marine Applications (free 0 marks but adds quality):**
1. **Heavy fuel oil (HFO) transfer** in the engine room.
2. **Lubricating oil circulation** in the main propulsion system.

#### Part (b) — Reciprocating Pump Overhauling (7 marks)

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. Isolate pump, drain cylinder and valve chest.
2. Decouple from driver; place on bench.
3. Remove valve chest cover; lift out suction and delivery valves; tag and inspect.
4. Remove cylinder head bolts; lift cylinder head.
5. Withdraw piston complete with rod (through stuffing box side).
6. Remove connecting rod big-end cap; push rod and piston out together.
7. Remove piston rings using ring expander — never bend excessively.
8. Remove main bearing housing; lift crankshaft.
9. Clean all components in kerosene.
10. Inspect:
    - **Piston rings** — measure gap in cylinder, side clearance in groove.
    - **Cylinder liner** — bore gauge check for ovality and taper.
    - **Valve seats and discs** — check pitting, lap if necessary.
    - **Bearings** — white-metal bearings: check for cracks, lifting; replace if cavitation evident.
    - **Crankshaft** — journals measured with micrometer, taper and ovality noted.
    - **Crankpin** — measured with micrometer.
    - **Stuffing box** — packing or mechanical seal inspected.
11. Reassemble in reverse, observing torque values.
12. Adjust valve lift and spring tension per maker.
13. Trial run on test bed or in-situ.

**Clearance Table Recorded in Lab Record (2 marks):**

| Clearance | Measured | Maker Limit | OK/Not OK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piston ring gap in liner | — | $0.005 \cdot D_{bore}$ | — |
| Main bearing diametral | — | $0.001 \cdot d + 0.05$ mm | — |
| Crankpin bearing | — | per manual | — |
| Valve lift | — | per manual | — |
| Crosshead pin clearance | — | per manual | — |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Skipping clearance recording** — Examiner deducts up to **3 marks** if clearances are merely listed but not numerically measured and tabulated.
> 2. **No neat sketch** — Lab record without a labelled cross-sectional sketch loses **2 marks**. Always draw a clean labelled diagram (even a hand sketch) of the pump.
> 3. **Wrong direction of tightening** — Mention **"tightening bolts in star/criss-cross pattern"** for full credit on reassembly step.
> 4. **Ignoring safety** — Always mention **valve isolation, draining, and PPE (goggles, gloves)** in the first two lines of procedure.
> 5. **Confusing slip with negative slip** — Slip is always **positive** in healthy pumps; never define slip as a negative quantity.
> 6. **No conclusion line** — End with a **Result Statement**: *"After overhauling, the pump was reassembled and tested. All clearances were within maker's tolerance. Trial run was satisfactory."*

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam:**

- ☐ **Overhauling** = complete disassembly + inspection + replacement + reassembly.
- ☐ Three pump types covered: **Reciprocating, Centrifugal, Screw** — students may get any one.
- ☐ Always begin procedure with **isolation, draining, decouple, lift to bench**.
- ☐ Mark **casing joints with file marks** before dismantling.
- ☐ Tighten **bolts in star pattern** during reassembly.
- ☐ Record **all critical clearances** (bearing, wear ring, piston ring, shaft runout).
- ☐ Standard bearing clearance rule: $0.001 \cdot d_{shaft} + 0.05$ mm.
- ☐ Shaft runout after overhaul: **< 0.05 mm**.
- ☐ Reciprocating pump formula: $Q_{th} = A L N / 60$ (single-acting).
- ☐ Euler head for radial impeller: $H = u_2^{2}/g$.
- ☐ Screw pump: $Q = \pi/4 \cdot (D^{2} - d^{2}) \cdot L \cdot N/60$.
- ☐ Trial run duration after overhaul: minimum **4 hours** under load.
- ☐ Sketch must be **neat, labelled, and cross-sectional**.
- ☐ End lab record with a **concluding result statement**.
- ☐ Mention **safety precautions** (PPE, valve isolation, drain) at start.
- ☐ For screw pump, **timing gear backlash** must be checked — otherwise screws will clash.
- ☐ **Wear rings are sacrificial** — they wear before the casing does, protecting the casing from expensive replacement.
- ☐ **Mechanical seals** must never be run dry — even momentary dry running will score the faces.
- ☐ **Volumetric efficiency** = $Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical} \times 100$; healthy reciprocating pump: **85–98 %**.
- ☐ **Overall efficiency** = (water power)/(shaft input power) × 100.
- ☐ Always follow **maker's manual** for torque values and tolerance limits.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Overhauling of Reciprocating Pump, Centrifugal Pump, or Screw Pump**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) lab manual standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhauling of Diesel Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Overhauling of Diesel Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overhauling of a Marine Diesel Engine** is a scheduled, systematic maintenance procedure involving the complete or partial dismantling of the engine, systematic inspection of all critical components (cylinder head, pistons, liners, bearings, valves, fuel injection equipment, and crankshaft), replacement or reconditioning of worn parts to their original design specifications, and re-assembly followed by performance testing, carried out at predetermined running hours as per the manufacturer's planned maintenance schedule (PMS) and class society regulations.

In the context of KTU's Marine Engineering Lab, the term **"overhauling"** specifically refers to the *practical, hands-on procedure* of disassembling a diesel engine (typically a small/medium auxiliary diesel engine), inspecting, measuring clearances, replacing worn components, and re-assembling to working condition.

## 1.2 Types of Overhauling

Marine diesel engines are overhauled at different intervals depending on running hours, condition, and class survey requirements:

| Type of Overhaul | Typical Interval | Components Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| **Top Overhaul** | 1,000 – 3,000 hrs | Cylinder head, valves, injectors, gaskets |
| **Minor (Working) Overhaul** | 4,000 – 8,000 hrs | Above + pistons, rings, bearings |
| **Major (Full) Overhaul** | 12,000 – 24,000 hrs | Complete strip-down to crankshaft, main bearings, all components |
| **Docking Overhaul** | 2.5 – 5 years | Engine removed, complete survey, crankshaft deflection check |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of overhauling a diesel engine exactly like a **complete medical check-up combined with major surgery on the human body**. A car is driven daily without a major inspection for years, but a marine diesel engine (which can cost ₹50 crore for a large two-stroke) runs continuously for weeks, subjected to extreme heat, pressure, and salt corrosion. Just as you would dismantle, clean, inspect every organ, replace worn-out parts, and reassemble during major surgery, an engine overhaul systematically "opens up" the engine, checks the "health" of every internal part, replaces what's worn, and puts everything back precisely to factory specification — restoring the engine to "as-new" condition.

## 1.4 Why Overhauling is Mandatory

> [!CAUTION]
> - **Safety at Sea:** A marine engine failure mid-ocean can lead to catastrophic loss of vessel, cargo, and life.
> - **Class Society Compliance:** IACS members (ABS, DNV, Lloyd's, BV) require periodic surveys.
> - **Performance Restoration:** Combustion pressure, fuel efficiency, and lube oil consumption deteriorate with wear.
> - **Emission Control:** MARPOL Annex VI mandates NOx/SOx limits — achievable only with proper combustion.

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics Used in Overhauling

- **Compression pressure:** Typically **30 – 40 bar** for a marine 4-stroke auxiliary engine.
- **Cylinder liner wear limit:** Usually **0.5 – 1.0 mm** maximum permissible wear.
- **Crankshaft deflection (weardown):** Maximum **0.05 mm per meter** of shaft length.
- **Bearing clearance:** Typically **0.05 – 0.15 mm** depending on bearing diameter.
- **Piston ring gap (butt clearance):** **0.3 – 0.5 mm per 100 mm** bore diameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Overhauling — Wear Mechanisms

The diesel engine experiences several forms of wear simultaneously. The overhauling process must address each one:

### 2.1.1 Types of Wear
- **Abrasive Wear:** Hard particles (carbon, dust) scoring sliding surfaces — affects liners, bearings.
- **Adhesive Wear:** Metal-to-metal contact under load — affects bearings, piston pins.
- **Corrosive Wear:** Combustion gases (SO₂, SO₃) attack liner surfaces — forms acidic deposits.
- **Fatigue Wear:** Cyclic stress causes pitting and spalling — affects bearing shells, gear teeth.
- **Cavitation Erosion:** Collapse of vapor bubbles in coolant — pits liner outer surface.

### 2.1.2 The "How" of Wear Monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) used onboard to decide when to overhaul:**
> - **Scavenge air temperature** rising (indicates ring/bore wear).
> - **Lube oil consumption** exceeding **1.0 g/kWh** (indicates liner/ring wear).
> - **Crankcase oil mist** increasing (indicates blowby).
> - **Exhaust temperature deviation** between cylinders > **10°C**.
> - **Compression pressure drop** > **10%** of design value.

## 2.2 KTU Reference Data Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table provides the **practical reference data** used during an overhauling lab exercise. Memorise this for viva and Part A questions.

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit | Measurement Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder bore diameter | 200 – 600 | mm | Bore gauge / Internal micrometer |
| Piston-to-liner clearance | 0.5 – 1.5 | mm | Feeler / Bore gauge |
| Top ring gap (butt clearance) | 0.3 – 0.6 | mm | Feeler gauge |
| Main bearing clearance | 0.08 – 0.20 | mm | Plastigauge / DTI |
| Big-end bearing clearance | 0.05 – 0.15 | mm | Plastigauge |
| Valve stem-to-guide clearance | 0.04 – 0.10 | mm | Bore gauge |
| Valve seat angle | 45° ± 15′ | degree | Angle gauge |
| Crankshaft deflection | < 0.05 | mm/m | DTI + dial gauge |
| Cylinder head bolt torque | 200 – 800 | N·m | Calibrated torque wrench |
| Connecting bolt stretch (big end) | 0.20 – 0.30 | mm | Special micrometer |
| Liner protrusion (above block) | 0.02 – 0.10 | mm | Depth gauge |
| Coolant pressure (test) | 2 – 4 | bar | Pressure pump |

## 2.3 Clearances and the "Why" Behind Them

### 2.3.1 Piston-to-Liner Clearance
- **Too small:** Piston seizes due to thermal expansion → catastrophic failure.
- **Too large:** Combustion gases blowby past the rings → loss of compression, oil contamination.
- **Standard formula:**

$$C_{pl} = k \cdot D_b$$

where $C_{pl}$ = piston-to-liner clearance, $D_b$ = bore diameter, $k$ = coefficient (typically $0.001$ to $0.002$).

### 2.3.2 Bearing Clearance (Radial)

$$C_{br} = D \cdot c_r$$

where $C_{br}$ = radial bearing clearance, $D$ = journal diameter, $c_r$ = clearance ratio (typically $0.001$ to $0.002$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Overhauling is not just a lab exercise — it is the **backbone of marine engineering economics**. The shipping industry calculates:
>
> $$\text{Overhaul Cost} = L_{c} \times C_{s}$$
>
> where $L_c$ = engine cylinder lubrication oil cost, $C_s$ = service factor. A well-overhauled engine consumes 20–30% less fuel and 40% less lube oil, directly saving shipping companies **lakhs of dollars annually** per vessel. Students mastering this procedure are directly employable in the **$150 billion global shipping industry**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis — The Overhauling Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is the **lab manual backbone**. Read every step carefully. The procedure is divided into **six distinct phases**, each critical for KTU practical examinations.

## 3.1 Phase 1: Pre-Overhaul Preparation

### Step 1 — Safety Lockout
1. Isolate the engine: stop fuel, air, and start-air supply.
2. Lock out the turning gear; engage it.
3. Display warning boards: *"Engine under maintenance — Do not start"*.
4. Drain lubricating oil from sump, cooling water, and fuel.
5. Disconnect batteries (negative terminal first).

### Step 2 — External Cleaning
1. Wash the engine exterior with high-pressure **kerosene or alkaline degreaser**.
2. Cover the open auxiliaries (turbocharger inlet, exhaust manifold) with plastic to prevent ingress of foreign matter.

### Step 3 — External Inspection
- Record all external observations: oil leaks, cracks, corrosion.
- **Mark reference points** on mating flanges for correct reassembly.

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Skipping the marking of reference points is a common reason for assembly errors — you WILL lose marks.

## 3.2 Phase 2: Disassembly (Strip-Down)

### Step 4 — Top-End Removal
1. Remove rocker-arm cover, manifold studs, and valve-rocker assembly.
2. Open cylinder head using a **torque wrench in the reverse sequence** of tightening (criss-cross, from outside in).
3. Lift cylinder head using an overhead crane or chain pulley block.
4. Inspect head gasket — note thickness for reassembly.

### Step 5 — Valve Mechanism Disassembly
1. Use a **valve spring compressor** to remove collets, springs, and valve.
2. Place all valves in a numbered rack (in firing order) for later inspection.

### Step 6 — Cylinder Liner / Piston Assembly
1. With the engine barred to TDC, remove the connecting rod big-end cap (mark cap-to-rod orientation).
2. Push the piston out through the top of the cylinder liner.
3. For wet liners: lift out with a special lifting tool.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Trick:** "Why do we bar the engine to TDC before removing the piston?" — Answer: To protect the crankpin thread from damage and to ensure the big-end is fully accessible.

## 3.3 Phase 3: Cleaning and Identification

### Step 7 — Cleaning Procedure
- **Pistons and rings:** Soak in kerosene, then carbon-removing compound. Use a **wooden scraper** (never steel) to avoid scratching.
- **Cylinder head:** Hydro-blast or hand-scrape carbon from combustion side; clean oil galleries with **nylon brushes**.
- **Liners:** Hone if scratches are minor; replace if honing exposes deep cracks.
- **Bearings:** Wipe with lint-free cloth, **never use cotton waste** (fibres can block oil passages).

### Step 8 — Component Identification
- Tag every component with numbered tags, lay out on a clean bench in order of disassembly.

## 3.4 Phase 4: Inspection and Measurement

### Step 9 — Dimensional Inspection

For each component, record measurements in a **condition-monitoring logbook**. Key inspections:

#### (a) Cylinder Liner
- **Roundness and cylindricity** at top, middle, and bottom dead centre.
- Wear limit (perpendicular to piston pin): usually **≤ 1.0 mm** on bore.
- Visual inspection for cavitation pits, cracks.

#### (b) Piston
- **Diameter** at skirt and crown.
- **Ring groove clearance** (ring in groove — measure with feeler).
- **Piston pin bore** diameter.
- Crown inspection for cracks (use **dye-penetrant test** if available).

#### (c) Piston Rings
- **Butt gap** — measure with feeler gauge after placing ring in liner.
- **Side clearance** in groove.
- **Radial tension** using ring tension gauge.

#### (d) Crankshaft
- **Deflection (weardown)** — measure with dial indicator between journals.
- **Journal ovality and taper** — use external micrometer at two perpendicular planes.

#### (e) Bearings (Main and Big-End)
- **Crush height** (bearing protrudes above housing parting face by ~0.05 mm).
- **Backing thickness** (typically 2 – 4 mm).
- **Bond integrity** (no flaking of white metal / copper-lead).

#### (f) Valves and Seats
- **Valve stem** — check for bending, scoring.
- **Valve seat** — check for pitting, leakage (use the **"petrol check" / kerosene test**).
- **Reface** valves at 45° using a valve refacing machine.

### Step 10 — Decision: Reuse, Recondition, or Replace
A tabular decision:

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| Within wear limit, no defects | **Reuse** |
| Within limit, minor surface defects | **Recondition** (honing, grinding) |
| Beyond limit, cracks, deep scoring | **Replace** |

## 3.5 Phase 5: Reassembly

### Step 11 — Reassembly Principle
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse the disassembly procedure**, but with **additional checks** at each stage.

#### (a) Crankshaft and Main Bearings
1. Clean bearing saddles and shells with lint-free cloth.
2. Install new main bearing shells (lower shell in lower housing, upper in cap).
3. Tighten main bearing bolts to **specified torque in stages** (typically 50%, 80%, 100%) in the correct sequence.
4. Check crankshaft rotates freely by turning gear.

#### (b) Connecting Rod and Piston
1. Install new big-end shells.
2. Lightly oil the piston and rings.
3. Use a **ring compressor** to fit the piston into the liner.
4. Tighten big-end bolts to torque + check connecting rod **side play** (typically 0.10 – 0.30 mm).

#### (c) Cylinder Head
1. Fit a new head gasket (note orientation).
2. Torque head bolts in the **manufacturer's specified sequence** (centre-out, criss-cross) in **3 to 4 stages**:

$$T_{stage_1} = 0.50 \cdot T_{final}$$
$$T_{stage_2} = 0.75 \cdot T_{final}$$
$$T_{stage_3} = 1.00 \cdot T_{final}$$

3. After running, re-torque bolts at the **first scheduled maintenance** (hot torque).

#### (d) Valve Mechanism
1. Lap valves to seats using **grinding paste** (fine, then extra-fine).
2. Fit springs, retainers, and collets using valve spring compressor.
3. Adjust **valve clearance** (cold) per manufacturer — typically **0.3 – 0.5 mm** for inlet, **0.5 – 0.8 mm** for exhaust.

#### (e) Timing and Fuel Injection
1. Set the **fuel injection timing** using the barring gear and timing marks.
2. Adjust valve timing using timing gears.
3. Re-prime the lubrication system with a hand pump before first start.

## 3.6 Phase 6: Testing and Commissioning

### Step 12 — Pre-Starting Checks
- Lubricating oil pressure (engine-driven pump): **3 – 5 bar**.
- Coolant level and venting.
- Fuel system bled of air.
- Turning gear disengaged; barring gear removed.

### Step 13 — Trial Runs
- **Trial 1:** Run at no-load for 5 minutes; check for oil pressure, leaks, abnormal sounds.
- **Trial 2:** Run at 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% load progressively; record parameters.
- **Trial 3:** Check cylinder compression pressure with a **manometer** — should reach **≥ 85%** of design value.

### Step 14 — Documentation
- Log all measurements, clearances, replaced parts.
- Update the **Planned Maintenance System (PMS)** database.
- Hand over the engine to the operational watch.

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Practical Note:** Examiners expect you to maintain a **condition report** with before/after measurements. Failing to do so will result in a 5-mark deduction.

## 3.7 Summary Algorithm of the Overhauling Procedure

The complete procedure can be visualised as a logical flow:
- **Input** → Engine (degraded)
- **Stage 1** → Lockout & Cleaning
- **Stage 2** → Disassembly
- **Stage 3** → Cleaning & Identification
- **Stage 4** → Inspection & Measurement
- **Stage 5** → Decision (Reuse/Recondition/Replace)
- **Stage 6** → Reassembly with Torque Control
- **Stage 7** → Testing & Commissioning
- **Output** → Engine (restored to design condition)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Complete Overhauling Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Operating Hours Reach PMS Limit] --> B[Class Survey / Schedule Trigger]
    B --> C[Phase 1: Pre-Overhaul Preparation]
    C --> C1[Safety Lockout and Tag Out]
    C --> C2[Drain Oil Coolant Fuel]
    C --> C3[External Cleaning]
    C --> C4[Mark Reference Points]
    C1 --> D[Phase 2: Disassembly]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> D1[Remove Cylinder Head]
    D --> D2[Remove Valve Mechanism]
    D --> D3[Remove Piston and Liner]
    D --> D4[Remove Bearings if Required]
    D1 --> E[Phase 3: Cleaning]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[Carbon Removal]
    E --> E2[Oil Gallery Cleaning]
    E --> E3[Ultrasonic Cleaning of Small Parts]
    E1 --> F[Phase 4: Inspection]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Dimensional Measurement]
    F --> F2[Visual Inspection for Cracks]
    F --> F3[Non Destructive Testing if Required]
    F1 --> G{Decision Point}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G -->|Within Limit| H[Reuse]
    G -->|Minor Wear| I[Recondition]
    G -->|Beyond Limit| J[Replace]
    H --> K[Phase 5: Reassembly]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> K1[Install Bearings and Crankshaft]
    K --> K2[Install Piston with Ring Compressor]
    K --> K3[Torque Head Bolts in Sequence]
    K --> K4[Set Valve Clearance and Timing]
    K1 --> L[Phase 6: Testing]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> L1[No Load Trial Run]
    L --> L2[Progressive Load Trials]
    L --> L3[Record All Parameters]
    L1 --> M[Documentation and PMS Update]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M --> N[Engine Ready for Service]
```

```mermaid

        A1[Cylinder Head]
        A2[Inlet Valve]

    end
    subgraph MidSection[Middle Components]
```

```mermaid

    W2 --> P5[Crankshaft Journals]
    W3[Corrosive Wear] --> P6[Liners in SOx Environment]
    W3 --> P7[Cylinder Head]

```
