# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define overhauling of a marine diesel engine. List the four main types of overhauling with their typical running-hour intervals.**

**Model Answer:**
Overhauling is the systematic disassembly, inspection, repair or replacement, and re-assembly of a diesel engine to restore it to original design condition. The four main types are:

| Type | Interval |
|---|---|
| Top Overhaul | 1,000 – 3,000 hrs |
| Minor (Working) Overhaul | 4,000 – 8,000 hrs |
| Major (Full) Overhaul | 12,000 – 24,000 hrs |
| Docking Overhaul | 2.5 – 5 years |

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the importance of measuring crankshaft deflection during overhauling. What is the acceptable limit?**

**Model Answer:**
Crankshaft deflection (weardown) indicates the **alignment of bearings and any uneven wear** of the journals. Excessive deflection causes **uneven bearing loading, vibration, and ultimately fatigue failure** of the crankshaft. The acceptable limit is **0.05 mm per metre length of shaft** (about 0.10 mm on a 2-metre shaft). Measurement is taken with a precision dial indicator between main journals, with the engine on the turning gear.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat flow diagram, the **complete step-by-step procedure for overhauling a 4-stroke marine diesel engine**, highlighting safety precautions at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **inspection and measurement procedures** for the following components during overhauling, with the relevant tolerances:
   1. Cylinder liner
   2. Piston rings
   3. Main and big-end bearings
   4. Valves and seats **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure:

**Phase 1 — Pre-Overhaul Preparation (1 mark):**
- Engine must be stopped, isolated, and **locked out with warning boards**.
- Lube oil, cooling water, and fuel must be drained.
- External cleaning with kerosene/alkaline degreaser.
- Reference marks on flanges and mating surfaces.

**Phase 2 — Disassembly (2 marks):**
- Remove rocker covers, valve mechanism, and cylinder head using torque wrench in **reverse criss-cross sequence**.
- Bar the engine to TDC; remove connecting rod big-end cap; push piston out.
- Use **overhead crane / chain block** to lift cylinder head.

**Phase 3 — Cleaning and Identification (1 mark):**
- Carbon removal using kerosene and wooden scraper (never metal on aluminium pistons).
- Tag every component with **numbered tags**; lay out on a clean bench.
- Clean oil galleries with **nylon brushes**.

**Phase 4 — Inspection and Measurement (1 mark):**
- Measure every wear surface using **vernier, micrometer, bore gauge, dial indicator, plastigauge, feeler gauge**.
- Compare with manufacturer's tolerance tables.
- Decision: reuse, recondition, or replace.

**Phase 5 — Reassembly (1 mark):**
- Reverse the disassembly with new gaskets and bearings.
- Torque head bolts in 3 stages: 50% → 75% → 100% in centre-out sequence.
- Set valve clearance cold: inlet 0.3 – 0.5 mm, exhaust 0.5 – 0.8 mm.
- Prime lube oil system before first start.

**Phase 6 — Testing (1 mark):**
- Trial runs at no-load, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% load.
- Record all parameters; update PMS logbook.

**Safety Precautions (covered throughout):**
- Lockout-tagout procedure mandatory.
- Use **certified lifting gear** for head lifting.
- Wear **PPE** (gloves, helmet, safety shoes).
- Never use **cotton waste** — use lint-free cloth.
- Keep fire extinguisher nearby.

#### Part (b) — Inspection Procedures:

**1. Cylinder Liner (2 marks):**
- Measure **bore diameter** at top, middle, and bottom using a **bore gauge** in two perpendicular planes.
- Check **roundness** and **cylindricity**.
- Look for **cavitation pitting** on the outer coolant surface.
- Acceptance: wear ≤ 1.0 mm on bore.

**2. Piston Rings (2 marks):**
- **Butt gap:** Place ring in liner, measure with feeler gauge (limit: 0.3 – 0.6 mm).
- **Side clearance in groove:** Use feeler between ring and groove (limit: 0.05 – 0.10 mm).
- **Radial tension:** Use a ring tension tester.
- Visual inspection for cracks, broken lands, and loss of tension.

**3. Main and Big-End Bearings (2 marks):**
- **Backing thickness** measured with micrometer; check for flaking.
- **Crush height** verified by installing in housing and observing the **protrusion** of the bearing shell above the parting face (~0.05 mm).
- **Radial clearance** measured with **plastigauge** placed on the journal, tightened to spec, then measured on a calibration card.
- Acceptance: 0.08 – 0.20 mm for main, 0.05 – 0.15 mm for big-end.

**4. Valves and Seats (1 mark):**
- **Valve stem-to-guide clearance** with bore gauge (limit: 0.04 – 0.10 mm).
- **Seat contact pattern:** Apply a thin layer of **engineer's blue** on the seat face, lap the valve against the seat with grinding paste, and check that a continuous ring of contact appears.
- **Kerosene leakage test:** Pour kerosene above the valve, observe underside for leaks.
- Reface at 45° using a valve refacing machine; lap with fine grinding paste.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **reassembly procedure** for a marine diesel engine after a complete overhaul, focusing on:
   1. Torque control of cylinder head bolts
   2. Piston ring installation
   3. Valve clearance adjustment
   4. Crankshaft deflection check **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **safety precautions and PPE** required during overhauling. Explain the importance of **lockout-tagout (LOTO)** in marine engine rooms. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Reassembly Procedure:

**1. Torque Control of Cylinder Head Bolts (2 marks):**
Head bolts must be tightened in the **manufacturer's specified sequence** (centre-outward, criss-cross pattern) in **3 to 4 stages**:

$$T_{stage_1} = 0.50 \cdot T_{final}$$
$$T_{stage_2} = 0.75 \cdot T_{final}$$
$$T_{stage_3} = 1.00 \cdot T_{final}$$

Example: For a final torque of 400 N·m, tighten in stages of **200 N·m → 300 N·m → 400 N·m**. A **calibrated torque wrench** is essential. After the engine reaches operating temperature, a **hot re-torque** is performed at the first scheduled maintenance to account for thermal settling.

**2. Piston Ring Installation (2 marks):**
- Use a **ring expander tool** (never over-stretch rings by hand).
- Stagger ring **butt gaps 120° apart** around the piston to prevent blowby.
- Top ring is usually **chrome-plated**; second ring is **tapered or scraper**.
- Apply a thin film of **cylinder oil** before insertion.
- Use a **ring compressor** to gently push the piston into the liner, ensuring the connecting rod is aligned with the cylinder axis.

**3. Valve Clearance Adjustment (1.5 marks):**
- With the engine cold, set cylinder to **TDC firing position** for the valve being adjusted.
- Insert feeler gauge between valve stem tip and rocker arm pad.
- **Typical cold clearances:** Inlet = 0.30 – 0.50 mm; Exhaust = 0.50 – 0.80 mm (4-stroke engines).
- Lock the locknut after adjustment.
- Verify all valves per firing order: 1-3-4-2 for 4-cylinder 4-stroke.

**4. Crankshaft Deflection Check (1.5 marks):**
- Mount a **precision dial indicator** on a stable magnetic base between two main journals.
- Rotate the crankshaft slowly using the turning gear through 360°.
- Read the **maximum-minus-minimum** reading — this is the deflection.
- **Acceptance limit: 0.05 mm per metre length of shaft.**
- If exceeded, investigate main bearing alignment, bearing wear, or crankshaft fatigue.

#### Part (b) — Safety Precautions and LOTO:

**Importance of LOTO in Marine Engine Rooms (3.5 marks):**
Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) is the **single most critical safety protocol** during engine overhauling. It prevents:
- **Accidental starting** of the engine by another crew member.
- **Electrical shock** from live starters and auxiliary circuits.
- **Hydraulic/pneumatic release** causing moving parts to shift.
- **Falling objects** from overhead cranes.

**LOTO Procedure:**
1. Isolate the engine — stop fuel, air, and starting air supply.
2. Engage the **turning gear** to lock the crankshaft.
3. Apply a **padlock** to the starting air valve, fuel shut-off, and electrical isolator.
4. Attach a **red tag** with the engineer's name, date, and reason: *"Engine under maintenance — Do not operate."*
5. Try to start the engine — it must NOT respond.
6. Only the **engineer who applied the lock** may remove it.

**PPE and Personal Safety (3.5 marks):**
- **Helmet / hard hat** — falling components during head lifting.
- **Safety goggles** — carbon particles, chemicals.
- **Heat-resistant gloves** — handling warm components.
- **Steel-toed safety shoes** — falling parts.
- **Respirator / dust mask** — when carbon scraping.
- **Coveralls** — protection from oil and grease.
- **Ear protection** — during trial runs.
- **First aid kit and fire extinguisher** — within reach.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Practical & Theory Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to bar the engine to TDC** before removing the big-end — risk of thread damage. **−2 marks**
> 2. **Not staggering the piston ring gaps** — leads to excessive blowby. **−2 marks**
> 3. **Tightening head bolts in one go instead of stages** — causes head warping. **−2 marks**
> 4. **Using cotton waste to clean bearings** — fibres block oil passages. **−2 marks**
> 5. **Not recording measurements** in the condition log — examiner cannot verify. **−3 marks**
> 6. **Skipping the LOTO procedure** in the viva — automatic **−3 marks**.
> 7. **Confusing "top overhaul" with "major overhaul"** in definitions. **−1 mark**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Reading Before KTU Exam):**

- ☐ **Overhauling** = Disassembly → Cleaning → Inspection → Reassembly → Testing.
- ☐ **4 types:** Top (1,000–3,000 hrs), Minor (4,000–8,000 hrs), Major (12,000–24,000 hrs), Docking (2.5–5 yrs).
- ☐ **LOTO is non-negotiable** — engine must be locked out before any work begins.
- ☐ **Use wooden scrapers** on aluminium pistons, **nylon brushes** in oil galleries, **lint-free cloth** on bearings.
- ☐ **Never use cotton waste** anywhere near bearings.
- ☐ **Head bolt tightening sequence:** centre-out, criss-cross, in **3 stages** (50% → 75% → 100%).
- ☐ **Ring gaps staggered 120° apart.**
- ☐ **Valve clearance cold:** Inlet 0.3–0.5 mm, Exhaust 0.5–0.8 mm.
- ☐ **Crankshaft deflection limit:** 0.05 mm/m.
- ☐ **Liner wear limit:** ≤ 1.0 mm on bore.
- ☐ **Big-end bolt stretch** is also measured, not just torque, on some engines.
- ☐ **Hot re-torque** of head bolts at first maintenance after overhaul.
- ☐ **Prime the lube oil system** before first start.
- ☐ **Trial runs at progressive loads:** 0% → 25% → 50% → 75% → 100%.
- ☐ **Update PMS logbook** with all measurements and replaced parts.
- ☐ **PPE mandatory:** helmet, gloves, goggles, steel-toed shoes, coveralls.
- ☐ **PPE acronym** to memorise for viva: Helmet, Goggles, Gloves, Shoes, Coveralls, Ear-plugs, Respirator.
- ☐ **Cylinder compression pressure** after overhaul: ≥ 85% of design value.
- ☐ **Coolant pressure test** at 2–4 bar after reassembly to check for leaks.
- ☐ **Marine classification societies** (IACS) require periodic surveys — overhauling is part of class compliance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Fuel Injection Valve and Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Fuel Injection Valve and Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Injection Pump (FIP):** A high-pressure positive displacement pump, typically of the jerk pump (inline or distribution type) construction, used in diesel engines to deliver precisely metered quantities of fuel to each individual cylinder at a predetermined pressure and exact timing relative to the engine cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Injection Valve (FIV) / Nozzle:** The final pressure-tight component in the fuel injection line that contains one or more fine orifices through which the high-pressure fuel is sprayed into the combustion chamber in a finely atomized, properly distributed, and deeply penetrating spray pattern.

Together, the **FIP + FIV** assembly constitutes the heart of any marine diesel engine's combustion system, directly determining fuel economy, exhaust emissions, scavenging, and brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a doctor giving injections using a syringe and needle:
- The **plunger inside the syringe barrel** is the *pump element* — it builds up pressure.
- The **spring-loaded needle valve** at the tip is the *nozzle* — it only opens when pressure is high enough and snaps shut instantly to stop flow.
- The **delivery valve** acts like a one-way flap that prevents back-flow of fuel into the syringe after injection.

In a marine engine, the *doctor* is the **camshaft**, the *plunger* is the **pump plunger**, and the *patient* is the **combustion chamber**. The same concept, scaled up thousands of times to fire fuel at pressures of **600–1000 bar** in slow-speed two-stroke marine main engines, or **1000–2000 bar** in modern common-rail systems.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Injection Pressure (Pinj):** **400 to 1800 bar** (depending on engine type)
> - **Spray Velocity:** **100 to 250 m/s** at nozzle exit
> - **Spray Angle:** **150° to 170°** for multi-hole diesel nozzles
> - **Plunger Diameter:** typically **8 mm to 22 mm**
> - **Nozzle Needle Lift:** **0.4 mm to 0.8 mm**
> - **Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)** of droplets: **10 µm to 40 µm** for good atomization

## 1.4 Classification Overview

| Component | Type | Typical Application |
|-----------|------|---------------------|
| Jerk Pump (Inline) | Individual plunger per cylinder | Medium-speed 4-stroke marine auxiliaries |
| Distributor Pump | Single plunger, rotary | Smaller gensets |
| Common Rail Pump | Engine-driven high-pressure pump + accumulator | Modern 2-stroke MC engines (e.g., MAN B&W ME) |
| Nozzle – Multi-hole | 5 to 14 holes | Direct injection 4-stroke |
| Nozzle – Pintle | Single hole with shaped pintle | Indirect injection / pre-chamber |
| Nozzle – Pintaux | Combination | Auxiliary engines |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The fuel injection system operates in **four sequential phases** for every power stroke:

**Phase 1 — Filling (Suction Stroke of Plunger)**
- The plunger moves downward by the **return spring**.
- The spill port is uncovered by the **helical groove (spill cut)** on the plunger.
- Low-pressure fuel (≈ 3–6 bar from the booster pump) enters through the **inlet port** into the **barrel**.

**Phase 2 — Pumping (Effective Stroke)**
- Cam lifts the plunger upward.
- Spill port is covered by the top land of the plunger.
- Fuel is compressed in the **high-pressure chamber** between the plunger top and the delivery valve.
- Pressure rises rapidly until it exceeds the **nozzle opening pressure (NOP)**.

**Phase 3 — Injection**
- The nozzle needle valve lifts against its spring.
- Fuel sprays through the orifices into the combustion chamber.
- Delivery valve opens, allowing high-pressure flow.

**Phase 4 — Spill-Over (End of Injection)**
- The helical groove reaches the spill port.
- High-pressure fuel escapes back to the suction side.
- Pressure collapses instantly.
- Nozzle needle snaps shut due to spring force.
- Delivery valve spring closes the valve, creating a sudden pressure drop in the line (retraction volume effect — prevents secondary injections/dribble).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why helical groove?** To vary the effective stroke length by rotating the plunger → metering fuel quantity without changing cam profile.
> - **Why delivery valve?** Without it, residual pressure remains in the line → causes dribbling and secondary injections.
> - **Why multi-hole nozzles?** Better air-fuel mixing for direct injection → lower smoke, higher thermal efficiency.
> - **Why high injection pressure?** Smaller droplet size (SMD ∝ 1/√ΔP) → faster evaporation → complete combustion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for Fuel Injection Calculations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot L_{eff}$ | Displacement volume per stroke | $mm^3$ |
| 2 | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{4 F_{spring}}{\pi d_{needle}^2}$ | Nozzle opening pressure from spring force | bar |
| 3 | $F_{spring} = k \cdot x$ | Hooke's law for nozzle spring (k = stiffness) | N |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Mass flow rate through nozzle hole | kg/s |
| 5 | $v_{spray} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Theoretical spray velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $SMD \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter inversely proportional to pressure | µm |
| 7 | $L_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Plunger stroke}}{2} \pm \Delta_{rot}$ | Effective stroke from plunger rotation | mm |
| 8 | $q_{cyc} = \dfrac{N_e \cdot b_e}{60 \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot z}$ | Fuel quantity per cycle per cylinder | mm³/stroke |
| 9 | $BP = \dfrac{P_{max} \cdot \pi d_p^2}{4}$ | Peak (peak firing) pressure on plunger | N |
| 10 | $\eta_{vol,pump} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{displacement}} \times 100$ | Volumetric efficiency of pump | % |

Where:
- $d_p$ = plunger diameter, $L_{eff}$ = effective stroke length
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/mm), $x$ = spring compression
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge ≈ 0.6–0.85 for sharp-edged orifices
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across nozzle
- $\rho$ = density of diesel fuel ≈ **840 kg/m³** at 15 °C
- $N_e$ = brake power, $b_e$ = BSFC, $n_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders, $z$ = strokes

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In shipboard practice, marine engineers must:
- Test **nozzle opening pressure** using a nozzle tester (pop tester).
- Inspect **spray pattern** visually for symmetry, angle, and atomization.
- Measure **back-leak** from the nozzle to assess needle-seat wear.
- Use **calibrated test oil** of known viscosity to validate pump output (mm³/stroke).
- For slow-speed MAN B&W / Wärtsilä engines, the modern **electronic common-rail** system replaces the traditional jerk pump with solenoid or servo-operated fuel valves — but the underlying hydraulic principles remain the same.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP)

The nozzle needle is held against its seat by a calibrated helical spring. When the high-pressure fuel acting on the needle differential area exceeds the spring preload, the needle lifts and injection begins.

$$
P_{NOP} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{needle,net}}
$$

$$
A_{needle,net} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (d_{needle}^2 - d_{pin}^2)
$$

$$
P_{NOP} = \dfrac{4 \cdot F_{spring}}{\pi \cdot (d_{needle}^2 - d_{pin}^2)}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example** (KTU-style):
> A marine nozzle has needle diameter $d_{needle} = 6$ mm, pin diameter $d_{pin} = 4$ mm, and spring preload force $F_{spring} = 150$ N. Determine the nozzle opening pressure.

$$
A_{needle,net} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.006^2 - 0.004^2)
$$

$$
A_{needle,net} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (36 - 16) \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
A_{needle,net} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-6} = 15.708 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2
$$

$$
P_{NOP} = \dfrac{150}{15.708 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.549 \times 10^{6} \, Pa \approx 95.5 \, bar
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real marine engines, NOP is typically **175 to 350 bar**, hence the spring force is much larger than this academic example.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Pump Stroke and Fuel Quantity

The actual quantity of fuel delivered per stroke depends on the **effective stroke** — the portion of the upward plunger travel during which the spill port is closed.

$$
q_{fuel} = A_{plunger} \cdot L_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_p^2 \cdot L_{eff}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
> A jerk pump has plunger diameter $d_p = 12$ mm and an effective stroke of $L_{eff} = 8$ mm. The engine runs at 600 rpm, 6 cylinders, 4-stroke, with BSFC = 210 g/kWh. Brake power = 1500 kW.

$$
q_{cycle} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.012)^2 \cdot 0.008 = 9.05 \times 10^{-7} \, m^3 = 905 \, mm^3
$$

Converting to mm³/stroke basis:

$$
q_{cyc} = \dfrac{N_e \cdot b_e}{60 \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot z}
$$

Since 4-stroke: $z = 2$ (working strokes per revolution)

Number of injections per second per cylinder:

$$
n_{inj} = \dfrac{rpm}{2 \times 60} = \dfrac{600}{120} = 5 \, inj/s
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.210}{3600} = 0.0875 \, kg/s
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{cyl} = \dfrac{0.0875}{6} = 0.01458 \, kg/s
$$

$$
q_{cyl,sec} = \dfrac{0.01458}{840} = 1.736 \times 10^{-5} \, m^3/s
$$

$$
q_{cyc} = \dfrac{1.736 \times 10^{-5}}{5} = 3.47 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3 = 3470 \, mm^3
$$

**Comparison:** The calculated theoretical $905 \, mm^3$ is less than required $3470 \, mm^3$, indicating the example plunger needs a longer effective stroke or larger diameter — useful for **matching pump to engine**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spray Velocity at Nozzle Exit

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the high-pressure side (pressure = $P_{inj}$) and the discharge side (combustion chamber pressure = $P_{cyl}$):

$$
P_{inj} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_{cyl} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2
$$

Assuming inlet velocity $v_1 \approx 0$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{spray}^2 = P_{inj} - P_{cyl}
$$

$$
v_{spray} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (P_{inj} - P_{cyl})}{\rho}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
> $P_{inj} = 600$ bar = $60 \times 10^6$ Pa, $P_{cyl} = 80$ bar = $8 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\rho = 840 \, kg/m^3$.

$$
v_{spray} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times (60 - 8) \times 10^6}{840}}
$$

$$
v_{spray} = \sqrt{\dfrac{104 \times 10^6}{840}} = \sqrt{1.238 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
v_{spray} \approx 351.9 \, m/s
$$

This matches the **100–400 m/s** range found in marine diesel nozzles — confirming realistic conditions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mass Flow Rate Through Nozzle Hole

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}
$$

For a nozzle with $n$ holes, each of diameter $d_h$:

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = n \cdot C_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_h^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}
$$

**Example:**
> 6 holes, $d_h = 0.3$ mm = $3 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $C_d = 0.75$, $\Delta P = 52$ MPa.

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = 6 \times 0.75 \times \dfrac{\pi (3 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 840 \times 52 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = 6 \times 0.75 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-8} \times \sqrt{8.736 \times 10^{10}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = 3.181 \times 10^{-7} \times 295.55 \approx 9.40 \times 10^{-5} \, kg/s \approx 0.094 \, g/s
$$

Over an injection duration of say **8 ms**:

$$
m_{inj} = 0.094 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 7.52 \times 10^{-7} \, kg
$$

Multiplied by injection events per second gives the per-cylinder fuel delivery.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Marine) | Effect of Increase |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Injection Pressure | $P_{inj}$ | 400–1800 bar | Smaller droplets, better atomization, higher penetration |
| Effective Stroke | $L_{eff}$ | 4–12 mm | More fuel per cycle, higher load capability |
| Nozzle Hole Diameter | $d_h$ | 0.2–0.5 mm | Higher flow rate, coarser spray |
| No. of Holes | $n$ | 4–14 | Better spray distribution |
| Spring Stiffness | $k$ | 30–80 N/mm | Higher NOP |
| Needle Lift | $h_{lift}$ | 0.4–0.8 mm | Greater flow area |
| Plunger Diameter | $d_p$ | 8–22 mm | Higher displacement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Jerk Pump Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cam Shaft Rotation] --> B[Cam Lobe Lifts Tappet]
    B --> C[Plunger Moves Upward in Barrel]
    C --> D{Spill Port Closed?}
    D -- No --> E[Fuel Remains at Low Pressure - Filling Phase]
    D -- Yes --> F[High Pressure Builds in Barrel Chamber]
    F --> G[Pressure Exceeds Delivery Valve Setting]
    G --> H[Delivery Valve Opens]
    H --> I[Fuel Flows Through High Pressure Pipe]
    I --> J{Nozzle Pressure Exceeds NOP?}
    J -- No --> K[Nozzle Needle Remains Seated]
    J -- Yes --> L[Nozzle Needle Lifts Against Spring]
    L --> M[Fuel Sprays Through Orifices]
    M --> N[Atomized Fuel Enters Combustion Chamber]
    N --> O[Helical Groove Reaches Spill Port]
    O --> P[High Pressure Collapses]
    P --> Q[Needle Spring Closes Nozzle]
    Q --> R[Delivery Valve Spring Closes Delivery Valve]
    R --> S[Plunger Returns Down - New Cycle Begins]
    E --> S
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Component Map — Fuel Injection Nozzle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_PRESSURE_LINE [High Pressure Fuel Inlet]
        P1[Pressure Pipe Connection]
    end
    subgraph NOZZLE_BODY [Nozzle Body and Holder]
        P1 --> P2[Filter Screen]
        P2 --> P3[Nozzle Holder Spring Chamber]
        P3 --> P4[Pressure Chamber]
        P4 --> P5[Needle Valve Differential Area]
        P5 --> P6[Needle Seat and Orifice Disc]
    end
    subgraph SPRAY_REGION [Spray Formation]
        P6 --> P7[Multiple Spray Jets]
        P7 --> P8[Combustion Chamber Air Charge]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL [Spring Loading]
        P9[Adjustment Screw] --> P3
        P10[Lock Nut] --> P9
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Pump Nozzle System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT [Engine Inputs]
        A1[Camshaft Profile] --> A2[Cam Lift Curve]
        A3[Governor / Rack Position] --> A4[Plunger Rotation Control]
        A2 --> A5[Pump Element]
        A4 --> A5
    end
    subgraph PUMP [Fuel Injection Pump]
        A5 --> B1[Plunger Barrel Assembly]
        B1 --> B2[Delivery Valve Assembly]
        B2 --> B3[High Pressure Pipe]
    end
    subgraph NOZZLE [Fuel Injection Valve]
        B3 --> C1[Nozzle Holder]
        C1 --> C2[Nozzle Needle and Spring]
        C2 --> C3[Nozzle Tip with Holes]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [Combustion Process]
        C3 --> D1[Spray Pattern Formation]
        D1 --> D2[Air Fuel Mixing]
        D2 --> D3[Ignition and Combustion]
    end
    subgraph FEEDBACK [Monitoring]
        D3 --> E1[Pressure Indicator]
        D3 --> E2[Exhaust Temperature]
        D3 --> E3[Fuel Consumption]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define nozzle opening pressure (NOP). State its significance in marine diesel engines.*

**Model Answer:**
Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP) is the minimum fuel pressure at the nozzle inlet that overcomes the needle valve spring preload and lifts the needle off its seat, initiating injection. NOP is significant because it:
- Determines the timing and sharpness of injection start.
- Affects atomization quality — higher NOP produces finer spray.
- Ensures proper atomization before the rise in combustion chamber pressure.
- Prevents dribble and secondary injections.
- Typical marine engine NOP ranges from **175 to 350 bar**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]** *What is the function of the helical groove on a jerk pump plunger?*

**Model Answer:**
The helical groove (also called the spill cut or top land groove) machined on the upper portion of the plunger serves two main functions:
1. **Metering** — by rotating the plunger via the control rack, the effective stroke length (and hence fuel quantity) is varied.
2. **Spill-over** — when the groove reaches the spill port, the high-pressure fuel is released back to the suction side, abruptly ending injection and preventing dribble.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *(a) With a neat sketch, describe the working of a multi-hole fuel injection valve.* (7 Marks)
*(b) Calculate the nozzle opening pressure of a marine diesel nozzle having a needle diameter of 8 mm, pin diameter of 5 mm, and a spring preload of 600 N. Also calculate the mass flow rate per hole if injection pressure is 500 bar, cylinder pressure is 70 bar, hole diameter is 0.35 mm, coefficient of discharge = 0.78, and fuel density = 850 kg/m³.* (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Working of a Multi-Hole Fuel Injection Valve (7 Marks)**

A multi-hole nozzle consists of:
1. **Nozzle body (nozzle holder)** — contains the spring, pressure chamber, and threaded connection to the high-pressure pipe.
2. **Nozzle needle** — precision-ground needle that mates with the conical seat.
3. **Needle spring** — calibrated helical spring pressing the needle onto its seat.
4. **Adjustment screw and lock nut** — to set NOP.
5. **Nozzle tip** — hardened steel disc with multiple precision orifices (typically 5–14 holes).

**Working sequence:**
- High-pressure fuel enters through the inlet to the pressure chamber around the needle differential area.
- When fuel pressure exceeds spring preload, the needle lifts by 0.4–0.8 mm.
- Fuel jets through all orifices simultaneously, forming a finely atomized cone-shaped spray.
- When pressure collapses (spill-over at pump), the spring pushes the needle back onto the seat, ending injection sharply.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Given:
- Needle diameter $d_{needle} = 8$ mm
- Pin diameter $d_{pin} = 5$ mm
- Spring preload $F_{spring} = 600$ N
- $P_{inj} = 500$ bar, $P_{cyl} = 70$ bar
- Hole diameter $d_h = 0.35$ mm
- $C_d = 0.78$, $\rho = 850$ kg/m³

**Step 1:** Net needle area:

$$
A_{net} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.008^2 - 0.005^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 39 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
A_{net} = 3.063 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2
$$

**Step 2:** Nozzle opening pressure:

$$
P_{NOP} = \dfrac{600}{3.063 \times 10^{-5}} = 19.59 \times 10^{6} \, Pa = 195.9 \, bar
$$

**Step 3:** Pressure differential across hole:

$$
\Delta P = 500 - 70 = 430 \, bar = 43 \times 10^{6} \, Pa
$$

**Step 4:** Mass flow rate per hole:

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} = 0.78 \times \dfrac{\pi (0.35 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 850 \times 43 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} = 0.78 \times 9.621 \times 10^{-8} \times \sqrt{7.31 \times 10^{10}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} = 7.505 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.704 \times 10^{5} = 0.0203 \, kg/s
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} \approx 20.3 \, g/s
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *(a) Explain with a block diagram the working of a jerk-type fuel injection pump.* (7 Marks)
*(b) A 4-stroke, 6-cylinder marine diesel engine develops 1200 kW at 720 rpm with a BSFC of 215 g/kWh. The jerk pump has a plunger diameter of 14 mm. Calculate the effective stroke length of the plunger.* (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Working of Jerk-Type Fuel Injection Pump (7 Marks)**

A jerk pump is a single-plunger, constant-stroke pump where fuel quantity is varied by rotating the plunger (changing effective stroke) rather than by varying the cam profile.

**Main components:**
- Cam, tappet, plunger, barrel, delivery valve, return spring, control rack.

**Working phases** (describe as in Section 2.1):
1. **Filling stroke** — plunger moves down, fuel enters barrel through inlet port.
2. **Pre-compression** — plunger moves up, port closes, fuel compresses.
3. **Effective stroke** — high pressure develops, delivery valve opens, fuel is forced to nozzle.
4. **Spill-over** — helical groove uncovers spill port → pressure collapses → delivery valve closes → nozzle snaps shut.

Block diagram covered in Section 4.1.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Given:
- 4-stroke, 6 cylinders, $N_e = 1200$ kW, $n = 720$ rpm
- $b_e = 215$ g/kWh, $d_p = 14$ mm

**Step 1:** Total fuel consumption:

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = \dfrac{N_e \cdot b_e}{3600} = \dfrac{1200 \times 215}{3600} = 71.67 \times 10^{-3} \, kg/s
$$

**Step 2:** Per cylinder (4-stroke → 1 injection per 2 revolutions):

$$
n_{inj} = \dfrac{720}{2 \times 60} = 6 \, inj/s
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{cyl} = \dfrac{71.67 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 11.945 \times 10^{-3} \, kg/s
$$

$$
q_{cyl} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{cyl}}{\rho \cdot n_{inj}} = \dfrac{11.945 \times 10^{-3}}{850 \times 6} = 2.342 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3/cycle
$$

Converting to mm³:

$$
q_{cyl} = 2.342 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^9 = 2342 \, mm^3/cycle
$$

**Step 3:** Plunger area:

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.014)^2 = 1.539 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2 = 153.94 \, mm^2
$$

**Step 4:** Effective stroke:

$$
L_{eff} = \dfrac{q_{cyl}}{A_p} = \dfrac{2342}{153.94} = 15.21 \, mm
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> - **Forgetting the pin diameter** when computing needle differential area → wrong NOP by ~30%.
> - **Using full plunger stroke** instead of effective stroke → overestimates fuel quantity.
> - **Mixing units** — convert bar to Pa (multiply by 10⁵) consistently.
> - **Skipping fuel density** in flow calculations — examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> - **Ignoring pressure differential** — using $P_{inj}$ instead of $\Delta P = P_{inj} - P_{cyl}$ in Bernoulli.
> - **Not mentioning the function of delivery valve** — a 2-mark item often missed in part (a) sketch explanations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Jerk pump** = constant stroke, variable effective stroke via plunger rotation.
> - **Spill port + helical groove** = metering + sharp cut-off mechanism.
> - **NOP** = spring force / net needle area = **175–350 bar** (typical marine).
> - **Delivery valve** prevents dribble and secondary injections.
> - **Multi-hole nozzle** → direct injection, better atomization for large chambers.
> - **Pintle nozzle** → indirect / pre-chamber engines, single-hole shaped spray.
> - **Higher injection pressure** → smaller SMD → better combustion → less smoke, lower BSFC.
> - **Spray velocity** $v = \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ ≈ 100–400 m/s at nozzle exit.
> - **Plunger diameter** typically 8–22 mm; effective stroke 4–15 mm.
> - **Test procedures**: spray pattern test, nozzle popping test, back-leak test, pump calibration on test bench.
> - **Modern common-rail** (e.g., MAN B&W ME-C, Wärtsilä X) uses accumulators but injectors follow identical hydraulic principles.
> - Always use $\rho_{diesel} \approx 840$ kg/m³ at 15 °C unless stated otherwise.
> - Always convert bar → Pa by multiplying by $10^5$ in SI equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Various Types of Filters and Incinerator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Various Types of Filters and Incinerator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Marine Filters

A **marine filter** is a mechanical or media-based purification device installed at critical points in a ship's fluid-handling systems (seawater, fuel oil, lubricating oil, bilge water, and air) to remove suspended solids, water, sludge, and other contaminants that would otherwise damage machinery, reduce efficiency, or violate environmental regulations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Marine filters are classified according to the fluid they process (fuel, lube, seawater, air, bilge) and the cleaning mechanism (mechanical straining, centrifugal separation, coalescence, magnetic attraction, or adsorption). Their selection is dictated by ISO 4406 cleanliness codes, viscosity of the medium, and the sensitivity of downstream equipment.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**

Imagine drinking tea through a tea strainer — the strainer holds back the tea leaves while letting the liquid pass. A marine filter does the same job, but for ship machinery:

- The **liquid** = fuel oil, lube oil, seawater, or air.
- The **"leaves"** = dirt particles, water droplets, metal wear debris, rust, and micro-organisms.
- The **"tea strainer"** = the filter element (mesh, cartridge, magnetic core, or coalescer pad).

If the strainer is missing or clogged, the "tea" (engine) gets choked, overheats, or wears out prematurely.

## 1.2 Definition of a Marine Incinerator

A **marine incinerator** is a self-contained waste-disposal unit that burns shipboard waste — including sludge from fuel/lube oil purifiers, bilge residues, garbage, and used oil — at high temperatures (typically **850 °C to 1,200 °C**) to reduce its volume by approximately 95–97 % and render it environmentally harmless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL Annex V & VI Compliance:** Under IMO MARPOL regulations, ships are prohibited from discharging certain types of waste into the sea. The incinerator is therefore **mandatory** on most oceangoing vessels (those over 400 GT) to legally dispose of ship-generated waste.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**

Think of a marine incinerator as a **hospital autoclave for ship waste** — it sterilises, shrinks, and converts hazardous, smelly sludge into a small pile of sterile ash. Just as a hospital cannot dump bio-waste in the bin, a ship cannot dump sludge overboard; both institutions need a controlled high-temperature destruction unit.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Highlighted)

| Parameter | Typical Shipboard Value |
|---|---|
| Filtration fineness (HFO service) | **10 µm – 25 µm** |
| Filtration fineness (MGO service) | **5 µm – 10 µm** |
| Lube oil cleanliness target | **ISO 4406 : 18/16/13** |
| Incinerator combustion temperature | **850 °C – 1,200 °C** |
| Volume reduction by incineration | **95 % – 97 %** |
| IMO required burn time | **≥ 2 seconds at 850 °C** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Filters

Marine filters are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

### 2.1.1 By Service Fluid

1. **Seawater Filters / Strainers** – fitted on cooling-water lines, fire pumps, and desalination plant intakes.
2. **Fuel Oil Filters** – protect boilers, main engines, and generators from particulates in HFO/MDO/MGO.
3. **Lubricating Oil Filters** – safeguard bearings, camshafts, and turbochargers.
4. **Air Filters** – protect turbochargers and compressed-air systems.
5. **Bilge Water Filters** – part of OWS (Oil-Water Separator) systems.

### 2.1.2 By Cleaning Mechanism

1. **Surface-type (Strainer)** – particles collected on the surface of a perforated plate or wire mesh.
2. **Depth-type (Cartridge)** – particles trapped within a porous medium.
3. **Centrifugal type** – uses rotational acceleration (purifier/clarifier — borderline filter).
4. **Magnetic type** – captures ferrous debris.
5. **Coalescer type** – merges tiny water droplets into larger ones for separation.
6. **Adsorbent type** – activated clay removes acids and polar compounds.

### 2.1.3 By Operation Mode

- **Simplex** (single element — must be stopped for cleaning)
- **Duplex** (two elements with changeover valve — cleaning online)
- **Auto-Backwash** (self-cleaning by reverse flow — no shutdown needed)

## 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Filter Type

### 2.2.1 Simplex Basket Strainer (Seawater Line)

A cylindrical body houses a perforated basket. Flow enters radially, passes through the basket, and exits axially. The basket is manually cleaned during port stays.

### 2.2.2 Duplex Filter (Fuel Oil)

Two identical chambers, each with its own filter element, are connected via a **changeover cock or valve**. When one chamber fouls, flow is switched to the second, and the dirty one is cleaned.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Tip:** Most HFO service lines use a **duplex filter of 60 µm to 100 µm fineness** as a *guard filter* (pre-filter), with a **fine filter of 10–25 µm** downstream before the engine.

### 2.2.3 Auto-Backwash Filter (Fuel Oil & Cooling)

A self-cleaning strainer that uses the pressure differential (ΔP) across the element to trigger a reverse-flow cleaning cycle. Used extensively on HFO service to handle high dirt-holding capacity.

### 2.2.4 Centrifugal Purifier (Lube & Fuel Oil)

Although technically a *separator*, it functions as a **pre-filter**. Rotational speeds of **6,000 – 9,000 rpm** generate a centrifugal force of 5,000–8,000 g, expelling water and sludge radially.

### 2.2.5 Edge-Type Filter (Lube Oil)

A stack of thin metal discs separated by spacer rings. Oil flows radially between the discs; particles larger than the spacer thickness are trapped at the disc edges. Self-cleaning by rotation of the disc stack against fixed scrapers.

### 2.2.6 Magnetic Filter

Permanent magnets or electromagnets capture ferrous wear particles — common on cylinder lube oil lines of large two-stroke engines.

### 2.2.7 Coalescer / Separator (Bilge & Fuel)

Uses **oleophilic / hydrophobic media** (e.g., PP fibres) to merge fine water droplets in fuel or to coalesce oil droplets in bilge water for OWS compliance (15 ppm discharge limit).

### 2.2.8 Air Intake Filter

Dry or oil-bath type — prevents dust, salt spray, and insects from entering turbochargers and engines.

## 2.3 Marine Incinerator — Types and Components

### 2.3.1 Types of Marine Incinerators

| Type | Heat Source | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Exhaust Gas Economiser (EGE) type** | Boiler exhaust (~300–400 °C) | Continuous sludge burning |
| **Auxiliary Boiler type** | Direct firing from boiler | Mixed waste |
| **Standalone Oil-Fired** | Dedicated burner | Most common — modern ships |
| **Dual Chamber (Sludge + Solid)** | Diesel + burner | Large vessels |
| **Hybrid (electrical pre-heat + oil)** | Electric + oil burner | Cold start assistance |

### 2.3.2 Main Components

- **Sludge Tank** (heater-stirred at **60–80 °C** to keep sludge pumpable).
- **Sludge Pump** (screw or piston type).
- **Burner Assembly** (diesel/HFO with ignition transformer).
- **Combustion Chamber** (refractory-lined).
- **Secondary Chamber** (post-combustion for gases, ≥ 850 °C, ≥ 2 s).
- **Flue Gas Fan / Induced Draft (ID) Fan**.
- **Stack** (with spark arrestor).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE — MEMORISE FOR KTU ESE**

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \,\mu \, L \, v}{d^{2}}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop in a clean porous element | Pa |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{\text{dirty}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}} \, (1 + \beta \, W)$ | Linear fouling model — $\beta$ = cake resistance coeff., $W$ = dirt mass per area | Pa |
| 3 | $E = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{upstream}}}{N_{\text{downstream}}}\right) \times 100$ | Filter efficiency based on particle count | % |
| 4 | $N_{\text{ISO}} = \log_{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{dirt}}}{1\,\text{mL}}\right) \text{ at 4 µm, 6 µm, 14 µm}$ | ISO 4406 cleanliness code | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{\text{red}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{ash}}}{V_{\text{sludge}}}\right)\times 100$ | Volume reduction in incinerator (typically 95–97 %) | % |
| 6 | $AFR_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{2\,(x + y/2 + z)}{x + y/4 - z/2}$ for $C_xH_yO_z$ | Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio | kg air / kg fuel |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{comb}} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{LHV}$ | Heat release rate in combustion chamber | kW |
| 8 | $A_{\text{filter}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{\,v\,} = \dfrac{\dot{V}\, \mu}{k \,\Delta P}$ | Filter area for required flow | m² |
| 9 | $g = \dfrac{\omega^{2}\,r}{9.81} \;\;[g\text{-force}]$ | Centrifugal acceleration in purifiers | g |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{thermal,inc}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{waste}}\,\text{LHV}_{\text{waste}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}\,\text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}} + \dot{m}_{\text{waste}}\,\text{LHV}_{\text{waste}}}\times 100$ | Thermal efficiency of waste-burning | % |

> **Memorise these variables:** $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $L$ = element depth (m), $v$ = face velocity (m/s), $d$ = pore diameter (m), $\beta$ = cake resistance coefficient (m/kg), $W$ = dirt loading (kg/m²), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = bowl radius (m).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Filter beta-ratio ($\beta_x$)** is the industry standard for filter rating — a $\beta_{10} = 200$ means 199 out of every 200 particles ≥ 10 µm are removed.
- **Incinerators** are governed by **MARPOL Annex VI** and **Resolution MEPC.244(66)** for NOx/SOx of flue gases.
- Modern **hybrid incinerators** integrate economiser sections, recovering waste heat to pre-heat the sludge — improving overall **waste-heat recovery** by **8–12 %**.
- Centrifugal purifiers + filters together achieve **two-stage contamination control** — bulk separation first, polishing filtration second.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hagen–Poiseuille Pressure Drop Across a Filter Element

A filter element can be modelled as a bundle of parallel capillary tubes of equivalent diameter $d$, length $L$, with $n$ tubes per unit area.

**Step 1 — Velocity in a single capillary:**

For fully-developed laminar flow through a single tube, the volumetric flow rate per tube by Hagen–Poiseuille is:

$$q = \frac{\pi \, d^{4}}{128 \, \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Total flow through the element:**

If the element has $N$ such parallel capillaries, the total volumetric flow is:

$$\dot{V} = N \cdot q = N \cdot \frac{\pi \, d^{4}}{128 \, \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Express $N$ via porosity $\varepsilon$ and area $A$:**

For a filter element of cross-sectional area $A$ and porosity $\varepsilon$ (fraction of void volume):

$$N = \frac{\varepsilon \, A}{\frac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}} = \frac{4 \, \varepsilon \, A}{\pi \, d^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into $\dot{V}$:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{4\,\varepsilon\,A}{\pi\,d^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{128\,\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\varepsilon\,A\,d^{2}}{32\,\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\Delta P$:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{32\,\mu\,L\,\dot{V}}{\varepsilon\,A\,d^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{32\,\mu\,L\,v}{d^{2}} \quad \text{where } v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{\varepsilon\,A}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** The pressure drop increases linearly with viscosity and length but **inversely with the square of pore diameter** — a halving of pore size quadruples the pressure drop. This is why KTU problems on filter sizing must consider both viscosity (temperature-dependent!) and pore size.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Filter Pressure Drop

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A marine lube oil filter element has pore diameter $d = 25 \,\mu\text{m}$, element depth $L = 0.010\,\text{m}$, oil viscosity $\mu = 0.045\,\text{Pa·s}$, and the oil flows at face velocity $v = 0.005\,\text{m/s}$. Compute the clean-element pressure drop.

**Step 1 — Convert pore diameter to SI:**

$$d = 25 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille derived equation:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{32\,\mu\,L\,v}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \times 0.045 \times 0.010 \times 0.005}{(25\times10^{-6})^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$32 \times 0.045 \times 0.010 \times 0.005 = 7.20 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$(25\times10^{-6})^{2} = 625 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^{2} = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{7.20 \times 10^{-5}}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.152 \times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} = 1.152\;\text{bar}$$

> **KTU Practical Insight:** A clean element shows ~1 bar drop. When fouled, $\Delta P$ may rise to **2.5–3.0 bar**, which is the typical alarm setpoint for filter changeout.

## 3.3 Derivation: Linear Fouling Model

A filter accumulates dirt over time. Assuming the cake adds resistance in series with the clean element:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}} + \Delta P_{\text{cake}}$$

**Step 1 — Cake resistance per unit area** (Kozeny–Carman style):

$$\Delta P_{\text{cake}} = \beta \cdot W \cdot \mu \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Substituting and grouping:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}} + \beta \cdot W \cdot \mu \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Factoring $\Delta P_{\text{clean}}$ (which is itself proportional to $\mu v$):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}}\left(1 + \beta\,W\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}}\,(1 + \beta\,W)\;}$$

**Step 4 — Time to blockage (linear model):**

If dirt deposits at a constant rate $\dot{W}$ (kg/m²·s):

$$t_{\text{blockage}} = \frac{W_{\max}}{\dot{W}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{alarm}}/\Delta P_{\text{clean}} - 1}{\beta\,\dot{W}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Incinerator Volume Reduction

> **Problem:**
> An incinerator receives $V_{\text{sludge}} = 800$ litres/day of oily sludge. After combustion, the residual ash volume is $V_{\text{ash}} = 24$ litres. Calculate the volume reduction ratio.

**Step 1 — Apply the volume reduction formula:**

$$V_{\text{red}} = \left(1 - \frac{V_{\text{ash}}}{V_{\text{sludge}}}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$V_{\text{red}} = \left(1 - \frac{24}{800}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.030) \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$\boxed{\;V_{\text{red}} = 97.0\,\%\;}$$

This falls within the **typical 95–97 % range** mandated by KTU coursework.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Filter Selection Decision Logic

| Service | Recommended Filter Type | Typical Fineness | ΔP Alarm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea-water cooling (main) | Basket simplex strainer | **1–2 mm** | 0.3 bar |
| Sea-water cooling (engine) | Duplex strainer | **0.5–1 mm** | 0.5 bar |
| HFO supply (booster) | Duplex + auto-backwash | **60 µm** | 1.5 bar |
| HFO to engine | Fine duplex cartridge | **10–25 µm** | 2.5 bar |
| MGO/MDO | Cartridge | **5–10 µm** | 1.5 bar |
| Lube oil (main engine) | Centrifugal + edge filter | **20–35 µm** | 1.0 bar |
| Cylinder lube (2-stroke) | Magnetic + edge | **34–50 µm** | 0.7 bar |
| Compressed air intake | Dry pleated / oil-bath | **5–10 µm** | 0.1 bar |
| Bilge water (OWS) | Coalescer + adsorbent | **15 ppm outlet** | 0.6 bar |

## 4.4 Filter-Element Cut-Away Description (Text Schematic)

```
  ┌──────────────────────────┐  ← End Cap (perforated)
  │ ▼ Radial flow outward    │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐    │
  │  │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │ ← Pleated Filter Media (depth-type)
  │  │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │    │   Surface area typically 0.5–3.0 m²
  │  │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │    │   Pore size 5–25 µm
  │  └──────────────────┘    │
  │ ▲ Radial flow outward    │
  ├──────────────────────────┤
  │  Perforated Inner Core   │  ← Steel or plastic core
  └──────────────────────────┘
          ▲ Dirty inlet
          ▼ Clean outlet
```

The fluid enters from the centre (or outside), passes **radially** through the pleated media, and exits through the perforated end caps. Pleating increases the **effective filtration area by 4–6×** compared to a flat element of the same envelope size.
