# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*List any six different types of filters used in marine engineering and state the service in which each is employed.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Simplex basket strainer** — used on seawater cooling lines.
2. **Duplex filter** — used on HFO supply lines to engines.
3. **Auto-backwash filter** — used on HFO service where continuous cleaning is needed.
4. **Centrifugal purifier/clarifier** — used on lube oil and fuel oil for bulk separation.
5. **Edge-type filter** — used on lubricating oil of crosshead engines.
6. **Coalescer filter** — used in bilge water OWS (oil-water separator).
7. (Bonus) **Magnetic filter** — used on cylinder-lube lines of large 2-stroke engines.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*State any three advantages of an incinerator on board a ship and mention the MARPOL regulations that govern its operation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Volume reduction** of sludge and waste by **95–97 %**, freeing storage space.
2. **Environmental compliance** with **MARPOL Annex V** (garbage) and **Annex VI** (air pollution).
3. **Self-sufficient waste disposal** during long voyages, eliminating reliance on port reception facilities.

**Governing regulations:**
- **MARPOL Annex V** – prohibits discharge of certain wastes at sea.
- **MARPOL Annex VI / Resolution MEPC.244(66)** – sets emission limits for SOx, NOx, and particulate matter from incinerator flue gases.
- **IMO Resolution MEPC.76(40)** – design and operation criteria for shipboard incinerators.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part-B question of 14 marks carries **internal choice**. Two fully independent alternative questions are given below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **duplex fuel oil filter** used in marine HFO service. Explain how the changeover from one chamber to another is carried out.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A marine HFO filter element has pore diameter $d = 30\;\mu\text{m}$, depth $L = 0.012\;\text{m}$, and HFO viscosity at service temperature $\mu = 0.060\;\text{Pa·s}$. The face velocity is $v = 0.004\;\text{m/s}$. Compute the **clean-element pressure drop**. If the cake resistance coefficient is $\beta = 5.0 \times 10^{6}\;\text{m/kg}$ and dirt loading is $W = 4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg/m}^{2}$, find the **fouled pressure drop**.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Construction:**
A duplex HFO filter consists of **two identical filter chambers** (A and B) connected in parallel to a common inlet and outlet manifold, with a **changeover cock or three-way valve** (often lever-operated on deck) on the inlet and outlet.

Each chamber houses a **fine filter element** (stainless-steel pleated cartridge, typical fineness **10–25 µm**), an **inlet pressure gauge**, an **outlet pressure gauge**, and a **drain valve** for air-bleeding and sludge draining.

**Working Principle:**
HFO is pumped from the service tank by a booster pump. Flow enters the inlet manifold. When cock A is open, the fuel passes through element A; dirt is retained on the element surface and clean fuel exits to the engine. Simultaneously, chamber B is isolated and can be cleaned.

**Changeover Procedure:**
1. **Open** the outlet cock of chamber B (new, clean chamber).
2. **Close** the outlet cock of chamber A (fouled chamber).
3. **Open** the inlet cock of chamber B.
4. **Close** the inlet cock of chamber A.
5. **Drain** and clean the fouled element A; refit and keep it ready as standby.

Pressure gauges on both sides of each element allow the engineer to compute the **differential pressure** and decide on changeover — typically when **ΔP exceeds 2.5 bar**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute clean-element ΔP using the derived formula:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{clean}} = \frac{32\,\mu\,L\,v}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (note unit conversions):**

- $d = 30\times10^{-6}\;\text{m}$, so $d^{2} = 9.0\times10^{-10}\;\text{m}^{2}$
- $\mu = 0.060\;\text{Pa·s}$, $L = 0.012\;\text{m}$, $v = 0.004\;\text{m/s}$

$$\Delta P_{\text{clean}} = \frac{32 \times 0.060 \times 0.012 \times 0.004}{9.0\times10^{-10}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$32 \times 0.060 \times 0.012 \times 0.004 = 9.216\times10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{clean}} = \frac{9.216\times10^{-5}}{9.0\times10^{-10}} = 1.024\times10^{5}\;\text{Pa} = 1.024\;\text{bar}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the linear fouling model:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{fouled}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}} \cdot (1 + \beta W)$$

**Step 6 — Compute the dimensionless term $\beta W$:**

$$\beta W = (5.0\times10^{6}) \times (4\times10^{-4}) = 2000$$

**Step 7 — Compute the fouled ΔP:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{fouled}} = 1.024\;\text{bar} \times (1 + 2000) = 1.024 \times 2001 = 2{,}049\;\text{bar}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Many students wrongly assume $\beta W$ is the *increase* rather than the *total multiplier*. The correct interpretation is $\Delta P_{\text{fouled}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}} \cdot (1 + \beta W)$, so for large $\beta W$ the fouled drop is dominated by the cake. **Memorise the $(1 + \beta W)$ factor and the unit of $\beta$ (m/kg).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> Students often forget to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ for $d$. A $d = 30\,\mu m$ in a $d^{2}$ denominator without conversion gives a $\Delta P$ off by a factor of $10^{12}$. **Always convert to SI units first.**

> **Result:** $\Delta P_{\text{clean}} \approx 1.02\;\text{bar}$ and $\Delta P_{\text{fouled}} \approx 2{,}049\;\text{bar}$ (the very high fouled value indicates the filter is well past the alarm threshold and the changeover procedure must be executed).

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **construction, working, and types** of marine incinerators with a labelled schematic. Explain the **pre-heating procedure** before burning oily sludge.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ship's incinerator handles **$V_{\text{sludge}} = 1200$ litres/day** of sludge. Combustion produces **$V_{\text{ash}} = 30$ litres/day**. The sludge calorific value is $\text{LHV}_{\text{sludge}} = 14\,\text{MJ/kg}$, density $\rho_{\text{sludge}} = 950\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. The auxiliary burner uses $\dot{m}_{\text{burner}} = 6\,\text{kg/h}$ of diesel (LHV = $42.5\;\text{MJ/kg}$).
Calculate:
1. The **volume reduction ratio** in %.
2. The **thermal contribution** of the sludge (kW).
3. The **incinerator thermal efficiency** $\eta_{\text{thermal}}$ in %.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Construction:**
A marine incinerator is a **double-chamber refractory-lined unit**. The **primary chamber** burns liquid sludge; the **secondary chamber** ensures complete combustion of gases and particulates. An **ID (induced-draft) fan** pulls flue gases through the chambers and discharges them via the **stack**. A **sludge tank** with **steam heating coils** and **mechanical stirrers** keeps sludge pumpable at **60–80 °C**.

**Working:**
- **Sludge pump** (screw/piston) injects sludge into the primary chamber through a **burner nozzle**.
- **Forced-draft fan** supplies combustion air.
- **Diesel pilot burner** atomises and ignites the sludge.
- Combustion gases flow into the **secondary chamber at 1,100 °C** and dwell for **≥ 2 seconds** to fully oxidise CO, hydrocarbons, and odours.
- **Flue gas** passes through an **economiser** (recovers heat to pre-heat sludge) and exits via the **stack**.

**Types (with key application):**

| Type | Heat Source | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Exhaust Gas Economiser (EGE) | Boiler exhaust | Continuous sludge disposal |
| Auxiliary-Boiler Firing | Boiler | Older ships |
| Standalone Oil-Fired | Dedicated burner | Most modern ships |
| Dual-Chamber | Diesel + oil | Large vessels (LNG, VLCC) |
| Hybrid (electric + oil) | Electric heater + oil | Cold-start assistance |

**Pre-heating Procedure for Oily Sludge:**
1. **Fill the sludge tank** from purifier discharge.
2. **Open the steam valve** to the heating coils.
3. **Start the mechanical stirrer** to prevent oil/water stratification.
4. **Heat to 60–80 °C** for at least 1 hour until sludge is homogeneous and pumpable.
5. **Start the ID fan** and verify draught on the stack gauge.
6. **Light the diesel pilot** and confirm stable flame in the primary chamber.
7. **Open the sludge pump** slowly; confirm atomisation through the nozzle.
8. **Monitor thermocouples** in both chambers — secondary chamber temperature must reach **≥ 850 °C** before waste burning continues.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Volume reduction ratio:**

$$V_{\text{red}} = \left(1 - \frac{V_{\text{ash}}}{V_{\text{sludge}}}\right)\times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{30}{1200}\right)\times 100$$

$$V_{\text{red}} = (1 - 0.025) \times 100 = 97.5\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Thermal contribution of sludge:**

- Mass flow of sludge: $\dot{m}_{\text{sludge}} = 1200\,\text{L/day} = 1.2\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{day}$
- Daily mass: $1.2 \times 950 = 1{,}140\;\text{kg/day}$
- Per second: $\dot{m}_{\text{sludge}} = 1{,}140 / 86{,}400 = 0.01319\;\text{kg/s}$

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{sludge}} = \dot{m}_{\text{sludge}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{sludge}} = 0.01319 \times 14{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{sludge}} = 184.7\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{sludge}}\,\text{LHV}_{\text{sludge}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{sludge}}\,\text{LHV}_{\text{sludge}} + \dot{m}_{\text{burner}}\,\text{LHV}_{\text{burner}}} \times 100$$

- Burner power: $\dot{m}_{\text{burner}} \times \text{LHV} = (6/3600) \times 42{,}500 = 0.001667 \times 42{,}500 = 70.83\;\text{kW}$

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{184.7}{184.7 + 70.83} \times 100 = \frac{184.7}{255.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 72.27\,\%\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout #3:**
> A common mistake is to compute the **efficiency as sludge-energy ÷ burner-energy** instead of the **correct denominator: total input energy = sludge + burner energy**. The sludge's own calorific value is *recovered* energy, not a *cost* — so the denominator must include both. Another error: using the **wrong time unit** (day vs second) for mass-flow. Always convert to **per-second** before multiplying by LHV in kJ/kg to get kW.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Exam**

- **Filters are classified by service fluid**, **cleaning mechanism**, and **operation mode** (simplex / duplex / auto-backwash).
- **Hagen–Poiseuille model** governs pressure drop in a clean element: $\Delta P = 32\,\mu\,L\,v / d^{2}$.
- **Linear fouling model:** $\Delta P_{\text{fouled}} = \Delta P_{\text{clean}}\,(1 + \beta W)$.
- **Filter beta ratio $\beta_x$** is the KTU/industry standard for efficiency rating.
- **ISO 4406** cleanliness code: three numbers (4 µm / 6 µm / 14 µm) — the **lower the better**.
- **Duplex filter changeover** sequence: open new inlet → close old inlet → open new outlet → close old outlet → drain & clean old chamber.
- **Centrifugal purifier** delivers **5,000–8,000 g** of separating force and handles **bulk** separation.
- **Edge-type filter** uses thin metal discs and self-cleans by rotation against scrapers.
- **Magnetic filter** traps **ferrous** wear debris in cylinder-lube circuits.
- **Coalescer** in OWS: inlet oil ≤ **15 ppm** at outlet (MARPOL limit).
- **Marine incinerator types:** EGE, auxiliary-boiler, standalone oil-fired, dual-chamber, hybrid.
- **Mandatory IMO conditions** for combustion: **≥ 850 °C** for **≥ 2 seconds** in the secondary chamber.
- **MARPOL Annex V** governs garbage and sludge disposal; **Annex VI** governs flue-gas emissions.
- **Volume reduction** by incineration: **95–97 %** — the ash-to-sludge ratio formula is $V_{\text{red}} = (1 - V_{\text{ash}}/V_{\text{sludge}})\times 100$.
- **Sludge pre-heating** to **60–80 °C** with steam coil + stirrer is essential for atomisation.
- **Pleating** increases filter area by **4–6×** over flat elements.
- **Always convert $\mu m$ → $m$** before plugging into a $d^{2}$ denominator.
- **Always convert L/day → kg/s** before multiplying by LHV (kJ/kg) to get kW.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** confusing $\beta$ in **filter beta ratio** with $\beta$ in **cake-resistance coefficient** — same symbol, different contexts!
- **Auto-backwash** triggers cleaning when **ΔP** exceeds a pre-set value (~0.5–1.0 bar) and continues for a few minutes.
- **Stack spark arrestor** is mandatory to prevent hot-particle emissions — required by SOLAS and Flag State.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Cold Starting Techniques of Diesel Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL607 - MARINE ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study of Cold Starting Techniques of Diesel Engines # 🔬 Study of Cold Starting Techniques of Diesel Engines ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal KTU Academic Definition **Cold Starting** of a diesel engine refers to the procedure of initiating combustion in the cylinders when the engine is at ambient (or near-ambient) temperature, without the assistance of normal operating thermal conditions. In marine engineering practice, **cold starting** is formally defined as the ability of a diesel engine to fire and run on its own power cycle from a fully cooled state, typically below **30 °C**, within the prescribed crank cycle count (usually 3 consecutive start attempts under **30 seconds** total as per IACS M43 / SOLAS standards). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCNSL607 – Module 2):** > A marine engineer must demonstrate the ability to identify, describe, and practically operate the **auxiliary systems** that enable a large, slow-speed, two-stroke marine diesel engine — or a medium-speed auxiliary diesel generator — to start from cold conditions using the **main starting air system**, **air distribution system**, and supplementary **warming-up devices**. ### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine waking up a **deeply sleeping giant** on a cold winter morning. The giant (the diesel engine) has thick, cold "blood" (lubricating oil that is highly viscous at low temperatures) and "lungs" (combustion air) that are dense and reluctant to mix with the cold "fuel." If you just shout "Start!" (push the starting button), the giant will groan, shake, but not rise. You therefore need three helpers to assist the giant: 1. **The Pusher (Compressed Air)** – a powerful blast that physically rotates the crankshaft. 2. **The Warm Blanket (Coolant / Lub. Oil Heaters)** – thins the oil so it can flow and warms the cylinders. 3. **The Spark in the Dark (Pilot Fuel / Glow Plug / Decompression)** – a small "nudge" of heat or auxiliary fuel to ignite the main charge, since a cold cylinder cannot reliably self-ignite fuel at low compression temperatures. Together, these three helpers constitute the **Cold Starting Techniques** studied in this experiment. ### 📏 Important Physical Constants & Standards - **Minimum starting air pressure** (typical 2-stroke marine): **30 bar (3.0 MPa)** - **Minimum starting air pressure** (typical 4-stroke auxiliary): **15–20 bar** - **Standard ambient reference temperature for "cold start" tests:** **0 °C and 30 °C** (per ISO 3046-1 and IACS M43) - **Cranking viscosity limit (lub oil):** **350 cSt at 40 °C** maximum at start - **Main starting air reservoir capacity:** Engine must complete **12 consecutive starts** without recharging - **Minimum starting air alarm:** **18 bar** (for 30 bar systems) > [!NOTE] > The **Compression Ignition Principle** requires the in-cylinder temperature to exceed the **Cetane-based auto-ignition temperature** (typically **210–250 °C** for marine residual fuels, and **250–300 °C** for low-cetane heavy fuels). When the engine is cold, compression alone may fail to reach this threshold — hence the need for cold starting aids. --- ## 2. Why "Cold" is a Problem — The Physical Reality | ❄️ Cold Condition | ⚠️ Effect on Engine Starting | |---|---| | Lubricating oil is **highly viscous (e.g., 1500 cSt @ 0 °C)** | High cranking resistance, poor film formation, bearing metal-to-metal contact | | Combustion chamber walls are **cold** | Heat loss during compression prevents reaching auto-ignition temperature | | Fuel (HFO) is **solid / semi-solid at < 40 °C** | Injectors choke, fuel atomization is poor | | Cylinder compression ratio is **temporarily effective-loss** | Cold clearances reduce actual compression | | Battery capacity drops **~20% at 0 °C** | Electric starting becomes unreliable | | Air receiver pressure may drop due to **temperature-density** effects | Reduced cranking torque | | Seals, O-rings, and gaskets are **stiff and leak-prone** | Pressure integrity compromised | # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔍 Classification of Cold Starting Techniques Cold starting methods for diesel engines are systematically classified under two main categories: ### A. Primary Starting Methods (Mandatory) > These provide the **rotational kinetic energy (cranking)** to the crankshaft. #### 1. Compressed Air Starting (Marine Standard) - **Used in:** All large 2-stroke main engines and most 4-stroke auxiliary engines - **Energy source:** Pneumatic energy stored at **30 bar (g)** in air receivers - **Working principle:** High-pressure air admitted through **starting valves** into cylinders during the **starting stroke (typically 5°–35° after TDC for 2-stroke)** pushes the piston down, rotating the crankshaft. #### 2. Electric Starting - **Used in:** Small auxiliary generators, emergency generators, lifeboat engines - **Energy source:** Lead-acid or Ni-Cd batteries at **24 V DC or 110 V DC** - **Components:** Starter motor, solenoid, battery bank, charging alternator #### 3. Hydraulic Starting - **Used in:** Special applications (submarines, some offshore engines) - **Energy source:** Pressurized hydraulic oil at **200–300 bar** - **Working principle:** Hydraulic motor drives the pinion which engages flywheel ring gear #### 4. Manual / Spring Starting - **Used in:** Very small engines (outboards, lifeboat engines) - **Methods:** Recoil starter, hand crank, spring-loaded starter --- ### B. Supplementary / Auxiliary Cold-Start Aids > These improve **combustion initiation** when the engine is cold. | S/N | Technique | Principle | Typical Application | |----|-----------|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Pre-heating of combustion air** | Electric/gas heater raises air temperature | Auxiliary gensets | | 2 | **Coolant pre-heating** | Keeps engine at 40–60 °C standby | All modern main engines | | 3 | **Lub oil pre-heater** | Reduces viscosity to < 350 cSt | All main & auxiliary engines | | 4 | **Fuel pre-heater (HFO)** | Lowers viscosity to 12–15 cSt | All 2-stroke main engines | | 5 | **Pilot injection of auxiliary fuel** | Injects small high-cetane fuel charge | Difficult start scenarios | | 6 | **Decompression starting** | Holds exhaust valves open, motor rotates freely, then closes | MAN B&W large 2-strokes | | 7 | **Flame / glow plug in inlet manifold** | Heats incoming air to 60–80 °C | Small/medium 4-strokes | | 8 | **Ether / Starting fluid injection** | Volatile fluid ignites easily, aids first cycle | Stationary engines | | 9 | **Auxiliary blower / turbocharger pre-lubrication** | Provides pre-warmed air charge | All turbocharged engines | --- ### C. Modern Electronic & Automated Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern **MAN B&W ME-C** and **Wärtsilä X-DF** engines integrate an **Engine Control System (ECS)** that automatically sequences: pre-lubrication → pre-heating → air admission → fuel injection ramping. This is a KTU 2024 Scheme high-priority topic for descriptive answers. --- ## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Description | Standard Value / Unit | |---|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------------| | 1 | $T_c = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$ | Adiabatic compression temperature rise | $T_c \geq 550\,^\circ C$ required for HFO | | 2 | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Geometric compression ratio | 2-stroke marine: **15:1 – 22:1** | | 3 | $P_{start} = P_{receiver} \cdot \eta_{valve}$ | Effective cylinder starting pressure | $\eta_{valve} \approx 0.85$ | | 4 | $V_{receiver} = \dfrac{n \cdot V_{cyl} \cdot P_{start}}{P_{receiver}}$ | Air receiver sizing | 12 starts minimum | | 5 | $\tau_{crank} = \dfrac{P_{crank} \cdot A \cdot r_{arm}}{2\pi}$ | Cranking torque | 0.6–1.0 × normal torque | | 6 | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = h_c \cdot A_w \cdot (T_g - T_w)$ | Heat loss to cold walls | $T_w$ = wall temperature | | 7 | $CET \approx 0.83 \cdot CN + 210$ | Empirical Cetane–Auto-ignition | $CET$ in °C, $CN$ = Cetane Number | | 8 | $W_{air} = n_{start} \cdot V_{cyl} \cdot P_{atm}$ | Work of compression per cycle | J/cycle | | 9 | $\nu_{oil} \propto e^{E/RT}$ | Viscosity–temperature (Arrhenius) | Lub oil flow characterization | | 10 | $T_{final} = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Final temperature after compression | $P_2$ = effective compression pressure | > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation. All equations above are **dimensionally consistent** and use **SI base units** as required by the KTU 2024 scheme paper-writing standards. --- ### 🔬 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry / Sector | Application of Cold Start Knowledge | |-------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Merchant Shipping** | SOLAS-mandated blackout-start capability of emergency gensets | | **Naval Vessels** | Rapid-start diesels for combat readiness (must start in < 30 s) | | **Arctic / Polar Class Vessels** | Extreme cold start at **−40 °C** using combined pre-heat + electric + air | | **Power Generation** | DG sets must start within 10 s of grid failure | | **Locomotive (Diesel-Electric)** | Cold start in −30 °C environments in Russia, Canada | | **Mining & Construction** | High-altitude cold-start where air density is low | | **Submarines** | Snorkel-depth cold restart of main propulsion diesels | # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Adiabatic Compression Temperature (The Core of Diesel Cold Start) > [!NOTE] > The diesel engine depends entirely on **adiabatic compression** to raise the in-cylinder air temperature above the fuel's **Cetane-based auto-ignition temperature (CET)**. When the cylinder walls are cold, heat escapes via conduction, reducing the actual $T_2$ achieved. Let us derive the ideal $T_2$ and then the actual $T_2$ with wall heat losses. ### Step 1 — Ideal Gas Law (Initial State) The air-fuel charge (before compression) is at intake conditions. $$P_1 \cdot V_1 = m \cdot R \cdot T_1$$ Where: - $P_1$ = Intake pressure ≈ **1.0 bar (abs)** - $V_1$ = Volume at BDC (Bottom Dead Center) = $V_s + V_c$ - $T_1$ = Intake temperature (cold start ambient, e.g., **30 °C = 303 K**) - $m$ = mass of air - $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/kg·K** ### Step 2 — Adiabatic Process Equation (Reversible) For a perfectly reversible adiabatic process: $$P_1 \cdot V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{\gamma}$$ This rearranges to: $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma} = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$$ ### Step 3 — Temperature Rise Equation By combining the adiabatic relation with the ideal gas law: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$ Substituting the compression ratio form: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation (Example for a Marine 2-Stroke) Let us evaluate for a typical MAN B&W 2-stroke engine: - Compression ratio: $r = 16$ - $\gamma = 1.4$ (air, constant specific heats) - $T_1 = 303\,K$ (cold ambient at 30 °C) $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 &= 303 \cdot (16)^{1.4 - 1} \\[4pt] &= 303 \cdot (16)^{0.4} \\[4pt] &= 303 \cdot 2.2974 \\[4pt] &= 696.1\,K \\[4pt] &= 423.0\,^\circ C \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Under ideal adiabatic compression, the air temperature would reach **423 °C**, which is well above the CET of marine diesel fuel (~250 °C). So why does the engine sometimes fail to start cold? ### Step 5 — Real Engine with Cold Wall Heat Loss The actual cycle experiences heat loss to cold cylinder walls. The corrected model is: $$T_{2,actual} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} - \Delta T_{wall}$$ Where the wall heat loss is: $$\Delta T_{wall} = \dfrac{h_c \cdot A_w \cdot (T_g - T_w)}{m \cdot c_v \cdot n}$$ Using typical values: - $h_c = 200\,W/m^2K$ (forced convection coefficient) - $A_w = 0.05\,m^2$ (effective wall area) - $T_g = 696\,K$, $T_w = 303\,K$ (cold) - $m \cdot c_v = 0.0035 \cdot 717 = 2.51\,J/K$ - $n = 1$ (one compression) $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_{wall} &= \dfrac{200 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (696 - 303)}{2.51 \cdot 1} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{10 \cdot 393}{2.51} \\[4pt] &= 1565.7\,K \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **hypothetically large** loss because the model is simplified. In reality, the loss is partial and time-dependent, but the *trend* is clear: cold walls **steal 30–50% of the available temperature rise** during the first 2–3 cranking strokes. This is why **pre-heating** of cylinder walls is a KTU high-priority topic. A more realistic empirical correction is: $$T_{2,actual} \approx \eta_{th} \cdot T_{2,ideal}$$ Where $\eta_{th} \approx 0.65$ to $0.85$ for warm engines and only **0.45 to 0.60** for cold engines. --- ## Derivation 2: Cranking Torque from Compressed Air A marine 2-stroke receives starting air at pressure $P_s$ into cylinder of bore $D$. The torque on the crankshaft is: $$\tau = \dfrac{P_s \cdot A_p \cdot r_{arm}}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m$$ For a multi-cylinder engine, the total cranking torque is the sum of individual cylinder contributions at the crank angle where starting air acts: $$\tau_{crank,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{cy}} \dfrac{P_{s,i} \cdot A_p \cdot r_{arm}}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m$$ Where: - $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ = piston area - $r_{arm}$ = crank radius = stroke/2 - $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency of starting gear (~0.85) - $N_{cy}$ = number of cylinders simultaneously receiving starting air **Numerical example:** For a 6-cylinder engine with $D = 0.5\,m$, stroke $L = 2.0\,m$, $P_s = 30 \cdot 10^5\,Pa$, and 2 cylinders receiving air at any instant: $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963\,m^2 \\[4pt] r_{arm} &= 1.0\,m \\[4pt] \tau_{crank,total} &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{30 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.1963 \cdot 1.0}{2\pi} \cdot 0.85 \\[4pt] &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{5.889 \times 10^5}{6.283} \ \cdot 0.85 \\[4pt] &= 2 \cdot 93742 \cdot 0.85 \\[4pt] &= 159362\,N \cdot m \end{aligned} $$ This is sufficient to overcome static friction and rotate a multi-tonne crankshaft from rest. --- ## Derivation 3: Air Receiver Sizing A marine starting air system must provide **12 consecutive starts** without compressor recharge (per SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 44 / IACS UR M43): $$V_{receiver} = \dfrac{n_{starts} \cdot V_{cyl} \cdot N_{cy} \cdot P_{start}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$$ For an 8-cylinder engine with: - $V_{cyl} = 0.5\,m^3$ (swept volume per cylinder) - $N_{cy} = 8$ - $P_{max} = 30\,bar$, $P_{min} = 18\,bar$ (cut-out differential) - $n_{starts} = 12$ $$ \begin{aligned} V_{receiver} &= \dfrac{12 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 8 \cdot 30}{30 - 18} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1440}{12} \\[4pt] &= 120\,m^3 \end{aligned} $$ This is typically split into **2–3 air bottles** of equal volume for redundancy. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: Block Diagram — Marine Diesel Cold Start System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB cA[Compressed Air Supply
Main Air Compressor 30 bar] cB[Air Treatment Unit
Cooler Drier Filter] cC[Starting Air Receiver
120 m3 Capacity 30 bar] cD[Starting Air Reducing Valve
30 to 15 bar for Aux] cE[Main Engine Starting Air
Distributor Block] cF[Starting Air Valves
Per Cylinder] cG[Main Engine Cylinders
2 Stroke Diesel] cH[Pre Heater Circuit
Coolant Lub Oil Fuel] cI[Auxiliary Blower
Pre Lubrication] cJ[Engine Control System
ECS PLC] cK[Starting Air Manifold
Common Rail] cL[Emergency Generator
Independent Air System] cA --> cB cB --> cC cC --> cD cC --> cE cE --> cF cF --> cG cD --> cK cK --> cL cH --> cG cI --> cG cJ --> cE cJ --> cH cJ --> cI cJ --> cL ``` **Reading the Diagram:** Compressed air from the **Main Air Compressor** is treated (cooled, dried, filtered) and stored in the **Starting Air Receiver** at 30 bar. From there, it branches into two streams: 1. **High-pressure stream (30 bar)** → directly feeds the main engine starting valves. 2. **Reduced-pressure stream (15 bar via reducing valve)** → feeds the auxiliary engines and emergency generator. The **Engine Control System (ECS)** orchestrates the **Pre-Heater Circuit**, the **Auxiliary Blower**, and the starting valve actuation in a sequenced manner. --- ## Diagram 2: Decompression Starting Sequence (Logic Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR a1[Operator Presses
Start Button] --> a2{Engine State
Cold or Warm} a2 -->|Cold| a3[Pre Lubrication
30 sec] a3 --> a4[Pre Heating
Coolant Oil ON] a4 --> a5[ECS Confirms
Readiness] a5 --> a6[Decompression
Exhaust Valves Held Open] a6 --> a7[Air Motor
Rotates Crankshaft
No Compression] a7 --> a8[After 2 Revolutions
Close Exhaust Valves] a8 --> a9[Normal Air Admission
at 30 bar] a9 --> a10[Fuel Injection
Begins] a10 --> a11{Engine Fires?} a11 -->|Yes| a12[Engine Running
Release Air] a11 -->|No after 3 tries| a13[Lockout Alarm
Investigate] a2 -->|Warm| a14[Direct Air Admission] a14 --> a10 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** The decompression method is used by large MAN B&W 2-stroke engines to overcome the high compression resistance when cold. The exhaust valves are held open by hydraulic pressure for the first 2 revolutions, allowing the air motor to spin the engine with virtually no compression load. Once the engine is rotating and the bearings are hydrodynamically lubricated, the exhaust valves close, and normal starting air is admitted. --- ## Diagram 3: Cold Start Decision Tree (Student Quick-Reference) ```mermaid flowchart TB b1[Diesel Engine
Cold Start Required] --> b2{Cylinder Size
and Type} b2 -->|Small 4 Stroke
below 500 kW| b3[Glow Plug
Plus Electric Start] b2 -->|Medium 4 Stroke
500 to 3000 kW| b4[Compressed Air
Plus Coolant Heater] b2 -->|Large 2 Stroke
Main Propulsion| b5[Compressed Air 30 bar
Plus Decompression
Plus Pre Heating] b3 --> b6[Fuel Cetane
Adequate] b4 --> b7[Air Pressure
Adequate] b5 --> b8[Lub Oil Viscosity
below 350 cSt] b6 -->|No| b9[Use Ether
or Pilot Fuel] b7 -->|No| b10[Check Compressor
and Receiver] b8 -->|No| b11[Activate
Lub Oil Heater] ``` --- ## Diagram 4: Pilot Fuel Cold-Start System ```mermaid flowchart TB p1[Pilot Fuel Tank
High Cetane Diesel] --> p2[Pilot Fuel Pump
Electric] p2 --> p3[Pilot Fuel Solenoid
Controlled by ECS] p3 --> p4[Injection Nozzle
in Inlet Manifold] p4 --> p5[Combustion Chamber
Hot Spot Created] p5 --> p6[Main Fuel Injector
Fires Successfully] p6 --> p7[Engine Running] p7 --> p8[ECS Closes
Pilot Solenoid] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** In this KTU-relevant cold-start method, a small quantity of **high-cetane pilot fuel** (CN > 55) is injected into the inlet manifold just before cranking. Because of its low auto-ignition temperature, it ignites easily, creating a localized hot spot that helps ignite the main fuel charge. The pilot system is **automatically disengaged** once the engine is running normally. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **Q:** Define cold starting of a diesel engine. State any two cold-starting techniques used in marine diesel engines. **Model Answer:** > **Cold starting** of a diesel engine is the process of initiating combustion and achieving self-sustained firing of the engine when it is in a thermally cool state, typically when the engine temperature is at or below ambient (≤ 30 °C), without the assistance of normal operating thermal conditions. During cold starting, the engine must overcome high lubricant viscosity, poor fuel atomization, and inadequate compression-induced temperature rise. > > **Two cold-starting techniques used in marine diesel engines are:** > 1. **Compressed air starting with cylinder pre-heating** — High-pressure air (30 bar) from main air receivers is admitted through starting valves while the cylinder liners, jackets, and lubricating oil are kept warm using electric/steam heaters. > 2. **Decompression starting** — The exhaust valves are held open for the first 2 crank revolutions using hydraulic pressure, allowing the air motor to rotate the engine with negligible compression load. The exhaust valves then close, and normal starting air is admitted. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **Q:** Why is a glow plug or pilot fuel injection used in cold-starting small/medium diesel engines? **Model Answer:** > A glow plug or pilot fuel injection is used because, in a cold engine, the cylinder walls absorb a significant portion of the heat generated during the compression stroke, preventing the in-cylinder air temperature from reaching the **auto-ignition temperature (CET)** of the main fuel (typically 250–300 °C for marine fuels). A glow plug is an electrically heated element fitted in the pre-chamber or swirl chamber of indirect-injection engines; it raises the local air temperature to 800–1000 °C, ensuring reliable ignition of the injected fuel. > > Similarly, **pilot fuel injection** delivers a small charge of high-cetane-number auxiliary fuel (CN > 55) into the inlet manifold, which has a much lower ignition temperature and ignites easily, creating a hot spot that ignites the main fuel charge. Both methods are **automatically disengaged** once the engine achieves normal combustion. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]** **Q:** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the **compressed air starting system** of a large marine two-stroke diesel engine. Discuss the components, working principle, and the safety devices incorporated in the system. Also explain how this system enables cold starting of the engine. #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Components and Working Principle of the Compressed Air Starting System** **Model Solution:** The compressed air starting system of a large marine two-stroke diesel engine consists of the following major components: **1. Main Air Compressor (Electric or Main Engine Driven)** - Typically a two-stage, water-cooled reciprocating compressor. - Delivers air at **30 bar (g)** discharge pressure. - Capacity sized to refill the air receivers within **1 hour** from minimum pressure. **2. Air Treatment Train** - **After-cooler:** Reduces air temperature to within 10 °C of cooling water temperature. - **Moisture separator:** Removes condensed water. - **Air dryer (desiccant or refrigerated type):** Reduces dew point to **−40 °C** to prevent moisture in starting valves. - **Fine filter (5 micron):** Removes oil aerosols and particulates. **3. Main Starting Air Receiver (Bottle)** - Capacity: **0.5–3 m³ per cylinder** of the engine. - Working pressure: **30 bar**, test pressure: **45 bar**. - Equipped with **safety relief valve**, **pressure gauge**, **drain valve**, and **manhole**. **4. Starting Air Reducing Station** - Reduces pressure from 30 bar to 7–10 bar for auxiliary engine starting. **5. Main Starting Air Pipe & Distributor** - Common rail runs the length of the engine. - Distributor block directs air to the correct cylinder at the correct timing. **6. Starting Air Valve (Per Cylinder) — Pneumatic or Hydraulic Actuated** - Admit air into the cylinder during the starting stroke (just before TDC, opens ~5° ATDC, closes ~35° ATDC on the downward power stroke). - Held closed by a powerful **disc spring** during normal running. **7. Starting Air Master Valve** - Manually or pneumatically operated **quick-closing valve** at the receiver outlet. - Used for emergency shutdown of starting air. **8. Safety & Control Devices** - Pressure relief valve (set at **33 bar**) - Bursting disc (set at **36 bar**) - Pressure transmitter for low-pressure alarm at **18 bar** and engine block at **15 bar** - Flame arrestor at receiver inlet - Drain traps with automatic blowdown **Working Principle:** 1. The engine is rotated by the **air motor** (when fitted) or directly by starting air. 2. The ECS signals the starting air valve of a specific cylinder to open. 3. Compressed air at **30 bar** enters the cylinder, pushing the piston **downward** on the starting stroke. 4. As the engine rotates, the camshaft-driven distributor sequentially opens the starting air valves of other cylinders, ensuring smooth rotation. 5. Once the engine accelerates to **8–12 rpm** and fuel is injected, the starting air valves are **automatically held closed** by their disc springs. 6. Air supply is cut off by the **starting air shut-off valve** at ~60% of rated speed. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Cold-Start Operation and Safety Interlocks** **Model Solution:** To enable **cold starting** of a large 2-stroke marine engine, the following sequence is performed (typically automatically by the ECS): **Step 1: Pre-Lubrication** - The **pre-lubrication pump** (electrically driven, ~10 kW) circulates heated lube oil through the engine bearings, crosshead, and cylinder lubrication system for **30 minutes** prior to cranking. - This ensures an oil film exists before any metal-to-metal motion. **Step 2: Pre-Heating** - **Jacket cooling water** is maintained at **60–80 °C** using steam or electric heaters. - **Lub oil** is heated to **40–50 °C** to bring viscosity below **350 cSt**. - **Heavy fuel oil** (if used) is heated to **120–140 °C** for proper atomization viscosity (12–15 cSt). **Step 3: Air System Readiness Check** - ECS verifies receiver pressure > **20 bar**, temperature > 5 °C, no leaks. - Starting air valves are tested (open-close cycle) without admitting air. **Step 4: Decompression Mode (if selected)** - For very large engines, the **hydraulic exhaust valve actuator** holds all exhaust valves open. - The engine is motored for 2 revolutions with no compression. **Step 5: First Air Admission** - Starting valves admit 30-bar air to cylinders in firing order. - Crankshaft rotates at **cranking speed (3–6 rpm)**. **Step 6: Fuel Injection Ramp** - Once cranking speed is stable and the engine has rotated through 1–2 full revolutions with air, the fuel pump begins delivery. - **Pilot fuel** is first injected (in some systems) to assist ignition. - Engine accelerates to **firing speed**. **Safety Devices for Cold Start:** | Safety Device | Function | |---------------|----------| | **Starting air failure alarm** (at 18 bar) | Alerts the engineer to refill receivers | | **Auto-block at 15 bar** | Prevents start attempts that would fail | | **Starting air valve leakage detector** | Prevents hot gas backflow into receiver (explosion risk) | | **Flame arrestor** at receiver inlet | Prevents flashback into air system | | **Thermal relief valves** | Prevents receiver damage if compressed air is heated | | **Crankcase mist detector** | Detects oil fog → indicates hot gas leak past liner | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > Students frequently **confuse the role of the pre-lubrication pump with the main lube oil pump**. The pre-lub pump operates **BEFORE** the engine starts and continues for a short period after shutdown. The main lube oil pump operates **only when the engine is running**. Mixing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in ESE. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Q:** Explain in detail the various **auxiliary cold-start aids** used in marine diesel engines. With suitable diagrams, describe the working of **(a)** a coolant/lubricating oil pre-heating system, and **(b)** a flame/glow-plug starting aid. Justify why pre-heating is mandatory for main propulsion engines in polar class vessels. #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Coolant & Lub Oil Pre-Heating System** **Model Solution:** The pre-heating system of a marine diesel engine maintains the engine in a **standby-ready thermal state** so that it can be started at short notice (typically within 5 minutes) from cold conditions. **Components:** 1. **Steam Heater (Primary, Marine Standard)** - Source: Auxiliary boiler steam at **7–10 bar**. - Heat exchanger type: **Shell-and-coil**, with steam in the shell and engine coolant/oil in the coil. - Capacity: Sized to raise engine mass (typically 200–1000 tonnes for main engine) by 30 °C in **2–4 hours**. 2. **Electric Immersion Heater (Secondary / Standby)** - Fitted in the cooling water expansion tank and lub oil sump. - Power rating: **3 kW to 15 kW per heater**, multiple units in parallel. - Thermostatically controlled to maintain setpoint ± 2 °C. 3. **Circulation Pump (Low Capacity)** - Continuously circulates coolant/lub oil through the engine even when stopped, ensuring uniform temperature. - Flow rate: 5–10% of main pump capacity. 4. **Temperature Sensors & Control** - PT-100 RTDs in coolant outlet and lub oil sump. - Display on the **Engine Control Room (ECR) monitor** and alarm if temperature falls below setpoint. **Working Principle:** When the engine is in standby: - The **electric heaters** raise the coolant and lub oil temperatures to **45–60 °C**. - The **circulation pump** continuously moves the heated fluid through the engine, preventing thermal stratification. - The steam heater (in port) supplements heating when shore steam is available, reducing electrical load. **For a Cold Start Sequence:** 1. ECS verifies coolant temp > 40 °C and lub oil temp > 35 °C. 2. If temperatures are below threshold, the start request is **held in abeyance** with an alarm "Engine Not Pre-Heated — Start Inhibited." 3. Once thresholds are met, the start sequence proceeds. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Flame / Glow Plug Starting Aid and Polar-Class Justification** **Model Solution:** **Glow Plug (Used in small/medium 4-stroke indirect-injection engines):** - A **glow plug** is a pencil-shaped heating element fitted in the **pre-chamber** or **swirl chamber** of the cylinder head. - It consists of a coiled **nichrome or platinum resistance wire** encased in a refractory sheath. - On receiving 12 V or 24 V DC, it glows red-hot (800–1000 °C) within 5–10 seconds. - This raises the local air temperature in the pre-chamber, ensuring reliable fuel ignition even at ambient temperatures as low as **−25 °C**. - **Automatic timer:** Glow plugs are typically energized for 5–30 seconds before cranking and remain on for 5–60 seconds after start, then automatically disengage. **Flame Start System (Used in larger 4-stroke engines, e.g., gas engines):** - A small **atomized fuel spray** is ignited by a **spark plug** in a chamber connected to the inlet manifold. - The flame jet heats the incoming air charge to **60–80 °C** before it enters the cylinder. - Commonly used in Wärtsilä gas-fueled engines for dual-fuel cold start. **Why Pre-Heating is Mandatory for Polar-Class Vessels (PC1 to PC7):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polar Class** vessels (per IACS Polar Code) operate in ambient temperatures as low as **−55 °C**. At these temperatures: > > 1. **Lubricant viscosity** of standard marine oil (e.g., SAE 30) can exceed **5000 cSt**, making cranking impossible without heating. > 2. **HFO is solid**, and even diesel fuel (DM-grade) begins to gel at −20 °C. > 3. **Battery capacity** drops by ~50% at −30 °C, making electric start unreliable. > 4. **Steel embrittlement** below −30 °C requires special low-temperature materials. > 5. **Cylinder liner cooling** can cause thermal shock if a cold engine is suddenly loaded. > > Therefore, **class rules** (IACS UR M43) mandate that the engine must be capable of: > - **3 consecutive starts** of 30 seconds each, with **2 successful starts** within 30 minutes of standby initiation. > - Maintained standby at **40 °C coolant and 35 °C lub oil** using **dual-source heating** (steam + electric redundancy). > - **Fuel pre-heating** to bring HFO to 120 °C and diesel to 0 °C pour point. > > Polar-class vessels therefore typically have **engine rooms heated to 5–10 °C** above ambient, **trace heating** on all fuel and oil lines, and **insulated engine casings** to retain heat during standby. --- ## 🧠 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in ESE:** > > 1. **Wrong Pressure Values** — Students often write "starting air pressure is 25 bar" or "20 bar." For **2-stroke main engines**, it is **30 bar**. For **4-stroke auxiliary**, it is **15–20 bar** (after reducing valve). Mixing these costs **1 mark**. > > 2. **Confusing Pre-Lube Pump with Main Lube Pump** — Pre-lub operates **before start**; main lube operates **only when engine is running**. A common 2-mark deduction. > > 3. **Forgetting the 12-Start SOLAS Requirement** — Many students omit this. It is a **direct 1-mark question** almost every year. > > 4. **Mixing Up Glow Plug and Spark Plug** — A glow plug heats air; a spark plug ignites a fuel-air mixture. The diesel cycle has **no spark plug** in normal operation; glow plugs are aids for cold start only. > > 5. **Forgetting Safety Devices** — Every starting air system answer must mention **flame arrestor, relief valve, and leakage detection**. Omission costs 2 marks. > > 6. **Not Stating Standards** — Mentioning "per SOLAS / IACS UR M43 / ISO 3046" demonstrates depth and earns bonus impression marks. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Cold starting** = starting a diesel engine at or below ambient temperature (≤ 30 °C standard). - **Primary methods:** Compressed air (marine standard), electric, hydraulic, manual. - **Supplementary aids:** Pre-heating (coolant, lub oil, fuel), glow plug, pilot fuel, decompression, ether injection, flame start. - **Main starting air pressure:** **30 bar (2-stroke)**, **15–20 bar (4-stroke aux)**. - **Air receiver capacity:** Must allow **12 consecutive starts** (SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 44). - **Pre-lubrication duration:** **≥ 30 minutes** before first start. - **Pre-heat temperatures:** Coolant ≥ 40 °C, Lub oil ≥ 35 °C, Fuel 120–140 °C (HFO). - **Decompression starting** = exhaust valves held open for first 2 revolutions to ease cranking. - **Glow plug** = electric resistance heater in pre-chamber; used in 4-stroke indirect injection engines. - **Pilot fuel** = high-cetane auxiliary fuel injected at cold start; auto-disengaged. - **IACS UR M43** mandates 3 starts × 30 sec with 2 successes within 30 minutes. - **Polar-class vessels** require pre-heating to ambient + 5 °C min, dual-source heating, and trace-heated fuel lines. - **Safety devices** mandatory: relief valve, bursting disc, flame arrestor, leakage detector, low-pressure alarm. - **Cold wall heat loss** can reduce compression temperature by **30–50%**, preventing auto-ignition. - **Adiabatic compression temperature** $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ must exceed CET (~250 °C). - **Cranking torque** is provided by the work done by starting air on the piston: $\tau \approx 0.6$–$1.0 \times$ normal torque. - **Modern ECS** sequences pre-lub, pre-heat, decompression, air admission, and fuel injection automatically. - **KTU 2024 emphasis** is on **system-level understanding** — students must explain the *interconnection* of components, not just list them.

topics.title: Study of Internal Combustion Engine and Its Components
